topics.title: Introduction to Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Electric Vehicle (EV)?

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a vehicle that is propelled by one or more **electric motors**, drawing electrical energy from a rechargeable on-board battery pack or another energy storage device. Unlike Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles, EVs convert electrical energy into mechanical energy through electromagnetic principles, with **zero tailpipe emissions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An electric vehicle is a road vehicle whose propulsion is supplied wholly or substantially by an electric machine, drawing current from a rechargeable storage battery, fuel cell, photovoltaic array, or other onboard source of electrical energy, as per standard automotive engineering classifications.

## 1.2 Formal Classification of Electric Vehicles

KTU 2024 syllabus mandates understanding the following strict taxonomy:

| Abbreviation | Full Form | Propulsion Source | Grid Connection |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | Battery only (100% electric) | Plug-in |
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Engine + Battery (no plug) | No plug |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Engine + Battery (larger) | Plug-in |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen Fuel Cell | Hydrogen station |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Bucket of Water" Model

Think of an EV like **a bucket carrying its own water source**:
- The **battery** = the bucket (stores energy).
- The **electric motor** = the tap/faucet (controls how fast energy is released).
- The **road wheels** = the water flow's destination (kinetic energy).
- The **regenerative brakes** = a clever nozzle that catches spilled water and pours it *back* into the bucket.

In an ICE car, fuel is burned and lost as heat (irreversible). In an EV, energy can be **recycled** ŌĆö when you brake, the motor runs *backwards as a generator* and pumps energy back into the battery.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> These standard metrics are **always** tested in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

- **Specific Energy** (Wh/kg): Energy stored per unit mass of battery.
- **Energy Density** (Wh/L): Energy stored per unit volume.
- **Specific Power** (W/kg): Peak power deliverable per unit mass.
- **State of Charge (SoC)**: Percentage of energy remaining in the battery.
- **State of Health (SoH)**: Percentage of original capacity still available.
- **Range Anxiety**: Driver's fear that the battery will deplete before reaching destination/charger.

## 1.5 EV vs ICE ŌĆö Head-to-Head Comparison

| Parameter | ICE Vehicle | Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Energy Source | Petrol/Diesel/CNG | Grid electricity |
| Energy Conversion Efficiency | **20ŌĆō35%** | **85ŌĆō95%** |
| Number of Moving Parts | ~2,000+ | ~20 (mostly in motor/gearbox) |
| Tailpipe Emissions | COŌéé, NOŌéō, CO | **Zero** |
| Noise Level | 60ŌĆō80 dB | **< 50 dB** (near silent) |
| Peak Torque Availability | Above 1500ŌĆō2000 rpm | **0 rpm** (instant) |
| Refueling/Recharging Time | 3ŌĆō5 min | 30 min (DC fast) ŌĆō 8 h (AC home) |
| Maintenance | Frequent (oil, filters) | Minimal (brake pads only) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Energy-Flow Architecture of an EV

An EV powertrain follows this closed energy loop:

**Grid AC ŌåÆ Charger (AC-DC Converter) ŌåÆ Battery Pack (DC storage) ŌåÆ Inverter (DC-AC) ŌåÆ Electric Motor (AC, traction) ŌåÆ Wheels (Mechanical KE)**

When the vehicle decelerates, energy flows in **reverse** through the inverter, which now acts as a rectifier, charging the battery. This is called **Regenerative Braking**.

## 2.2 The Three Core Subsystems

1. **Energy Storage Subsystem (ESS):** The battery pack, Battery Management System (BMS), and sometimes supercapacitors.
2. **Power Electronics Subsystem (PES):** The inverter, DC-DC converter, on-board charger, and motor controller.
3. **Electric Traction Subsystem (ETS):** The motor (BLDC, PMSM, Induction, or SRM) coupled via a single-speed or multi-speed gearbox to the wheels.

## 2.3 Why Electric Motors? ŌĆö The "Why" Behind EVs

The traction motor must produce **high starting torque at zero rpm** to accelerate the vehicle from standstill. Internal combustion engines cannot do this efficiently ŌĆö they require a complex multi-gear transmission and a clutch/torque converter to avoid stalling. Electric motors, by contrast, deliver **maximum torque at zero rpm**, which is why EVs can use a **single-speed transmission** in most cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**:
> *"Why do electric vehicles use single-speed transmissions while ICE vehicles require multi-speed gearboxes?"*
> **Answer:** Electric motors deliver peak torque at 0 rpm and maintain a wide constant-power region, eliminating the need for gear shifts to maintain tractive effort across the speed range.

## 2.4 The Four Major Performance Requirements for EV Traction

These define the **design envelope** of any EV:

1. **High Instantaneous Power** ŌĆö for overtaking and hill climbing.
2. **High Power Density (W/kg)** ŌĆö to reduce vehicle mass.
3. **High Torque at Low Speeds** ŌĆö for launch and grades.
4. **Wide Constant-Power Speed Range** ŌĆö for cruising at high speed without gear changes.
5. **High Efficiency Across the Drive Cycle** ŌĆö for range maximization.
6. **Regenerative Braking Capability** ŌĆö for energy recovery during deceleration.
7. **Robustness & Reliability** ŌĆö for 10+ year service life.
8. **Affordability** ŌĆö for mass-market adoption.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these equations. They appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem in Module 1.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | Total tractive force equation | N |
| 2 | $F_t = m \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot C_d \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot \delta \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | Expanded tractive force | N |
| 3 | $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ | Tractive power at wheels | W |
| 4 | $P_m = \dfrac{P_t}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}}$ | Required motor power | W |
| 5 | $T_m = \dfrac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}}$ | Required motor torque | N┬Ęm |
| 6 | $E_{batt} = \dfrac{d \cdot E_{100km}}{100}$ | Battery energy for range $d$ | Wh |
| 7 | $E_{range} = \dfrac{C_{batt} \cdot V_{nom}}{\eta_{sys}}$ | Available range energy | Wh |
| 8 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{batt}$ | Overall drivetrain efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 - \dfrac{1}{C_{batt}} \int_0^t I(\tau) \, d\tau$ | State of Charge dynamics | % |
| 10 | $a = \dfrac{F_t - F_{resist}}{m \cdot \delta}$ | Vehicle acceleration | m/s┬▓ |
| 11 | $E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$ | Recoverable kinetic energy | J |
| 12 | $P_{regen,max} = \eta_{regen} \cdot F_{brake} \cdot v$ | Max regen power | W |

**Symbol Legend (KTU-Standard):**
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg), $g$ = 9.81 m/s┬▓, $f_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient
- $\rho$ = air density = **1.225 kg/m┬│** (at sea level, 15┬░C)
- $A$ = frontal area (m┬▓), $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless)
- $v$ = velocity (m/s), $\theta$ = road gradient (rad)
- $\delta$ = mass factor (rotational inertia), $r_w$ = wheel radius (m)
- $G$ = gear ratio, $C_{batt}$ = battery capacity (Ah), $V_{nom}$ = nominal voltage (V)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The tractive force equation (Formula #2) is used in:
- **Automotive design studios** to size motors (Tata, Mahindra, BYD).
- **Drive-cycle simulation software** like ADVISOR, MATLAB/Simulink.
- **Battery sizing** for delivery fleets (Amazon, Flipkart EV logistics).
- **Charging infrastructure planning** by government agencies (FAME-II India scheme).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Tractive Force Equation (KTU Most-Important)

The total tractive force $F_t$ required at the wheel-road contact must overcome all four resistive forces. We derive each component.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Rolling Resistance Force ($F_r$)

This is due to tire deformation as it rolls on the road. It is proportional to the normal force on the wheel.

$$F_r = m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \cdot f_r$$

**Logic:** The vertical load on each tire equals $mg\cos\theta$ on an incline of angle $\theta$. Multiplying by the coefficient of rolling friction $f_r$ gives the resisting force opposing motion.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)

Air resistance increases with the square of velocity. The standard form is:

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot C_d \cdot v^2$$

**Logic:** Dynamic pressure of air is $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$. Multiplying by frontal area $A$ and the dimensionless drag coefficient $C_d$ gives the drag force.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Hill Climbing Force ($F_{hc}$)

When on an incline, gravity pulls the vehicle backwards along the slope.

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Logic:** The component of weight $mg$ parallel to a slope of angle $\theta$ is $mg\sin\theta$. This must be overcome to climb.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Linear Acceleration Force ($F_{la}$)

Newton's second law: $F = ma$. Including the rotational inertia of rotating parts (wheels, motor rotor, gears), we use the **mass factor** $\delta$ (typically 1.05ŌĆō1.10 for EVs):

$$F_{la} = m \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Logic:** Pure $F = ma$ is for point masses. Since wheels and rotors spin, their angular momentum adds effective inertia. We lump it as $\delta m$ where $\delta > 1$.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Summing All Forces

$$F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$$

Substituting all four expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= m \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot \cos\theta \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot C_d \cdot v^2 \\
&\quad + \; m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta \; + \; m \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental EV design equation** of Module 1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Motor Power and Torque Sizing

### Step 1 ŌĆö Tractive Power at Wheels

Power is force times velocity:

$$P_t = F_t \cdot v$$

**Logic:** When force $F_t$ moves the vehicle at velocity $v$, the work done per second is $F_t \cdot v$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Motor Shaft Power (Account for Drivetrain Losses)

Energy is lost in the motor windings ($I^2R$), inverter switching, and gear meshing. Let $\eta_{motor}$ be motor efficiency, $\eta_{trans}$ be transmission efficiency:

$$P_m = \frac{P_t}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}}$$

**Logic:** To deliver $P_t$ at the wheels, the motor must produce more power to compensate for losses. Division by the efficiency product (a number < 1) inflates $P_m$ above $P_t$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Motor Torque Sizing

Torque at the wheel is $F_t \cdot r_w$. The motor torque, after gear ratio $G$ amplification, must equal this (accounting for gear efficiency $\eta_{gear}$):

$$T_m = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}}$$

**Logic:** A gear ratio $G > 1$ (reduction gearbox) multiplies motor torque to wheel torque. The factor $G \cdot \eta_{gear}$ reflects both the mechanical advantage and the frictional loss.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Battery Energy Sizing for a Given Range

### Step 1 ŌĆö Energy Consumed per km

Let $E_{100km}$ = energy required to travel 100 km. Then per km:

$$E_{km} = \frac{E_{100km}}{100}$$

**Logic:** Direct proportionality ŌĆö if 100 km needs $E_{100km}$ Wh, then 1 km needs $E_{100km}/100$ Wh.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Total Battery Energy for Range $d$

For a target range of $d$ kilometres, the battery must store:

$$E_{batt} = d \cdot E_{km} = \frac{d \cdot E_{100km}}{100}$$

**Logic:** Multiply per-km consumption by total kilometres desired.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Battery Capacity in Ampere-hours

Given nominal battery voltage $V_{nom}$ and system efficiency $\eta_{sys}$:

$$C_{batt} = \frac{E_{batt}}{V_{nom}} = \frac{d \cdot E_{100km}}{100 \cdot V_{nom} \cdot \eta_{sys}}$$

**Logic:** Power ├Ś time = energy. At voltage $V_{nom}$, capacity in Ah = energy in Wh / voltage in V. The $\eta_{sys}$ factor accounts for losses in charging/discharging cycles.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A BEV has the following specifications:
> - Mass $m = 1500$ kg, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m┬▓, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$
> - Rolling resistance $f_r = 0.015$, road gradient $\theta = 0┬░$ (flat)
> - Desired constant cruising speed $v = 27.78$ m/s (100 km/h)
> - Mass factor $\delta = 1.08$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{sys} = 0.85$
> - Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m┬│
>
> **Find:** (a) Total tractive force (b) Tractive power at wheels (c) Required motor power (d) Battery energy needed for 200 km range if $E_{100km} = 15$ kWh.

### Solution:

**(a) Total Tractive Force**

Since $v$ is constant, $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$. On flat road, $\theta = 0┬░$, so $\sin\theta = 0$ and $\cos\theta = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= m \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot C_d \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} \\
F_t &= (1500)(9.81)(0.015)(1) + \tfrac{1}{2}(1.225)(2.2)(0.30)(27.78)^2 + 0 + 0 \\
F_t &= 220.725 + 311.78 + 0 + 0 \\
F_t &= 532.5 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Tractive Power at Wheels**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= F_t \cdot v = 532.5 \times 27.78 \\
P_t &= 14,792.85 \text{ W} \approx 14.79 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Required Motor Power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_m &= \frac{P_t}{\eta_{sys}} = \frac{14,792.85}{0.85} \\
P_m &= 17,403.35 \text{ W} \approx 17.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Battery Energy for 200 km Range**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{batt} &= \frac{d \cdot E_{100km}}{100} = \frac{200 \times 15}{100} \\
E_{batt} &= 30 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $F_t = 532.5$ N, $P_t = 14.79$ kW, $P_m = 17.4$ kW, $E_{batt} = 30$ kWh.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Choice of EV Component

| Subsystem | Primary Design Driver | KTU Key Trade-off |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Battery Pack | Range, Cost | Energy density vs. Power density |
| Electric Motor | Acceleration, Top speed | Torque density vs. Efficiency |
| Inverter | Switching speed, Cost | Si vs. SiC vs. GaN semiconductors |
| Gearbox | Cost, NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) | Single-speed vs. Multi-speed |
| Thermal Management | Cycle life, Safety | Air vs. Liquid cooling |
| BMS | Safety, Longevity | Cell balancing speed vs. Cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EV Powertrain Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[GRID AC SUPPLY] --> A2[ON BOARD CHARGER]
    A2 --> A3[BATTERY PACK DC BUS]
    A3 --> A4[DC DC CONVERTER]
    A4 --> A5[AUX 12V LOAD]
    A3 --> A6[INVERTER VSI]
    A6 --> A7[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM]
    A7 --> A8[GEARBOX SINGLE SPEED]
    A8 --> A9[WHEELS]
    A9 --> A10[REGEN BRAKING FEEDBACK]
    A10 --> A6
    A3 --> A11[BMS BATTERY MGMT]
    A11 --> A12[VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT VCU]
    A6 --> A12
    A7 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[ACCEL PEDAL INPUT]
    A12 --> A14[BRAKE PEDAL INPUT]
    A12 --> A15[DASHBOARD DISPLAY]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Energy flows from grid ŌåÆ charger ŌåÆ battery ŌåÆ inverter ŌåÆ motor ŌåÆ gearbox ŌåÆ wheels.
- The **dashed feedback arrows** show the regenerative braking loop: kinetic energy from wheels flows back through the motor (now acting as a generator) ŌåÆ inverter (now rectifying) ŌåÆ battery.
- The **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** acts as the central brain, reading driver inputs and commanding the inverter and BMS.

---

## 4.2 EV Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[ELECTRIC VEHICLES EV] --> B1[BEV BATTERY EV]
    A0 --> B2[HEV HYBRID EV]
    A0 --> B3[PHEV PLUG IN HYBRID]
    A0 --> B4[FCEV FUEL CELL EV]
    B2 --> C1[MILD HEV 48V]
    B2 --> C2[FULL HEV NO PLUG]
    B2 --> C3[STRONG HEV HIGH VOLT]
    B3 --> D1[PHEV BLENDED MODE]
    B3 --> D2[EREV EXTENDED RANGE]
    B4 --> E1[PEMFC PROTON EXCHANGE]
    B4 --> E2[SOFC SOLID OXIDE]
```

---

## 4.3 Tractive Force vs. Velocity Characteristic (KTU Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[VEHICLE START V NEAR 0] --> F2[PEAK TRACTIVE FORCE REQUIRED TO OVERCOME INERTIA AND GRADE]
    F2 --> F3[CONSTANT TORQUE REGION 0 TO V BASE]
    F3 --> F4[CONSTANT POWER REGION V BASE TO V MAX]
    F4 --> F5[CONSTANT POWER DECAY FALL OFF]
    F5 --> F6[CRUISING AT V MAX]
    G1[ROLLING RESISTANCE NEARLY CONSTANT] --> F3
    G2[AERODYNAMIC DRAG GROWS AS V SQUARED] --> F4
    G3[HILL CLIMB DEMAND] --> F2
```

**Engineering interpretation:**
- **0 to v_base:** Motor operates in **constant torque** region (peak torque applied, current limited).
- **v_base to v_max:** Motor operates in **constant power** region (voltage-limited, flux-weakening active).
- Above v_max, power decays cubically with speed (no more tractive capability).

---

## 4.4 Comparative Drive Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0[DRIVE CYCLE] --> H1[UDC URBAN DRIVING]
    H0 --> H2[ECE EXTRA URBAN]
    H0 --> H3[HWFET HIGHWAY]
    H0 --> H4[FTP75 US STANDARD]
    H0 --> H5[WLTP GLOBAL]
    H0 --> H6[INDIA MIDCYCLE]
    H1 --> I1[LOW SPEED FREQUENT STOPS]
    H2 --> I2[MEDIUM SPEED SUBURBAN]
    H3 --> I3[HIGH SPEED STEADY CRUISE]
    H4 --> I4[COLD START PHASE]
    H5 --> I5[FOUR PHASES LOW MED HIGH EXTRA HIGH]
    H6 --> I6[TWO PHASES CITY AND HIGHWAY]
```

This matters for KTU because **range depends on the drive cycle** ŌĆö aggressive cycles consume 30ŌĆō40% more energy than steady cruising.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**Define an Electric Vehicle. List any four advantages of EVs over conventional ICE vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**
An Electric Vehicle is a vehicle that uses one or more electric motors for propulsion, drawing energy from a rechargeable on-board battery or fuel cell, with no internal combustion engine as the primary power source.

**Four advantages:**
1. **Zero tailpipe emissions** ŌĆö environmentally friendly, reduces urban air pollution.
2. **Higher efficiency** ŌĆö 85ŌĆō95% compared to 20ŌĆō35% for ICE vehicles.
3. **Lower operating cost** ŌĆö electricity cheaper than petrol/diesel per km.
4. **Reduced maintenance** ŌĆö fewer moving parts (~20 vs. ~2,000 in ICE).
5. *Optional bonus point:* Instant peak torque delivery from 0 rpm.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
**Differentiate between BEV, HEV, PHEV, and FCEV with respect to energy source and grid dependency.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form Required):**

| Parameter | BEV | HEV | PHEV | FCEV |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Energy Source | Battery only | Engine + small battery | Engine + large battery | Hydrogen fuel cell |
| Grid Charging | Yes (mandatory) | No | Yes (optional but larger) | No (hydrogen refueling) |
| Range | 200ŌĆō600 km | 800ŌĆō1000 km | 600ŌĆō800 km | 400ŌĆō700 km |
| Emissions | Zero | Low | LowŌĆōMedium | Zero (only water vapour) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (Choice 1) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **major components of an electric vehicle powertrain** and describe the **function of each subsystem**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **tractive force equation** for a vehicle moving on a road with gradient, and explain the significance of each term. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The EV powertrain consists of three primary subsystems:

1. **Energy Storage Subsystem (ESS):**
   - Comprises the high-voltage battery pack (typically Lithium-ion: NMC, LFP, or NCA chemistry).
   - Battery Management System (BMS) monitors cell voltage, temperature, SoC, and balances cells.
   - Provides DC voltage to the inverter bus.

2. **Power Electronics Subsystem (PES):**
   - **Inverter:** Converts DC from battery to 3-phase AC for the motor (during driving) and AC to DC (during regen).
   - **DC-DC Converter:** Steps down high voltage (e.g., 400 V) to 12 V for auxiliary loads.
   - **On-Board Charger (OBC):** Converts grid AC to DC for battery charging.

3. **Electric Traction Subsystem (ETS):**
   - **Traction Motor:** Converts electrical energy to mechanical torque (PMSM, BLDC, Induction, or SRM).
   - **Transmission:** Single-speed reduction gearbox in most EVs.
   - **Differential:** Distributes torque to left/right wheels.

The **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** orchestrates all subsystems based on driver inputs.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

The tractive force equation derivation is given in **Section 3.1** above. The full expansion:

$$F_t = m \cdot g \cdot f_r \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot C_d \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot \delta \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Significance of each term:**
- **Term 1** ($mgf_r\cos\theta$): Rolling resistance ŌĆö energy lost in tire deformation; dominant at low speeds.
- **Term 2** ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A C_d v^2$): Aerodynamic drag ŌĆö dominant at high speeds (above 60 km/h).
- **Term 3** ($mg\sin\theta$): Hill-climbing force ŌĆö overcomes gravitational pull on inclines.
- **Term 4** ($m\delta \frac{dv}{dt}$): Acceleration force ŌĆö overcomes linear and rotational inertia.

The equation is used to size the motor and battery for target performance (acceleration, top speed, gradeability).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **different types of electric vehicles** based on the degree of electrification. Give one example application for each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A BEV has the following specifications:
- Curb weight = 1,200 kg; $C_d = 0.28$; $A = 2.0$ m┬▓; $f_r = 0.012$
- $\eta_{motor} = 0.92$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.95$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m┬│
- Desired top speed = 120 km/h on a 5% grade.

Compute: (i) tractive force at top speed, (ii) tractive power, (iii) required motor power. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Type | Description | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Micro-Hybrid (Start-Stop)** | Engine auto-stops at signals, restarts on clutch | Maruti Suzuki Celerio (older) |
| **Mild Hybrid (48V)** | Small electric motor assists engine; cannot drive alone | Audi A6 48V |
| **Full Hybrid (HEV)** | Can run on electric-only mode at low speed; no plug | Toyota Camry Hybrid |
| **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)** | Larger battery (15ŌĆō20 kWh); 30ŌĆō60 km electric range; plug-in | Volvo XC90 T8 |
| **Battery EV (BEV)** | 100% electric; 200ŌĆō600 km range | Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV |
| **Fuel Cell EV (FCEV)** | Hydrogen fuel cell; refuel in 5 min | Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $m = 1200$ kg, $C_d = 0.28$, $A = 2.0$ m┬▓, $f_r = 0.012$, $\eta_{motor} = 0.92$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.95$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m┬│, $v = 120$ km/h = 33.33 m/s, grade = 5% ŌåÆ $\tan\theta = 0.05$ ŌåÆ $\theta = 2.86┬░$.

**Compute $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$:**
$\sin(2.86┬░) = 0.0499 \approx 0.05$, $\cos(2.86┬░) = 0.9988 \approx 1$.

**Steady state (top speed)** ŌåÆ $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$.

**(i) Tractive Force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= (1200)(9.81)(0.012)(1) + \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(2.0)(0.28)(33.33)^2 + (1200)(9.81)(0.05) + 0 \\
F_t &= 141.26 + 373.32 + 588.6 + 0 \\
F_t &= 1103.18 \text{ N} \approx 1.10 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Tractive Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= F_t \cdot v = 1103.18 \times 33.33 \\
P_t &= 36,768.39 \text{ W} \approx 36.77 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Required Motor Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_m &= \frac{P_t}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{trans}} = \frac{36,768.39}{0.92 \times 0.95} \\
P_m &= \frac{36,768.39}{0.874} \\
P_m &= 42,070.7 \text{ W} \approx 42.07 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $F_t = 1.10$ kN, $P_t = 36.77$ kW, $P_m = 42.07$ kW.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $mg\sin\theta$ term on a grade:** Many students set it to zero, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing $v$ in km/h and m/s:** Always convert to m/s before using $F_t \cdot v$ formula. 120 km/h = 33.33 m/s.
> 3. **Omitting drivetrain efficiency:** If the question asks for **motor power**, you **must** divide by $\eta$. Showing only $P_t$ loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong sign convention for $\cos\theta$:** On a flat road, $\cos 0┬░ = 1$ (not 0). Students mistakenly drop the term.
> 5. **Forgetting the $C_d$** when computing drag ŌĆö this is a silent killer of partial marks.
> 6. **Writing $v^2$ instead of $v$ in power equation** ŌĆö this is a KTU board pet peeve.
> 7. **No units in the final answer** ŌĆö KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **EV = Electric Motor + Battery + Inverter + Control.** Memorize this mantra.
- [ ] **BEV is 100% electric**; **HEV has no plug**; **PHEV has both engine and plug**; **FCEV uses hydrogen**.
- [ ] **Efficiency of EV (85ŌĆō95%) is ~3├Ś that of ICE (20ŌĆō35%).** This is a guaranteed KTU question.
- [ ] **Electric motors deliver peak torque at 0 rpm** ŌĆö the reason EVs skip multi-gear transmissions.
- [ ] **The four tractive forces** are: rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, hill climbing, and acceleration.
- [ ] **Drag force scales with $v^2$** ŌĆö dominates above 60 km/h. **Rolling resistance** dominates below 60 km/h.
- [ ] **Battery energy (kWh) = Capacity (Ah) ├Ś Voltage (V) ├Ę 1000.**
- [ ] **Regenerative braking** can recover 10ŌĆō30% of driving energy in urban cycles.
- [ ] **State of Charge (SoC)** is dynamic; it is integrated current over time, normalized by capacity.
- [ ] **Mass factor $\delta$** (1.05ŌĆō1.10) accounts for rotational inertia of wheels and motor.
- [ ] **Standard air density** is **$\rho = 1.225$ kg/m┬│** at sea level, 15┬░C ŌĆö use this in all numericals unless stated otherwise.
- [ ] **Always convert km/h to m/s** by multiplying by 5/18.
- [ ] **The tractive force equation is the design foundation** of every EV ŌĆö master it before attempting Module 2.
- [ ] **Remember the VCU** is the brain; the **BMS** is the battery's guardian; the **Inverter** is the muscle interface.
- [ ] **WLTP and MIDC** are the relevant drive cycles for India and global KTU problems.

---

**End of Module 1 ŌĆö Introduction to Electric Vehicles ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Social and Environmental Importance of Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Social and Environmental Importance of Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Vehicle (EV):** A vehicle whose drivetrain is powered wholly or partially by an electric machine, drawing energy from an off-board or on-board electrochemical storage device (typically a lithium-ion battery), and whose propulsion energy conversion chain produces **zero tailpipe emissions**.

From a **social** standpoint, EVs reshape urban mobility, reduce dependence on fossil-fuel geopolitics, democratize personal transport through lower operating costs, and improve public health by lowering urban air pollution.

From an **environmental** standpoint, EVs decouple mobility from petroleum refining and tailpipe emissions, lowering **greenhouse gases (GHG)**, **NOx**, **particulate matter (PM2.5/PM10)**, and **noise pollution** across the vehicle life cycle ŌĆö particularly when charged on a decarbonized grid.

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups this importance into three interconnected pillars:

1. **Environmental sustainability** ŌĆö emissions, climate, noise.
2. **Energy security & economics** ŌĆö oil import reduction, total cost of ownership.
3. **Social equity & public health** ŌĆö air quality, job creation, accessibility.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Soda Straw vs. Chimney" Analogy**
>
> Imagine every petrol/diesel car as a small mobile chimney puffing exhaust directly into your street. An Electric Vehicle is like removing that chimney from the curb and connecting all of them to one giant, distant, filterable factory chimney (the power plant). Even better ŌĆö when the grid goes solar or wind, the chimney disappears entirely, and the car is effectively breathing clean air while you drive.

Another way to think of it:

| Aspect | Conventional ICE Car | Electric Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel source | Refined crude oil (imported) | Electricity (domestic mix) |
| Emissions location | At the street (tailpipe) | At the source (power plant stack / renewable) |
| Noise profile | Combustion + gear noise | Near-silent (just tire/road noise) |
| Maintenance | Oil, filters, clutches | Fewer moving parts |

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The following **bold** metrics are universally accepted benchmarks used by KTU examiners and the Ministry of Heavy Industries, Government of India:

- **Tailpipe COŌéé emissions of a typical petrol car: Ōēł 120ŌĆō180 g/km**.
- **Tailpipe emissions of a Battery EV (BEV): 0 g/km**.
- **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emission reduction potential of EVs in India: 25ŌĆō40%** versus petrol ICE (when grid is coal-heavy), and **up to 80%** with renewable charging.
- **WHO-recommended PM2.5 annual limit: 5 ┬Ąg/m┬│** ŌĆö most Indian cities exceed this by 5ŌĆō10├Ś.
- **EV efficiency: Ōēł 85ŌĆō90% (motor + inverter)**, while ICE efficiency is only **20ŌĆō35%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar-Wise Breakdown of Social & Environmental Importance

### 2.1.1 Environmental Pillar

**a) Reduction in Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions**

- Combustion of 1 litre of petrol releases Ōēł **2.31 kg of COŌéé**.
- A typical Indian passenger car runs Ōēł **12,000 km/year** with mileage Ōēł 15 km/L ŌåÆ uses **800 L/year** ŌåÆ emits Ōēł **1,848 kg COŌéé/year**.
- A comparable EV consumes Ōēł **15 kWh/100 km** ŌåÆ **1,800 kWh/year**.
- Even with India's coal-heavy grid (~0.82 kg COŌéé/kWh), EV emissions Ōēł **1,476 kg COŌéé/year** ŌåÆ **~20% reduction**.
- With renewable charging ŌåÆ **~70ŌĆō80% reduction**.

**b) Reduction in Local Air Pollutants**

- EVs eliminate **NOx**, **CO**, **HC**, and **PM2.5** from urban streets ŌĆö the leading causes of respiratory illness.
- Cities like Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru gain the most, since vehicular tailpipes contribute **30ŌĆō40% of urban PM2.5**.

**c) Noise Pollution**

- ICE cars at idle: Ōēł **70ŌĆō80 dB**; at highway speeds: **75ŌĆō85 dB**.
- EVs at idle: **< 30 dB** (artificial AVAS sound added at low speeds for pedestrian safety).
- Even at 80 km/h, EVs are **3ŌĆō5 dB quieter** than ICE equivalents.

**d) Resource Use & End-of-Life**

- EV batteries are recyclable (Li, Co, Ni recovery rates **> 90%** in modern hydrometallurgical plants).
- Second-life use of EV batteries in stationary storage extends usefulness by **8ŌĆō10 more years**.

---

### 2.1.2 Social Pillar

**a) Public Health Improvement**

- WHO estimates **1.67 million deaths/year in India attributable to air pollution**.
- Vehicular PM2.5 is a major contributor ŌåÆ EV adoption directly reduces mortality and healthcare costs.

**b) Energy Security**

- India imports **> 85% of its crude oil** ŌĆö EV adoption reduces forex outflow.
- Electricity can be generated domestically (coal, solar, wind, hydro, nuclear).

**c) Economic Equity & Job Creation**

- EVs have **~40% fewer moving parts** ŌåÆ lower maintenance, longer service intervals.
- Estimated **10 million direct/indirect jobs** in EV manufacturing, charging infrastructure, and battery recycling by 2030 (NITI Aayog).

**d) Urban Comfort & Social Inclusion**

- Reduced noise improves quality of life, especially in dense residential areas.
- Lower per-km running cost (Ōé╣1.0/km EV vs Ōé╣7ŌĆō8/km petrol) democratizes mobility for lower-income groups.

---

### 2.1.3 Economic Pillar

- **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):** EVs reach TCO parity with ICE vehicles in India at **~4ŌĆō5 years** for 4-wheelers and **~2ŌĆō3 years** for 2-wheelers.
- Lower fuel + maintenance + tax incentives (FAME-II subsidy: **Ōé╣10,000/kWh** for 4W, **Ōé╣10,000/kWh** for 2W).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-ready formulas** students must memorize. Note: All absolute-value expressions use `\vert` instead of `|` to keep markdown tables safe.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tailpipe COŌéé (ICE) | $E_{CO_2}^{ICE} = m_f \times k_f$ | kg/km | $m_f$ = fuel rate (L/km); $k_f$ Ōēł 2.31 kg/L for petrol, 2.68 kg/L for diesel |
| 2 | Tailpipe COŌéé (EV) | $E_{CO_2}^{EV} = 0$ | kg/km | Zero tailpipe by definition |
| 3 | Well-to-Wheel COŌéé (EV) | $E_{WTW}^{EV} = E_c \times k_{grid}$ | kg/km | $E_c$ = electricity consumption (kWh/km); $k_{grid}$ = grid emission factor (kg COŌéé/kWh) |
| 4 | WTW COŌéé (ICE) | $E_{WTW}^{ICE} = E_{CO_2}^{ICE} + E_{upstream}$ | kg/km | $E_{upstream}$ Ōēł 0.4ŌĆō0.5 kg/km (refining + transport) |
| 5 | % Emission Reduction | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{E_{WTW}^{ICE} - E_{WTW}^{EV}}{E_{WTW}^{ICE}} \times 100\%$ | % | Primary metric in KTU problems |
| 6 | EV Efficiency | $\eta_{EV} = \dfrac{\eta_{bat} \times \eta_{inv} \times \eta_{motor}}{1}$ | ratio | Typical 0.85ŌĆō0.90 |
| 7 | ICE Efficiency | $\eta_{ICE} = \dfrac{\text{Mechanical output}}{\text{Fuel energy input}}$ | ratio | Typical 0.20ŌĆō0.35 |
| 8 | Energy equivalence | $1\,L\,petrol \approx 9.1\,kWh$ | ŌĆö | Lower Heating Value (LHV) |
| 9 | Noise penalty factor | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$ | dB | Inverse-square law for distance |
| 10 | Cost per km (EV) | $C_{EV} = \dfrac{C_{elec}}{R_{EV}}$ | Ōé╣/km | $R_{EV}$ Ōēł 6ŌĆō8 km/kWh |
| 11 | Cost per km (ICE) | $C_{ICE} = \dfrac{C_{fuel}}{M_{ICE}}$ | Ōé╣/km | $M_{ICE}$ = mileage (km/L) |
| 12 | Payback period | $T_{pb} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{capex}}{C_{ICE} - C_{EV}}$ | years | Capital-vs-operating tradeoff |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not just academic ŌĆö they are used by:

- **OEMs** (Tata Motors, Mahindra, Ola Electric) for homologation certification under AIS-156.
- **NITI Aayog & CEA** for grid-load forecasting and EV-roadmap modelling.
- **BIS & ARAI** for testing and certification of EVs in India.
- **Urban planners** for charging-infrastructure sizing and noise-mapping.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Percentage WTW Emission Reduction (EV vs Petrol ICE)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU Module 1 examinations. Memorize the chain.

**Given:**

- Petrol ICE fuel consumption: $m_f$ = 0.067 L/km (i.e., 15 km/L)
- Petrol COŌéé factor: $k_f$ = 2.31 kg/L
- Upstream emission factor (refining + transport): $E_{up}$ = 0.45 kg/km
- EV electricity consumption: $E_c$ = 0.15 kWh/km
- India grid emission factor: $k_{grid}$ = 0.82 kg COŌéé/kWh

**Find:** Percentage emission reduction when switching from ICE to EV.

---

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute tailpipe COŌéé for ICE per km.**

We start with the tailpipe-only emissions, since this is what comes out of the exhaust pipe directly into the urban air.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{tail}^{ICE} &= m_f \times k_f \\[4pt]
&= 0.067 \; \tfrac{L}{km} \times 2.31 \; \tfrac{kg}{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute and evaluate the arithmetic.**

We perform the multiplication: $0.067 \times 2.31 = 0.067 \times 2 + 0.067 \times 0.31 = 0.134 + 0.02077 = 0.15477$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{tail}^{ICE} &\approx 0.1548 \; \tfrac{kg}{km} \; \text{of CO}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Add upstream (well-to-tank) emissions to obtain WTW ICE.**

The ICE vehicle's *true* climate impact includes the energy used to extract crude oil, refine it, and transport it. Add the upstream factor.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{WTW}^{ICE} &= E_{tail}^{ICE} + E_{up} \\[4pt]
&= 0.1548 + 0.45 \; \tfrac{kg}{km} \\[4pt]
&= 0.6048 \; \tfrac{kg}{km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute WTW EV emissions.**

The EV has zero tailpipe emissions. Its only climate impact is the electricity-generation emissions at the source power plant. For India's predominantly coal grid:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{WTW}^{EV} &= E_c \times k_{grid} \\[4pt]
&= 0.15 \; \tfrac{kWh}{km} \times 0.82 \; \tfrac{kg}{kWh} \\[4pt]
&= 0.123 \; \tfrac{kg}{km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the absolute and percentage reduction.**

Subtract EV emissions from ICE emissions, then divide by ICE emissions and multiply by 100 to express as a percentage.

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{red} &= \dfrac{E_{WTW}^{ICE} - E_{WTW}^{EV}}{E_{WTW}^{ICE}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.6048 - 0.123}{0.6048} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.4818}{0.6048} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 79.66 \;\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Even with India's coal-dominant grid, EVs deliver approximately **~80% WTW COŌéé reduction per km** when **upstream emissions of petrol are properly accounted for**. This is the *headline number* KTU examiners expect.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sound Level Reduction at a Pedestrian

**Given:** ICE car noise at source $L_{p1}$ = 75 dB at $r_1$ = 1 m; pedestrian at $r_2$ = 5 m.

**Formula (inverse-square, free-field):**

$$L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{p2} &= 75 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{5}{1}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate the logarithm.**

$\log_{10}(5) \approx 0.699$, so $20 \times 0.699 = 13.98$ dB.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{p2} &= 75 - 13.98 \; dB \\[4pt]
&\approx 61.0 \; dB
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply same to EV (source noise Ōēł 60 dB at 1 m due to absence of combustion):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{p2}^{EV} &= 60 - 13.98 \approx 46.0 \; dB
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Difference in perceived noise at the pedestrian:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_p &= 61.0 - 46.0 = 15.0 \; dB
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 10 dB reduction is *perceived* as halving of loudness. Therefore the EV is **~3├Ś quieter** at the pedestrian ŌĆö a major social benefit in dense urban areas.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (ICE vs EV ŌĆö KTU Ready)

| Parameter | Petrol ICE | Battery EV | Social/Environmental Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| WTW COŌéé | 0.60 kg/km | 0.12 kg/km | **80% lower** |
| NOx tailpipe | 0.05 g/km | 0 g/km | Urban air-quality gain |
| PM2.5 tailpipe | 0.004 g/km | 0 g/km | Respiratory health |
| Noise at 5 m | 61 dB | 46 dB | **15 dB quieter** |
| Energy efficiency | 22% | 87% | Resource conservation |
| Moving parts | ~2,000 | ~200 | Less raw-material extraction |
| Fuel cost/km | Ōé╣7.5 | Ōé╣1.2 | **84% saving** |
| Maintenance cost/km | Ōé╣2.0 | Ōé╣0.6 | 70% saving |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper, Module 1]**

### Q1. Define the term "Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emissions" in the context of Electric Vehicles.

**Model Answer:**
Well-to-Wheel (WTW) emissions refer to the **total greenhouse-gas emissions produced across the entire energy chain** of a vehicle ŌĆö from raw energy extraction ("Well") to final useful work at the wheels ("Wheel"). For an ICE vehicle, this includes crude oil extraction, refining, transport, and combustion. For an EV, it includes power-plant fuel extraction, electricity generation, transmission, and the motor's electrical-to-mechanical conversion. **EVs have zero tailpipe emissions but non-zero WTW emissions**, depending on the grid mix.

---

### Q2. List any **three** social benefits of widespread Electric Vehicle adoption.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Improved public health** due to elimination of tailpipe NOx and PM2.5 in cities.
2. **Lower per-km travel cost** (~Ōé╣1.2/km vs Ōé╣7ŌĆō8/km for petrol), democratizing mobility.
3. **Generation of new jobs** in EV manufacturing, charging infrastructure, and battery recycling (~10 million by 2030 as per NITI Aayog).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Style, Module 1, Choice Pattern A & B]**

---

### ­¤ōś QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain in detail the **environmental importance** of Electric Vehicles. Cover GHG reduction, local air quality, and noise pollution. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Greenhouse Gas Reduction:**
The transportation sector contributes ~14% of India's total COŌéé emissions. A typical petrol car emits Ōēł **0.155 kg COŌéé/km** at the tailpipe and Ōēł **0.605 kg COŌéé/km** on a well-to-wheel basis. A comparable EV emits **0 kg at the tailpipe** and only **~0.12 kg COŌéé/km** WTW with India's grid. This translates to a **~80% reduction** in lifecycle COŌéé, accelerating India's Net-Zero 2070 target.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Local Air Quality:**
ICE vehicles emit NOx (~0.05 g/km), CO, unburnt hydrocarbons, and PM2.5 (~0.004 g/km). In cities like Delhi, vehicular emissions account for **30ŌĆō40% of ambient PM2.5**, far exceeding the WHO limit of 5 ┬Ąg/m┬│. EVs eliminate these street-level emissions entirely, reducing incidence of asthma, bronchitis, and cardiovascular disease ŌĆö saving an estimated **1.67 million pollution-related deaths annually** in India.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Noise Pollution:**
At a typical pedestrian distance of 5 m, an ICE car produces ~61 dB; an EV produces ~46 dB ŌĆö a **15 dB reduction**, perceived as a **3├Ś drop in loudness**. This improves urban liveability, sleep quality, and reduces stress-related disorders.

**Conclusion:** EVs simultaneously address three major environmental crises ŌĆö climate change, urban air quality, and noise pollution ŌĆö making them a cornerstone of sustainable urban transport.

---

#### (b) Derive the percentage reduction in Well-to-Wheel COŌéé emissions when a typical Indian petrol car is replaced by a battery EV. Assume standard values and justify each step. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given (Standard KTU Assumptions):**
- Petrol mileage: 15 km/L ŌåÆ fuel rate $m_f$ = 1/15 Ōēł 0.067 L/km
- Petrol COŌéé factor $k_f$ = 2.31 kg/L
- Upstream emission $E_{up}$ = 0.45 kg/km
- EV consumption $E_c$ = 0.15 kWh/km
- India grid factor $k_{grid}$ = 0.82 kg COŌéé/kWh

**Step 1 ŌĆö Tailpipe COŌéé (ICE):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{tail}^{ICE} &= m_f \times k_f \\[4pt]
&= 0.067 \times 2.31 \approx 0.1548 \; \tfrac{kg}{km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö WTW COŌéé (ICE):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{WTW}^{ICE} &= 0.1548 + 0.45 = 0.6048 \; \tfrac{kg}{km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö WTW COŌéé (EV):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{WTW}^{EV} &= 0.15 \times 0.82 = 0.123 \; \tfrac{kg}{km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Percentage Reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{red} &= \dfrac{0.6048 - 0.123}{0.6048} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&\approx 79.66\% \;\approx 80\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Switching from a petrol ICE to a comparable EV gives **~80% reduction in WTW COŌéé emissions per km**, even on India's coal-heavy grid. With renewable charging, this can exceed 90%.

---

### ­¤ōŚ QUESTION B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)

#### (a) Discuss the **social importance** of Electric Vehicles in India. How do EVs improve public health and energy security? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Public Health:** Air pollution causes **~1.67 million premature deaths/year in India**, with vehicular tailpipe emissions (NOx, PM2.5, CO) contributing 30ŌĆō40% of urban air pollution. EVs eliminate these street-level pollutants, directly reducing:
- Asthma and COPD cases
- Cardiovascular mortality
- Pediatric lung-development disorders
- Healthcare expenditure (~Ōé╣2.6 lakh crore/year estimated by World Bank for India's pollution costs)

**Energy Security:** India imports **>85% of its crude oil**, costing ~$120ŌĆō160 billion/year in foreign exchange. EVs, powered by electricity from **domestic sources** (coal, solar, wind, hydro, nuclear), reduce:
- Forex outflow on crude imports
- Vulnerability to geopolitical oil-price shocks
- Strategic dependence on volatile regions

**Broader Social Benefits:**
- **Lower per-km cost** (Ōé╣1.2 vs Ōé╣7.5) democratizes mobility for lower-income groups.
- **Quieter cities** reduce noise-stress and improve mental health.
- **Job creation** in EV manufacturing, charging, and battery recycling ŌĆö estimated 10 million jobs by 2030.

---

#### (b) A driver travels **15,000 km/year**. Compare the annual COŌéé emissions and running cost for a petrol car (mileage 15 km/L, petrol Ōé╣105/L) and an EV (efficiency 6 km/kWh, electricity Ōé╣8/kWh). Use the WTW approach. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Annual fuel/electricity used:**

For petrol: $15{,}000 / 15 = 1000$ L/year.  
For EV: $15{,}000 / 6 = 2500$ kWh/year.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Annual cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{ICE} &= 1000 \times 105 = \text{Ōé╣}1{,}05{,}000 \\[4pt]
C_{EV} &= 2500 \times 8 = \text{Ōé╣}20{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Savings} &= 1{,}05{,}000 - 20{,}000 = \text{Ōé╣}85{,}000 \; \text{per year}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Annual WTW COŌéé:**

ICE: $0.6048 \times 15{,}000 = 9072$ kg COŌéé/year.  
EV: $0.123 \times 15{,}000 = 1845$ kg COŌéé/year.  
Reduction: $9072 - 1845 = 7227$ kg Ōēł **7.2 tonnes COŌéé/year saved per vehicle**.

**Conclusion:** The EV saves the driver **Ōé╣85,000/year** in fuel and avoids **~7.2 tonnes of COŌéé emissions annually** ŌĆö equivalent to planting ~120 mature trees every year.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "tailpipe" with "well-to-wheel" emissions.** EVs have **zero tailpipe** emissions but **non-zero WTW** emissions. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if this distinction is not made.
> 2. **Forgetting upstream emissions for ICE.** A common error is to compute only tailpipe COŌéé for petrol cars. Always include the **~0.45 kg/km upstream factor**.
> 3. **Using the wrong COŌéé factor.** Petrol = 2.31 kg/L; Diesel = 2.68 kg/L; CNG Ōēł 2.0 kg/L. Mixing these up costs **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring dB scale.** "Lower noise" without numerical support (e.g., "15 dB lower") is treated as a vague answer and earns only partial credit.
> 5. **Not stating WHO / NITI Aayog / FAME-II reference standards** in the social-importance questions. Quoting authoritative numbers boosts marks.
> 6. **Sign error in percentage reduction.** Always subtract EV from ICE and divide by ICE ŌĆö not the reverse.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> Ō£ģ **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- Ō£ģ EV = **zero tailpipe emissions** + non-zero WTW emissions depending on grid.
- Ō£ģ India grid emission factor Ōēł **0.82 kg COŌéé/kWh** (coal-heavy).
- Ō£ģ Petrol COŌéé factor Ōēł **2.31 kg/L**; Diesel Ōēł **2.68 kg/L**.
- Ō£ģ Typical WTW COŌéé reduction of EV vs ICE in India Ōēł **80%** (current grid).
- Ō£ģ EVs are **~15 dB quieter** at pedestrian distance (~3├Ś perceived reduction).
- Ō£ģ EVs eliminate **NOx, CO, HC, PM2.5** street-level pollutants.
- Ō£ģ Energy efficiency: ICE ~22%; EV ~87%.
- Ō£ģ Per-km running cost: ICE ~Ōé╣7.5; EV ~Ōé╣1.2 (~84% cheaper).
- Ō£ģ Lower moving parts ŌåÆ 70% lower maintenance cost.
- Ō£ģ India targets Net-Zero by **2070**; EVs are central to this goal.
- Ō£ģ FAME-II subsidy: **Ōé╣10,000/kWh** on battery capacity.
- Ō£ģ India imports >85% of crude oil ŌĆö EVs improve **energy security**.
- Ō£ģ ~**1.67 million deaths/year** in India linked to air pollution ŌĆö EVs reduce this.
- Ō£ģ **10 million jobs** projected in EV ecosystem by 2030 (NITI Aayog).
- Ō£ģ Payback period of EVs in India: **4ŌĆō5 years for cars**, **2ŌĆō3 years for 2-wheelers**.
- Ō£ģ Battery recycling recovery rates **>90%** for Li, Co, Ni using modern processes.
- Ō£ģ Second-life battery use in stationary storage extends lifespan by **8ŌĆō10 years**.
- Ō£ģ AI-Markdown rule reminder: Always distinguish **tailpipe vs upstream vs WTW** in every numerical answer.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification Based on Propulsion System, Electric Components Used in Electric Vehicles, Block Diagram of Electric Drive, Classification of Electric Motor Drives for EV, Role of Power Electronics Converters in Electric Propulsion System - DC-DC Converters and DC-AC Converters (Basic Principles Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Classification of Electric Vehicles Based on Propulsion System

Electric vehicles can be classified into **three main categories** based on the degree of electrification and the propulsion architecture employed:

### (a) Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)
A **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** is a purely electric vehicle that derives all its propulsion energy exclusively from an on-board **rechargeable battery pack**. It has **no internal combustion engine (ICE)** and no tailpipe emissions. The battery is charged from an external power source (grid or renewable energy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** BEVs are zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs) where the propulsion is provided solely by one or more electric motors powered by a chemical battery (typically Li-ion). Range depends directly on battery State of Charge (SoC).

### (b) Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)
A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** combines an ICE with an electric motor and a small battery. The battery is charged only through **regenerative braking** and the **ICE-driven generator** ŌĆö **not from an external plug-in source**. HEVs are classified further into:
- **Micro/Mild Hybrid** ŌĆō 12V/48V electrical system, supports start-stop and mild assist.
- **Full Hybrid** ŌĆō Can run on ICE alone, electric alone, or combined (e.g., Toyota Prius).
- **Series Hybrid** ŌĆō ICE drives only a generator; wheels are driven solely by the electric motor.
- **Parallel Hybrid** ŌĆō Both ICE and electric motor can directly drive the wheels through a mechanical coupling.
- **Series-Parallel Hybrid** ŌĆō Combines both architectures (e.g., Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive).

### (c) Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)
A **PHEV** is a hybrid vehicle with a **larger battery** that can be charged from an **external plug-in source** in addition to regenerative braking. It typically offers **30ŌĆō80 km** of pure electric range, after which it operates as a conventional HEV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)** is also an emerging classification where a **hydrogen fuel cell** generates electricity on-board to power the electric motor. Although outside the strict three-class KTU definition, FCEVs are often mentioned in KTU supplementary questions on alternative propulsion.

---

## 1.2 Electric Components Used in Electric Vehicles

The major electric/electronic components that constitute an EV powertrain are:

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Traction Battery Pack** | Stores electrical energy (DC); typically Li-ion, rated 200ŌĆō800 V |
| **Electric Motor (Traction Motor)** | Converts electrical energy to mechanical energy (propulsion) |
| **Power Electronics Converter (PEC)** | Controls voltage/current/frequency between battery and motor |
| **Battery Management System (BMS)** | Monitors cell voltage, temperature, SoC, SoH; ensures safety |
| **On-board Charger (OBC)** | AC-to-DC converter for charging from grid supply |
| **DC-DC Converter (Auxiliary)** | Steps down high-voltage DC to 12V/48V for auxiliaries |
| **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** | Brain of the EV; manages torque, braking, energy flow |
| **Thermal Management System** | Cools/heats battery, motor, and PEC |
| **Sensors** | Current, voltage, speed, position, temperature sensors |
| **Inverter** | DC to AC converter for AC motor drives |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The EV as a Human Body

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of an EV as a human body.
> - **Battery** ŌåÆ The **stomach** (stores food/energy).
> - **Electric Motor** ŌåÆ The **muscles** (does the physical work).
> - **Power Electronics Converter** ŌåÆ The **nervous system** (controls how much and how fast the muscles move).
> - **VCU** ŌåÆ The **brain** (decides what to do and when).
> - **BMS** ŌåÆ The **immune system** (monitors health and protects against damage).

Just as a human cannot run on raw food without the digestive and nervous systems processing it, an EV cannot use battery energy directly without the converter and controller orchestrating the energy flow.

---

## 1.4 Why Classification Matters for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Questions on EV classification frequently test the **ability to differentiate** between BEV, HEV, and PHEV. Students often lose marks by:
> 1. Confusing HEV with PHEV (forgetting the **external plug-in** aspect of PHEV).
> 2. Misclassifying Series vs. Parallel hybrid configurations.
> 3. Failing to mention that **FCEVs use hydrogen** and **BEVs use only batteries**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōŚ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Propulsion System Classification

### ­¤öŗ Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) ŌĆö Architecture
A BEV uses a **single energy source (battery)** feeding **one or more electric motors** through a power electronic converter. The drivetrain is mechanically simple ŌĆö usually a **single-speed transmission** because electric motors deliver peak torque at zero RPM, eliminating the need for multi-gear boxes.

> **Block Flow:** `Battery ŌåÆ Inverter ŌåÆ Motor ŌåÆ Wheels`

**Mathematical Energy Model:**
The total stored energy in a BEV is given by:

$$E_{stored} = V_{batt} \times C_{batt}$$

where $V_{batt}$ is the nominal battery voltage (in **V**) and $C_{batt}$ is the battery capacity (in **Ah**). The unit of stored energy is **Watt-hours (Wh)**.

The driving range $R$ of a BEV is approximated by:

$$R = \frac{E_{stored} \times \eta_{drivetrain}}{E_{consumption\_per\_km}}$$

where $\eta_{drivetrain}$ is the combined efficiency of motor + power electronics + transmission.

---

### ŌÜĪ Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) ŌĆö Sub-Classification

#### Series Hybrid
- **ICE** drives a **generator only**.
- Generated electricity charges the battery or directly powers the motor.
- **No mechanical link** between ICE and wheels.

#### Parallel Hybrid
- **ICE and Motor** are mechanically coupled (typically via a clutch and planetary gear set).
- Both can drive the wheels **simultaneously or independently**.

#### Series-Parallel Hybrid
- Combines both; uses a **Power Split Device (PSD)** ŌĆö usually a planetary gear set.
- Optimizes for both efficiency and performance.

---

### ­¤öī Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)
- Has a **larger battery (8ŌĆō18 kWh)** than HEV (1ŌĆō2 kWh).
- Supports **external AC charging (Level 1/Level 2)**.
- Operates in **Charge-Depleting (CD)** mode first, then **Charge-Sustaining (CS)** mode.

---

## 2.2 Block Diagram of an Electric Drive

The **Electric Drive** is the heart of any EV's propulsion system. It consists of four essential blocks:

1. **Power Source** (Battery / Fuel Cell / Ultracapacitor)
2. **Power Electronic Converter** (DC-DC, DC-AC, AC-DC)
3. **Electric Motor** (DC, Induction, PMSM, SRM, BLDC)
4. **Controller / Sensor Feedback Loop**

### Universal Block Diagram

> **Battery ŌåÆ Power Electronic Converter ŌåÆ Electric Motor ŌåÆ Mechanical Load (Wheels)**
> **Ōåæ**
> **Controller (with current, speed, position sensors)**

The **controller** receives feedback signals from sensors and regulates the converter's switching to maintain the desired motor speed/torque.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The power electronics converter and the controller together form the **"drive electronics"** ŌĆö without them, the motor cannot be efficiently controlled, even if the battery and motor are perfect.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Electric Motor Drives for EV

The choice of motor fundamentally affects EV performance, efficiency, and cost. KTU 2024 requires classification into **four major types**:

| Motor Type | Symbol | Key Feature | EV Application |
|------------|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **DC Motor** | DCM | Simple control, high starting torque | Low-speed EVs (e-bikes, forklifts) |
| **Induction Motor (IM)** | IM | Rugged, low cost, no rare-earth magnets | Tesla Model S (early versions) |
| **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** | PMSM | High power density, high efficiency | Nissan Leaf, Tesla Model 3 |
| **Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)** | BLDC | Electronic commutation, trapezoidal EMF | Two-wheelers (Ather, Ola) |
| **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** | SRM | No magnets, fault tolerant | Research/Commercial vehicles (Renault Fluence Z.E.) |

### Why PMSM Dominates Modern EVs?
- **High efficiency (95ŌĆō97%)**
- **Compact size** and high power density
- **Excellent torque-speed characteristics** suited for traction

---

## 2.4 Role of Power Electronics Converters in EV Propulsion

Power electronics converters are the **enablers of efficient, controlled electric propulsion**. They perform:

1. **DC-DC Conversion** ŌĆö Step up/down voltage for battery management and auxiliary supply.
2. **DC-AC Conversion (Inversion)** ŌĆö Convert DC battery to variable-frequency AC for AC motors.
3. **AC-DC Conversion (Rectification)** ŌĆö Convert grid AC to DC for battery charging (OBC).
4. **Bidirectional Power Flow** ŌĆö Enable regenerative braking by reversing power flow.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-FREQUENCY in KTU university examinations. Memorize the units and standard symbols.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_{stored} = V_{batt} \times C_{batt}$ | Battery energy storage | Wh |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{E_{stored} \times \eta}{E_{km}}$ | Vehicle driving range | km |
| 3 | $P_{motor} = T \times \omega$ | Mechanical power = torque ├Ś angular speed | W (with $T$ in Nm, $\omega$ in rad/s) |
| 4 | $V_{oc} = E - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | DC motor armature equation | V |
| 5 | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Motor efficiency | % |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | Duty cycle of a chopper | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_o = D \times V_s$ | Buck converter output voltage | V |
| 8 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D}$ | Boost converter output voltage | V |
| 9 | $V_{LL,rms} = 0.612 \times m_a \times V_{DC}$ | PWM inverter line-line RMS voltage | V |
| 10 | $m_a = \dfrac{V_{control}}{V_{tri}}$ | Modulation index of PWM inverter | dimensionless |
| 11 | $f_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Switching frequency of converter | Hz |
| 12 | $T_{em} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times \dfrac{P}{2} \times \lambda_m \times I_q$ | PMSM electromagnetic torque | Nm |
| 13 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \times f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed of AC motor | RPM |
| 14 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | dimensionless |
| 15 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times J \times \omega^2$ | Regenerative braking energy (kinetic) | J |

> **Standard SI Units Used Throughout:** Volt (V), Ampere (A), Watt (W), Newton-meter (Nm), rad/s, Hertz (Hz), Tesla (T).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:** Every commercial EV manufacturer (Tesla, BYD, Tata, Ola) optimizes the **Battery-Converter-Motor** trio. The choice of converter topology (e.g., **two-level vs. three-level NPC inverter** for PMSM) directly impacts **switching losses, EMI, and vehicle range**. A 1% efficiency gain in the inverter can yield ~5ŌĆō8 km of additional range per charge cycle ŌĆö making these formulas commercially critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤ōÖ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Buck Converter Output Voltage

The **Buck Converter** is a step-down DC-DC converter used extensively in EVs for:
- Auxiliary power supply (HV battery ŌåÆ 12V/48V)
- Battery charging/discharging control

### Circuit Operation Principle
A buck converter consists of:
- A **switch (S)** ŌĆö typically an IGBT or MOSFET
- A **diode (D)** ŌĆö freewheeling
- An **inductor (L)** ŌĆö energy storage
- A **capacitor (C)** ŌĆö output filter
- A **load resistor (R)**

### Assumptions
1. Switch is ideal (zero voltage drop, zero switching time).
2. Inductor current is **continuous (CCM mode)**.
3. Output voltage is **ripple-free** (large C).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the duty cycle $D$:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}$$

where $T_{on}$ is the switch ON-time and $T_s$ is the total switching period.

**Step 2:** During the **ON state** ($T_{on}$), the switch is closed. The diode is reverse-biased. The inductor sees:

$$V_L = V_s - V_o = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** The change in inductor current during ON time:

$$\Delta I_{L,ON} = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{on}}{L}$$

**Step 4:** During the **OFF state** ($T_{off}$), the switch is open. The diode conducts (freewheeling). The inductor sees:

$$V_L = -V_o = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

**Step 5:** The change in inductor current during OFF time:

$$\Delta I_{L,OFF} = \frac{-V_o \cdot T_{off}}{L}$$

**Step 6:** In **steady-state**, the net change in inductor current over one cycle is zero (volt-second balance):

$$\Delta I_{L,ON} + \Delta I_{L,OFF} = 0$$

**Step 7:** Substituting:

$$\frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{on}}{L} - \frac{V_o \cdot T_{off}}{L} = 0$$

**Step 8:** Simplify (multiply by L):

$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{on} = V_o \cdot T_{off}$$

**Step 9:** Rearrange:

$$V_s \cdot T_{on} = V_o \cdot (T_{on} + T_{off}) = V_o \cdot T_s$$

**Step 10:** Therefore, the output voltage is:

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}$$

**Step 11:** Substitute $D = T_{on}/T_s$:

$$\boxed{V_o = D \cdot V_s}$$

> **Conclusion:** For $0 < D < 1$, the output voltage is always less than the input voltage, confirming step-down (buck) operation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Boost Converter Output Voltage

The **Boost Converter** is a step-up DC-DC converter used to:
- Boost low battery voltage to higher DC-link voltage for the motor inverter.
- Interface ultracapacitors to the main DC bus.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** During **ON state**, switch closed, diode reverse-biased. Inductor stores energy:

$$V_L = V_s = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Change in inductor current (ON):

$$\Delta I_{L,ON} = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{L}$$

**Step 3:** During **OFF state**, switch open, diode forward-biased. Inductor releases energy to load:

$$V_L = V_s - V_o = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Change in inductor current (OFF):

$$\Delta I_{L,OFF} = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{off}}{L}$$

**Step 5:** Apply volt-second balance ($\Delta I_{L,ON} + \Delta I_{L,OFF} = 0$):

$$\frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{L} + \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot T_{off}}{L} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Multiply by $L$ and substitute $T_{on} = D \cdot T_s$, $T_{off} = (1-D) \cdot T_s$:

$$V_s \cdot D \cdot T_s + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s = 0$$

**Step 7:** Divide by $T_s$:

$$V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_o)(1-D) = 0$$

**Step 8:** Expand:

$$V_s \cdot D + V_s - V_s \cdot D - V_o + V_o \cdot D = 0$$

**Step 9:** Simplify ($V_s \cdot D$ cancels):

$$V_s - V_o + V_o \cdot D = 0$$

**Step 10:** Rearrange:

$$V_s = V_o(1-D)$$

**Step 11:** Final result:

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D}}$$

> **Conclusion:** For $0 < D < 1$, $V_o > V_s$, confirming step-up (boost) operation. As $D \to 1$, $V_o \to \infty$ (in ideal case).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Basic Principle of a DC-AC Inverter (Square Wave)

The **inverter** converts DC battery voltage to variable-frequency, variable-amplitude AC for AC motor drives.

### Single-Phase Half-Bridge Inverter Output Voltage

The output voltage (across the load) is determined by which switch is conducting.

**Case 1:** Switch $S_1$ ON, $S_2$ OFF:
$$v_o = +\frac{V_{DC}}{2}$$

**Case 2:** Switch $S_2$ ON, $S_1$ OFF:
$$v_o = -\frac{V_{DC}}{2}$$

**The RMS value of the square-wave output:**

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T/2} \left(\frac{V_{DC}}{2}\right)^2 dt + \frac{1}{T} \int_{T/2}^{T} \left(-\frac{V_{DC}}{2}\right)^2 dt}$$

$$V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \cdot 2 \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DC}}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{T}{2}} = \frac{V_{DC}}{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

Wait ŌĆö correct computation:

$$V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_{DC}}{2}$$

(since both halves contribute the same magnitude squared over the full period).

**Conclusion:** Square-wave inverter gives a **fixed amplitude** AC output, which is unsuitable for variable-speed motor control. This motivates **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** techniques.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Converter Selection in EV

| Application | Converter Type | Purpose | KTU Example |
|-------------|----------------|---------|-------------|
| HV battery ŌåÆ 12V auxiliaries | **Buck DC-DC** | Step-down | Lights, ECU |
| Ultracapacitor ŌåÆ DC bus | **Boost DC-DC** | Step-up | Acceleration assist |
| Battery ŌåÆ Traction motor (AC) | **DC-AC Inverter** | DC to variable-freq AC | PMSM, IM drive |
| Grid AC ŌåÆ Battery | **AC-DC Rectifier (PFC)** | Charging | On-board charger |
| Regenerative braking | **Bidirectional DC-DC** | Energy recovery | Battery charging |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ­¤ōÆ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: EV Classification Based on Propulsion

```mermaid

    A[Electric Vehicle Classification]

```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block Diagram of Electric Drive System

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Battery Pack HV DC Source] --> C1[Power Electronic Converter]
    S1[Sensors Current Voltage Speed Position] --> CTRL[Controller VCU ECU]
    CTRL --> C1
    C1 --> M1[Electric Traction Motor]
    M1 --> T1[Transmission Single Speed or Multi Speed]
    T1 --> W1[Wheels Mechanical Load]
    S1 -.-> M1

    TH1[Thermal Management] -.-> C1
    TH1 -.-> M1
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Power Electronics Converters in EV Propulsion

```mermaid
graph TD
    PE[Power Electronics Converters in EV]

    DC2 --> DC2c[Multilevel Inverter]

    AC1 --> AC1b[PFC Rectifier]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Electric Motor Drives for EV

```mermaid

    M3 --> M3b[Synchronous Reluctance Motor SynRM]
```

# ­¤ōÆ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
**Classify electric vehicles based on propulsion system. Differentiate between HEV and PHEV in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

Electric vehicles are classified into three categories based on the propulsion energy source:

1. **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV):** Powered solely by an external-chargeable battery; zero tailpipe emission.
2. **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV):** Combines ICE and electric motor; battery charged by regenerative braking only.
3. **Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV):** Larger battery; supports external plug-in charging; offers pure electric range.

| Parameter | HEV | PHEV |
|-----------|-----|------|
| External charging | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Battery size | 1ŌĆō2 kWh | 8ŌĆō18 kWh |
| Pure electric range | <5 km | 30ŌĆō80 km |
| Fuel efficiency | Moderate | High (in CD mode) |

**(3 Marks Awarded)**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
**State the function of a Battery Management System (BMS) and a Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) in an electric vehicle.**

**Model Answer:**

- **BMS (Battery Management System):** Monitors individual cell voltages, pack temperature, State of Charge (SoC), and State of Health (SoH). It protects the battery from over-charge, over-discharge, over-current, and thermal runaway. It also performs **cell balancing** to ensure uniform cell aging.

- **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit):** Acts as the central "brain" of the EV. It receives inputs from the accelerator pedal, brake pedal, and sensors; computes the required torque; and commands the inverter and motor accordingly. It also coordinates regenerative braking and energy management between the battery and motor.

**(3 Marks Awarded)**

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes students make in Part B questions on this topic:
> 1. Confusing **duty cycle (D)** of buck vs. boost ŌĆö remember **buck: $V_o = D \cdot V_s$**, **boost: $V_o = V_s / (1-D)$**.
> 2. Failing to draw **neat, labeled block diagrams** ŌĆö diagrams carry **3ŌĆō4 marks** by themselves.
> 3. Not specifying **units** in numerical problems.
> 4. Skipping the **explanation of physical significance** of results.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]**

#### (a) Draw the block diagram of an electric drive system and explain the function of each block. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The block diagram of an electric drive system consists of the following four main blocks:

1. **Power Source** (Battery / Fuel Cell):
   - Provides the DC electrical energy to the system.
   - In a BEV, this is the high-voltage Li-ion battery pack (300ŌĆō800 V).
   - Function: Energy storage and supply.

2. **Power Electronic Converter (PEC):**
   - Converts and conditions electrical power (DC-DC, DC-AC, AC-DC).
   - Controls the magnitude, frequency, and direction of power flow.
   - In an AC motor drive, it is typically a **PWM inverter** (DCŌåÆAC).

3. **Electric Motor (Traction Motor):**
   - Converts electrical energy to mechanical energy.
   - Types: PMSM, IM, BLDC, SRM.
   - Output: Torque and speed for propulsion.

4. **Controller + Sensors:**
   - Sensors measure current, voltage, speed, rotor position.
   - Controller (VCU) compares actual vs. reference and commands the PEC via gate pulses.
   - Implements closed-loop control (e.g., FOC, V/f control).

```
[Power Source] ŌåÆ [Power Electronic Converter] ŌåÆ [Electric Motor] ŌåÆ [Mechanical Load]
                         Ōåæ                                            Ōåō
                  [Controller] ŌåÉŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆö [Sensors] ŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌĆöŌöś
```

**Marks Distribution:** Block Diagram (3) + Function of each block (3) + Working principle (1).

---

#### (b) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **boost converter** and derive the expression for its output voltage. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit:** A boost converter consists of a DC source $V_s$, an inductor $L$, a controllable switch (IGBT/MOSFET), a diode $D$, a filter capacitor $C$, and a load $R$.

**Working Principle:**

- **Mode 1 (Switch ON, $T_{on}$):** The switch is closed. The diode is reverse-biased. Current through inductor rises linearly; energy is stored in $L$. The load is isolated from the source; capacitor $C$ discharges into the load.

$$V_L = V_s = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

- **Mode 2 (Switch OFF, $T_{off}$):** The switch is open. The diode is forward-biased. The inductor releases its stored energy to the load **in series with** the source. Thus the load sees a voltage greater than $V_s$.

$$V_L = V_s - V_o = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

**Derivation of Output Voltage:**

Applying volt-second balance over one switching period $T_s$:

$$V_s \cdot T_{on} = (V_o - V_s) \cdot T_{off}$$

Substituting $T_{on} = D \cdot T_s$ and $T_{off} = (1-D) \cdot T_s$:

$$V_s \cdot D = (V_o - V_s) \cdot (1-D)$$

Expanding:

$$V_s \cdot D = V_o - V_o \cdot D - V_s + V_s \cdot D$$

$$0 = V_o(1 - D) - V_s$$

$$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D}$$

For $0 < D < 1$, $V_o > V_s$ ŌĆö confirming step-up operation.

**Numerical example (if asked):** If $V_s = 200$ V and $D = 0.6$:

$$V_o = \frac{200}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{200}{0.4} = 500 \text{ V}$$

**Marks Distribution:** Circuit diagram (2) + Working (2) + Derivation (3).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]**

#### (a) Classify electric motor drives used in electric vehicles. Compare the performance of PMSM and Induction Motor for EV application. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of EV Motor Drives:**

Electric motor drives for EV application are classified into four categories:

1. **DC Motor Drives** ŌĆö Separately excited, series, shunt. (Simple control, high maintenance due to brushes.)
2. **Induction Motor (IM) Drives** ŌĆö Squirrel cage, wound rotor. (Rugged, low cost, no magnets.)
3. **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) Drives** ŌĆö Surface-mounted, interior-mounted. (High efficiency, high power density.)
4. **Special Motor Drives** ŌĆö BLDC, Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM), Synchronous Reluctance Motor (SynRM). (Fault tolerant, magnet-free in some cases.)

**Comparison: PMSM vs. Induction Motor (IM)**

| Parameter | PMSM | Induction Motor |
|-----------|------|-----------------|
| Efficiency | 95ŌĆō97% (higher) | 90ŌĆō93% |
| Power density | High | Moderate |
| Cooling | More complex (rotor magnets) | Simpler (robust rotor) |
| Cost | High (rare-earth magnets) | Lower |
| Maintenance | Low (no brushes) | Low |
| Field weakening | Limited | Easy |
| Starting torque | Excellent | Good (with V/f control) |
| Reliability | Sensitive to temperature | Very robust |
| EV examples | Nissan Leaf, Tesla Model 3 | Tesla Roadster (early), Tata Tiago EV |

**Conclusion:** PMSM is preferred for high-performance EVs due to superior efficiency and power density, while IM is preferred for cost-sensitive, rugged applications.

**Marks Distribution:** Classification (2) + Tabular comparison (3) + Conclusion (2).

---

#### (b) Explain the basic principle of a **DC-AC inverter** used in EV propulsion. Discuss the role of PWM technique. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Basic Principle of DC-AC Inverter:**

A DC-AC inverter converts the DC voltage from the traction battery into a variable-frequency, variable-amplitude AC voltage required to drive AC traction motors (PMSM, IM).

**Single-Phase Half-Bridge Inverter:**
- Consists of 2 switches ($S_1$, $S_2$) and 2 capacitors forming a voltage divider.
- When $S_1$ is ON, output = $+V_{DC}/2$.
- When $S_2$ is ON, output = $-V_{DC}/2$.
- The output is a **square wave** (basic, low-quality).

**Three-Phase Two-Level Inverter (Standard EV Topology):**
- 6 switches (IGBTs/SiC MOSFETs), arranged in 3 legs.
- Each leg produces a phase voltage displaced by 120┬░.
- The line-to-line voltage is a **6-step waveform** in square-wave mode.

**Role of PWM (Pulse Width Modulation):**

Square-wave inverters cannot control the output voltage amplitude; the RMS value is fixed at $V_{DC}/\sqrt{3}$. This causes:
- High harmonic distortion (THD > 30%)
- Large torque ripples
- Acoustic noise in motor
- Poor dynamic response

**PWM technique** overcomes these limitations by switching the inverter switches at a high frequency (5ŌĆō20 kHz) with varying pulse widths:

1. **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM):** Pulses are modulated such that the average output voltage follows a sinusoidal reference.
2. **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM):** Advanced technique; uses space vectors of the 6 switches to synthesize a rotating voltage vector with 15% higher DC bus utilization than SPWM.
3. **Modulation Index $m_a$** controls output amplitude:

$$m_a = \frac{V_{control,peak}}{V_{tri,peak}}$$

**Output RMS Voltage:**

$$V_{LL,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot m_a \cdot \frac{V_{DC}}{2} = 0.612 \cdot m_a \cdot V_{DC}$$

**Benefits in EV:**
- Variable voltage and frequency ŌåÆ variable speed motor control
- Reduced harmonic distortion ŌåÆ smooth torque, less motor heating
- Regenerative braking capability through controlled firing of switches

**Marks Distribution:** Inverter principle (2) + Square wave operation (1) + PWM explanation (3) + EV relevance (1).

---

## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ŌÜĪ HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS ŌĆö Master These Before Exam Day ŌÜĪ**

### Ō£ģ EV Classification
- **BEV** ŌåÆ Battery only, no ICE, external charging, zero emission.
- **HEV** ŌåÆ ICE + small battery, no plug-in, regenerative charging only.
- **PHEV** ŌåÆ ICE + large battery, plug-in supported, has both CD and CS modes.
- **FCEV** ŌåÆ Hydrogen fuel cell, water vapor emission.

### Ō£ģ Electric Drive Block Diagram
- **Four blocks:** Power Source + Power Electronic Converter + Electric Motor + Controller/Sensors.
- **Key rule:** Without PEC, motor cannot be efficiently controlled.

### Ō£ģ EV Motor Drive Classification
- **4 families:** DC, IM, PMSM, Special (BLDC/SRM).
- **PMSM** dominates modern EVs (high efficiency, compact).
- **IM** is rugged and low-cost (used in commercial vehicles).
- **BLDC** is popular in 2-wheelers (Ather, Ola, TVS).
- **SRM** is fault-tolerant and magnet-free.

### Ō£ģ Power Electronics Converters
- **DC-DC Buck:** $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ (step-down).
- **DC-DC Boost:** $V_o = V_s / (1-D)$ (step-up).
- **DC-AC Inverter:** Converts DC to variable-frequency AC for PMSM/IM.
- **PWM techniques:** SPWM, SVPWM ŌĆö reduce harmonics, allow variable V/f.
- **SVPWM** gives 15% higher DC bus utilization than SPWM.
- **Modulation index $m_a$** controls output amplitude.
- **Bidirectional converters** enable regenerative braking.

### Ō£ģ Critical Numerical Relationships
- Synchronous speed: $N_s = (120 \cdot f) / P$
- Slip: $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$
- Battery energy: $E = V \times C$ (in Wh)
- Range: $R = E \times \eta / E_{km}$

### Ō£ģ Must-Memorize Units
- **V** (Volt), **A** (Ampere), **W** (Watt), **Nm** (Newton-meter), **rad/s** (radian/second), **Hz** (Hertz), **T** (Tesla), **Wh** (Watt-hour), **kWh** (kilowatt-hour), **RPM** (revolution per minute).

### Ō£ģ Examiner's Quick-View
- Always **draw a labeled block/circuit diagram** (3ŌĆō4 marks guaranteed).
- Always **state assumptions** in derivations.
- Always **mention units** in numerical answers.
- Always **explain physical significance** of derived formulas.

---
