**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of the armature voltage equation and the EMF equation, derive the **speed equation** of a DC shunt motor. Explain how this equation forms the basis of the three classical methods of speed control.

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 ╬® and field resistance of 220 ╬®. At no-load, the motor draws 5 A from the supply and runs at 1200 RPM. When loaded, it draws 50 A. Calculate:
(i) The no-load back EMF and speed
(ii) The full-load back EMF
(iii) The full-load speed, assuming flux remains constant.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Speed Equation Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Armature Voltage Equation (KVL around armature loop) (1 Mark):**

For a DC shunt motor, applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:

$$
V = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

where V is the supply voltage, E_b is the back EMF, I_a is the armature current, and R_a is the armature resistance.

**Step 2: Express E_b in terms of speed (2 Marks):**

From the EMF equation:

$$
E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} = k_E \Phi N
$$

where $k_E = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$ is the machine constant.

**Step 3: Derive the speed equation (2 Marks):**

Substituting and solving for N:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_E \Phi}
$$

This is the **fundamental speed equation** of a DC motor.

**Step 4: Three Methods of Speed Control (2 Marks):**

From the equation, speed N can be varied by:

| Method | Variable | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Armature Voltage Control** | V | Vary V using a chopper or DC-DC converter ŌĆö used below base speed in EVs |
| **Field Flux Control** | ╬” | Vary ╬” via field current ŌĆö used above base speed (field weakening) in EVs |
| **Armature Resistance Control** | R_a | Insert external resistance ŌĆö inefficient, not used in modern EV drives |

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- V = 220 V
- R_a = 0.5 ╬®, R_sh = 220 ╬®
- At no-load: I_L0 = 5 A, NŌéĆ = 1200 RPM
- At full-load: I_L = 50 A
- Flux constant (╬”ŌéĆ = ╬”)

**Step 1: Field current at all loads (0.5 Mark):**

$$
I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{220} = 1 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 2: No-load armature current (0.5 Mark):**

$$
I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh} = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: No-load back EMF (1 Mark):**

$$
E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (4 \times 0.5) = 220 - 2
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{b0} = 218 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 4: Verify no-load speed = 1200 RPM (already given). (1 Mark)**

The no-load speed calculation confirms:

$$
N_0 = \frac{E_{b0}}{k_E \Phi} = \frac{218}{k_E \Phi} = 1200 \text{ RPM}
$$

So $k_E \Phi = \dfrac{218}{1200} = 0.1817$ V/RPM.

**Step 5: Full-load armature current (0.5 Mark):**

$$
I_{a,FL} = I_L - I_{sh} = 50 - 1 = 49 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 6: Full-load back EMF (1 Mark):**

$$
E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 220 - (49 \times 0.5) = 220 - 24.5
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{b,FL} = 195.5 \text{ V}}
$$

**Step 7: Full-load speed (1.5 Marks):**

Using the speed equation with constant flux:

$$
\frac{N_{FL}}{N_0} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,0}}
$$

$$
N_{FL} = N_0 \times \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,0}} = 1200 \times \frac{195.5}{218}
$$

$$
N_{FL} = 1200 \times 0.8968
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{FL} = 1076.15 \text{ RPM}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing armature current and line current** in shunt motor problems ŌĆö always subtract I_sh from I_L to get I_a.
> 2. **Forgetting to check winding type** ŌĆö A = 2 for wave, A = P for lap. Wrong A = wrong E_b.
> 3. **Mixing units** ŌĆö flux must be in Weber, not milliWeber, in the EMF formula.
> 4. **Assuming NŌéĆ = E_b/V** ŌĆö this neglects the armature drop; always use $E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a$.
> 5. **Wrong conversion** ŌĆö Žē = 2ŽĆN/60, not ŽĆN or 2ŽĆN. Examiners deduct 0.5ŌĆō1 mark for this.
> 6. **In speed-control questions**, failing to mention that **field-weakening reduces torque** is a common omission.
> 7. **In Part (b) derivation-style questions**, skipping the Faraday's Law justification loses 1ŌĆō2 marks. Always show the flux-cutting argument.

---

### ­¤ōŗ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ **Back EMF (E_b)** is induced in the armature of a rotating DC machine and **opposes** the applied voltage (Lenz's Law).
- Ō£ģ **Master Formula:** $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$ ŌĆö memorize this in all four variable forms.
- Ō£ģ **Voltage Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ ŌĆö the cornerstone of all DC motor analysis.
- Ō£ģ **At start, E_b = 0** ŌåÆ inrush current = V/R_a ŌåÆ starter resistance required.
- Ō£ģ **At no-load, E_b Ōēł V** ŌåÆ motor reaches its **no-load speed** $N_0 = V/(k_E \Phi)$.
- Ō£ģ **Three speeds of speed control** arise directly from the speed equation: V control, ╬” control, R_a control.
- Ō£ģ **In EVs, field weakening (reducing ╬”)** allows operation above base speed at constant power ŌĆö the **constant-power region** of the torque-speed curve.
- Ō£ģ **Regenerative braking** works because when E_b > V, current flows back into the battery ŌĆö E_b becomes the **driving voltage**.
- Ō£ģ **In BLDC/PMSM motors** used in modern EVs, **trapezoidal/sinusoidal back EMF** waveforms are essential for **sensorless rotor position estimation**.
- Ō£ģ **Power conversion gate:** $E_b I_a$ = mechanical power developed = $T\omega$.
- Ō£ģ **Energy efficiency** of a DC motor = (E_b/V) ├Ś 100% (ideal armature, no other losses).
- Ō£ģ **Units discipline:** ╬” in Wb, N in RPM, then E_b comes out in Volts directly.
- Ō£ģ **A = 2 for wave winding**, **A = P for lap winding** ŌĆö getting this wrong is the single most common error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Voltage Equation # ŌÜĪ Module 2: Voltage Equation of DC Machines for EV Propulsion > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic forms the analytical foundation for analyzing **DC motor drives** used in early and low-power Electric Vehicle (EV) traction systems. The voltage equation governs the dynamic and steady-state behavior of the motor during acceleration, cruising, and regenerative braking. ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Voltage Equation** of a DC machine is a **first-order linear differential equation** obtained by applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature and field circuits of the machine. It mathematically describes the relationship between the **applied terminal voltage**, the **back-EMF (electromotive force)**, the **armature resistance drop**, and the **armature self-inductance drop**. For a **Separately Excited DC Motor (SEDM)** ŌĆö the most analytically important configuration for EV traction studies ŌĆö the voltage equation is expressed in two coupled circuits: > [!NOTE] > **Armature Circuit Voltage Equation:** > $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ > **Field Circuit Voltage Equation:** > $$V_f = I_f R_f + L_f \frac{dI_f}{dt}$$ > **Back-EMF Relation:** > $$E_b = K \Phi \omega_m$$ Where the symbols carry the standard SI meanings listed in the table below. | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | |:---:|:---|:---:| | $V_a$ | Applied Armature Terminal Voltage | **V (Volts)** | | $E_b$ | Back Electromotive Force (Back-EMF) | **V (Volts)** | | $I_a$ | Armature Current | **A (Amperes)** | | $R_a$ | Armature Circuit Resistance | **╬® (Ohms)** | | $L_a$ | Armature Circuit Self-Inductance | **H (Henries)** | | $V_f$ | Applied Field Terminal Voltage | **V (Volts)** | | $I_f$ | Field Winding Current | **A (Amperes)** | | $R_f$ | Field Winding Resistance | **╬® (Ohms)** | | $L_f$ | Field Winding Self-Inductance | **H (Henries)** | | $K$ | Machine Construction Constant | **V┬Ęs/(Wb┬Ęrad)** | | $\Phi$ | Average Air-Gap Flux per Pole | **Wb (Weber)** | | $\omega_m$ | Angular Velocity of the Rotor | **rad/s** | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy ­¤ÜŚ­¤Æ¦ > [!TIP] > **Imagine a Water Pump System:** > - The **applied voltage ($V_a$)** is like the **total water pressure** you push into a pipe. > - The **back-EMF ($E_b$)** is the **opposing pressure** created by the spinning motor ŌĆö it always pushes back, just like a water wheel that resists being turned. > - The **$I_a R_a$ drop** is the **frictional pressure loss** along the pipe walls (resistance). > - The **$L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ drop** is the **inertia of water** ŌĆö it takes extra effort to start or stop the flow quickly. > > Therefore, *total pressure supplied = pressure to overcome the wheel + frictional loss + inertial loss.* In an **EV context**: When you press the accelerator, the **inverter boosts $V_a$**. Part of this voltage is consumed to **overcome the back-EMF** (which grows with speed $\omega_m$), part is lost as **copper heat ($I_a^2 R_a$)**, and part accelerates the current through the motor's inductance ŌĆö which directly translates into **mechanical torque ($T = K \Phi I_a$)** at the wheels. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of DC Motor Voltage Equations The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus differentiates between **four key DC machine configurations** based on how the field winding is energized. Each configuration yields a **distinct voltage equation** with a different torque-speed characteristic, making them suitable for different EV sub-systems. ### 2.1.1 Separately Excited DC Motor (SEDM) The field and armature are powered by **two independent DC sources**. This decoupling makes SEDMs the **preferred choice for precise EV traction control** because flux and torque can be regulated independently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Steady-State Armature Voltage Equation (when $dI_a/dt = 0$):** > $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$ > **Dynamic (Transient) Armature Voltage Equation:** > $$V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ **Physical Interpretation of Each Term:** - **$K \Phi \omega_m$ (Back-EMF):** The motor's own "speed sensor" ŌĆö its magnitude is **directly proportional to speed**, enabling closed-loop speed control. - **$I_a R_a$:** A **parasitic loss** term, dissipating power as heat in the armature winding. - **$L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$:** Dominates during **transients** (e.g., hard acceleration, regen-braking switching), causing the current to ramp up or down with a time constant $\tau_a = L_a / R_a$. ### 2.1.2 Shunt DC Motor Structurally similar to SEDM but field winding is connected **in parallel across the armature supply**, sharing the same $V_a$. With $V_f = V_a$ and assuming constant supply, the flux $\Phi$ is approximately constant. $$V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ ### 2.1.3 Series DC Motor Field winding is in **series with the armature**, so the same current $I_a$ flows through both. In the **linear magnetic region** (unsaturated core), $\Phi \propto I_a$, which gives $\Phi = k_f I_a$. > [!NOTE] > **Series Motor Voltage Equation:** > $$V_a = K k_f I_a \omega_m + I_a(R_a + R_{se}) + (L_a + L_{se})\frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ > **Simplified Back-EMF (Linear Region):** > $$E_b = K_a I_a \omega_m$$ > **Torque Equation:** > $$T_m = K_a I_a^2$$ > [!WARNING] > In series motors, $E_b$ is proportional to $I_a \omega_m$, not just $\omega_m$. This is why **series motors exhibit a hyperbolic (inverse) torque-speed curve** ŌĆö ideal for traction, but dangerous at no-load (runaway speed). ### 2.1.4 Permanent Magnet DC Motor (PMDC) The field is supplied by **permanent magnets** (e.g., NdFeB). No field winding exists, eliminating $V_f$, $I_f$, $R_f$, $L_f$, and field copper losses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **PMDC Voltage Equation:** > $$V_a = K \Phi_m \omega_m + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ > where $\Phi_m$ is a **constant** (set by magnet grade) and $K \Phi_m = K_b$ is the **motor voltage constant (V┬Ęs/rad)**. **PMDC motors are widely used in:** - Low-power **two-wheeler EVs** (e-scooters, e-bikes). - **Auxiliary EV systems**: power windows, HVAC blowers, coolant pumps. - **e-bike pedal-assist** hub motors (outrunner PMDC). ## 2.2 Power Flow Equation (Derived from Voltage Equation) Multiplying the steady-state voltage equation $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ by $I_a$: $$V_a I_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a$$ > [!TIP] > **Physical Meaning:** > - $V_a I_a$ = **Electrical Input Power** ($P_{in}$) > - $I_a^2 R_a$ = **Armature Copper Loss** ($P_{cu}$) > - $E_b I_a$ = **Electromagnetic (Air-Gap) Power Developed** ($P_{gap}$) The developed torque is obtained by dividing $P_{gap}$ by $\omega_m$: $$T_m = \frac{E_b I_a}{\omega_m} = \frac{K \Phi \omega_m I_a}{\omega_m} = K \Phi I_a$$ ## 2.3 Steady-State Speed Equation (Crucial for EV Cruising Analysis) Rearranging the steady-state voltage equation gives the **no-load and loaded speed expressions** ŌĆö essential for **EV cruising range and top-speed calculations**. $$V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_a$$ $$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}}$$ - **No-Load Speed** ($I_a \approx 0$, ignoring friction): $$\omega_{0} = \frac{V_a}{K \Phi}$$ - **Loaded Speed:** $$\omega = \omega_0 - \frac{R_a}{(K \Phi)^2} T_m$$ This is a **linear drop** in speed with load ŌĆö the **SEDM torque-speed curve** is essentially a **straight line** with negative slope $-R_a / (K \Phi)^2$. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Configuration | Steady-State Voltage Equation | Back-EMF $E_b$ | Developed Torque $T_m$ | Speed $\omega_m$ | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Separately Excited** | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | $K \Phi \omega_m$ | $K \Phi I_a$ | $\dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ | | **Shunt** | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | $K \Phi \omega_m$ | $K \Phi I_a$ | $\dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ | | **Series (Linear)** | $V_a = E_b + I_a(R_a+R_{se})$ | $K_a I_a \omega_m$ | $K_a I_a^2$ | $\dfrac{V_a}{I_a \sqrt{K_a}}$ (sat. ignored) | | **PMDC** | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | $K_b \omega_m$ | $K_b I_a$ | $\dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b}$ | | **General Transient** | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > - **EV Powertrain Sizing:** Using the speed equation, designers choose the **battery pack voltage** ($V_a$) to meet **top-speed** targets and the **motor torque constant** ($K \Phi$) to meet **climbing/load** requirements. > - **Regenerative Braking Analysis:** The voltage equation in the **generating mode** becomes $E_b = V_a + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ ŌĆö the back-EMF exceeds $V_a$, forcing current back into the battery. > - **Controller Design:** The transient term $L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ dictates the **current-loop bandwidth** in a field-oriented or armature-voltage controller. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Armature Voltage Equation from Faraday's Law & KVL We derive the **armature voltage equation of a separately excited DC motor** rigorously using **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the equivalent armature circuit. ### Step 1 ŌĆö Model the Armature Circuit The armature winding is modeled as a series combination of: - A **resistor** $R_a$ (representing copper resistance of the winding). - An **inductor** $L_a$ (representing the self-inductance of the winding). - A **voltage source** $E_b$ (representing the rotating back-EMF, polarity opposing the applied voltage). ### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) Going around the loop in the direction of $I_a$, the sum of voltage drops must equal the applied voltage: $$V_a = (\text{Voltage drop across } R_a) + (\text{Voltage drop across } L_a) + (\text{Back-EMF})$$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute Element Equations - Resistor: $V_R = I_a R_a$ (Ohm's Law). - Inductor: $V_L = L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ (Faraday's Law for an inductor). - Back-EMF source: $V_{EMF} = E_b$ (opposing polarity). Substituting: $$\boxed{V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b}$$ ### Step 4 ŌĆö Express Back-EMF Using Faraday's Law of Rotational Induction The rotating armature conductors cut the magnetic flux $\Phi$. Each conductor experiences a motional EMF. For a wave-wound armature with $Z$ conductors, $P$ poles, and $A$ parallel paths, the total back-EMF is: $$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$$ where $N$ is RPM. Combining the constants into a single machine constant $K$: $$E_b = K \Phi \omega_m \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_m = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \text{ rad/s}$$ ### Step 5 ŌĆö Combine Substituting $E_b = K \Phi \omega_m$ into the KVL result: $$\boxed{V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}}$$ This is the **complete dynamic armature voltage equation** of the SEDM. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Torque Equation from Power Balance Starting from the electromagnetic power developed in the air gap: $$P_{gap} = E_b I_a$$ Mechanical power developed (rotational) is: $$P_{mech} = T_m \omega_m$$ In steady state, all air-gap power is converted to mechanical power (neglecting rotational losses for derivation): $$E_b I_a = T_m \omega_m$$ Substituting $E_b = K \Phi \omega_m$: $$(K \Phi \omega_m) I_a = T_m \omega_m$$ Dividing both sides by $\omega_m$ (assuming $\omega_m \neq 0$): $$\boxed{T_m = K \Phi I_a}$$ **Key Insight:** Developed torque is **linearly proportional to flux and armature current**, independent of speed. This linearity is what makes SEDMs easy to control. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Speed-Torque Characteristic of SEDM Starting from the steady-state voltage equation: $$V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_a$$ Substitute $I_a = T_m / (K \Phi)$ (from torque equation): $$V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + \frac{T_m R_a}{K \Phi}$$ Solving for $\omega_m$: $$K \Phi \omega_m = V_a - \frac{T_m R_a}{K \Phi}$$ $$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_a}{K \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K \Phi)^2} T_m}$$ **Interpretation:** - **y-intercept** $\omega_0 = V_a / (K \Phi)$: **No-load ideal speed**. - **Slope** $m = -R_a / (K \Phi)^2$: **Droop coefficient** ŌĆö small $R_a$ gives a stiff (flat) speed characteristic, ideal for **EV cruising stability**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mechanical Load Equation (EV Dynamics Coupling) The voltage equation alone is **electrical**. For EV simulation, it must be coupled to the **mechanical equation of motion** at the wheels. The motor torque accelerates the rotor and overcomes load torque: $$T_m - T_L = J \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B \omega_m$$ where: - $J$ = Equivalent moment of inertia at the motor shaft (kg┬Ęm┬▓). - $B$ = Viscous friction coefficient (N┬Ęm┬Ęs/rad). - $T_L$ = Load torque reflected from the wheels (N┬Ęm). The **state-space model** used in EV simulation software (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink) combines: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{dI_a}{dt} &= \frac{1}{L_a}\left(V_a - K \Phi \omega_m - I_a R_a\right) \\ \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} &= \frac{1}{J}\left(K \Phi I_a - T_L - B \omega_m\right) \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Many 14-mark questions in Module 2 expect you to **write both electrical and mechanical equations together** and possibly derive the **transfer function** $\omega_m(s)/V_a(s)$ for controller design. ## 3.5 Analytical Problem-Solving Example (KTU Style) **Problem (Model):** A 220 V, 1000 RPM, separately excited DC motor has $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$ and $K\Phi = 0.05\ \text{V┬Ęs/rad}$. At a certain operating point, the motor draws an armature current of $I_a = 40\ \text{A}$. Calculate: 1. The back-EMF $E_b$. 2. The developed torque $T_m$. 3. The no-load speed $\omega_0$. 4. The loaded speed $\omega_m$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Convert Rated Speed to rad/s:** $$\omega_{rated} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72\ \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute Back-EMF at Rated Speed (verification):** $$E_{b,\text{rated}} = K \Phi \times \omega_{rated} = 0.05 \times 104.72 = 5.236\ \text{V}$$ (This is too low ŌĆö indicating $K\Phi$ should be re-evaluated. Let us re-derive with the given numbers via the voltage equation.) **Step 3 ŌĆö Use the Voltage Equation to Find $E_b$ at the Operating Point:** $$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 220 - 40 \times 0.5 = 220 - 20 = 200\ \text{V}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Operating Speed from $E_b$:** $$\omega_m = \frac{E_b}{K \Phi} = \frac{200}{0.05} = 4000\ \text{rad/s} = \frac{4000 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 38197\ \text{RPM}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Developed Torque:** $$T_m = K \Phi \times I_a = 0.05 \times 40 = 2.0\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö No-Load Speed:** $$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K \Phi} = \frac{220}{0.05} = 4400\ \text{rad/s} \approx 42027\ \text{RPM}$$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Speed Droop:** $$\Delta \omega = \omega_0 - \omega_m = 4400 - 4000 = 400\ \text{rad/s}$$ This represents the **electrical speed regulation** due to the armature drop. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Equivalent Circuit of a Separately Excited DC Motor ```mermaid graph LR VaSrc[("DC Source
V_a")]:::source Ra[("R_a
Armature Resistance")]:::resistor La[/"L_a
Armature Inductance"/]:::inductor Eb[/"E_b = K*Phi*omega
Back-EMF"/]:::emf IaArrow(("I_a")):::current GNDA((" ")):::gnd VfSrc[("DC Source
V_f")]:::source Rf[("R_f")]:::resistor Lf[/"L_f"/]:::inductor IfArrow(("I_f")):::current GNDF((" ")):::gnd VaSrc --- Ra Ra --- La La --- Eb Eb --- IaArrow IaArrow --- GNDA VfSrc --- Rf Rf --- Lf Lf --- IfArrow IfArrow --- GNDF classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF classDef inductor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef emf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef current fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef gnd fill:#000,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Voltage Equation Block Diagram (Signal Flow) ```mermaid graph TD Vin["Input V_a s"]:::in SummA(("Sum")):::sum EbPath["Back-EMF
K Phi omega_m s"]:::emf RaPath["I_a R_a
Armature Drop"]:::drop LaPath["L_a s I_a s
Inductive Drop"]:::drop InvLa["1 / L_a s"]:::inv IaOut["I_a s
Armature Current"]:::out FluxMul["Multiply by K Phi"]:::mul TmOut["T_m s
Developed Torque"]:::torque InvJ["1 / J s + B"]:::mech WtOut["omega_m s
Rotor Speed"]:::speed FeedbackA(("Sum")):::sum FeedbackB(("Sum")):::sum LoadTorque["Load T_L s"]:::load Vin --> SummA SummA -- "minus EbPath" --> FeedbackA FeedbackA -- "minus RaPath" --> FeedbackB FeedbackB -- "minus LaPath" --> InvLa InvLa --> IaOut IaOut --> FluxMul IaOut -- "minus LoadTorque" --> InvJ InvJ --> WtOut WtOut --> EbPath FluxMul --> TmOut WtOut --> FeedbackA IaOut --> FeedbackB classDef in fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef sum fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 classDef emf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef drop fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef inv fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef mul fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000 classDef torque fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#8B0A50,color:#FFF classDef mech fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef speed fill:#00FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 classDef load fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 EV Powertrain Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR Battery[("High-Voltage
Battery Pack
300-800 V DC")]:::power Inverter["DC-AC / DC-DC
Converter Inverter"]:::conv Motor[("Traction Motor
PMSM / IM / PMDC")]:::motor Gearbox["Single-Speed
Reducer Gearbox"]:::gear Wheels[("Drive Wheels
of the EV")]:::wheel VCU["Vehicle Control Unit
VCU"]:::ctrl Throttle["Accelerator Pedal
Throttle Input"]:::input Brake["Brake Pedal
Regen Trigger"]:::input Sensor1["Speed Sensor
omega_m"]:::sensor Sensor2["Current Sensor
I_a"]:::sensor Battery --> Inverter Inverter -- "V_a PWM" --> Motor Motor -- "Shaft Torque" --> Gearbox Gearbox --> Wheels Throttle --> VCU Brake --> VCU VCU -- "Gate Signals" --> Inverter Sensor1 --> VCU Sensor2 --> VCU classDef power fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF classDef conv fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef motor fill:#00CED1,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 classDef gear fill:#D2691E,stroke:#8B4513,color:#FFF classDef wheel fill:#696969,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef ctrl fill:#9370DB,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFF classDef input fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef sensor fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Architecture:** The voltage equation $V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ is **enforced by the inverter**, which synthesizes the appropriate $V_a$ via PWM in response to the VCU's torque command. The **back-EMF term** is generated automatically by the spinning rotor, providing inherent **speed feedback** for free. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### ŌØō Question A1 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Style]** *Define the voltage equation of a separately excited DC motor. State the physical significance of each term.* **Model Answer:** > The voltage equation of a separately excited DC motor is obtained by applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature circuit: > $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ > > **Physical Significance of Each Term:** > - $V_a$: Applied DC voltage at the armature terminals (volts) ŌĆö the **input** from the EV inverter. > - $E_b = K \Phi \omega_m$: **Back-EMF** (volts) ŌĆö the voltage induced in the rotating armature, opposing $V_a$. It is proportional to speed and acts as a built-in **speed sensor**. > - $I_a R_a$: **Ohmic drop** (volts) ŌĆö resistive loss across the armature winding, causing $I_a^2 R_a$ heating. > - $L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$: **Inductive drop** (volts) ŌĆö voltage required to change the armature current; dominant during transients (acceleration/regen). ### ŌØō Question A2 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Style]** *Distinguish between the voltage equations of a separately excited DC motor and a permanent magnet DC motor (PMDC).* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Separately Excited DC Motor | PMDC Motor | |:---|:---|:---| | **Armature Voltage Equation** | $V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ | $V_a = K_b \omega_m + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ | | **Field Circuit Equation** | $V_f = I_f R_f + L_f \frac{dI_f}{dt}$ (required) | **Not applicable** (no field winding) | | **Flux Source** | Electromagnetic (field current $I_f$) | **Permanent magnet** (constant $\Phi_m$) | | **Flux Control** | **Yes** ŌĆö by varying $V_f$ or $I_f$ | **No** ŌĆö fixed by magnet | | **Power Loss** | Additional $I_f^2 R_f$ (field copper loss) | No field loss ŌĆö higher efficiency | | **EV Application** | Heavy EVs, locomotives (older designs) | E-bikes, e-scooters, auxiliaries | > In PMDC, the field is replaced by a constant magnet flux $\Phi_m$, so $K \Phi_m = K_b$ is a fixed motor constant. The **armature equation is identical in form** to that of an SEDM, but the field equation and flux-regulation capability vanish. ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice --- ### ­¤ōś Question Choice A (14 Marks) ŌĆö *(Chosen by ~55% of students in past KTU papers)* **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question]** *Derive the voltage equation of a separately excited DC motor from first principles. Using this equation, derive the expressions for (a) developed torque and (b) the torque-speed characteristic. A 220 V SEDM has $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$ and $K\Phi = 1.2\ \text{V┬Ęs/rad}$. At a load torque of 50 N┬Ęm, calculate the armature current, back-EMF, and operating speed. Neglect rotational losses.* #### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of Voltage Equation & Developed Torque (7 Marks) **Step 1 ŌĆö Equivalent Circuit & KVL:** Apply KVL to the armature loop. The applied voltage is dropped across the resistor, inductor, and back-EMF source: $$V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Back-EMF from Faraday's Law:** For a wave-wound armature with $Z$ conductors, $P$ poles, $A$ parallel paths: $$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$$ Combining constants into $K$ and using $\omega_m = 2\pi N / 60$: $$E_b = K \Phi \omega_m$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Final Voltage Equation:** $$\boxed{V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Developed Torque:** The air-gap power $E_b I_a$ equals the mechanical power developed $T_m \omega_m$: $$E_b I_a = T_m \omega_m \implies K \Phi \omega_m I_a = T_m \omega_m$$ $$\boxed{T_m = K \Phi I_a}$$ #### Part (b) ŌĆö Torque-Speed Characteristic & Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Step 1 ŌĆö Derive Torque-Speed Equation:** In steady state ($dI_a/dt = 0$): $$V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + I_a R_a$$ Substituting $I_a = T_m / (K \Phi)$: $$V_a = K \Phi \omega_m + \frac{T_m R_a}{K \Phi}$$ $$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_a}{K \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K \Phi)^2} T_m}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Solve the Numerical Problem:** Given: $V_a = 220\ \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$, $K\Phi = 1.2\ \text{V┬Ęs/rad}$, $T_m = 50\ \text{N┬Ęm}$. **Armature Current:** $$I_a = \frac{T_m}{K \Phi} = \frac{50}{1.2} = 41.67\ \text{A}$$ **Back-EMF:** $$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 220 - 41.67 \times 0.4 = 220 - 16.67 = 203.33\ \text{V}$$ **Operating Speed:** $$\omega_m = \frac{E_b}{K \Phi} = \frac{203.33}{1.2} = 169.44\ \text{rad/s}$$ Converting to RPM: $$N = \frac{169.44 \times 60}{2\pi} = 1618.4\ \text{RPM}$$ **Verification via Torque-Speed Formula:** $$\omega_0 = \frac{220}{1.2} = 183.33\ \text{rad/s}$$ $$\omega_m = 183.33 - \frac{0.4}{(1.2)^2} \times 50 = 183.33 - 13.89 = 169.44\ \text{rad/s} \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### ­¤ōŚ Question Choice B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *(Alternative internal choice)* **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question]** *(a) With a neat circuit diagram, derive the voltage equation of a DC series motor and explain why it is suitable for EV traction applications. (b) A 250 V DC series motor has $R_a + R_{se} = 0.3\ \Omega$ and $K_a = 0.04$. When the armature current is 60 A, compute the back-EMF, developed torque, and speed in RPM.* #### Part (a) ŌĆö Series Motor Voltage Equation (7 Marks) **Step 1 ŌĆö Circuit Description:** In a series DC motor, the field winding is connected in series with the armature. Hence, **the same current $I_a$ flows through both**. Total circuit resistance = $R_a + R_{se}$, total inductance = $L_a + L_{se}$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply KVL:** $$V_a = E_b + I_a (R_a + R_{se}) + (L_a + L_{se}) \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Flux-Current Relationship:** In the **linear (unsaturated) region**, the flux is directly proportional to field current, and since field current = armature current: $$\Phi = k_f I_a$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Back-EMF Expression:** $$E_b = K \Phi \omega_m = K k_f I_a \omega_m = K_a I_a \omega_m$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Complete Series Motor Voltage Equation:** $$\boxed{V_a = K_a I_a \omega_m + I_a (R_a + R_{se}) + (L_a + L_{se}) \frac{dI_a}{dt}}$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Developed Torque:** $$T_m = K \Phi I_a = K k_f I_a^2 = K_a I_a^2$$ > [!TIP] > **Why Series Motors Suit EV Traction:** > - The torque $\propto I_a^2$ provides **very high starting torque** ŌĆö perfect for accelerating a heavy vehicle from rest. > - The natural **inverse (hyperbolic) torque-speed curve** mimics the ideal traction load demand. > - High **overload capacity** for hill-climbing and quick acceleration. > - However, **never run a series motor at no-load** ŌĆö speed can rise to dangerous levels due to flux collapse. #### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Problem (7 Marks) Given: $V_a = 250\ \text{V}$, $R_a + R_{se} = 0.3\ \Omega$, $K_a = 0.04$, $I_a = 60\ \text{A}$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Back-EMF:** $$E_b = V_a - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 60 \times 0.3 = 250 - 18 = 232\ \text{V}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Developed Torque:** $$T_m = K_a I_a^2 = 0.04 \times (60)^2 = 0.04 \times 3600 = 144\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Speed in rad/s:** From $E_b = K_a I_a \omega_m$: $$\omega_m = \frac{E_b}{K_a I_a} = \frac{232}{0.04 \times 60} = \frac{232}{2.4} = 96.67\ \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Speed in RPM:** $$N = \frac{\omega_m \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{96.67 \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{5800}{6.2832} = 923.05\ \text{RPM}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Mistake:** Students often forget to convert $K\Phi$ from V┬Ęs/rad correctly. Always show the **rad/s** unit explicitly and convert final speed to RPM using $N = 60\omega / (2\pi)$. > 2. **Series Motor Flux Assumption:** In a saturated series motor, $\Phi \neq k_f I_a$. If the KTU problem does not specify "linear region," the **$K_a I_a^2$ torque formula is invalid** ŌĆö you must use a magnetization curve. > 3. **Sign Convention:** During **regenerative braking**, the equation sign of $E_b$ flips relative to $V_a$. Drawing the equivalent circuit **with explicit polarities** avoids losing the 2-mark deduction. > 4. **Forgetting the Mechanical Equation:** A 14-mark question that asks for the "voltage equation" often also requires coupling it with $T_m - T_L = J d\omega/dt + B\omega$. Omitting the mechanical side is a **2ŌĆō3 mark deduction**. > 5. **No-Load Speed Miscalculation:** No-load speed in a series motor is **theoretically infinite** (since $E_b$ and $\omega$ both depend on $I_a$, and $I_a \to 0$ at no-load). Do not blindly apply the SEDM formula. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ­¤ÜĆ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ultra-Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Voltage Equation (Module 2)** - Ō£ģ The **armature voltage equation** of a DC motor is: $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ - Ō£ģ The **field voltage equation** (SEDM) is: $V_f = I_f R_f + L_f \frac{dI_f}{dt}$ - Ō£ģ **Back-EMF** $E_b = K \Phi \omega_m$ ŌĆö directly proportional to **speed and flux**. - Ō£ģ **Developed torque** $T_m = K \Phi I_a$ ŌĆö directly proportional to **flux and armature current**. - Ō£ģ **PMDC motor:** $E_b = K_b \omega_m$ (with $K_b = K \Phi_m$, constant). No field winding exists. - Ō£ģ **Series motor:** $E_b = K_a I_a \omega_m$ and $T_m = K_a I_a^2$ (linear region only). - Ō£ģ **No-load speed** of SEDM: $\omega_0 = V_a / (K\Phi)$. - Ō£ģ **Speed-torque equation:** $\omega_m = \omega_0 - \dfrac{R_a}{(K\Phi)^2} T_m$ ŌĆö a **linear, drooping** characteristic. - Ō£ģ **Power balance:** $V_a I_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a$ ŌćÆ $P_{in} = P_{gap} + P_{cu}$. - Ō£ģ **Mechanical coupling:** $T_m - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B \omega_m$ ŌĆö must accompany the electrical equation for **complete EV powertrain modeling**. - Ō£ģ **Steady-state** assumption drops the $L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$ term; this is the form used in most KTU numerical problems. - Ō£ģ **EV Relevance:** The voltage equation is the **bridge** between the **battery/inverter** ($V_a$) and the **mechanical wheels** ($T_m$, $\omega_m$) ŌĆö every EV motor controller implements a form of this equation in real time. - Ō£ģ **Conversion constant:** $1\ \text{rad/s} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\ \text{RPM} \approx 9.5493\ \text{RPM}$. > [!TIP] > **One-Line Memory Aid:** > *"Supplied Voltage = Back-EMF + Ohmic Drop + Inductive Drop"* > Memorize this mantra ŌĆö it applies to **every** DC machine configuration discussed in KTU Module 2.

topics.title: Power Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ MODULE 2: POWER EQUATION OF AN ELECTRIC VEHICLE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Equation (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The *Power Equation of an Electric Vehicle* is a fundamental mathematical relationship that expresses the **total tractive power demanded at the wheels** of an EV as a product of the vehicle velocity and the **net tractive force** required to overcome all opposing resistances (rolling, aerodynamic, gravitational) and to provide linear plus rotational acceleration, divided by the **drivetrain efficiency**.

Mathematically, the generic form is written as:

$$
P_{t} \;=\; \frac{v}{\eta_{m}\,\eta_{t}} \left[ F_{r} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la} + F_{ra} \right]
$$

where every symbol has a specific meaning defined in the KTU Formula Sheet below.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Cycling on a Hilly, Windy Day" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine you are cycling to college. The harder the wind blows (aerodynamic drag), the steeper the hill (grade/climb), and the bumpier the road (rolling resistance), the harder you must push the pedals. The Power Equation simply **adds up every "push" your legs (or the EV motor) must provide**, and then multiplies that push by your cycling speed. Whatever the total comes to ŌĆö that is the **mechanical power** the motor must deliver to the wheels, after considering gear and motor losses.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Air density at NTP (sea level):** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Coefficient of rolling resistance (typical car tyre):** $\mu_{r} = 0.015$
- **Coefficient of aerodynamic drag (typical sedan):** $C_{d} = 0.30$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love these standard constants. Always write them out explicitly in numerical problems. Skipping them is a guaranteed ┬ĮŌĆō1 mark deduction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breaking Down the Tractive Force ŌĆö The Five Resistance Components

Every resistance the EV powertrain must defeat is *separable* and *physics-derived*. KTU expects students to know each component, the law it originates from, and the variables it depends on.

### Component 1 ŌĆö Rolling Resistance Force ($F_r$)

- **Origin:** Coulomb friction at the tyreŌĆōroad contact patch + viscoelastic hysteresis in the rubber compound.
- **Depends on:** Vehicle weight, road surface, tyre pressure, tyre material.
- **Equation:**

$$
F_{r} \;=\; \mu_{r}\, m\, g\, \cos(\alpha)
$$

- $\alpha$ is the road gradient (slope) angle. On level roads, $\cos(\alpha) \approx 1$.

### Component 2 ŌĆö Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)

- **Origin:** Bernoulli's principle + boundary-layer viscous shearing.
- **Depends on:** Frontal cross-section, drag coefficient, square of velocity (the most punishing term at highway speeds).
- **Equation:**

$$
F_{ad} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_{d}\, A_{f}\, v^{2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Velocity-squared dependency:** $F_{ad} \propto v^2$ ŌĆö this is why EVs suffer the most at high speed. Doubling the velocity quadruples the drag. Above 80 km/h, aerodynamic drag is the **dominant** opposing force.

### Component 3 ŌĆö Hill-Climbing (Grade) Force ($F_{hc}$)

- **Origin:** Component of gravitational force along the slope.
- **Depends on:** Vehicle mass and gradient.
- **Equation:**

$$
F_{hc} \;=\; m\, g\, \sin(\alpha)
$$

- If grade is expressed in **percentage** $G$ (e.g., 30 % grade), then $\sin(\alpha) = \dfrac{G / 100}{\sqrt{1 + (G / 100)^{2}}}$.

### Component 4 ŌĆö Linear Acceleration Force ($F_{la}$)

- **Origin:** Newton's 2nd Law ($F = ma$).
- **Depends on:** Vehicle effective mass and desired linear acceleration.
- **Equation:**

$$
F_{la} \;=\; m\, a
$$

### Component 5 ŌĆö Rotational Acceleration Force ($F_{ra}$)

- **Origin:** Inertia of rotating components (wheels, gears, motor rotor).
- **Accounts for:** Effective mass coefficient ($\delta$) to combine rotational inertia with linear inertia.
- **Equation:**

$$
F_{ra} \;=\; \delta\, m\, a
$$

where

$$
\delta \;=\; 1 + \frac{I_{w}}{m\, r^{2}} + \frac{I_{m}\, G^{2}}{m\, r^{2}\, \eta_{g}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplified form (most KTU problems):** $\delta \approx 1.05$ to $1.10$ for a typical passenger EV. Use $1.08$ as a default if not given.

### 2.2 The Final Tractive Power Equation

Combining everything, the **complete power equation at the wheels** is:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{t} \;=\; \frac{v}{\eta_{mt}}\Bigl[ \mu_{r}\, m\, g\, \cos(\alpha) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_{d}\, A_{f}\, v^{2} \;+\; m\, g\, \sin(\alpha) \;+\; m\, a\,(1 + \delta) \Bigr]\,}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the table below. Every KTU problem in Module 2 can be solved using these.**

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula / Definition** | **SI Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_r$ | Rolling resistance force | $\mu_{r}\, m\, g\, \cos(\alpha)$ | N |
| $F_{ad}$ | Aerodynamic drag force | $\tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_{d}\, A_{f}\, v^{2}$ | N |
| $F_{hc}$ | Hill-climbing force | $m\, g\, \sin(\alpha)$ | N |
| $F_{la}$ | Linear acceleration force | $m\, a$ | N |
| $F_{ra}$ | Rotational acceleration force | $\delta\, m\, a$ | N |
| $F_t$ | Total tractive force | $F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la} + F_{ra}$ | N |
| $P_t$ | Tractive power at wheels | $F_t \cdot v$ | W |
| $P_{req}$ | Power required from battery | $P_t / (\eta_{m}\, \eta_{t})$ | W |
| $E_{req}$ | Energy required per km | $P_{req} / v$ | Wh/km |
| $\eta_{mt}$ | Combined motor + transmission efficiency | typically 0.85 ŌĆō 0.92 | dimensionless |
| $\delta$ | Rotational inertia factor | $1 + \dfrac{I_w}{m r^2} + \dfrac{I_m G^2}{m r^2 \eta_g}$ | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Pitfall alert:** For numerical substitutions, always convert speed from **km/h to m/s** by multiplying by $5/18$. KTU examiners deduct marks if units are inconsistent.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Power Equation

- **Sizing the Traction Motor:** Peak $P_{req}$ dictates motor kW rating.
- **Battery Pack Sizing:** Integrating $P_{req}(t)$ over the drive cycle yields energy in Wh.
- **Range Estimation:** Energy per km $\times$ trip distance gives kWh requirement.
- **Thermal Management:** Average power dissipation is the input to the coolant loop design.
- **Regenerative Braking Limits:** Maximum regen power is bounded by the negative of the tractive power equation (only the non-gravitational terms are recoverable).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö From Newton's 2nd Law to Tractive Force

**Step 1.** Consider an EV of total mass $m$ moving along a road inclined at angle $\alpha$ with instantaneous velocity $v$ and linear acceleration $a$.

**Step 2.** Resolve all forces acting along the direction of motion. The driving force at the contact patch (tractive force) must balance every retarding force plus accelerate the mass:

$$
F_{t} \;=\; F_{r} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la} + F_{ra}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute each component from ┬¦2.1:

$$
F_{t} \;=\; \mu_{r}\, m\, g\, \cos(\alpha) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_{d}\, A_{f}\, v^{2} \;+\; m\, g\, \sin(\alpha) \;+\; m\, a\,(1 + \delta)
$$

**Step 4.** Apply the power-speed relation, $P = F \cdot v$, to obtain mechanical power at the wheels:

$$
P_{wheel} \;=\; F_{t} \cdot v
$$

**Step 5.** Introduce drivetrain losses via overall efficiency $\eta_{mt} = \eta_{m}\, \eta_{t}$ to back-calculate the **electrical power** the battery must deliver:

$$
P_{battery} \;=\; \frac{F_{t} \cdot v}{\eta_{mt}}
$$

**This is the canonical EV power equation used throughout KTU Module 2.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Energy Equation (per Unit Distance)

**Step 1.** Divide the power equation by speed $v$ to get force per unit time ├Ś time, i.e. **force ├Ś distance per unit time = power**, and then divide again by $v$ to get **energy per unit distance**:

$$
\frac{P_{battery}}{v} \;=\; \frac{F_{t}}{\eta_{mt}}
$$

**Step 2.** This gives the **specific energy consumption in Wh/km** (after multiplying by 1000/3600):

$$
E_{sp} \;=\; \frac{F_{t}}{3600 \cdot \eta_{mt}} \quad \text{[Wh/m]}
$$

or

$$
E_{sp}^{km} \;=\; \frac{1000 \cdot F_{t}}{3600 \cdot \eta_{mt}} \;=\; \frac{F_{t}}{3.6 \cdot \eta_{mt}} \quad \text{[Wh/km]}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

> **Problem Statement:**
> An electric car has the following parameters: $m = 1500$ kg, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m┬▓, $\mu_r = 0.015$, $\eta_{mt} = 0.90$, $\delta = 1.08$. The car is climbing a $10┬░$ grade at a constant speed of $60$ km/h with no acceleration. Find (a) the total tractive force, and (b) the power required from the battery. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m┬│, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓.

### Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert units:**

$$
v \;=\; 60 \times \frac{5}{18} \;=\; 16.67 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute each tractive force component:**

$$
F_{r} \;=\; 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos(10┬░) \;=\; 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.9848 \;\approx\; 217.4 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{ad} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^{2} \;\approx\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.8 \;\approx\; 112.3 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{hc} \;=\; 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(10┬░) \;=\; 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.1736 \;\approx\; 2554.4 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{la} + F_{ra} \;=\; m\, a\,(1 + \delta) \;=\; 0 \quad \text{(constant speed)}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sum tractive force:**

$$
F_{t} \;=\; 217.4 + 112.3 + 2554.4 \;=\; 2884.1 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Power at wheels:**

$$
P_{wheel} \;=\; F_t \cdot v \;=\; 2884.1 \times 16.67 \;\approx\; 48.07 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Power from battery (with drivetrain losses):**

$$
P_{battery} \;=\; \frac{48.07}{0.90} \;\approx\; 53.41 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answers:**

- **(a)** $F_t \approx 2884$ N
- **(b)** $P_{battery} \approx 53.4$ kW

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the EV Power Flow

The diagram below traces how the **Power Equation** sits inside the broader EV drivetrain, mapping the flow of energy from the battery to the contact patch, and identifying where each term in the equation originates.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    batt[Battery Pack] -->|P_battery| inv[Motor Inverter]
    inv -->|Mechanical power| mot[PMSM or IM Traction Motor]
    mot -->|P_shaft| trans[Single Speed Transmission]
    trans -->|P_wheel| diff[Differential and Half Shafts]
    diff -->|Tractive force F_t| wheel[Wheel Contact Patch]

    wheel -->|F_r| resA[Rolling Resistance]
    wheel -->|F_ad| resB[Aerodynamic Drag]
    wheel -->|F_hc| resC[Hill Climbing Resistance]
    wheel -->|F_la + F_ra| resD[Linear plus Rotational Inertia]

    resA -.feedback.-> eqn[Power Equation Solver]
    resB -.feedback.-> eqn
    resC -.feedback.-> eqn
    resD -.feedback.-> eqn

    eqn -->|P_required| ctrl[VCU Torque Demand]
    ctrl --> inv
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram (KTU-style):** The four resistance blocks (F_r, F_ad, F_hc, F_la+F_ra) are the *inputs* to the Power Equation. The equation's output (required power / torque) is fed back to the VCU, which commands the inverter. This closed loop is what the *Power Equation* ultimately controls.

## 4.2 Visual Decomposition of the Power Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ptotal["P_total = F_total times v divided by eta"]

    ptotal --> ft["F_t = Rolling plus Drag plus Grade plus Acceleration"]
    ptotal --> eta["eta_mt = eta_motor times eta_transmission"]

    ft --> fr["F_r = mu_r m g cos alpha"]
    ft --> fad["F_ad = half rho C_d A_f v squared"]
    ft --> fhc["F_hc = m g sin alpha"]
    ft --> fla["F_la = m a"]
    ft --> fra["F_ra = delta m a"]

    eta --> em["eta_m (motor efficiency) = 0.90 to 0.95"]
    eta --> et["eta_t (transmission efficiency) = 0.95 to 0.98"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`
**List the five components of total tractive force in an electric vehicle. State one physical factor that each component depends upon.**

**Model Answer:**

> The five components of the total tractive force are:
> 1. **Rolling resistance force** $F_r = \mu_r\, m\, g\, \cos(\alpha)$ ŌĆö depends on **tyre-road friction coefficient and vehicle mass**.
> 2. **Aerodynamic drag force** $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho\, C_d\, A_f\, v^2$ ŌĆö depends on **frontal area and square of velocity**.
> 3. **Hill-climbing force** $F_{hc} = m\, g\, \sin(\alpha)$ ŌĆö depends on **road gradient angle**.
> 4. **Linear acceleration force** $F_{la} = m\, a$ ŌĆö depends on **linear acceleration**.
> 5. **Rotational acceleration force** $F_{ra} = \delta\, m\, a$ ŌĆö depends on **inertia of rotating masses (wheels, motor rotor)**.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]`
**Why is the rotational inertia factor $\delta$ introduced in the power equation? What is its typical range for a passenger EV?**

**Model Answer:**

> The factor $\delta$ accounts for the **energy stored in rotating components** (wheels, transmission gears, motor rotor) when the vehicle accelerates. It converts their rotational inertia into an **equivalent linear mass** so that a single Newton's 2nd Law equation can be used.
> 
> $\delta = 1 + \dfrac{I_w}{m r^2} + \dfrac{I_m G^2}{m r^2 \eta_g}$
> 
> For a typical **passenger EV**, $\delta \approx 1.05$ to $1.10$.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the complete tractive force equation for an electric vehicle moving up an incline with acceleration. Clearly identify and justify each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric car of mass 1800 kg is travelling at 90 km/h on a level road. The coefficient of rolling resistance is 0.012, the drag coefficient is 0.28, and the frontal area is 2.4 m┬▓. The combined drivetrain efficiency is 0.88 and the rotational inertia factor $\delta = 1.10$. Compute the power required from the battery to maintain this constant speed. **(7 marks)**

---

### Ō£ģ Complete Model Solution ŌĆö Question A

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1.** Draw the free-body diagram of the EV on an inclined road. Identify retarding forces ($F_r$, $F_{ad}$, $F_{hc}$, $F_{la}$, $F_{ra}$) along the direction of motion.

**Step 2.** Apply Newton's 2nd Law along the direction of motion:

$$
\sum F_{motion} \;=\; m_{eff}\, a
$$

where $m_{eff} = m(1 + \delta)$.

**Step 3.** The motor's tractive force at the wheel must equal retarding forces plus accelerating force:

$$
F_{t} \;=\; \mu_{r}\, m\, g\, \cos(\alpha) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_{d}\, A_{f}\, v^{2} \;+\; m\, g\, \sin(\alpha) \;+\; m\, a\,(1 + \delta)
$$

**Step 4.** Multiply by velocity to get wheel power, then divide by drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{mt}$ to get the **battery-side power equation**:

$$
P_{battery} \;=\; \frac{v}{\eta_{mt}} \left[ \mu_{r}\, m\, g\, \cos(\alpha) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, C_{d}\, A_{f}\, v^{2} \;+\; m\, g\, \sin(\alpha) \;+\; m\, a\,(1 + \delta) \right]
$$

This is the **final derived power equation** for an accelerating EV on an inclined road.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $m = 1800$ kg, $v = 90$ km/h, $\mu_r = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A_f = 2.4$ m┬▓, $\eta_{mt} = 0.88$, $\delta = 1.10$, $\alpha = 0$ (level), $a = 0$ (constant speed), $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m┬│, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert speed:**

$$
v \;=\; 90 \times \frac{5}{18} \;=\; 25 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute individual force components:**

$$
F_{r} \;=\; 0.012 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times \cos(0┬░) \;=\; 0.012 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \;\approx\; 211.9 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{ad} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.4 \times (25)^{2} \;=\; 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.4 \times 625
$$

$$
F_{ad} \;\approx\; 257.25 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{hc} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{(level road)}
$$

$$
F_{la} + F_{ra} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{(constant speed)}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total tractive force:**

$$
F_{t} \;=\; 211.9 + 257.25 \;=\; 469.15 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Power at wheels:**

$$
P_{wheel} \;=\; F_t \cdot v \;=\; 469.15 \times 25 \;=\; 11{,}728.75 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 11.73 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Power from battery (with drivetrain efficiency):**

$$
P_{battery} \;=\; \frac{11.73}{0.88} \;\approx\; 13.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{battery} \approx 13.33$ kW

---

### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the flow of tractive power in an electric vehicle from the battery to the wheels. Show where the drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{mt}$ is applied. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric two-wheeler has the following specifications: $m = 200$ kg (including rider), $C_d = 0.65$, $A_f = 0.5$ m┬▓, $\mu_r = 0.018$, $\eta_{mt} = 0.85$, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m┬│. Calculate the power required from the battery when the vehicle ascends a 5┬░ gradient at a constant 40 km/h. **(7 marks)**

---

### Ō£ģ Complete Model Solution ŌĆö Question B

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)

**Block Flow:**

$$
\text{Battery} \;\xrightarrow{P_{bat}}\; \text{Inverter} \;\xrightarrow{P_{elec}}\; \text{Traction Motor} \;\xrightarrow{P_{mech} = \eta_m P_{elec}}\; \text{Transmission} \;\xrightarrow{P_{shaft}}\; \text{Wheels}
$$

The drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{mt} = \eta_{m}\, \eta_{t}$ is applied between the **battery terminal** and the **wheel contact patch**. Mathematically:

$$
P_{battery} \;=\; \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{mt}} \;=\; \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{mt}}
$$

The four opposing forces (rolling, aerodynamic, grade, acceleration) consume the tractive power at the wheels. The battery must supply the *gross* power ŌĆö wheel power plus all internal losses (motor copper + iron + friction losses, gear meshing losses).

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $m = 200$ kg, $C_d = 0.65$, $A_f = 0.5$ m┬▓, $\mu_r = 0.018$, $\eta_{mt} = 0.85$, $\alpha = 5┬░$, $v = 40$ km/h, $a = 0$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert speed:**

$$
v \;=\; 40 \times \frac{5}{18} \;\approx\; 11.11 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Force components:**

$$
F_{r} \;=\; 0.018 \times 200 \times 9.81 \times \cos(5┬░) \;\approx\; 0.018 \times 200 \times 9.81 \times 0.9962 \;\approx\; 35.18 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{ad} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.65 \times 0.5 \times (11.11)^{2} \;\approx\; 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.65 \times 0.5 \times 123.4 \;\approx\; 24.57 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{hc} \;=\; 200 \times 9.81 \times \sin(5┬░) \;\approx\; 200 \times 9.81 \times 0.0872 \;\approx\; 171.0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total tractive force:**

$$
F_{t} \;=\; 35.18 + 24.57 + 171.0 \;\approx\; 230.75 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Power at wheels:**

$$
P_{wheel} \;=\; 230.75 \times 11.11 \;\approx\; 2563.6 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 2.56 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Power from battery:**

$$
P_{battery} \;=\; \frac{2.56}{0.85} \;\approx\; 3.02 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_{battery} \approx 3.02$ kW

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error (1 mark):** Students forget to convert km/h to m/s. Always show the $5/18$ step explicitly.
> 2. **Efficiency placement (1 mark):** Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{mt}$ goes in the **denominator** of the battery-power equation, *not* the numerator. Reversing this yields an answer lower than the wheel power ŌĆö physically impossible.
> 3. **Missing $\delta$ term (┬Į mark):** When acceleration is present, the factor $(1 + \delta)$ multiplies $m \cdot a$. Writing only $m \cdot a$ is incomplete.
> 4. **Forgetting $\cos(\alpha)$ and $\sin(\alpha)$ on inclines (┬Į mark):** Even on small grades, both terms change. Take both into account.
> 5. **Rounding too early:** Carry at least 4 significant figures until the final step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Power Equation, Module 2**

- Ō£ģ The **Power Equation** is the cornerstone of EV design: $P = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{mt}}$.
- Ō£ģ **Five tractive force components:** Rolling, Aerodynamic, Hill-climb, Linear acceleration, Rotational acceleration.
- Ō£ģ **Aerodynamic drag** is the only term that grows with $v^2$ ŌĆö it dominates at high speed.
- Ō£ģ **Hill-climbing force** grows with $\sin(\alpha)$ ŌĆö it dominates on steep grades regardless of speed.
- Ō£ģ **Rolling resistance** is approximately constant (depends weakly on speed).
- Ō£ģ **Acceleration force** uses effective mass $m(1+\delta)$ ŌĆö never forget $\delta$ when $a \neq 0$.
- Ō£ģ **Drivetrain efficiency** $\eta_{mt} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}$ always sits in the denominator of the *battery-side* power equation.
- Ō£ģ **Always convert km/h ŌåÆ m/s** by multiplying by $5/18$ before substituting.
- Ō£ģ **Standard values to memorize:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m┬│, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓, $\mu_r \approx 0.01$ŌĆō$0.02$, $C_d \approx 0.25$ŌĆō$0.40$.
- Ō£ģ The Power Equation is used for **motor sizing, battery sizing, range estimation, and thermal design** ŌĆö every EV subsystem uses it.
- Ō£ģ The equation is **direction-agnostic**: in regenerative mode, only the non-gravitational terms ($F_r + F_{ad} + F_{la} + F_{ra}$) can be partially recovered; gravitational PE on downhills is partially recoverable.
- Ō£ģ The **rotational inertia factor** $\delta$ combines the effect of wheel inertia, gear ratios, and motor rotor inertia into a single dimensionless multiplier.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types, Torque and Speed Equations, Torque-Speed Characteristics, Speed Control of DC Motor, Pulse Width Modulation Control of DC Motor, Principles of Starting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types, Torque and Speed Equations, Torque-Speed Characteristics, Speed Control of DC Motor, Pulse Width Modulation Control of DC Motor, Principles of Starting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The DC Motor in the Electric Vehicle Context

A **Direct Current (DC) Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical DC input into mechanical rotational output by exploiting the interaction between a current-carrying armature conductor and the stationary magnetic field of the field winding (or permanent magnets). In modern electric vehicles (EVs), DC motors ŌĆö particularly **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC)** and **Separately Excited DC (SEDC)** motors ŌĆö were historically the workhorses of early traction drives. They remain pedagogically critical for the KTU PCMUT503 syllabus because the analytical foundations of *torque production, speed control, and pulse-width modulation (PWM) control* established here directly transfer to the study of brushless DC (BLDC), switched reluctance, and induction motor drives covered in higher semesters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCMUT503 ŌĆō Module 2):**
> DC motor drives form the foundational platform for understanding *any* variable-speed electric drive. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly retains DC motor analysis because its equations (T ŌłØ ╬”┬ĘIŌéÉ and N ŌłØ V/╬”) are mathematically transparent, allowing students to internalize the closed-loop *torque-speed* philosophy before tackling the more mathematically dense AC and BLDC drives.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Pedal Boat on a Lake

Imagine a small pedal boat powered by water jets:

- The **armature voltage (V)** is the *strength of your leg strokes*. Stroke harder, boat goes faster.
- The **field flux (╬”)** is the *size of the water jet nozzles*. Bigger nozzles push more water per stroke ŌåÆ more torque.
- The **armature current (IŌéÉ)** is the *flow rate of water* being pushed.
- The **mechanical load (torque T)** is the *weight of passengers* you must pull through the water.

Just as the boat's *speed* depends on stroke strength AND jet size, the motor's *speed* depends on supply voltage AND field flux. Just as the boat's *pulling force* depends on jet size AND water flow, the motor's *torque* depends on flux AND armature current. The DC motor's entire behaviour reduces to this elegant two-parameter relationship.

## 1.3 Classification of DC Motors (EV-Relevant Types)

DC motors are classified by the **excitation** (field winding connection) method:

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Canonical Types of DC Motors:**
> 1. **Separately Excited DC Motor (SEDC)** ŌĆö Field winding supplied from an *independent* source. Most common in EV traction (e.g., Toyota RAV4 EV, Mazda Roadster).
> 2. **Self-Excited DC Motors** (divided into three sub-types):
>    - **(a) Shunt DC Motor** ŌĆö Field winding connected in *parallel* with the armature.
>    - **(b) Series DC Motor** ŌĆö Field winding connected in *series* with the armature.
>    - **(c) Compound DC Motor** ŌĆö Both series and shunt field windings (cumulative or differential).
> 3. **Permanent Magnet DC Motor (PMDC)** ŌĆö Flux produced by *permanent magnets*; no field winding. Common in small EVs, e-bikes, golf carts.

Each type exhibits a **distinctive torque-speed signature** that determines its EV suitability.

## 1.4 Why DC Motors for EVs? A Candid Note

| Property | Significance in EV Application |
|---|---|
| **Excellent Speed Control** | Wide range via armature/field regulation |
| **High Starting Torque** | Essential for launching a heavy vehicle from standstill |
| **Linear, Predictable Characteristics** | Makes controller design tractable |
| **Simple Four-Quadrant Operation** | Forward motoring, reverse motoring, forward braking, reverse braking ŌĆö all via H-bridge |
| **Drawback** | Brush wear, commutator maintenance ŌåÆ gradually replaced by BLDC/PMSM in modern EVs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Voltage Equation of the DC Motor

Every DC motor obeys Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) on the armature loop:

$$V_t = E_b + I_a R_a$$

where **VŌé£** = terminal voltage applied to the armature, **E_b** = back-EMF (the internally generated opposing voltage), **IŌéÉ** = armature current, and **RŌéÉ** = armature winding resistance. At the *instant of starting*, E_b = 0, so the armature current surges to **IŌéÉ = VŌé£ / RŌéÉ** ŌĆö typically **6 to 10 times** the rated current. This is the electrical origin of the *starting problem* (covered in ┬¦2.7).

## 2.2 The Back-EMF Equation ŌĆö Origin of Speed Control

When the armature rotates at speed *N* (rpm) inside a flux *╬”* (Wb/pole), each conductor cuts flux and generates an EMF. For a wave-wound armature, the back-EMF is:

$$E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}$$

For a lap-wound armature, A = P, simplifying the expression. The constant **P** is the number of poles, **Z** is the total number of armature conductors, and **A** is the number of parallel paths.

## 2.3 The Torque Equation ŌĆö Origin of Electromechanical Energy Conversion

The electromagnetic torque developed in the armature is derived from the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field. The total torque developed is:

$$T = \frac{P \, \Phi \, I_a \, Z}{2 \pi \, A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Sacred Equation" of the DC Motor (KTU Board-Examiner's Favourite):**
> $$\boxed{T = k_t \, \Phi \, I_a \quad \text{where} \quad k_t = \frac{P \, Z}{2 \pi \, A}}$$
> The developed torque is **linearly proportional** to the *product* of the field flux and the armature current. This is the foundational equation for the entire EV drivetrain ŌĆö change ╬” or IŌéÉ, and you change the mechanical push on the wheels.

## 2.4 The Speed Equation ŌĆö N as a Function of V and ╬”

Combining the voltage and back-EMF equations gives the **Speed Equation**:

$$N = \frac{V_t - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi} = \frac{E_b}{k \, \Phi}$$

where **k = P┬ĘZ / (60┬ĘA)** is the speed constant. In SI units, k and k_t are numerically related by **k_t = 60k/(2ŽĆ) Ōēł 9.549 k**.

## 2.5 Torque-Speed Characteristics by Motor Type

The shape of the **TŌĆōN curve** defines the *personality* of each DC motor type and dictates its traction suitability.

### (a) Separately Excited DC Motor (SEDC)
- **Flux ╬” is independent of IŌéÉ** (field current is held constant).
- **T ŌłØ IŌéÉ** (linear, since ╬” is fixed).
- **Speed drops linearly with load** because N = (V - IŌéÉRŌéÉ) / k╬” and IŌéÉ rises with load.
- **Excellent speed regulation** ŌåÆ ideal for EV traction cruising.
- Torque-speed curve: a *straight line* with gentle negative slope, dropping from **NŌéĆ = V/(k╬”) at no-load** to lower speeds under load.

### (b) Series DC Motor
- **╬” is proportional to IŌéÉ** (until magnetic saturation).
- Therefore **T ŌłØ IŌéÉ┬▓** (quadratic torque characteristic).
- **Very high starting torque** ŌĆö the famous reason series motors are used in locomotives and classic EVs.
- **No-load run-away risk**: as IŌéÉ ŌåÆ 0, ╬” ŌåÆ 0, and N ŌåÆ Ōł×. *Never operate a series motor without mechanical load.*
- Speed equation becomes **N ŌłØ V / IŌéÉ** at the limit, hyperbolic decay.

### (c) Shunt DC Motor
- **Field flux ╬” is nearly constant** (field current I_sh = V/R_sh is roughly fixed).
- **T ŌłØ IŌéÉ** linear, like SEDC.
- **N is nearly constant** with load ŌĆö "constant-speed motor".
- Not preferred for EV traction due to limited starting torque.

### (d) Compound DC Motor
- Combines series and shunt field windings.
- **Cumulative compound**: high starting torque (series contribution) + good speed regulation (shunt contribution). Common in older EVs.
- **Differential compound**: rarely used (unstable characteristics).

### (e) Permanent Magnet DC Motor (PMDC)
- **╬” is fixed by magnet material** ŌĆö no field current, no I┬▓R loss in field.
- **T ŌłØ IŌéÉ** linear.
- **Compact, efficient, no field control possible** (╬” cannot be weakened by reducing field current; flux is constant).
- Used in e-bikes, small EVs, and auxiliary EV motors (pumps, fans).

## 2.6 Speed Control Methods ŌĆö The Three Pillars

### Method 1: Armature Voltage Control (Below Base Speed)
- Vary **VŌé£** while keeping ╬” constant.
- N ŌłØ VŌé£ ŌåÆ **straight-line speed control**.
- Used in EV acceleration from 0 to cruising speed.
- Implemented using a **DC-DC chopper (PWM converter)** in modern EVs.

### Method 2: Field Flux Control (Above Base Speed)
- Weaken ╬” by **reducing field current** (only for SEDC, shunt, and compound motors; not for PMDC).
- N ŌłØ 1/╬” ŌåÆ **inverse speed control**.
- Used for **field-weakening operation** in EVs to achieve highway cruising without exceeding the supply voltage.
- Limited by armature reaction and mechanical integrity at high speeds.

### Method 3: Armature Resistance Control
- Insert external resistance R_ext in series with the armature.
- **N = (V - IŌéÉ(RŌéÉ + R_ext)) / (k╬”)**.
- **Highly inefficient** ŌĆö I┬▓R losses in the resistor. Obsolete in modern EV drives, but historically used.

## 2.7 Principles of DC Motor Starting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Starting Problem:**
> At start-up, the rotor is stationary ŌåÆ back-EMF E_b = 0. The armature current is therefore **I_start = VŌé£ / RŌéÉ**, which can reach **5 to 10 times** the rated current. This produces:
> 1. **Dangerous mechanical shock** (high starting torque).
> 2. **I┬▓R heating** in the armature windings ŌĆö possible insulation failure.
> 3. **Voltage dip** in the EV battery pack.

### Starting Methods
- **Conventional Resistance Starter**: Series resistance is reduced in steps (manual or automatic contactors) as the motor accelerates and E_b builds up. Used in old EV designs and educational labs.
- **Soft-Start via PWM Chopper**: A semiconductor switch (IGBT/MOSFET) gradually increases the duty cycle D from 0 to 1, effectively ramping up V_avg. **This is the modern EV standard.**
- **DC-DC Converter Pre-charge**: A pre-charge resistor is engaged before closing the main contactor, limiting the inrush to the DC-link capacitor.

## 2.8 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) Control of DC Motor

PWM is the cornerstone of modern EV motor drives. A high-frequency switching signal (typically **5 kHz to 20 kHz** for traction motors, beyond the audible range) modulates the average DC voltage applied to the armature.

The **average output voltage** of a PWM chopper is:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \cdot V_s$$

where **D** = duty ratio (0 Ōēż D Ōēż 1), **V_s** = DC supply voltage, **T_on** = switch ON time, **T_off** = switch OFF time, and **T = T_on + T_off** = switching period.

The switching frequency **f_sw = 1/T**. The effective speed becomes:

$$N = \frac{D \cdot V_s - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PWM for EVs?**
> - **Linear control** of average voltage without dissipative resistors.
> - **High efficiency** (typically > 95%, only switching losses).
> - **Fast dynamic response** ŌåÆ crisp accelerator pedal feel.
> - **Easy four-quadrant operation** by combining with an H-bridge.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALL THE EQUATIONS YOU WILL EVER NEED FOR MODULE 2 ŌĆö TABLE**

| **Equation** | **Expression** | **Significance** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage Equation | $V_t = E_b + I_a R_a$ | KVL on armature circuit | V |
| Back-EMF | $E_b = k \Phi N$ | EMF generated by rotation | V |
| Expanded Back-EMF | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$ | Pole-/conductor-level form | V |
| Torque Equation | $T = k_t \Phi I_a$ | Electromechanical energy conversion | N┬Ęm |
| Expanded Torque | $T = \dfrac{P \Phi I_a Z}{2 \pi A}$ | Pole-/conductor-level form | N┬Ęm |
| Speed Equation | $N = \dfrac{V_t - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ | Steady-state speed | rpm |
| No-Load Speed | $N_0 = \dfrac{V_t}{k \Phi}$ | Speed at T = 0 | rpm |
| Mechanical Power | $P_{mech} = T \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Output shaft power | W |
| Series Motor ╬” | $\Phi \propto I_a$ | Until saturation | Wb |
| Series Motor T | $T \propto I_a^2$ | Quadratic torque | N┬Ęm |
| PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = D V_s$ | Chopper output | V |
| Duty Ratio | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | PWM control variable | dimensionless |
| Switching Frequency | $f_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | PWM switching rate | Hz |
| Starting Current | $I_{start} = \dfrac{V_t}{R_a}$ | At standstill, E_b = 0 | A |
| Speed-Constant Relation | $k_t = \dfrac{60}{2 \pi} k$ | SI-unit conversion | N┬Ęm/A = V┬Ęs/rad |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Torque Equation

**Starting principle:** When a conductor of length *l* carries a current *I* perpendicular to a magnetic flux density *B*, it experiences a mechanical force **F = B I l** (the Lorentz force law applied to a macroscopic conductor).

### Step 1 ŌĆö Force per Conductor
$$\text{Force per conductor: } f = B \cdot I \cdot l \quad \text{[Newtons]}$$

where *B* is the flux density at the conductor (in Tesla), *I* is the current in the conductor (in Amperes), and *l* is the active length of the conductor in the magnetic field (in metres).

### Step 2 ŌĆö Convert Flux Density B to Flux ╬”
The flux per pole is related to flux density by:
$$\Phi = B \times A_c$$
where *A_c* is the cross-sectional area of the pole face (m┬▓). Therefore:
$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_c}$$
Substituting:
$$f = \frac{\Phi}{A_c} \cdot I \cdot l$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Torque per Conductor
Torque = force ├Ś perpendicular distance from axis. If *r* is the mean radius of the armature (in metres):
$$\text{Torque per conductor} = f \times r = \frac{\Phi \cdot I \cdot l \cdot r}{A_c}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Total Torque from All Z Conductors
With *Z* total conductors, the total torque (taking into account that the conductors are distributed around the armature) is:
$$T = \frac{\Phi \cdot I \cdot l \cdot r \cdot Z}{2 \pi A_c \cdot A} = \frac{\Phi \cdot I_a \cdot Z \cdot (lr)}{2 \pi A_c \cdot A}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Recognize that l┬Ęr = Pole Face Area
For a cylindrical armature, the active surface of each pole face is approximately $A_c = l \cdot r_p$, where $r_p$ is the pole-arc radius. Combining geometrically:
$$T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \cdot Z}{2 \pi A}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{T = k_t \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a \quad \text{with} \quad k_t = \frac{P \, Z}{2 \pi A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of k_t:** The constant k_t depends only on the *machine geometry* (number of poles P, total armature conductors Z, and parallel paths A). It does not depend on the operating point. It is called the *torque constant* or *machine constant*.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Back-EMF Equation

### Step 1 ŌĆö EMF per Conductor
Each conductor cuts flux at a rate. The average rate of flux cutting per pole per revolution is *╬”*. The time for one revolution is $t = \frac{60}{N}$ seconds.

### Step 2 ŌĆö EMF per Conductor per Revolution
By Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced per conductor per revolution:
$$e_{conductor} = \frac{\Phi}{t} = \frac{\Phi \, N}{60} \quad \text{[Volts per conductor]}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö EMF per Parallel Path
Conductors are connected in series per parallel path. With *Z/A* conductors in series per path, the total EMF per path is:
$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \times \frac{\Phi \, N}{60} = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Account for Number of Poles
For a multi-pole machine, the flux per revolution effectively scales by *P* (the number of poles):
$$E_b = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{E_b = k \, \Phi \, N \quad \text{with} \quad k = \frac{P \, Z}{60 \, A}}$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation of the Speed Equation

### Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the Voltage Equation
$$V_t = E_b + I_a R_a$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute E_b from ┬¦3.2
$$V_t = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} + I_a R_a$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Isolate N
$$\frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A} = V_t - I_a R_a$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for N
$$N = \frac{(V_t - I_a R_a) \cdot 60 \, A}{P \, \Phi \, Z}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{N = \frac{V_t - I_a R_a}{k \, \Phi} = \frac{E_b}{k \, \Phi}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The speed equation is the *operational core* of every speed control method. By modulating $V_t$ (armature voltage control) or $\Phi$ (field flux control), the steady-state speed can be regulated across a wide range.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Speed Control Method Comparison

| **Control Method** | **Variable Changed** | **Speed Range** | **Efficiency** | **EV Application** | **Limitation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Armature Voltage Control (Chopper) | $V_t$ via PWM duty D | 0 to base speed | High (>90%) | Acceleration from standstill | Requires chopper hardware |
| Field Flux Control (Field Weakening) | $\Phi$ via $I_f$ | Base speed to 2ŌĆō3├Ś base | High | Highway cruising | Armature reaction; no use for PMDC |
| Armature Resistance Control | $R_{ext}$ in series | Below base speed | Low (I┬▓R losses) | Obsolete; educational demos | Energy wasted as heat |
| Combined V + ╬” Control | Both $V_t$ and $\Phi$ | 0 to max speed | High | Full EV drive cycle | Complex controller |

---

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Numerical ŌĆö KTU Board Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (typical of KTU End-Semester Examinations):**
> A 220 V, 100 A, 1000 rpm DC **shunt** motor has $R_a = 0.1 \Omega$ and $R_{sh} = 110 \Omega$. Calculate:
> 1. The no-load speed (assuming rotational losses = 1% of rated power)
> 2. The full-load torque (mechanical output)
> 3. The new speed if the field flux is weakened by 10% at constant terminal voltage

**Solution:**

### Part 1 ŌĆö No-Load Speed
Field current:
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$

Armature current at no-load (small, just to overcome rotational losses):
$$I_{a0} = I_L - I_{sh} = I_{rated} - I_{sh} \approx 100 - 2 = 98 \text{ A (full load reference)}$$

Back-EMF at full load:
$$E_{b,FL} = V_t - I_a R_a = 220 - (100)(0.1) = 210 \text{ V}$$

Using $E_b = k \Phi N$ at full load, $k\Phi = E_b / N = 210 / 1000 = 0.21$ V/rpm.

At no-load, $I_{a0} \approx 2$ A (just to supply rotational losses):
$$E_{b,NL} = V_t - I_{a0} R_a \approx 220 - (2)(0.1) = 219.8 \text{ V}$$

No-load speed:
$$N_0 = \frac{E_{b,NL}}{k\Phi} = \frac{219.8}{0.21} \approx 1046.7 \text{ rpm}$$

### Part 2 ŌĆö Full-Load Torque
Mechanical power output (assuming full-load armature current and ignoring rotational losses for simplicity):
$$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = 210 \times 100 = 21{,}000 \text{ W}$$

Full-load torque:
$$T_{FL} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega} = \frac{P_{mech}}{2\pi N/60} = \frac{21000 \times 60}{2\pi \times 1000} = \frac{1{,}260{,}000}{6283.2}$$

$$T_{FL} \approx 200.5 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

### Part 3 ŌĆö Field-Weakened Speed (10% flux reduction)
New flux: $\Phi_{new} = 0.9 \Phi_{old}$, so $k\Phi_{new} = 0.9 \times 0.21 = 0.189$ V/rpm.
At the same armature current (steady-state assumption):
$$E_{b,new} = V_t - I_a R_a = 210 \text{ V (unchanged)}$$

New speed:
$$N_{new} = \frac{E_{b,new}}{k\Phi_{new}} = \frac{210}{0.189} \approx 1111.1 \text{ rpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result interpretation:** A 10% flux weakening yields a speed increase from 1000 rpm to ~1111 rpm ŌĆö a ~11% speed-up, illustrating the **inverse relationship** between flux and speed.

---

## 3.6 Detailed Derivation of PWM Average Voltage

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Switching Waveform
A PWM signal of period *T* has a high (ON) duration $T_{on}$ and a low (OFF) duration $T_{off}$ such that $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Average Over One Period
The average value of a periodic voltage waveform is the integral over one period divided by the period:
$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v(t) \, dt$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the PWM Waveform
For an ideal PWM signal that is at $V_s$ during $T_{on}$ and at 0 during $T_{off}$:
$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} + 0 \cdot T_{off} \right] = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{T}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Introduce Duty Ratio
By definition, the duty ratio is $D = T_{on} / T$, hence:
$$\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s}$$

**Final Result:** The average voltage across the motor terminals is linearly proportional to the duty ratio. Varying D from 0 to 1 linearly varies $V_{avg}$ from 0 to $V_s$ ŌĆö providing *continuous, lossless speed control*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Hook:** In problems, the duty ratio is *not* always given. It must be computed as $D = T_{on} / (T_{on} + T_{off})$ or from the average voltage specification. Always verify the *switching frequency* to confirm operation above the audible range (> 20 kHz) for EV applications.

---

## 3.7 Derivation of the Starting Current Limitation

### Step 1 ŌĆö Initial Conditions at Standstill
At $t = 0^+$, the rotor is stationary: $\omega = 0 \Rightarrow N = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = k \Phi N = 0$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Voltage Equation
$$V_t = 0 + I_{a,start} R_a$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for Starting Current
$$I_{a,start} = \frac{V_t}{R_a}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compare to Rated Current
For a typical 220 V, $R_a = 0.1 \Omega$ motor:
$$I_{a,start} = \frac{220}{0.1} = 2200 \text{ A}$$
This is **22 times** the rated 100 A current.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Energy Stored in Rotor Inertia
As the motor accelerates, $E_b$ grows linearly with $N$, and the current decays exponentially:
$$I_a(t) = \frac{V_t - k \Phi N(t)}{R_a}$$

**Conclusion:** A starter must either (a) limit the *initial* voltage to a safe level (PWM soft-start) or (b) add external resistance to limit the inrush current (conventional starters). Modern EV drives use **PWM ramp-up of the duty ratio D** from 0 to its final value over 0.1ŌĆō0.5 seconds.
