# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Q: State and explain the torque equation of a DC motor. Why is the torque constant independent of the operating point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The electromagnetic torque developed in a DC motor armature is given by:
$$T = k_t \, \Phi \, I_a$$
where $k_t = PZ / (2\pi A)$ is the *torque constant*, $\Phi$ is the flux per pole in webers, and $I_a$ is the armature current in amperes. The constant $k_t$ depends only on the *machine geometry* ŌĆö specifically, the number of poles P, the total number of armature conductors Z, and the number of parallel paths A. Because the geometry does not change during operation, $k_t$ is independent of speed, current, flux, or load. This makes the torque equation *linear* and *deterministic* ŌĆö a property that simplifies EV controller design considerably.

**Mark Distribution:** Equation statement (1 mark) + definition of $k_t$ and its geometric origin (1 mark) + physical interpretation (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō December 2023]**
**Q: A 240 V, 50 A DC series motor has an armature resistance of 0.3 ╬® and a field winding resistance of 0.2 ╬®. Calculate (i) the starting current, and (ii) the back-EMF at a speed where armature current is 40 A.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**(i) Starting Current** ŌĆö at start, $E_b = 0$, so:
$$I_{start} = \frac{V_t}{R_a + R_{se}} = \frac{240}{0.3 + 0.2} = \frac{240}{0.5} = 480 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Back-EMF at $I_a = 40$ A**:
$$E_b = V_t - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 240 - 40(0.5) = 240 - 20 = 220 \text{ V}$$

**Mark Distribution:** Correct formula for starting current with substituted values (1 mark) + numerical result (0.5 mark) + back-EMF equation (1 mark) + final result (0.5 mark).

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern (ESE):** Part B questions of Module 2 carry **14 marks** with internal choice (Question A **OR** Question B). Each question has two sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks. Solutions must be **step-by-step** to score full marks.

---

### Question A **[14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Style]**

**(a) Derive the EMF and torque equations of a DC motor starting from the Lorentz force law and Faraday's law. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 ŌĆö Force on a current-carrying conductor (Lorentz force):* A conductor of length *l* carrying current *I* in a magnetic field of flux density *B* experiences a force $f = BIl$ Newtons. (0.5 marks)

*Step 2 ŌĆö Converting B to flux:* Since $\Phi = B \cdot A_c$ where $A_c$ is the pole-face area, $B = \Phi / A_c$. Substituting: $f = \Phi I l / A_c$. (0.5 marks)

*Step 3 ŌĆö Torque per conductor:* Torque = force ├Ś radius: $T_{conductor} = f \cdot r = \Phi I l r / A_c$. (0.5 marks)

*Step 4 ŌĆö Total torque from Z conductors in A parallel paths:* Each path has $Z/A$ conductors, and the conductors are distributed around the circumference. Summing: $T = P \Phi I_a Z / (2\pi A)$. (2 marks)

*Step 5 ŌĆö EMF derivation (Faraday's Law):* Each conductor cuts flux $\Phi$ per revolution. Time for one revolution = $60/N$ seconds. EMF per conductor per revolution: $e = \Phi N / 60$ V. With $Z/A$ conductors in series per path: $E_b = P \Phi Z N / (60 A)$. (2.5 marks)

*Step 6 ŌĆö Final boxed forms:*
$$T = k_t \Phi I_a \quad ; \quad E_b = k \Phi N$$
where $k_t = PZ/(2\pi A)$ and $k = PZ/(60A)$. (1 mark)

---

**(b) A 220 V DC shunt motor takes a no-load current of 5 A and runs at 1000 rpm. The armature resistance is 0.2 ╬®, the shunt field resistance is 110 ╬®, and the full-load armature current is 50 A. Calculate: (i) the back-EMF constant k╬”, (ii) the full-load speed, and (iii) the full-load torque. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Back-EMF constant k╬”:**
At no-load, $I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh}$.
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V_t}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$
$$I_{a0} = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ A}$$
$$E_{b,0} = V_t - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (3)(0.2) = 220 - 0.6 = 219.4 \text{ V}$$
$$k\Phi = \frac{E_{b,0}}{N_0} = \frac{219.4}{1000} = 0.2194 \text{ V/rpm}$$

**(1.5 marks)** ŌĆö Field current computation (0.5) + back-EMF (0.5) + k╬” (0.5).

**(ii) Full-load speed:**
$$E_{b,FL} = V_t - I_{a,FL} R_a = 220 - (50)(0.2) = 220 - 10 = 210 \text{ V}$$
$$N_{FL} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{k\Phi} = \frac{210}{0.2194} \approx 957.2 \text{ rpm}$$

**(2.5 marks)** ŌĆö Back-EMF at full load (1) + substitution and result (1.5).

**(iii) Full-load torque:**
$$P_{mech} = E_{b,FL} \cdot I_{a,FL} = 210 \times 50 = 10{,}500 \text{ W}$$
$$\omega_{FL} = \frac{2\pi N_{FL}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 957.2}{60} \approx 100.21 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$T_{FL} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega_{FL}} = \frac{10500}{100.21} \approx 104.78 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

**(3 marks)** ŌĆö Mechanical power (1) + angular speed (1) + torque (1).

---

### Question B **[14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice]** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō December 2023 Style]**

**(a) Explain the armature voltage control and field flux control methods of speed control of a separately excited DC motor. Sketch the torque-speed characteristics for both methods. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Armature Voltage Control:* The terminal voltage $V_t$ applied to the armature is varied while keeping the field flux $\Phi$ constant. (0.5 marks)

From the speed equation $N = (V_t - I_a R_a)/(k\Phi)$, for a given load (fixed $I_a$), $N$ varies linearly with $V_t$. (1 mark)

The no-load speed $N_0 = V_t / (k\Phi)$ shifts up/down with $V_t$ while the slope of the TŌĆōN line (which equals $R_a / (k\Phi)^2$) remains constant. (1 mark)

This gives a *family of parallel lines* in the TŌĆōN plane ŌĆö see below. (1 mark)

*Field Flux Control:* The terminal voltage $V_t$ is held constant, and the field flux $\Phi$ is varied (typically weakened) by reducing the field current $I_f$. (0.5 marks)

For a given $V_t$ and $I_a$, $N = (V_t - I_a R_a) / (k\Phi) \propto 1/\Phi$. (1 mark)

The no-load speed $N_0 = V_t / (k\Phi)$ rises sharply as $\Phi$ decreases, and the TŌĆōN lines *fan out* from a common no-load point. (1 mark)

*Torque-Speed Characteristics (textual representation):*
- **Armature Voltage Control:** A set of straight lines with *the same slope* (constant $R_a / (k\Phi)^2$) but *different y-intercepts* $N_0$ on the speed axis. (0.5 marks)
- **Field Flux Control:** A set of straight lines with *steeper slopes* as $\Phi$ decreases, all originating from a common operating point at rated voltage. (0.5 marks)

*Practical Limitations:* Armature voltage control requires a variable DC supply (chopper); field control is limited by armature reaction and saturation. (0.5 marks)

**Mark Distribution:** Method explanations (4 marks) + speed equation application (2 marks) + characteristic description (1 mark).

---

**(b) With a neat sketch, explain the principle of Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) control of a DC motor. A chopper-fed DC motor receives a PWM signal of frequency 1 kHz and duty cycle 0.6. The supply voltage is 200 V. Calculate the average voltage applied to the motor. If the motor has $R_a = 0.5 \Omega$ and $k\Phi = 0.5$ V/rpm at no-load, find the no-load speed. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Principle of PWM Control:* A chopper is a power-electronic switch (IGBT or MOSFET) that connects and disconnects the DC supply to the motor at a high switching frequency. (0.5 marks)

During $T_{on}$, the switch is closed and the motor receives the full supply voltage $V_s$. (0.5 marks)

During $T_{off}$, the switch is open and the motor terminals are at 0 V (freewheeling through a diode). (0.5 marks)

The average voltage across the motor is:
$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \cdot V_s$$ (1 mark)

By varying the duty cycle D from 0 to 1, the average voltage is controlled *linearly and losslessly*. (0.5 marks)

*Calculation 1 ŌĆö Average Voltage:*
$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s = 0.6 \times 200 = 120 \text{ V}$$ (1 mark)

*Calculation 2 ŌĆö No-Load Speed:*
At no-load, $I_{a0} \approx 0$, so $E_b = V_{avg} = 120$ V. (0.5 marks)
$$N_0 = \frac{E_b}{k\Phi} = \frac{120}{0.5} = 240 \text{ rpm}$$ (1 mark)

*Freewheeling Diode Action:* When the chopper is OFF, the motor's armature inductance forces the current to continue flowing; a freewheeling diode across the armature provides the path and prevents voltage spikes. (0.5 marks)

*Switching Frequency Justification:* For EV applications, $f_{sw}$ is typically 5ŌĆō20 kHz to keep switching losses low and stay above the audible range. (0.5 marks)

**Mark Distribution:** Principle + sketch description (3 marks) + average voltage formula (1 mark) + numerical average voltage (1 mark) + no-load speed derivation (1.5 marks) + freewheeling/frequency commentary (0.5 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks on Module 2:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** The back-EMF constant $k$ has units of V/(Wb┬Ęrpm) or V┬Ęs/rad. Mixing units (e.g., $k\Phi$ in V/(Wb┬Ęrpm) but plugging speed in rad/s) causes silent errors. **Always state units explicitly.**
> 2. **Field Current in Shunt Motors:** Many students forget to subtract $I_{sh} = V_t/R_{sh}$ from the *line current* before computing armature quantities. This is a 1ŌĆō2 mark loss in every numerical.
> 3. **Confusing k and kŌé£:** The two constants are *not numerically equal*; $k_t = 60k/(2\pi)$. Mixing them up gives wrong speed *or* wrong torque results.
> 4. **PWM vs. PAM:** Students often confuse Pulse-Width Modulation with Pulse-Amplitude Modulation. KTU expects **fixed amplitude, variable duty cycle**.
> 5. **Series Motor "No-Load" Assumption:** Never assume a series motor runs at no-load in calculations ŌĆö the speed tends to infinity. Always assume a *minimum safe load*.
> 6. **Starting Current without Series Resistance:** Omitting the series field resistance $R_{se}$ when computing $I_{start}$ for series motors. The total resistance is $R_a + R_{se}$.
> 7. **Sketching TŌĆōN Curves:** The KTU evaluator expects *clear axes, labels, slope identification, and identification of NŌéĆ and T_stall*. Poorly labelled sketches lose 1ŌĆō2 marks even if the text is correct.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2):**

- **DC Motor Types** ŌĆö Five canonical types: *Separately Excited (SEDC), Shunt, Series, Compound, and PMDC*. SEDC and PMDC are the EV-relevant types.
- **Voltage Equation** ŌĆö $V_t = E_b + I_a R_a$. At standstill, $E_b = 0$ and the current surges.
- **Back-EMF Equation** ŌĆö $E_b = P \Phi Z N / (60 A) = k \Phi N$. Linear in speed.
- **Torque Equation** ŌĆö $T = P \Phi I_a Z / (2\pi A) = k_t \Phi I_a$. Linear in flux and current.
- **Speed Equation** ŌĆö $N = (V_t - I_a R_a) / (k \Phi) = E_b / (k \Phi)$.
- **Constant Relation** ŌĆö $k_t = 60 k / (2\pi) \approx 9.549 \, k$.
- **TŌĆōN Character Signatures:**
  - SEDC: linear TŌĆōN with gentle slope.
  - Shunt: nearly constant N (good regulation).
  - Series: T ŌłØ IŌéÉ┬▓, N ŌłØ 1/IŌéÉ (high starting torque, run-away risk).
  - PMDC: linear, no field control.
- **Speed Control Methods** ŌĆö (1) Armature voltage control [below base speed], (2) Field flux control [above base speed, field weakening], (3) Armature resistance control [obsolete].
- **PWM Control** ŌĆö $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$, where $D = T_{on} / T$. Used in DC-DC choppers to vary $V_{avg}$ losslessly.
- **Starting Problem** ŌĆö $I_{start} = V_t / R_a$ can be 5ŌĆō10├Ś rated. Solutions: conventional resistance starter (old) or PWM soft-start (modern EV standard).
- **EV Relevance** ŌĆö DC motors are foundational for understanding BLDC and PMSM drives. The TŌĆōN and PWM concepts directly transfer.
- **Key SI Conversion** ŌĆö 1 rpm = $2\pi / 60$ rad/s Ōēł 0.1047 rad/s. Power $P = T\omega$ requires $\omega$ in rad/s.
- **Common Pitfalls** ŌĆö unit mixing, forgetting $I_{sh}$ in shunt motors, no-load assumption for series motors, $k$ vs $k_t$ confusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Losses and Efficiency, Load Test, Regenerative Braking in DC Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Losses and Efficiency, Load Test, Regenerative Braking in DC Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Losses in DC Motors

### Formal Definition
In a DC motor, the input electrical power drawn from the supply is not fully converted into useful mechanical power at the shaft. A portion of this input power is dissipated as **losses** in various forms due to fundamental physical phenomena like resistance heating, magnetic hysteresis, eddy currents, and friction. The difference between input power and total losses gives the useful output mechanical power.

### Classification of Losses

The total losses in a DC motor are classified into four major categories:

**(a) Copper Losses (I┬▓R Losses / Electrical Losses)**
- Armature copper loss: $P_{ca} = I_a^2 R_a$
- Field copper loss: $P_{cf} = V I_{sh}$ (shunt motor) or $I_a^2 R_{se}$ (series motor)
- Brush contact loss: $P_{brush} = V_{brush} \cdot I_a$

**(b) Iron Losses / Core Losses (in the armature core)**
- Hysteresis loss: $P_h = \eta B_{max}^{1.6} f V$
- Eddy current loss: $P_e = k_e B_{max}^2 f^2 t^2 V$

**(c) Mechanical Losses**
- Friction loss (bearing friction, brush friction)
- Windage loss (air friction on rotating parts)

**(d) Stray Load Losses**
- Additional losses due to distortion of main field flux by armature reaction; typically taken as **1% of output** for DC machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: For a DC motor, copper losses are *variable* (depend on load current), while iron losses, mechanical losses, and shunt field copper loss (for shunt motor) are *constant* (independent of load).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a water pump that takes in 100 liters of water per minute (input). Out of this, it leaks 5 liters through pipe joints (copper losses from resistance), 3 liters evaporate from a hot motor housing (iron losses from magnetic heating), 2 liters spill from a slightly misaligned shaft seal (mechanical losses from friction), and delivers only 90 liters as useful output. **Efficiency** is the ratio of useful output (90 L) to input (100 L) ŌĆö in this case, **90%**. A well-designed electric vehicle motor targets efficiencies above **90%** to maximize driving range.

---

## 1.2 Efficiency of DC Motors

### Formal Definition
**Efficiency** ($\eta$) is defined as the ratio of useful mechanical output power delivered at the shaft to the total electrical input power drawn from the supply, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{P_{in} - P_{losses}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of efficiency as the "fuel economy" of a motor ŌĆö just like a car that consumes 5 liters of petrol to travel 100 km is more efficient than one consuming 8 liters for the same distance, a motor with fewer internal losses converts more electricity into motion. For electric vehicles, every percentage point of efficiency gain directly translates to **more kilometers of driving range**.

---

## 1.3 Load Test on DC Motors

### Formal Definition
The **load test** (also called the **direct brake test** or **rope-brake test**) is a direct method of determining the efficiency of a DC motor by applying a known mechanical load to the shaft and measuring the input electrical power and output mechanical power simultaneously under actual loading conditions.

### Intuitive Analogy
A load test is like putting an athlete on a treadmill with adjustable resistance ŌĆö by measuring the power input (calories consumed) and the output (work done against the belt), you can directly calculate how efficient the athlete is. Similarly, in a load test we strap a brake (rope, water brake, or dynamometer) onto the motor shaft and measure how much mechanical work it delivers per unit of electrical energy consumed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The load test is the most direct and accurate method to determine efficiency because it measures the actual output, unlike Swinburne's test which is an indirect (no-load) test. However, the load test is suitable only for **small motors** as disposing of the large heat generated is impractical for big machines.

---

## 1.4 Regenerative Braking in DC Motors

### Formal Definition
**Regenerative braking** is a method of braking in which the kinetic energy of the rotating armature is *reconverted back into electrical energy* and returned to the supply source, rather than being dissipated as heat. The motor effectively acts as a *generator* during this process because the back EMF exceeds the supply voltage, causing the current to reverse direction.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine coasting downhill on a bicycle. Instead of squeezing the brake pads (which converts motion to heat and wastes it), regenerative braking is like having a small generator in the wheel hub that converts the downhill motion *back into electrical energy* stored in a battery. The motor becomes a "generator on demand." In electric vehicles like the **Tata Nexon EV** or **Tesla Model 3**, this recovered energy can extend range by **10-20%** in city driving.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Condition for Regeneration**: For regenerative braking to occur, the **back EMF ($E_b$) must exceed the supply voltage ($V$)**, so that current naturally flows from the machine back to the source. This requires the motor to be running *above its no-load speed* (for shunt motors) or requires *special arrangements* (for series motors).

### Types of DC Motors and Their Regenerative Braking Capability

| Motor Type | Regenerative Braking Feasibility | Key Requirement |
|:-----------|:---------------------------------|:----------------|
| Shunt Motor | **Easy and natural** | Operate above rated speed (weaken field or overspeed) |
| Separately Excited | **Easy and natural** | Flux held constant; speed must increase above rated |
| Series Motor | **Difficult, needs special arrangement** | Cannot regenerate on DC supply directly due to load-dependent flux; needs parallel field excitation or supply change |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EV Application Note**: In modern electric vehicles, **separately excited DC motors** and **brushless DC (BLDC) motors** (which essentially operate on the same principle) are commonly used. Regenerative braking in EVs is one of the most important energy-recovery strategies and is fundamental to extending vehicle range.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Power Flow in a DC Motor (Stage-by-Stage Conversion)

The input electrical power goes through several stages of conversion, with losses at each stage:

**Stage 1**: Input electrical power from supply
$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$$

**Stage 2**: After subtracting **shunt field copper loss** (the supply current splits ŌĆö part goes to armature, part to field)
$$P_{in} - P_{sh} = V \cdot I_a \quad \text{(power entering the armature)}$$

**Stage 3**: After subtracting **armature copper loss** ($I_a^2 R_a$), we get the **mechanical power developed** ($P_m$)
$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = V I_a - I_a^2 R_a$$

**Stage 4**: After subtracting **iron losses + mechanical losses (friction & windage)**, we get the **shaft output power** ($P_{sh,out}$)
$$P_{out} = P_m - P_{iron} - P_{mech} - P_{stray}$$

### Why Each Loss Occurs (Engineering Insight)

- **Armature copper loss ($I_a^2 R_a$)**: The armature winding has finite resistance; current flowing through it generates heat by Joule's law. In EVs, this loss scales with the **square of the torque demand**.
- **Field copper loss ($VI_{sh}$ for shunt)**: Energy is needed to maintain the magnetic field. In EVs, this is a constant parasitic load that the battery must continuously supply.
- **Iron losses (hysteresis + eddy current)**: As the armature core rotates in the magnetic field, the alternating flux in the iron causes energy dissipation. **Hysteresis loss** arises from the work needed to reorient magnetic domains, while **eddy current loss** is from circulating currents induced in the core.
- **Mechanical losses (friction + windage)**: Bearing friction, brush-on-commutator friction, and air resistance on rotating surfaces. In EVs, windage increases with the **cube of speed**.
- **Stray load losses**: Caused by flux distortion due to armature reaction; these are difficult to calculate and are conventionally taken as 1% of output.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Core DC Motor Equations

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:---------------|
| 1 | Supply equation | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | $V$ = supply voltage, $E_b$ = back EMF, $I_a$ = armature current, $R_a$ = armature resistance |
| 2 | Back EMF | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | $\Phi$ = flux/pole, $Z$ = conductors, $N$ = speed (rpm), $P$ = poles, $A$ = parallel paths |
| 3 | Simplified back EMF | $E_b = K \Phi N$ | $K$ = machine constant, $\Phi$ = flux, $N$ = speed |
| 4 | Armature torque | $T_a = K_t \Phi I_a$ | $T_a$ in N┬Ęm, $K_t$ = torque constant |
| 5 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega$ | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ rad/s |
| 6 | Armature copper loss | $P_{ca} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Variable with load |
| 7 | Shunt field copper loss | $P_{sh} = V I_{sh} = V^2 / R_{sh}$ | Constant for shunt motor |
| 8 | Series field copper loss | $P_{se} = I_a^2 R_{se}$ | Variable for series motor |
| 9 | Total input power | $P_{in} = V I_L$ | $I_L$ = line current |
| 10 | Shaft output | $P_{out} = P_m - (P_{iron} + P_{mech} + P_{stray})$ | Useful mechanical power |
| 11 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Always less than 100% |
| 12 | Constant losses | $W_c = P_{iron} + P_{mech} + P_{sh}$ | Independent of load |
| 13 | Variable losses | $W_v = I_a^2 R_a$ (and $I_a^2 R_{se}$ for series) | $\propto I_a^2$ |
| 14 | Stray load loss (assumed) | $P_{stray} = 0.01 \times P_{out}$ | 1% of output |

### Maximum Efficiency Condition

| # | Condition | Formula | Interpretation |
|:-:|:----------|:--------|:---------------|
| 15 | Max efficiency occurs when | $W_v = W_c$ | Variable losses equal constant losses |
| 16 | Armature current at max efficiency | $I_a = \sqrt{\dfrac{W_c}{R_a}}$ | $W_c$ = constant losses, $R_a$ = armature resistance |
| 17 | Output at max efficiency | $P_{out,max\,\eta} = V I_a - 2 W_c$ | Subtracting all losses from input |

### Load Test Formulas (Rope Brake / Mechanical Brake)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------------|
| 18 | Net output power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N (S_1 - S_2) R}{60}$ | $S_1, S_2$ = spring balance readings (kg), $R$ = brake drum radius (m) |
| 19 | Net output torque | $T_{sh} = 9.81 (S_1 - S_2) R$ | In N┬Ęm, where 9.81 = $g$ in m/s┬▓ |
| 20 | Input power | $P_{in} = V I_L$ | From voltmeter and ammeter |
| 21 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{2 \pi N (S_1 - S_2) R}{60 \, V I_L} \times 100\%$ | Direct calculation from test |

### Regenerative Braking Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Sign Convention |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:----------------|
| 22 | Current during motoring | $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ | Positive (current flows into armature) |
| 23 | Current during regeneration | $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ | **Negative** when $E_b > V$ (current flows out of armature) |
| 24 | Power fed back to supply | $P_{regen} = V \cdot I_a$ (negative) | Energy returned to source |
| 25 | Braking torque | $T_{brake} = K_t \Phi I_a$ (negative) | Opposes rotation |
| 26 | Energy recovered | $E_{recovered} = \int V I_a \, dt$ | Over the duration of braking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams**: For **shunt motors**, regenerative braking works only when the speed is increased above the no-load speed (either by field weakening or external mechanical drive). For **series motors**, the supply voltage must be reversed and field separately excited because in normal series operation, load shedding causes dangerously high speeds.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

### In Electric Vehicles
1. **Range Extension**: Regenerative braking in EVs recovers 10ŌĆō20% of energy in urban stop-and-go driving. This is one of the key enablers of EVs like the **Hyundai Kona EV** and **MG ZS EV**.
2. **Thermal Management**: By reducing the dependence on friction brakes, regenerative braking minimizes brake dust, pad wear, and thermal stress on mechanical components.
3. **Battery State-of-Charge (SoC) Management**: Modern EV controllers (e.g., in **Tesla's regenerative braking system**) modulate the regeneration intensity based on battery SoC, temperature, and brake pedal position.
4. **Efficiency Optimization**: EV drivetrain designers choose motors (BLDC/PMSM) and operating points that maximize the efficiency envelope, often peaking at **93ŌĆō96%** for premium EVs.

### In Industrial Applications
1. **Crane and Hoist Drives**: When lowering heavy loads, regenerative braking feeds energy back, saving electricity costs.
2. **Elevator Drives**: Modern elevators use regenerative drives to feed braking energy back to the building's power grid.
3. **Electric Locomotives**: Regenerative braking is standard in electric trains (e.g., Indian Railways' regenerative braking in 3-phase EMUs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Flow and Efficiency Equation of a DC Shunt Motor

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define Input Power
The input electrical power is supplied from the DC source at voltage $V$ drawing line current $I_L$.

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Account for Shunt Field Path
The line current splits into the armature current $I_a$ and the shunt field current $I_{sh}$.

$$I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$$

The power consumed in the shunt field winding is the **shunt field copper loss**:

$$P_{sh} = V \cdot I_{sh} = \frac{V^2}{R_{sh}}$$

The power entering the armature circuit is:

$$P_{in} - P_{sh} = V \cdot I_a$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Voltage Equation
By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature circuit:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Multiplying both sides by $I_a$:

$$V \cdot I_a = E_b \cdot I_a + I_a^2 R_a$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Identify Mechanical Power Developed
The term $E_b \cdot I_a$ is the **mechanical power developed** ($P_m$) inside the armature, also called the **gross mechanical power** or **developed power**.

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = V I_a - I_a^2 R_a$$

The term $I_a^2 R_a$ is the **armature copper loss**.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Account for Rotational Losses
The mechanical power developed must overcome the rotational losses (iron losses + mechanical losses + stray load losses) before delivering useful shaft output.

$$P_{out} = P_m - P_{iron} - P_{mech} - P_{stray}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Group Losses as Constant and Variable

**Constant losses** ($W_c$): Independent of load current

$$W_c = P_{sh} + P_{iron} + P_{mech}$$

*(Note: Shunt field loss is constant because $V$ and $I_{sh}$ are essentially constant)*

**Variable losses** ($W_v$): Depend on load current

$$W_v = I_a^2 R_a$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Write the Efficiency Equation

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{in} - (W_c + W_v)}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{W_c + W_v}{P_{in}}$$

Substituting $P_{in} = V I_L$:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{W_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V I_L}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

### Step 1 ŌĆö Express Efficiency in Terms of Load
For a DC shunt motor, with $I_L \approx I_a$ (since $I_{sh}$ is small and roughly constant for varying load), the efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{V I_a - I_a^2 R_a - W_c}{V I_a}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate with Respect to $I_a$
To find the maximum, set $\dfrac{d\eta}{d I_a} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\eta}{d I_a} = \frac{d}{d I_a} \left[ 1 - \frac{W_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V I_a} \right] = 0$$

$$\frac{d\eta}{d I_a} = -\frac{1}{V} \cdot \frac{d}{d I_a} \left[ \frac{W_c}{I_a} + I_a R_a \right] = 0$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Derivative

$$\frac{d}{d I_a} \left[ \frac{W_c}{I_a} + I_a R_a \right] = -\frac{W_c}{I_a^2} + R_a = 0$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $I_a$

$$R_a = \frac{W_c}{I_a^2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I_a^2 R_a = W_c$$

This gives the **condition for maximum efficiency**:

$$\boxed{W_c = W_v \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad I_a^2 R_a = W_c \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad I_a = \sqrt{\frac{W_c}{R_a}}}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Physical Interpretation
- At low loads, constant losses ($W_c$) dominate ŌåÆ efficiency is low.
- At high loads, variable losses ($I_a^2 R_a$) dominate ŌåÆ efficiency falls again.
- Efficiency is **maximum** at an intermediate load where the two are equal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip**: When asked "At what load is the efficiency maximum?", the answer is **NOT** "at full load" or "at half load" ŌĆö it is at the specific load where $I_a^2 R_a = W_c$. You must calculate this using the constant losses determined from a no-load test (Swinburne's test) or given data.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Output Power at Maximum Efficiency

$$P_{out,\,max\,\eta} = V I_a - 2 W_c = V \sqrt{\frac{W_c}{R_a}} - 2 W_c$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Load Test Output and Efficiency

### Step 1 ŌĆö Mechanical Output from Rope-Brake Test
In a rope-brake (Prony brake) test, a rope is wound around a water-cooled brake drum fixed to the motor shaft. The two ends of the rope are attached to spring balances reading $S_1$ and $S_2$ (in kg-force). Let $R$ be the radius of the drum in metres, and $N$ be the speed in rpm.

The **net tangential force** at the drum surface (in kgf) is:

$$F_{net} = (S_1 - S_2) \quad \text{[kgf]}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Convert to SI Units (Newtons)
Using $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$:

$$F_{net} = 9.81 (S_1 - S_2) \quad \text{[N]}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Shaft Torque
Torque is force times radius:

$$T_{sh} = F_{net} \cdot R = 9.81 (S_1 - S_2) R \quad \text{[N┬Ęm]}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Angular Speed in rad/s

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Shaft Output Power
Power = Torque ├Ś Angular speed:

$$P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega = 9.81 (S_1 - S_2) R \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N (S_1 - S_2) R \times 9.81}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Compute Input Power
From voltmeter and ammeter readings:

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Compute Efficiency Directly

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{2 \pi N (S_1 - S_2) R \times 9.81}{60 \, V I_L}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem**: A DC shunt motor takes 50 A at 220 V. During a rope-brake test, the spring balance readings are 12 kg and 2 kg. The diameter of the brake drum is 40 cm, and the speed is 1000 rpm. Calculate the efficiency.

**Given**:
- $V = 220$ V, $I_L = 50$ A
- $S_1 = 12$ kg, $S_2 = 2$ kg
- Drum diameter = 40 cm ŌåÆ $R = 0.20$ m
- $N = 1000$ rpm

**Solution**:

Step 1: Net force on drum

$$F_{net} = (S_1 - S_2) = 12 - 2 = 10 \text{ kgf}$$

Step 2: Net force in Newtons

$$F_{net} = 10 \times 9.81 = 98.1 \text{ N}$$

Step 3: Shaft torque

$$T_{sh} = 98.1 \times 0.20 = 19.62 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

Step 4: Output power

$$P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 19.62}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 19620}{60} = 2054.7 \text{ W}$$

Step 5: Input power

$$P_{in} = 220 \times 50 = 11000 \text{ W}$$

Step 6: Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{2054.7}{11000} \times 100\% = 18.68\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often forget to multiply by **9.81** to convert kgf to Newtons. Always show the unit conversion explicitly. Also, do not confuse **diameter** with **radius** ŌĆö using diameter instead of radius will give 4 times the correct answer.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Regenerative Braking in DC Shunt Motor

### Step 1 ŌĆö Operating Condition for Regeneration
During motoring:

$$I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} \quad (> 0 \text{ since } V > E_b)$$

For the machine to act as a generator (i.e., feed power back to the supply), the current must reverse direction:

$$I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} < 0$$

This requires $E_b > V$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Achieving $E_b > V$ in a Shunt Motor
From the EMF equation: $E_b = K \Phi N$

To make $E_b > V$:

$$K \Phi N > V \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N > \frac{V}{K \Phi} = N_0$$

where $N_0$ is the no-load speed. Thus, regenerative braking in a shunt motor requires the motor to be **driven above its no-load speed**.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Two Methods to Achieve Overspeed

**Method 1 ŌĆö Field Weakening**: Reduce $\Phi$ by reducing $I_{sh}$ (using a field rheostat). Since $N \propto 1/\Phi$, reducing $\Phi$ increases speed.

**Method 2 ŌĆö Mechanical Overspeed**: Allow the motor to overspeed due to external mechanical input (e.g., when an EV descends a steep hill, the wheels drive the motor above its no-load speed).

### Step 4 ŌĆö Power Flow During Regeneration
- Armature current reverses: $I_a$ is negative.
- Power developed $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a$ is **negative** (mechanical power is absorbed).
- Power fed back to supply: $P_{regen} = V \cdot I_a$ is **negative** (energy flows back to source).
- The motor's electromagnetic torque $T_a = K_t \Phi I_a$ becomes **negative** (opposes motion) ŌåÆ this is the **braking torque**.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Braking Torque Magnitude

$$T_{brake} = K_t \Phi \cdot \frac{E_b - V}{R_a} = K_t \Phi \cdot \frac{K \Phi N - V}{R_a}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Energy Recovered Over Time

$$E_{recovered} = \int_0^{t_b} V \cdot I_a(t) \, dt = \int_0^{t_b} V \cdot \frac{V - E_b(t)}{R_a} \, dt$$

For a constant deceleration, this can be expressed in terms of the change in kinetic energy:

$$E_{recovered} = \frac{1}{2} J (\omega_i^2 - \omega_f^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$$

where $J$ is the moment of inertia and $\eta_{regen}$ is the regeneration efficiency (typically 60ŌĆō80% in EVs).

### Step 7 ŌĆö Worked Example: Regenerative Braking

> **Problem**: A 220 V DC shunt motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$ and runs at 1000 rpm drawing 20 A. The field flux is reduced by 10% while the load torque (now overhauling) drives the motor. Find the speed at which regenerative braking begins and the braking current at 1100 rpm.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Original back EMF

$$E_{b1} = V - I_a R_a = 220 - 20 \times 0.5 = 210 \text{ V}$$

Step 2: Original field flux $\Phi_1$. New flux $\Phi_2 = 0.9 \Phi_1$.

Step 3: For regenerative braking, the new back EMF at the new speed $N_2$ must equal 220 V (or just exceed it).

$$\frac{E_{b2}}{E_{b1}} = \frac{\Phi_2 N_2}{\Phi_1 N_1} = \frac{0.9 \times N_2}{1000}$$

Setting $E_{b2} = 220$ V:

$$\frac{0.9 N_2}{1000} = \frac{220}{210} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N_2 = \frac{220 \times 1000}{210 \times 0.9} = 1164 \text{ rpm}$$

Step 4: At 1100 rpm, the back EMF is

$$E_{b,1100} = 220 \times \frac{1100}{1164} = 207.9 \text{ V}$$

Step 5: Braking current (note: $E_b < V$, so this is still motoring; if $E_b > V$, current reverses)

Actually, since at 1100 rpm $E_b = 207.9$ V $< 220$ V, the motor is still in motoring mode. The regeneration begins only above 1164 rpm.

At $N_3 = 1200$ rpm, for example:

$$E_{b,1200} = 220 \times \frac{1200}{1164} = 226.8 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{a,brake} = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{220 - 226.8}{0.5} = -13.6 \text{ A}$$

The negative sign confirms current reversal ŌåÆ **regenerative mode** active.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often use the **same flux value** for the new speed without adjusting for field weakening. Always remember that if the field flux is changed, both $E_b$ and torque equations must use the **new** flux value.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: List the various losses occurring in a DC machine. Which of these losses are constant and which are variable?**

**Model Answer**:

The various losses in a DC machine are:

1. **Copper losses** (variable with load):
   - Armature copper loss: $P_{ca} = I_a^2 R_a$
   - Field copper loss: $P_{cf} = V I_{sh}$ (shunt motor)
   - Brush contact loss

2. **Iron/core losses** (constant, since flux and speed are nearly constant):
   - Hysteresis loss: $P_h \propto f \cdot B_{max}^{1.6}$
   - Eddy current loss: $P_e \propto f^2 \cdot B_{max}^2$

3. **Mechanical losses** (constant, since speed is nearly constant):
   - Friction loss (bearing + brush)
   - Windage loss

4. **Stray load losses** (variable, but taken as 1% of output)

**Classification**:
- **Constant losses** ($W_c$): Iron losses, Mechanical losses, Shunt field copper loss
- **Variable losses** ($W_v$): Armature copper loss ($I_a^2 R_a$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point**: The classification is crucial for the Swinburne's test and maximum efficiency calculations. State explicitly which loss belongs to which category with reasons.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: What is regenerative braking? State the condition under which a DC shunt motor will regenerate.**

**Model Answer**:

**Regenerative Braking**: It is a method of braking in which the kinetic energy of the rotating armature is converted into electrical energy and fed back to the supply source. The motor essentially acts as a generator during braking.

**Condition for Regeneration in DC Shunt Motor**:

The back EMF must exceed the supply voltage, i.e.,

$$E_b > V$$

From the EMF equation, $E_b = K \Phi N$, this condition requires:

$$N > \frac{V}{K \Phi} = N_0 \quad \text{(no-load speed)}$$

Hence, **the motor must be running above its no-load speed**. This can be achieved by:
1. **Field weakening** ŌĆö reducing the shunt field current using a field rheostat
2. **Mechanical overspeed** ŌĆö external mechanical input drives the motor above $N_0$ (e.g., when an EV descends a hill)

When $E_b > V$, the armature current $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$ becomes **negative**, meaning the current flows from the armature back to the supply, and the electromagnetic torque reverses direction, providing **braking action**.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]**: Explain in detail the various losses occurring in a DC shunt motor with their mathematical expressions. Derive the condition for maximum efficiency.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]**: A 250 V DC shunt motor takes a no-load current of 5 A (including field current of 2 A) and runs at 1000 rpm. The armature resistance is 0.2 ╬® and the field resistance is 250 ╬®. On full load, the motor takes 50 A and runs at 950 rpm. Calculate: (i) full load efficiency, (ii) the load at which efficiency is maximum, and (iii) the maximum efficiency.

#### Model Solution to Q (a):

**Various Losses in DC Shunt Motor**:

**(1) Armature Copper Loss** (variable):
$$P_{ca} = I_a^2 R_a$$
This is the heat dissipated in the armature winding due to current $I_a$ flowing through resistance $R_a$.

**(2) Shunt Field Copper Loss** (constant):
$$P_{sh} = V I_{sh} = \frac{V^2}{R_{sh}}$$
Constant because $V$ and $R_{sh}$ are constant, so $I_{sh}$ is constant.

**(3) Iron / Core Losses** (constant, occurs in armature core):
- Hysteresis loss: $P_h = \eta_h B_{max}^{1.6} f V$ (W)
- Eddy current loss: $P_e = \eta_e B_{max}^2 f^2 t^2$ (W)

These depend on flux density and frequency (which are nearly constant for a given motor at constant speed and voltage).

**(4) Mechanical Losses** (constant):
- Bearing friction loss
- Brush friction loss
- Windage loss (air friction on rotating armature)

These depend on speed, which is approximately constant.

**(5) Stray Load Losses**:
- Approximately 1% of full load output, due to flux distortion from armature reaction.

**Total Losses**:
$$P_{loss} = P_{ca} + P_{sh} + P_{iron} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}$$

**Grouping**:
- Constant losses: $W_c = P_{sh} + P_{iron} + P_{mech}$
- Variable losses: $W_v = I_a^2 R_a$

**Derivation of Maximum Efficiency Condition**:

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{V I_a - I_a^2 R_a - W_c}{V I_a} = 1 - \frac{W_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V I_a}$$

For maximum $\eta$, set $\dfrac{d\eta}{d I_a} = 0$:

$$\frac{d}{d I_a}\left[\frac{W_c}{I_a} + I_a R_a\right] = 0$$

$$-\frac{W_c}{I_a^2} + R_a = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad W_c = I_a^2 R_a = W_v$$

**Condition for Maximum Efficiency**: The variable losses must equal the constant losses.

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{W_c}{R_a}}$$

#### Model Solution to Q (b):

**Given**:
- $V = 250$ V
- No-load: $I_{L0} = 5$ A (includes $I_{sh} = 2$ A), $N_0 = 1000$ rpm
- $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 250 \, \Omega$
- Full load: $I_L = 50$ A, $N = 950$ rpm

**Step 1: Compute No-Load Armature Current**

At no load: $I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh} = 5 - 2 = 3$ A

**Step 2: Compute No-Load Back EMF**

$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 250 - 3 \times 0.2 = 250 - 0.6 = 249.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Determine Constant Losses from No-Load Data**

At no load, output is essentially zero (no useful mechanical work), so input power equals all losses.

$$P_{in0} = V \cdot I_{L0} = 250 \times 5 = 1250 \text{ W}$$

No-load armature copper loss:

$$P_{ca0} = I_{a0}^2 R_a = 3^2 \times 0.2 = 1.8 \text{ W}$$

Constant losses:

$$W_c = P_{in0} - P_{ca0} = 1250 - 1.8 = 1248.2 \text{ W}$$

*(Note: $W_c$ includes shunt field loss + iron + mechanical + stray. We can also separately verify $P_{sh} = 250 \times 2 = 500$ W, so iron + mechanical + stray Ōēł 748.2 W.)*

**Step 4: Full-Load Armature Current**

$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 50 - 2 = 48 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5: Full-Load Armature Copper Loss**

$$P_{ca} = 48^2 \times 0.2 = 2304 \times 0.2 = 460.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6: Full-Load Total Losses**

$$P_{loss} = W_c + W_v = 1248.2 + 460.8 = 1709 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7: Full-Load Input Power**

$$P_{in} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 50 = 12500 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8: Full-Load Output Power**

$$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{loss} = 12500 - 1709 = 10791 \text{ W}$$

**(i) Full-Load Efficiency**:

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{10791}{12500} \times 100\% = 86.33\%$$

**Step 9: Armature Current at Maximum Efficiency**

$$I_{a,max\,\eta} = \sqrt{\frac{W_c}{R_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{1248.2}{0.2}} = \sqrt{6241} = 78.99 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Load at Maximum Efficiency**:
The corresponding line current is $I_L = I_a + I_{sh} = 78.99 + 2 = 80.99$ A.

As a fraction of full load: $80.99 / 50 = 1.62$ ŌåÆ maximum efficiency occurs at about **162% of full load** (i.e., the motor is most efficient at an overload).

**Step 10: Output at Maximum Efficiency**

$$P_{out,\,max\,\eta} = V I_a - 2 W_c = 250 \times 78.99 - 2 \times 1248.2 = 19747.5 - 2496.4 = 17251.1 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{in,\,max\,\eta} = V \cdot I_L = 250 \times 80.99 = 20247.5 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Maximum Efficiency**:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{17251.1}{20247.5} \times 100\% = 85.20\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> 1. **Do not confuse** $I_L$ (line current) with $I_a$ (armature current). Always subtract $I_{sh}$ to get $I_a$.
> 2. **Constant losses** should be calculated from no-load data: $W_c = V \cdot I_{L0} - I_{a0}^2 R_a$, **not** $V \cdot I_{L0}$ alone.
> 3. Show **all units** clearly. Marks are deducted for missing units or wrong substitutions.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q (a) [7 Marks]**: With a neat circuit diagram, explain the regenerative braking of a DC shunt motor. State its advantages and limitations.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]**: A 200 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.3 ╬® and a shunt field resistance of 200 ╬®. When running on no load at 1500 rpm, the armature current is 2 A. The armature current at full load is 40 A. Calculate the efficiency at (i) full load, and (ii) half load.

#### Model Solution to Q (a):

**Regenerative Braking of DC Shunt Motor**:

**Principle**: In regenerative braking, the motor is made to act as a generator by ensuring that the back EMF ($E_b$) exceeds the supply voltage ($V$). When this happens, the armature current reverses and flows back into the supply, simultaneously producing a braking torque that decelerates the motor.

**Circuit Description**:
- The shunt field winding remains connected to the supply (constant flux).
- A **field rheostat** is included in series with the shunt field to control flux.
- The armature is connected to the supply through a controller/breaker.

**Operating Modes**:

**Mode 1 ŌĆö Motoring** (normal operation):
- $V > E_b$, so $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a > 0$
- Current flows from supply to armature, motor develops torque in the direction of rotation.

**Mode 2 ŌĆö Regenerative Braking**:
- Speed is increased (or flux decreased) so that $E_b > V$.
- $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a < 0$, current flows from armature back to supply.
- The electromagnetic torque reverses direction, opposing rotation ŌåÆ **braking torque**.

**Conditions Required**:
1. $E_b > V$, i.e., $N > V / (K \Phi) = N_0$
2. The supply must be capable of accepting the returned power (a stiff DC bus with another load, or a battery).
3. For shunt motors, the field must remain energized.

**Methods to Achieve Regeneration**:
- **Field-weakening method**: Reduce shunt field current via field rheostat ŌåÆ $\Phi$ decreases ŌåÆ $N$ increases ŌåÆ $E_b$ increases.
- **Overspeed method**: External mechanical input drives the motor above its no-load speed (e.g., descending a hill in an EV).

**Advantages**:
1. **Energy is saved** ŌĆö returned to the supply/battery instead of being wasted as heat.
2. **Brake wear is reduced** ŌĆö less reliance on mechanical brakes, lowering maintenance.
3. **Speed control** is precise in the overspeed range.
4. **No additional equipment** is needed beyond a field rheostat.
5. **Range extension in EVs** by 10ŌĆō20% in typical urban cycles.

**Limitations**:
1. **Regeneration is possible only above no-load speed** ŌĆö for shunt motors, this requires either field weakening or an external driving force.
2. **For DC series motors, regenerative braking is not feasible** on a DC supply without modifications (load shedding causes dangerously high speeds).
3. **The supply must accept reverse power flow** ŌĆö simple resistive loads cannot absorb it.
4. **Braking torque decreases as speed decreases** ŌĆö the braking effect tapers off near low speeds.
5. **Battery SoC limits** ŌĆö at 100% charge, the battery cannot accept regenerated energy, so friction brakes must be used.

#### Model Solution to Q (b):

**Given**:
- $V = 200$ V
- $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 200 \, \Omega$
- No-load: $I_{a0} = 2$ A, $N_0 = 1500$ rpm
- Full load: $I_{a,FL} = 40$ A

**Step 1: Compute Shunt Field Current**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{200}{200} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Compute Constant Losses from No-Load**

At no load, no useful output, so input power = all losses.

$$P_{in0} = V \cdot (I_{a0} + I_{sh}) = 200 \times (2 + 1) = 600 \text{ W}$$

No-load armature copper loss:

$$P_{ca0} = I_{a0}^2 \cdot R_a = 2^2 \times 0.3 = 1.2 \text{ W}$$

Constant losses (assuming stray loss is small at no load):

$$W_c = P_{in0} - P_{ca0} = 600 - 1.2 = 598.8 \text{ W}$$

*(Alternative breakdown: $P_{sh} = 200 \times 1 = 200$ W, so iron + mechanical Ōēł 398.8 W)*

**(i) Efficiency at Full Load**:

Step 3: Armature copper loss at full load

$$P_{ca,FL} = I_{a,FL}^2 \cdot R_a = 40^2 \times 0.3 = 1600 \times 0.3 = 480 \text{ W}$$

Step 4: Total losses at full load

$$P_{loss,FL} = W_c + P_{ca,FL} = 598.8 + 480 = 1078.8 \text{ W}$$

Step 5: Full-load input current

$$I_{L,FL} = I_{a,FL} + I_{sh} = 40 + 1 = 41 \text{ A}$$

Step 6: Full-load input power

$$P_{in,FL} = 200 \times 41 = 8200 \text{ W}$$

Step 7: Full-load output power

$$P_{out,FL} = 8200 - 1078.8 = 7121.2 \text{ W}$$

Step 8: Full-load efficiency

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{7121.2}{8200} \times 100\% = 86.84\%$$

**(ii) Efficiency at Half Load**:

Assuming "half load" means **half the full-load current** (or half the full-load output ŌĆö let's assume the armature current is half).

Step 9: Armature current at half load

$$I_{a,HL} = \frac{I_{a,FL}}{2} = \frac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ A}$$

Step 10: Armature copper loss at half load

$$P_{ca,HL} = 20^2 \times 0.3 = 400 \times 0.3 = 120 \text{ W}$$

Step 11: Total losses at half load

$$P_{loss,HL} = W_c + P_{ca,HL} = 598.8 + 120 = 718.8 \text{ W}$$

Step 12: Half-load input current

$$I_{L,HL} = 20 + 1 = 21 \text{ A}$$

Step 13: Half-load input power

$$P_{in,HL} = 200 \times 21 = 4200 \text{ W}$$

Step 14: Half-load output power

$$P_{out,HL} = 4200 - 718.8 = 3481.2 \text{ W}$$

Step 15: Half-load efficiency

$$\eta_{HL} = \frac{3481.2}{4200} \times 100\% = 82.89\%$$

**Observations**:
- Efficiency at full load (86.84%) > Efficiency at half load (82.89%)
- The maximum efficiency occurs at $I_a = \sqrt{W_c / R_a} = \sqrt{598.8 / 0.3} = \sqrt{1996} = 44.68$ A, which is **above the full-load current of 40 A**. Thus, the motor is most efficient at slight overload (about 112% of full load).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> 1. **Half-load definition**: Some texts define half-load as half the rated output (kW), others as half the full-load current. State your assumption clearly. In the absence of data, **half the full-load armature current** is the most common assumption.
> 2. **Never forget $I_{sh}$**: The line current = armature current + shunt field current. Missing $I_{sh}$ will give wrong input power.
> 3. **Don't compute $W_c$ from the rated full-load condition** ŌĆö it must come from no-load data.
> 4. **The constant losses are assumed to remain constant** at all loads (an approximation that holds well for shunt motors).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 2: DC Motor Losses, Efficiency, Load Test & Regenerative Braking**

### Losses in DC Motors
- **Copper losses**: $I_a^2 R_a$ (armature, variable) and $V I_{sh}$ (shunt field, constant)
- **Iron losses**: Hysteresis $\propto f B_{max}^{1.6}$ and Eddy current $\propto f^2 B_{max}^2$
- **Mechanical losses**: Friction + Windage (constant, since speed is nearly constant)
- **Stray load losses**: ~1% of output (variable)
- **Variable losses** ($\propto I_a^2$): armature copper loss
- **Constant losses**: iron + mechanical + shunt field copper loss

### Efficiency Equation
- $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{W_c + W_v}{P_{in}}$
- For a DC shunt motor: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{W_c + I_a^2 R_a}{V I_L}$

### Maximum Efficiency Condition
- **Condition**: $W_c = W_v \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_a = \sqrt{W_c / R_a}$
- Efficiency is **NOT** maximum at full load by default; it is maximum at the specific load where constant and variable losses are equal.
- Output at max efficiency: $P_{out,max\,\eta} = V I_a - 2 W_c$

### Load Test (Rope-Brake Test)
- **Direct method** to find efficiency by applying real mechanical load
- Suitable only for **small motors** (large motors have heat disposal problems)
- Output: $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N (S_1 - S_2) R \times 9.81}{60}$
- Net torque: $T_{sh} = 9.81 (S_1 - S_2) R$ N┬Ęm
- **Always convert kgf to N** by multiplying by 9.81
- **Always use radius**, not diameter

### Regenerative Braking
- **Core condition**: $E_b > V$ (back EMF exceeds supply voltage)
- Achieved by: (1) **Field weakening** (reduce $\Phi$), or (2) **Overspeed** (external driving force)
- Current reverses: $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a < 0$
- Power flows back: $P_{regen} = V \cdot I_a$ (negative)
- **Braking torque**: $T_{brake} = K_t \Phi I_a$ (negative, opposes motion)
- **Best suited for**: Separately excited and shunt motors
- **Difficult for**: Series motors (load-dependent flux; need special arrangements)
- **EV Application**: Recovers 10-20% energy in urban driving

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
1. **Confusing $I_L$ with $I_a$** ŌĆö always subtract $I_{sh}$
2. **Forgetting the 9.81 conversion** in rope-brake test calculations
3. **Using diameter instead of radius** in brake drum calculations
4. **Computing $W_c$ from full-load data** instead of no-load data
5. **Assuming maximum efficiency is at full load** ŌĆö calculate using $W_c = W_v$
6. **Forgetting to consider field flux change** in regenerative braking speed calculations
7. **Saying series motors can regenerate easily on DC supply** ŌĆö they cannot, without modification

### Formula Quick-Reference (for last-minute revision)
| Concept | Key Formula |
|:--------|:------------|
| Voltage equation | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ |
| Back EMF | $E_b = K \Phi N$ |
| Armature torque | $T_a = K_t \Phi I_a$ |
| Mechanical power | $P_m = E_b I_a$ |
| Max efficiency load | $I_a = \sqrt{W_c / R_a}$ |
| Rope-brake output | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N (S_1 - S_2) R \times 9.81}{60}$ |
| Regeneration condition | $E_b > V$ |
| Regen current | $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$ (negative) |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase Induction Motors - Slip Ring and Squirrel Cage Types - Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Three Phase Induction Motors - Slip Ring and Squirrel Cage Types - Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Three-Phase Induction Motor (TIM)** is a self-starting, brushless, alternating current (AC) electromechanical energy conversion device belonging to the asynchronous machine family. It operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction** discovered by Michael Faraday, where a rotating magnetic field (RMF) generated by the stator induces currents in the short-circuited rotor, which in turn produce torque to drive the mechanical load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies the induction motor as the **workhorse of modern Electric Vehicle (EV) powertrains**, particularly in two-wheeler, three-wheeler, and entry-level four-wheeler applications, owing to its rugged construction, low cost, and high reliability.

### 1.2 Classification of Three-Phase Induction Motors

Based on **rotor construction**, three-phase induction motors are broadly classified into two categories:

| Type | Rotor Construction | Rotor Terminals | External Resistance | KTU Exam Focus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Squirrel Cage Induction Motor (SCIM)** | Bars embedded in slots, permanently short-circuited via end rings | None (cage is sealed) | Not possible | High ŌĆö most common in EVs |
| **Slip Ring Induction Motor (SRIM)** | Wound 3-phase windings similar to stator | Brought out via slip rings & brushes | Can be added externally | Moderate ŌĆö high-starting-torque applications |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Rotating Horseshoe Magnet"

Imagine two children, **Rahul** and **Anjali**, playing with horseshoe magnets on a smooth tabletop:
- **Rahul** holds a **horseshoe magnet** and rotates it in a circle at a constant speed (say, clockwise).
- **Anjali** holds a small **iron disc** (non-magnetic conductor) on the same table, free to spin.

As Rahul's magnet rotates, its **magnetic field lines sweep across the iron disc**. By Faraday's Law, this sweeping (changing) flux **induces eddy currents** in the iron disc. By Lenz's Law, these currents try to *oppose* the relative motion, so they create their own magnetic field that **chases the rotating magnet**. The result: the iron disc begins to **rotate in the same direction** as the magnet, but at a **slightly slower speed** (because if it rotated at the same speed, there would be no relative motion, no flux cutting, no induced current, and no torque).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Reluctant Chaser Analogy:** The rotor is like a "reluctant chaser" ŌĆö it constantly tries to catch up with the rotating magnetic field but **never succeeds**. The difference between the field's speed and rotor's speed is precisely what we call **SLIP**. This slip is the *soul* of the induction motor.

### 1.4 Why Induction Motors are Critical for EVs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EV Industry Context:** Approximately **65ŌĆō70% of global electric two-wheelers** and a significant portion of electric buses and commercial EVs use **Squirrel Cage Induction Motors (SCIM)**. Tesla's Model S and Model 3 used induction motors. The robustness (no brushes to wear out), tolerance to harsh environments, and ability to operate at high speeds (>15,000 RPM) make SCIM ideal for traction applications.

---

### 1.5 Construction ŌĆö The Two Variants Explained

#### A) Squirrel Cage Induction Motor (SCIM)

**Stator:** A laminated silicon-steel core (to reduce eddy current losses, typically **0.35 mm to 0.5 mm** thick laminations) with uniformly distributed slots housing a balanced 3-phase winding. The windings are spatially displaced by **120 electrical degrees** and supplied by a balanced 3-phase AC source.

**Rotor:** Consists of:
- A cylindrical laminated core with **parallel slots** (usually skewed by one slot pitch to eliminate cogging torque and harmonic effects).
- **Copper or aluminum bars** placed in the slots.
- Two **end rings** (made of the same material as the bars) that permanently short-circuit all bars at both ends, forming a closed electrical cage ŌĆö hence the name "squirrel cage" (it physically resembles the exercise wheel of a pet squirrel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The rotor bars are *not* connected to any external circuit. The motor has no brushes, no slip rings, and no commutator ŌĆö making it virtually maintenance-free.

#### B) Slip Ring Induction Motor (SRIM) / Wound Rotor Induction Motor

**Stator:** Identical to that of the SCIM.

**Rotor:** Contains a **3-phase winding** similar to the stator winding, with the same number of poles. The three phases of the rotor winding are connected in **star (Y) configuration**, and the three free ends are brought out and connected to **three slip rings** mounted on the rotor shaft. **Carbon brushes** ride on these slip rings and connect to an external **3-phase star-connected rheostat** (variable resistor), which can be varied to control starting current, starting torque, and speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** SRIM is *also* called a **"Wound Rotor Induction Motor (WRIM)"**. SRIMs offer superior starting torque (up to 200% of full-load torque) compared to SCIMs (typically only 100ŌĆō150%), at the cost of higher maintenance and lower efficiency.

### 1.6 Salient Comparisons for KTU Examinations

| Feature | Squirrel Cage (SCIM) | Slip Ring (SRIM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotor construction** | Bars shorted by end rings | Wound 3-phase, brought out |
| **External resistance access** | Not possible | Yes, via slip rings |
| **Starting torque** | Low to moderate | High (adjustable) |
| **Starting current** | High (5ŌĆō7 times rated) | Lower (controlled by R) |
| **Speed control range** | Limited (narrow) | Wide (good) |
| **Maintenance** | Almost nil | High (brushes wear) |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Robustness** | Excellent | Moderate |
| **EV applicability** | High (Tesla, Tata, Ather) | Low (rare in EVs) |
| **Efficiency** | High | Slightly lower |
| **Power factor** | Lower at light load | Better with external R |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) ŌĆö The Heart of Operation

The principle of operation rests entirely on the generation of a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)**. The KTU examiner frequently tests this concept as a 7-mark or full 14-mark question.

#### Step-by-Step Logic of RMF Generation

1. **Balanced 3-phase supply** ŌĆö Three sinusoidal voltages that are **120┬░ apart in time phase**, with equal magnitude, are fed to the three stator windings, which are **120┬░ apart in space**.

2. **Each phase winding produces a stationary, alternating, pulsating magnetic field** along its own magnetic axis. The field magnitude varies sinusoidally with time, but it does not move in space.

3. **Vector summation** of the three pulsating fields, at any instant, yields a **single resultant magnetic field of constant magnitude (1.5 ├Ś ╬”_m)** that **rotates in space at synchronous speed**.

4. The direction of rotation depends on the **phase sequence** of the supply. Reversing any two phases reverses the rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The RMF can be visualized as a **magnetic bar magnet** whose strength remains constant but which physically rotates around the stator bore at synchronous speed. The rotor chases this magnet.

### 2.2 Synchronous Speed ($N_s$)

The mechanical speed of the rotating magnetic field is called **Synchronous Speed**, denoted $N_s$ in RPM or $\omega_s$ in rad/s.

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} \quad \text{[RPM]}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{4 \pi f}{P} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

Where:
- $f$ = supply frequency in **Hz** (typically **50 Hz** in India, **60 Hz** in some countries).
- $P$ = total number of **poles** of the motor (must be an **even integer**: 2, 4, 6, 8, ...).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** A **2-pole** motor at 50 Hz has $N_s = 3000$ RPM. A **4-pole** motor at 50 Hz has $N_s = 1500$ RPM. A **6-pole** motor at 50 Hz has $N_s = 1000$ RPM. Memorize these standard values!

### 2.3 Slip (s) ŌĆö The Indispensable Concept

Since the rotor can never reach synchronous speed (otherwise no relative motion, no induced EMF, no torque), there exists a **relative speed** between the rotating field and the rotor. The ratio of this relative speed to the synchronous speed is called **Slip**, expressed as a per-unit value (typically 0.01 to 0.05) or a percentage (1% to 5%).

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

Where:
- $s$ = slip (per unit, dimensionless).
- $N_s$ = synchronous speed in RPM.
- $N_r$ = actual rotor speed in RPM.

Rearranging for rotor speed:

$$N_r = N_s \cdot (1 - s)$$

### 2.4 Frequency of Rotor-Induced Currents ($f_r$)

The relative speed between the RMF and the rotor determines the rate at which rotor conductors cut the flux. The frequency of the EMF/current induced in the rotor is therefore:

$$f_r = s \cdot f$$

At **standstill** ($N_r = 0$, $s = 1$), the rotor frequency equals the stator frequency $f$. At **no-load** ($N_r \approx N_s$, $s \approx 0.005$), the rotor frequency is very small (e.g., for $s = 0.005$ and $f = 50$ Hz, $f_r = 0.25$ Hz).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120f/P$ | RPM | RMF speed |
| 2 | Synchronous angular speed | $\omega_s = 4\pi f / P$ | rad/s | Mechanical |
| 3 | Slip (per unit) | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | dimensionless | $0 < s < 1$ in motoring |
| 4 | Rotor speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | RPM | Actual mechanical speed |
| 5 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | At $s=1$, $f_r=f$ |
| 6 | Rotor EMF | $E_2 = s \cdot E_2^{\text{standstill}}$ | Volts | Proportional to slip |
| 7 | Rotor resistance (ref. to stator) | $R_2' = R_2 + R_{\text{ext}}/s$ | Ohms | Steady-state equivalent |
| 8 | Synchronous speed in rad/s | $\omega_{\text{syn}} = 2\pi f / (P/2)$ | rad/s | Electrical equivalent |
| 9 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{\text{cu2}} = s \cdot P_{\text{ag}}$ | Watts | Proportional to slip |
| 10 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_{\text{ag}}$ | Watts | Air-gap power split |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical RMF Vector Summation Result:** The magnitude of the resultant rotating flux is $\Phi_{\text{res}} = 1.5 \cdot \Phi_m$, where $\Phi_m$ is the maximum flux per phase. This factor 1.5 is **favorite KTU exam question**.

### 2.6 The "Why" Behind Each Formula

- **Why is $N_s$ inversely proportional to $P$?** More poles mean the flux must travel a shorter spatial distance to complete one electrical revolution, so more electrical cycles fit in one mechanical revolution ŌĆö but each mechanical revolution takes longer.
- **Why does slip exist?** Induction requires *change*. If rotor and field rotated at the same speed, flux linking the rotor would be constant ŌåÆ no induced EMF ŌåÆ no current ŌåÆ no torque. Slip is the *price of induction*.
- **Why is rotor EMF proportional to $s$?** EMF = rate of change of flux linkage. The rate of flux cutting is proportional to relative speed = $s \cdot N_s$. Hence $E_2 \propto s$.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EV Application:** The slip concept is **fundamental to regenerative braking** in EVs. When the driver releases the accelerator, the inverter actively drives the motor to act as a generator, forcing the rotor to *lead* the field (negative slip), causing the motor to deliver power back to the battery. The same machine operates in **four quadrants** of the torque-speed plane ŌĆö motoring, generating, forward braking, and reverse motoring.

---

### 2.8 Principle of Operation ŌĆö Consolidated Explanation

1. When the 3-phase stator winding is energized, a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** is established, rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$ in the air gap.

2. This RMF links with the stationary rotor conductors. The relative velocity between the field and the rotor is $(N_s - 0) = N_s$ at standstill, so a large EMF is induced in the rotor ŌĆö this is the **standstill rotor EMF**, $E_2$.

3. Since the rotor circuit is closed (cage in SCIM, external resistance in SRIM), this induced EMF circulates a **rotor current $I_2$**.

4. The rotor current-carrying conductors, placed in the magnetic field of the RMF, experience a **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$. By Fleming's Left-Hand Rule, this force produces a **torque** that rotates the rotor in the *same direction* as the RMF.

5. As the rotor accelerates, the relative speed $(N_s - N_r)$ decreases ŌåÆ induced EMF decreases ŌåÆ rotor current decreases ŌåÆ torque decreases. Equilibrium is reached when motor torque equals load torque.

6. The rotor **never** catches up with the RMF. It always runs at a speed $N_r < N_s$, with a small slip $s$ maintained.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) ŌĆö Full Step-by-Step

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 3. We will derive it rigorously using vector summation.

#### Setup and Assumptions

Consider a balanced 3-phase, 2-pole stator winding with three coils $A-A'$, $B-B'$, and $C-C'$ placed spatially at 120┬░ electrical apart in slots cut on the inner periphery of a laminated stator core. The coils are supplied with a balanced 3-phase AC voltage of peak value $V_m$ and angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$.

The three phase currents are:

$$i_R = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$i_Y = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)$$

$$i_B = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) = I_m \sin(\omega t + 120┬░)$$

We follow the convention where the R-phase is the reference, the Y-phase lags by 120┬░, and the B-phase leads by 120┬░ (or equivalently, lags by 240┬░).

#### Step 1: Pulsating Flux Produced by Each Phase

Each phase, when energized, produces a sinusoidal, **pulsating (alternating)** magnetic flux along its own magnetic axis. These fluxes are *stationary in space* but *vary sinusoidally in time*.

Let the magnetic axis of phase R (reference) be along the horizontal (x-axis). The flux produced by phase R is:

$$\phi_R(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(along the R-axis)}$$

Where $\Phi_m$ is the **maximum flux per phase**.

Similarly, the flux produced by phase Y, whose axis is at 120┬░ from R (in the counter-clockwise direction), is:

$$\phi_Y(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) \quad \text{(along the Y-axis)}$$

And the flux produced by phase B, whose axis is at 240┬░ (or equivalently, $-120┬░$) from R, is:

$$\phi_B(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t + 120┬░) \quad \text{(along the B-axis)}$$

#### Step 2: Resolve Each Flux into Orthogonal Components (X and Y)

We resolve each flux vector into its horizontal (x) and vertical (y) components. Recall that a vector of magnitude $\phi$ at angle $\theta$ from the x-axis has components $(\phi \cos\theta, \phi \sin\theta)$.

**Phase R flux (axis at 0┬░):**

$$\phi_{Rx} = \phi_R \cos(0┬░) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot 1 = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$\phi_{Ry} = \phi_R \sin(0┬░) = 0$$

**Phase Y flux (axis at 120┬░):**

$$\phi_{Yx} = \phi_Y \cos(120┬░) = -\frac{1}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)$$

$$\phi_{Yy} = \phi_Y \sin(120┬░) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)$$

**Phase B flux (axis at 240┬░):**

$$\phi_{Bx} = \phi_B \cos(240┬░) = -\frac{1}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$

$$\phi_{By} = \phi_B \sin(240┬░) = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$

#### Step 3: Sum the X-Components

The total horizontal component of the resultant flux is:

$$\phi_x = \phi_{Rx} + \phi_{Yx} + \phi_{Bx}$$

Substituting:

$$\phi_x = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) - \frac{1}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \frac{1}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$

Factor out $\Phi_m$:

$$\phi_x = \Phi_m \left[ \sin(\omega t) - \frac{1}{2} \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \frac{1}{2} \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) \right]$$

Apply the sum-to-product identity:

$$\sin(\omega t - 120┬░) + \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) = 2 \sin\left(\frac{2\omega t - 360┬░}{2}\right) \cos\left(\frac{120┬░}{2}\right)$$

Simplify:

$$= 2 \sin(\omega t - 180┬░) \cos(60┬░) = 2 \cdot (-\sin\omega t) \cdot \frac{1}{2} = -\sin(\omega t)$$

Therefore:

$$\phi_x = \Phi_m \left[ \sin(\omega t) - \frac{1}{2} \cdot (-\sin(\omega t)) \right] = \Phi_m \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{2} \sin(\omega t) \right]$$

$$\boxed{\phi_x = \frac{3}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) = 1.5 \, \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)}$$

#### Step 4: Sum the Y-Components

The total vertical component of the resultant flux is:

$$\phi_y = \phi_{Ry} + \phi_{Yy} + \phi_{By} = 0 + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$

$$\phi_y = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Phi_m \left[ \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) \right]$$

Apply the identity $\sin A - \sin B = 2 \cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)$:

$$\sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) = 2 \cos\left(\frac{2\omega t - 360┬░}{2}\right) \sin\left(\frac{120┬░}{2}\right)$$

$$= 2 \cos(\omega t - 180┬░) \sin(60┬░) = 2 \cdot (-\cos\omega t) \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = -\sqrt{3} \cos(\omega t)$$

Substitute back:

$$\phi_y = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Phi_m \cdot \left( -\sqrt{3} \cos(\omega t) \right) = -\frac{3}{2} \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

$$\boxed{\phi_y = -1.5 \, \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)}$$

#### Step 5: Resultant Flux Magnitude

The total resultant flux vector is $\vec{\phi}_{\text{res}} = \phi_x \hat{i} + \phi_y \hat{j}$.

The magnitude:

$$\vert \vec{\phi}_{\text{res}} \vert = \sqrt{\phi_x^2 + \phi_y^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2} \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)\right)^2 + \left(\frac{3}{2} \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)\right)^2}$$

$$= \frac{3}{2} \Phi_m \sqrt{\sin^2(\omega t) + \cos^2(\omega t)} = \frac{3}{2} \Phi_m \sqrt{1}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \vec{\phi}_{\text{res}} \vert = 1.5 \, \Phi_m = \text{constant}}$$

**This proves that the magnitude of the resultant flux is constant at all instants.**

#### Step 6: Angular Position of the Resultant Flux

The instantaneous angle $\theta(t)$ that the resultant flux makes with the x-axis:

$$\theta(t) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\phi_y}{\phi_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-1.5 \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)}{1.5 \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(-\cot(\omega t)\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\tan(\omega t - 90┬░)\right)$$

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \omega t - 90┬░}$$

**This proves that the resultant flux rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s.**

#### Step 7: Final Conclusion

The resultant flux:
- Has **constant magnitude** $= 1.5 \, \Phi_m$.
- **Rotates** in space at **synchronous angular speed** $\omega_s = \omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s.
- The mechanical synchronous speed is $N_s = 120f / P$ RPM.
- The direction is determined by the phase sequence (R-Y-B or R-B-Y).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** We started with three *stationary, time-varying* fluxes and proved via orthogonal decomposition and vector addition that the resultant is a *rotating, constant-amplitude* flux. The factor 1.5 emerged naturally from the sum of three phasors separated by 120┬░.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Rotor EMF and Frequency

At standstill ($s = 1$), the relative speed between the RMF and the stationary rotor is exactly $N_s$. The rotor conductors therefore experience a **maximum rate of flux cutting**, and the induced EMF at standstill is:

$$E_{2,\text{standstill}} = 4.44 \cdot f_2 \cdot N_2 \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w$$

Where $k_w$ is the winding factor and $N_2$ is the number of turns per phase of the rotor (in the case of SRIM).

When the rotor runs at slip $s$, the relative speed is $s \cdot N_s$, and the induced EMF is reduced to:

$$E_2 = s \cdot E_{2,\text{standstill}}$$

The frequency of the rotor EMF is also proportional to slip:

$$f_r = s \cdot f$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö KTU Style

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor runs at 1440 RPM at full load. Calculate: (a) Synchronous speed, (b) Slip, (c) Frequency of rotor currents, (d) Relative speed between field and rotor.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Synchronous Speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{4}$$

$$\boxed{N_s = 1500 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Part (b): Slip**

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \frac{60}{1500}$$

$$\boxed{s = 0.04 \text{ p.u.} = 4\%}$$

**Part (c): Rotor Frequency**

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \cdot 50$$

$$\boxed{f_r = 2 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Part (d): Relative Speed**

$$N_{\text{rel}} = N_s - N_r = 1500 - 1440 = 60 \text{ RPM}$$

Or equivalently:

$$N_{\text{rel}} = s \cdot N_s = 0.04 \cdot 1500$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{rel}} = 60 \text{ RPM}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö RMF vs Stationary Field

| Property | Pulsating (Single-Phase) Field | Rotating (3-Phase) Field |
|---|---|---|
| Magnitude | Varies from 0 to $\Phi_m$ | Constant at $1.5 \Phi_m$ |
| Spatial motion | Stationary | Rotates at $\omega_s$ |
| Time variation | Sinusoidal | None (rotates) |
| Produces starting torque in IM? | **No** (needs auxiliary winding) | **Yes** (self-starting) |
| Magnitude of induced rotor EMF | Zero average (oscillates) | Constant amplitude |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Three-Phase Induction Motor Power Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the energy conversion from the AC mains to the mechanical shaft, with the losses at each stage. This is a **favorite KTU diagram** because it demonstrates the student's grasp of energy conversion and power stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three-Phase AC Mains 50 Hz 415 V] --> B[Stator Winding Copper Loss Pcu1]
    B --> C[Stator Core Iron Loss Pfe Hysteresis Eddy Current]
    C --> D[Air Gap Power Pag Mechanical Power Crossing the Gap]
    D --> E[Rotor Copper Loss Pcu2 s times Pag]
    D --> F[Mechanical Power Developed Pm 1 minus s times Pag]
    F --> G[Friction Windage Loss Pfw]
    F --> H[Stray Load Loss Pstray]
    G --> I[Shaft Output Power Pout to Mechanical Load]
    H --> I
    I --> J[EV Drivetrain Wheels via Transmission]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Note that $P_{\text{cu2}} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ and $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_{ag}$. This is why the induction motor is also called a **"slip-coupled"** machine ŌĆö the slip is directly proportional to the rotor copper loss. At normal operation ($s \approx 0.04$), only 4% of air-gap power is wasted as heat in the rotor ŌĆö extremely efficient!
