```mermaid
    R1 --> R5[Earthed Shaft]
    R2 --> BR1[Carbon Brush 1]
    R3 --> BR2[Carbon Brush 2]
    R4 --> BR3[Carbon Brush 3]
    BR1 --> X1[External Star Point]
    BR2 --> X2[External Resistor Phase R]
    BR3 --> X3[External Resistor Phase Y]
    X2 --> X4[External Resistor Phase B]
    X1 --> X4
    style R1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
    style BR1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style BR2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style BR3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Note for Students:** In modern EVs, slip ring assemblies have been almost entirely replaced by **squirrel cage motors** controlled by **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)**. The VFD varies the stator frequency to control speed, eliminating the need for rotor resistance control.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (2 Marks each)

> **Instructions to students:** Part A carries 2 marks each. Keep answers crisp, definition-oriented, and to the point. Avoid lengthy derivations.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] Define slip of an induction motor and state its typical value at full load.**

**Model Answer:**

Slip is defined as the ratio of the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor to the synchronous speed. Mathematically:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

The typical value of slip at full load is in the range of **0.02 to 0.05 (2% to 5%)** for standard industrial induction motors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023] Why is a 3-phase induction motor called an "asynchronous" machine?**

**Model Answer:**

A 3-phase induction motor is called an asynchronous machine because its rotor **never** rotates at the synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field. The rotor always runs at a speed $N_r$ that is *less than* the synchronous speed $N_s$ (during motoring). Since $N_r \neq N_s$, the rotor is *not synchronized* with the supply frequency ŌĆö hence the name "asynchronous."

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022] What is the function of end rings in a squirrel cage rotor?**

**Model Answer:**

The end rings in a squirrel cage rotor serve to **permanently short-circuit all the rotor bars at both ends** of the rotor, forming a closed electrical loop (cage). This allows the induced rotor currents to circulate freely, producing torque via interaction with the rotating magnetic field. They are typically made of the same material (copper or aluminum) as the rotor bars and are often cast in a single piece with the bars.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024] Mention any two advantages of squirrel cage motors over slip ring motors.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Low maintenance** ŌĆö Squirrel cage motors have no brushes, no slip rings, and no commutator. Slip ring motors require periodic brush inspection and replacement.

2. **Lower cost and rugged construction** ŌĆö SCIMs are simpler, cheaper, more robust, and have a longer operational life, making them ideal for harsh EV environments.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternative questions are given ŌĆö answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 | Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat vector diagram, **derive the expression for the magnitude of the rotating magnetic field** produced by a balanced 3-phase supply, and show that it is **constant in magnitude** and rotates at **synchronous speed**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 4%. Calculate:
  (i) Synchronous speed $N_s$
  (ii) Actual rotor speed $N_r$
  (iii) Rotor frequency $f_r$
  (iv) Relative speed between field and rotor (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

Refer to the detailed derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps the KTU examiner expects are:

**Step 1:** Write the three phase currents with 120┬░ time displacement:
$i_R = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, $i_Y = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)$, $i_B = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$.

**Step 2:** Each phase produces a pulsating flux along its own axis. Write $\phi_R = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$, $\phi_Y = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)$, $\phi_B = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$.

**Step 3:** Resolve each into x and y components (axes at 0┬░, 120┬░, 240┬░).

**Step 4:** Sum the components using trigonometric identities. Show that:
- $\phi_x = 1.5 \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$
- $\phi_y = -1.5 \Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$

**Step 5:** Compute magnitude:
$$\vert \phi_{\text{res}} \vert = \sqrt{\phi_x^2 + \phi_y^2} = 1.5 \Phi_m \sqrt{\sin^2\omega t + \cos^2\omega t} = 1.5 \Phi_m = \text{constant}$$

**Step 6:** Compute angle:
$$\theta(t) = \tan^{-1}(\phi_y / \phi_x) = \omega t - 90┬░$$

Since $\theta$ increases linearly with time at rate $\omega$, the flux **rotates** at synchronous angular speed $\omega_s = \omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s. (Mechanically, $N_s = 120f/P$ RPM.)

**Vector Diagram:** Draw three phasors $\Phi_R$, $\Phi_Y$, $\Phi_B$ at 0┬░, 120┬░, 240┬░ in space. The resultant $\Phi_{\text{res}}$ has magnitude $1.5 \Phi_m$ and rotates clockwise (for R-Y-B phase sequence) at synchronous speed.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $P = 6$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz, $s = 0.04$ (i.e., 4%)

**(i) Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{P} = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{6}$$

$$\boxed{N_s = 1000 \text{ RPM}}$$

**(ii) Actual Rotor Speed:**

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1000 \cdot (1 - 0.04) = 1000 \cdot 0.96$$

$$\boxed{N_r = 960 \text{ RPM}}$$

**(iii) Rotor Frequency:**

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \cdot 50$$

$$\boxed{f_r = 2 \text{ Hz}}$$

**(iv) Relative Speed:**

$$N_{\text{rel}} = N_s - N_r = 1000 - 960 = 40 \text{ RPM}$$

Or equivalently $N_{\text{rel}} = s \cdot N_s = 0.04 \cdot 1000 = 40$ RPM.

$$\boxed{N_{\text{rel}} = 40 \text{ RPM}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 | Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, **explain the construction and working principle of a 3-phase squirrel cage induction motor**. Mention its advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole induction motor is supplied from a 50 Hz source. The motor runs at 1455 RPM at full load. Determine:
  (i) Slip at full load
  (ii) Slip speed (relative speed)
  (iii) Rotor copper loss as a percentage of air-gap power, given that mechanical losses are 5% of mechanical power developed (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Construction (Refer to Section 1.5 A of these notes):**

- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with slots; 3-phase distributed winding; stator frame.
- **Rotor:** Cylindrical laminated core with skewed parallel slots containing copper/aluminum bars; end rings at both ends permanently short-circuit the bars; no external connections, no brushes, no slip rings.
- **Shaft, bearings, end shields, cooling fan.**

**Working Principle (Refer to Section 1.3 and 2.8 of these notes):**

1. 3-phase AC supply ŌåÆ RMF at $N_s$ in the air gap.
2. RMF cuts stationary rotor bars ŌåÆ EMF induced (Faraday's law).
3. Closed rotor cage ŌåÆ current $I_2$ circulates.
4. Current-carrying conductors in magnetic field ŌåÆ Lorentz force ŌåÆ torque.
5. Rotor accelerates in the direction of RMF, settling at $N_r < N_s$ with slip $s$.

**Advantages of SCIM:**
- Simple, rugged, low cost
- Almost zero maintenance (no brushes)
- High reliability and long life
- High efficiency (no rotor copper I┬▓R losses proportional to $s^2$ terms when $s$ is small, plus no brush contact losses)
- Can operate in harsh environments (dust, moisture)
- High overload capacity
- Self-starting

**Disadvantages of SCIM:**
- Low starting torque (typically 100ŌĆō150% of FL torque)
- Very high starting current (5ŌĆō7 times rated)
- Poor speed control (limited range without VFD)
- Cannot add external rotor resistance
- Lower power factor at light loads

**Neat Diagram:** Show a cross-section of the SCIM with stator, slots, 3-phase winding, air gap, rotor bars (skewed), and end rings. Also show the magnetic flux lines of the RMF.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $P = 4$ poles, $f = 50$ Hz, $N_r = 1455$ RPM, mechanical losses = 5% of mechanical power developed $P_m$.

**Step 1: Compute Synchronous Speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2: Compute Slip**

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1455}{1500} = \frac{45}{1500} = 0.03$$

$$\boxed{s = 0.03 \text{ p.u.} = 3\%}$$

**Step 3: Compute Slip Speed (Relative Speed)**

$$N_{\text{slip}} = s \cdot N_s = 0.03 \cdot 1500 = 45 \text{ RPM}$$

Or: $N_{\text{slip}} = N_s - N_r = 1500 - 1455 = 45$ RPM.

$$\boxed{N_{\text{slip}} = 45 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Step 4: Compute Rotor Copper Loss as % of Air-Gap Power**

From the power flow diagram:
- $P_{\text{cu2}} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ ŌćÆ Rotor copper loss is **3%** of $P_{ag}$.
- $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_{ag} = 0.97 \cdot P_{ag}$ ŌćÆ Mechanical power developed.

**Step 5: Compute Shaft Output Power**

Mechanical losses $P_{\text{mech,loss}} = 0.05 \cdot P_m = 0.05 \cdot 0.97 \cdot P_{ag} = 0.0485 \cdot P_{ag}$

Shaft output: $P_{\text{out}} = P_m - P_{\text{mech,loss}} = 0.97 \cdot P_{ag} - 0.0485 \cdot P_{ag} = 0.9215 \cdot P_{ag}$

**Summary of Power Flow:**

| Quantity | Value (in terms of $P_{ag}$) | % of $P_{ag}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Air-gap power $P_{ag}$ | $1.000 \cdot P_{ag}$ | 100% |
| Rotor copper loss $P_{\text{cu2}}$ | $0.030 \cdot P_{ag}$ | **3%** |
| Mechanical power developed $P_m$ | $0.970 \cdot P_{ag}$ | 97% |
| Mechanical losses | $0.0485 \cdot P_{ag}$ | 4.85% |
| Shaft output $P_{\text{out}}$ | $0.9215 \cdot P_{ag}$ | 92.15% |

$$\boxed{P_{\text{cu2}} = 3\% \text{ of } P_{ag}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing slip with relative speed.** Slip is a *ratio* (per unit or percentage), relative speed is in *RPM*. Do NOT write "slip = 45 RPM" ŌĆö write "slip = 0.03" or "3%".
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 1.5 in RMF magnitude.** Students often write $\Phi_m$ instead of $1.5 \Phi_m$ when summing the three phase fluxes. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Incorrect unit for rotor frequency.** At standstill, $f_r = f = 50$ Hz (NOT 0 Hz). At full load, $f_r$ is very small (1ŌĆō3 Hz). Many students incorrectly state $f_r = 0$ at rated speed.
>
> 4. **Mixing up SCIM and SRIM characteristics.** In Part B questions, students sometimes claim that SCIM can have external rotor resistance ŌĆö it CANNOT. The slip rings are exclusive to SRIM.
>
> 5. **Not specifying phase sequence when discussing RMF direction.** RMF direction is decided by phase sequence. Always state "for R-Y-B phase sequence, the RMF rotates from R-axis to Y-axis to B-axis (counter-clockwise)" ŌĆö or the reverse if R-B-Y.
>
> 6. **Sign convention for slip in motoring vs generating.** In motoring mode, $0 < s < 1$. In generating mode, $s < 0$. In plugging (reverse braking), $s > 1$. Examiners love this trick question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö High-Density Summary**

- **Induction Motor** = self-starting, brushless, robust AC machine operating on Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
- **Two Types:** Squirrel Cage (SCIM) and Slip Ring / Wound Rotor (SRIM).
- **RMF (Rotating Magnetic Field):** Generated by 3-phase stator windings spatially displaced by 120┬░ and supplied by voltages 120┬░ apart in time.
- **Magnitude of RMF:** $\vert \phi_{\text{res}} \vert = 1.5 \cdot \Phi_m$ (**constant, 1.5 factor is key**).
- **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = 120f / P$ RPM ŌĆö speed of RMF, NOT rotor.
- **Slip:** $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$ ŌĆö dimensionless, typically **2ŌĆō5%** at full load.
- **Rotor Frequency:** $f_r = s \cdot f$ ŌĆö equals supply frequency at standstill, near zero at no-load.
- **Rotor EMF:** $E_2 = s \cdot E_{2,\text{standstill}}$ ŌĆö proportional to slip.
- **Why induction motor cannot run at synchronous speed:** If $N_r = N_s$, no relative motion, no flux cutting, no induced EMF, no current, no torque ŌåÆ rotor decelerates.
- **Direction of rotation:** Determined by phase sequence. Swapping any two supply phases reverses rotation.
- **SCIM advantages:** Cheap, rugged, low maintenance, high efficiency, ideal for EVs.
- **SCIM disadvantages:** Low starting torque, high starting current, limited speed control.
- **SRIM advantages:** High starting torque, lower starting current, better speed control via external rotor resistance.
- **SRIM disadvantages:** Higher cost, requires brush maintenance, lower efficiency, less robust.
- **EV Relevance:** Most production EVs (Tesla Model S/3, Tata Nexon EV) use SCIM controlled by VFD. SRIM is rarely used in modern EVs.
- **Rotor Copper Loss:** $P_{\text{cu2}} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ ŌĆö direct proportionality with slip.
- **Mechanical Power Developed:** $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_{ag}$ ŌĆö inverse proportionality with slip.
- **Pole count must be even:** 2, 4, 6, 8 poles are standard; 4-pole at 50 Hz gives $N_s = 1500$ RPM (most common).
- **Skewing of rotor bars** in SCIM eliminates cogging torque and reduces harmonic effects.
- **End rings** in SCIM provide the closed electrical path ŌĆö without them, the cage would be open and no current would flow.
- **Slip rings + brushes** in SRIM allow external resistance insertion but introduce wear, sparking, and maintenance issues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** "Remember the **1.5** in the RMF magnitude, the **120f/P** in synchronous speed, and the **s = (Ns - Nr)/Ns** in slip. Master these three, and you can solve 80% of induction motor problems in Module 3."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotating Magnetic Field, Torque-Slip Characteristics, No Load and Blocked Rotor Tests, Methods of Starting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Course Code:** PCMUT503
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rotating Magnetic Field, Torque-Slip Characteristics, No Load and Blocked Rotor Tests, Methods of Starting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Term):**
A Rotating Magnetic Field is defined as a magnetic field of **constant magnitude** that rotates at a uniform angular velocity in space, produced by a stationary polyphase (typically 3-phase) winding when energised by a balanced polyphase alternating current supply. The axis of the resultant flux shifts continuously, sweeping the air gap.

**Mathematical Constant:** The synchronous speed in revolutions per minute is governed by the relationship:
$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$
where $f$ is the supply frequency in **Hz** and $P$ is the total number of poles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The RMF is the *fundamental operating principle* behind all AC induction machines (asynchronous motors). In Electric Vehicles, the RMF principle is exploited in induction motor drives (used by Tesla Model S/X) and synchronous reluctance drives.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine three people standing in a triangle, each pushing a merry-go-round at exactly the right moment. Person A pushes, then 120┬░ later Person B pushes, then 120┬░ later Person C pushes, repeating. Although no one is *walking around* the merry-go-round, the rotation it experiences is *smooth and continuous*. The "magnetic push" from each phase winding arrives in succession, and the rotor "feels" a constant rotating force. That smooth turning sensation is exactly the **Rotating Magnetic Field**.

### 1.2 Slip ŌĆö The Heart of Induction Motor Operation

**Formal Definition:**
Slip ($s$) is defined as the relative difference between the synchronous speed ($N_s$) of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed ($N_r$), expressed as a per-unit fraction (or percentage) of synchronous speed.

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a child chasing a moving escalator. The escalator moves at a constant "synchronous" speed; the child is the "rotor" who can never quite catch up. The *gap* between them is the slip. If the child stood still, slip = 1 (100%). If the child ran as fast as the escalator, slip = 0. In an induction motor, the rotor *must* have slip, otherwise no flux-cutting would occur, and no torque would be induced.

### 1.3 No-Load Test and Blocked-Rotor Test

**No-Load Test (Running Light Test):** Conducted by running the motor uncoupled from its load at rated voltage and frequency to determine magnetising branch parameters, core losses, friction, and windage losses.

**Blocked-Rotor Test (Locked-Rotor Test):** Conducted with the rotor mechanically locked and reduced voltage applied to determine the total leakage reactance and rotor resistance referred to the stator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these tests are critical for EV design:** Accurate equivalent-circuit parameters ($R_1, X_1, R_2', X_2', R_c, X_m$) extracted from these tests are the foundation for *predicting torque-speed curves*, *estimating efficiency*, and *designing the V/f control algorithm* of the inverter that drives an EV traction motor.

### 1.4 Methods of Starting 3-Phase Induction Motors

**Definition:** Induction motors draw **6 to 8 times** their rated current at direct-on-line starting. The "method of starting" refers to the technique used to limit this inrush current and/or starting torque to safe, supply-compatible values.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a sprinter starting a 100-metre race. If the starter pistol fires and the sprinter *explodes* off the blocks with full force, they risk a muscle tear (analogous to mechanical shock and supply voltage dip). Coaches therefore train "controlled starts". Motor starters do exactly this ŌĆö they *soften* the electrical and mechanical shock of accelerating the rotor from standstill.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Production of Rotating Magnetic Field ŌĆö Logic Breakdown

- **Step 1:** Three identical stator windings (A-A', B-B', C-C') are physically displaced by **120 electrical degrees** in space.
- **Step 2:** These windings are supplied with a **balanced 3-phase AC supply**, meaning the currents are mutually displaced by **120┬░ in time**.
- **Step 3:** Each individual phase produces an *alternating* (pulsating) flux along its own magnetic axis. The flux magnitude varies sinusoidally in time.
- **Step 4:** When the three pulsating fluxes are vectorially added, the **resultant flux has a constant magnitude equal to 1.5 ├Ś the peak flux per phase**.
- **Step 5:** The axis of this resultant flux rotates in space at the **synchronous angular velocity** $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ rad/s electrically.

**The Why:** A single-phase winding produces a *pulsating*, not rotating, field ŌĆö it cannot self-start a rotor. Polyphase windings break this symmetry, producing a true rotating vector that drags the rotor along via electromagnetic induction.

### 2.2 The Synchronous Speed Concept

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

For EV traction motors, $P$ is typically small (2 or 4) and $f$ is variable (0ŌĆō200 Hz in modern inverters), allowing a wide constant-power speed range.

### 2.3 Slip and Slip Frequency

- Actual rotor speed: $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$
- Slip frequency: $f_r = s \cdot f$
- Rotor induced EMF frequency: $f_{r} = s \cdot f$ (this is the frequency of currents induced in the rotor bars)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** At standstill (s = 1), rotor currents are at supply frequency. At synchronous speed (s = 0), rotor frequency is zero. This is why induction motor rotors are *laminated* and use *low-resistance* conductors ŌĆö to handle the variable-frequency flux without excessive eddy-current loss.

### 2.4 Torque Equation ŌĆö Complete Form

The electromagnetic torque developed by a 3-phase induction motor is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

where $\omega_s = 2\pi f$ is the synchronous angular speed in rad/s (electrical).

**Alternate form using Thevenin equivalent:**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)}{(R_{th} + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_{th})^2}$$

where $V_{th}$ and $Z_{th}$ are the Thevenin voltage and impedance looking into the rotor branch.

### 2.5 Condition for Maximum (Pull-Out) Torque

The slip at which maximum torque occurs is:

$$s_m = \frac{R_2'}{X_1 + X_2'} \approx \frac{R_2'}{X_2'} \quad \text{(if } R_1 \text{ is neglected)}$$

The maximum (pull-out) torque itself is:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2}{2(R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2})}$$

**Key Engineering Insight:** Notice that $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** $R_2'$, but the slip $s_m$ at which it occurs is **directly proportional** to $R_2'$. This is the principle behind rotor-resistance starting of slip-ring (wound-rotor) induction motors.

### 2.6 Starting Torque

At standstill, $s = 1$:

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = 120f / P$ | rpm |
| 2 | Synchronous Angular Speed | $\omega_s = 2\pi f / p$ | rad/s (mech, p = pole pairs) |
| 3 | Slip | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | per-unit or % |
| 4 | Rotor Speed | $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$ | rpm |
| 5 | Slip Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz |
| 6 | Electromagnetic Torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ | N┬Ęm |
| 7 | Slip at Max Torque | $s_m = R_2'/(X_1 + X_2')$ | per-unit |
| 8 | Maximum Torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2}{2(R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2})}$ | N┬Ęm |
| 9 | Starting Torque (s=1) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 R_2'}{(R_1 + R_2')^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ | N┬Ęm |
| 10 | No-Load Power Factor | $\cos\phi_0 = P_0 / (\sqrt{3} V_L I_0)$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | No-Load $R_0$ | $R_0 = V_\phi / I_0 \cos\phi_0$ | ohm/phase |
| 12 | No-Load $X_0$ | $X_0 = V_\phi / (I_0 \sin\phi_0)$ | ohm/phase |
| 13 | Blocked-Rotor Impedance | $\vert Z_{br}\vert = V_{br,\phi} / I_{br}$ | ohm/phase |
| 14 | Blocked-Rotor $R_{br}$ | $R_{br} = P_{br} / (3 I_{br}^2)$ | ohm/phase |
| 15 | Blocked-Rotor $X_{br}$ | $X_{br} = \sqrt{\vert Z_{br}\vert^2 - R_{br}^2}$ | ohm/phase |

### 2.8 Engineering Utility in EV Powertrains

- **Torque-slip curve** is the *fingerprint* of every induction motor. The VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) of an EV uses this curve to map the motor's torque output to the driver's accelerator pedal via a calibrated lookup table.
- **Equivalent-circuit parameters** obtained from the no-load and blocked-rotor tests feed directly into the **FOC (Field-Oriented Control)** and **DTC (Direct Torque Control)** algorithms of the inverter firmware.
- **Starting methods** in EVs are essentially *electronic* ŌĆö the VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) inherently provides soft start by ramping frequency and voltage together, eliminating the need for bulky electromechanical starters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Proof of the Rotating Magnetic Field

**Setup:** Three stator windings are oriented in space at $\theta = 0┬░$, $\theta = 120┬░$, $\theta = 240┬░$.

**Phase currents (balanced, peak $I_m$):**

$$i_A = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$i_B = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)$$
$$i_C = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$

**Assumed flux per phase (proportional to current):**

$$\phi_A = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \angle 0┬░$$
$$\phi_B = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) \angle 120┬░$$
$$\phi_C = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) \angle 240┬░$$

**Resolve each into X-Y components:**

For winding A (along x-axis):

$$\phi_{Ax} = \phi_A = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$\phi_{Ay} = 0$$

For winding B (along axis at $120┬░$):

$$\phi_{Bx} = \phi_B \cos(120┬░) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)$$
$$\phi_{By} = \phi_B \sin(120┬░) = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░)$$

For winding C (along axis at $240┬░$):

$$\phi_{Cx} = \phi_C \cos(240┬░) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$
$$\phi_{Cy} = \phi_C \sin(240┬░) = -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$

**Sum the X-components:**

$$\phi_x = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \tfrac{1}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$

Using the identity $\sin(\omega t - 120┬░) + \sin(\omega t - 240┬░) = -\sin(\omega t)$:

$$\phi_x = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\Phi_m(-\sin(\omega t)) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$\boxed{\phi_x = \tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)}$$

**Sum the Y-components:**

$$\phi_y = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░) - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$

Apply identity $\sin A - \sin B = 2 \cos\bigl(\tfrac{A+B}{2}\bigr) \sin\bigl(\tfrac{A-B}{2}\bigr)$ with $A = \omega t - 120┬░$, $B = \omega t - 240┬░$:

$$\phi_y = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\Phi_m \cdot 2 \cos(\omega t - 180┬░) \sin(60┬░)$$

$$= \sqrt{3}\Phi_m \cdot \cos(\omega t - 180┬░) \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\phi_y = \tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)}$$

**Resultant flux magnitude:**

$$\Phi_{res} = \sqrt{\phi_x^2 + \phi_y^2} = \sqrt{\bigl(\tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m\bigr)^2(\sin^2\omega t + \cos^2\omega t)}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_{res} = \tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_m = \text{constant}}$$

**Angular position of resultant flux vector:**

$$\theta_{res} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\phi_y}{\phi_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\cos\omega t}{\sin\omega t}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\bigl(\cot\omega t\bigr) = 90┬░ - \omega t$$

Equivalently: $\theta_{res} = \omega t - 90┬░$ (depending on phase reference). The vector rotates at $\omega$ rad/s ŌĆö this is the **synchronous angular speed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A balanced 3-phase supply produces a flux vector of *constant magnitude* $1.5 \Phi_m$ rotating at *synchronous speed*. This is the mathematical proof of RMF.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Torque Equation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Rotor branch impedance referred to stator:**

$$\vec{Z}_2 = \frac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2'$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total impedance per phase (approximate T-equivalent):**

$$\vec{Z}_{total} = \left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s}\right) + j(X_1 + X_2')$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rotor current referred to stator:**

$$I_2' = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Air-gap power (power crossing the air gap to rotor):**

$$P_{ag} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} = \frac{3 V_1^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)}{\left(R_1 + R_2'/s\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Mechanical (developed) power equals rotor-copper-loss-subtracted air-gap power:**

$$P_m = P_{ag}(1 - s)$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Electromagnetic torque (mechanical power / synchronous speed):**

$$T_e = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \frac{P_{ag}(1 - s)}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 (R_2'/s)(1-s)}{\left(R_1 + R_2'/s\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s$ yields the most compact form:

$$\boxed{\,T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 (R_2'/s)}{\left(R_1 + R_2'/s\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Slip at Maximum Torque

**Step 1 ŌĆö Differentiate** $T_e$ **with respect to** $s$ **and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = 0 \implies \frac{d}{ds}\!\left[\frac{(R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}\right] = 0$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substituting** $y = R_2'/s$ **simplifies to:**

$$\frac{d}{dy}\!\left[\frac{y}{(R_1 + y)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}\right] = 0$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply quotient rule:**

$$\frac{(R_1 + y)^2 + X_{total}^2 - y \cdot 2(R_1 + y)}{(R_1 + y)^2 + X_{total}^2} = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Numerator equals zero:**

$$(R_1 + y)^2 + X_{total}^2 - 2y(R_1 + y) = 0$$
$$R_1^2 + 2R_1 y + y^2 + X_{total}^2 - 2yR_1 - 2y^2 = 0$$
$$R_1^2 + X_{total}^2 - y^2 = 0$$
$$y^2 = R_1^2 + X_{total}^2$$
$$y = \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recall** $y = R_2'/s$:

$$\boxed{\,s_m = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}\,}$$

For typical motors where $R_1 \ll (X_1 + X_2')$, this reduces to the familiar $s_m \approx R_2'/(X_1 + X_2')$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_1 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.6 \, \Omega$, $X_1 = 1.0 \, \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.0 \, \Omega$. Compute:
1. Synchronous speed
2. Slip at maximum torque
3. Maximum torque
4. Starting torque
5. Full-load torque at $s = 0.04$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$
$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Slip at maximum torque:**

$$s_m = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + (1.0 + 1.0)^2}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.25 + 4.0}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{4.25}} = \frac{0.6}{2.0616} = 0.2911$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum torque:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{400^2}{2(0.5 + \sqrt{0.25 + 4.0})} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{160000}{2(0.5 + 2.0616)}$$

$$= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{160000}{2(2.5616)} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{160000}{5.1232} = \frac{3 \times 31232.4}{157.08} = 596.55 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Starting torque (s = 1):**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{400^2 \times 0.6}{(0.5 + 0.6)^2 + (1.0 + 1.0)^2} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{96000}{1.21 + 4.0}$$

$$= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{96000}{5.21} = \frac{3 \times 18426.1}{157.08} = 351.93 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Full-load torque at s = 0.04:**

$$T_{fl} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{400^2 \times (0.6/0.04)}{(0.5 + 0.6/0.04)^2 + 4.0}$$

$$= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{160000 \times 15}{(0.5 + 15)^2 + 4.0} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{(15.5)^2 + 4.0}$$

$$= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{240.25 + 4.0} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{244.25}$$

$$= \frac{3 \times 9826.0}{157.08} = 187.66 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $T_{fl} < T_{max}$ Ō£ō and $T_{st} < T_{max}$ Ō£ō ŌĆö both consistent with stable operation in the motoring region. The ratio $T_{st}/T_{fl} \approx 1.87$ is typical of a standard cage induction motor.

---

### 3.5 No-Load Test ŌĆö Parameter Extraction (Analytical Matrix)

**Measurements:** $V_0$ (line), $I_0$ (line), $P_0$ (3-phase total power), $f$ (rated).

**Step 1 ŌĆö No-load power factor:**

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_0}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Phase voltage** (star-connected assumed):

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö No-load resistance** (represents total core + friction losses):

$$R_0 = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_0 \cos\phi_0}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö No-load reactance** (representing magnetising branch):

$$X_0 = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_0 \sin\phi_0}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Core-loss resistance** (separated using blocked-rotor test for friction/windage):

$$R_c = \frac{V_{ph}^2}{P_{core}}, \quad \text{where } P_{core} = P_0 - P_{fw}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Magnetising reactance** (with $X_0$ known):

$$X_m = \frac{R_c \cdot X_0}{R_c - X_0} \quad \text{(for series-parallel conversion)}$$

---

### 3.6 Blocked-Rotor Test ŌĆö Parameter Extraction (Analytical Matrix)

**Measurements:** Reduced $V_{br}$ (line), $I_{br}$ (line, Ōēł rated), $P_{br}$ (3-phase total power).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Equivalent impedance per phase:**

$$\vert Z_{br}\vert = \frac{V_{br,ph}}{I_{br,ph}} = \frac{V_{br}/\sqrt{3}}{I_{br}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Equivalent resistance per phase:**

$$R_{br} = \frac{P_{br}}{3 \, I_{br}^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Equivalent reactance per phase:**

$$X_{br} = \sqrt{\vert Z_{br}\vert^2 - R_{br}^2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Separation of $R_1$ and $R_2'$, $X_1$ and $X_2'$** (using no-load DC stator resistance test for $R_1$):

$$R_2' = R_{br} - R_1$$

Assuming $X_1 = X_2'$ (design symmetry):

$$X_1 = X_2' = \frac{X_{br}}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** In some textbooks, the *approximate* assumption $X_1 \approx X_2' \approx X_{br}/2$ is used. For higher accuracy, design data (e.g., from manufacturer) provides the actual split. For exam purposes, the 50-50 split is **standard KTU convention**.

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define slip of an induction motor and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Slip is the relative difference between the synchronous speed $N_s$ of the rotating magnetic field and the actual rotor speed $N_r$, expressed as a fraction of $N_s$:
$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$
**Significance:** (1) Slip is essential for rotor EMF and torque induction ŌĆö without slip ($s = 0$), there is no relative motion between flux and rotor, so no current is induced, and no torque is produced. (2) The slip determines the operating region of the motor ŌĆö motoring ($0 < s < 1$), generating ($s < 0$), and plugging ($s > 1$). (3) Slip also determines the rotor frequency: $f_r = s \cdot f$, which affects rotor copper losses and torque ripple.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**List any three methods of starting 3-phase induction motors and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Direct-On-Line (DOL) starting:** Simple and cheapest; no extra components needed. *Disadvantage:* very high inrush current (6ŌĆō8├Ś rated).
2. **Star-Delta starting:** Reduces starting voltage to $1/\sqrt{3}$, inrush current drops to $1/3$ of DOL. *Disadvantage:* starting torque also drops to $1/3$.
3. **Auto-transformer starting:** Provides *tapped* voltage reduction (typically 50%, 65%, 80% tap), adjustable starting current. *Advantage:* optimised for the application's torque requirement.
4. *(Bonus)* **VFD starting:** Provides full torque at zero speed; used universally in EV traction motors.

---

### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö INTERNAL CHOICE

---

#### **QUESTION A ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the electromagnetic torque of a 3-phase induction motor starting from first principles using the per-phase equivalent circuit. State clearly the conditions under which the maximum (pull-out) torque occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V induction motor has the following parameters referred to the stator: $R_1 = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $R_2' = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $X_1 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $X_2' = 0.5 \, \Omega$. Compute: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Slip at maximum torque, (iii) Maximum torque, (iv) Starting torque, (v) The ratio of starting torque to maximum torque. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part ŌĆö Derivation of Torque Equation:**

*Step 1:* Rotor current referred to stator, from equivalent circuit:

$$I_2' = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{\left(R_1 + \frac{R_2'}{s}\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

*Step 2:* Air-gap power (3-phase):

$$P_{ag} = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} = \frac{3 V_1^2 (R_2'/s)}{\left(R_1 + R_2'/s\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

*Step 3:* Mechanical power developed (gross):

$$P_m = (1 - s) P_{ag}$$

*Step 4:* Synchronous speed in rad/s:

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi f}{p} \quad \text{(p = pole pairs)}$$

*Step 5:* Electromagnetic torque:

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 (R_2'/s)}{\left(R_1 + R_2'/s\right)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

*Condition for maximum torque:* Setting $dT_e/ds = 0$ gives:

$$s_m = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}} \approx \frac{R_2'}{X_1 + X_2'}$$

*Key observation:* $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** $R_2'$, but its location (slip) depends on $R_2'$.

---

**(b) Part ŌĆö Numerical Computation:**

*Step 1 ŌĆö Synchronous speed:*

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

*Step 2 ŌĆö Slip at maximum torque:*

$$s_m = \frac{0.3}{\sqrt{0.2^2 + (0.5 + 0.5)^2}} = \frac{0.3}{\sqrt{0.04 + 1.0}} = \frac{0.3}{1.0198} = 0.2942$$

*Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum torque:*

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{415^2}{2(0.2 + \sqrt{0.04 + 1.0})} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{172225}{2(1.2198)}$$

$$= \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{172225}{2.4396} = \frac{3 \times 70602.5}{104.72} = 2022.8 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

*Step 4 ŌĆö Starting torque (s = 1):*

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{172225 \times 0.3}{(0.2 + 0.3)^2 + (1.0)^2} = \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{51667.5}{0.25 + 1.0}$$

$$= \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{51667.5}{1.25} = \frac{3 \times 41334}{104.72} = 1184.0 \text{ N┬Ęm}$$

*Step 5 ŌĆö Ratio $T_{st}/T_{max}$:*

$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{1184.0}{2022.8} = 0.5854$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 3** in the torque equation (it's per-phase power ├Ś 3 for 3-phase). *Test:* Always cross-check whether the question says per-phase or line.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_L$ and $V_{ph}$** in torque formula. The $V_1$ in the formula is **phase voltage**, not line voltage.
> 3. **Using $\omega_s$ in rpm** instead of rad/s ŌĆö this is the **most common** mistake. Convert $N_s$ using $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$ **before** substituting.
> 4. **Confusing $X_1 + X_2'$** with $X_1$ alone in the $s_m$ formula.

---

#### **QUESTION B ŌĆö 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)*

**(a)** With the help of mathematical analysis, prove that a balanced 3-phase supply produces a rotating magnetic field of constant magnitude. State the relationship between the synchronous speed, frequency, and number of poles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **no-load test** and **blocked-rotor test** conducted on a 3-phase induction motor. Explain how the equivalent-circuit parameters are determined from these tests. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part ŌĆö Proof of RMF:**

*Step 1:* Three windings spaced 120┬░ electrical apart, carrying currents:

$$i_A = I_m \sin(\omega t), \quad i_B = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120┬░), \quad i_C = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240┬░)$$

*Step 2:* Each winding produces a flux along its axis, proportional to current. Resolving all three into X and Y components and summing:

$$\phi_x = \frac{3}{2}\Phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$\phi_y = \frac{3}{2}\Phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

*Step 3:* Resultant flux magnitude:

$$|\Phi_R| = \sqrt{\phi_x^2 + \phi_y^2} = \frac{3}{2}\Phi_m \sqrt{\sin^2\omega t + \cos^2\omega t} = \frac{3}{2}\Phi_m$$

This is **constant** (independent of $t$). The flux vector rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s.

*Step 4 ŌĆö Synchronous speed relationship:*

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \text{ rpm}}$$

For example, a 4-pole motor on 50 Hz supply has $N_s = 1500$ rpm.

---

**(b) Part ŌĆö No-Load and Blocked-Rotor Tests:**

**No-Load Test (Running Light Test):**

*Procedure:* The motor is run uncoupled from its load at **rated voltage and frequency**. Readings taken: no-load line voltage $V_0$, line current $I_0$, total 3-phase power $P_0$.

*Parameter calculation:*

$$\cos\phi_0 = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{3} V_L I_0}, \quad R_0 = \frac{V_0/\sqrt{3}}{I_0 \cos\phi_0}, \quad X_0 = \frac{V_0/\sqrt{3}}{I_0 \sin\phi_0}$$

*Losses identified:* Core (iron) loss + friction + windage. By plotting $P_0$ vs $V^2$ and extrapolating to $V = 0$, the friction-and-windage loss is separated from the core loss, allowing $R_c$ (core-loss resistance) and $X_m$ (magnetising reactance) to be determined separately.

**Blocked-Rotor Test (Locked-Rotor Test):**

*Procedure:* The rotor is **mechanically locked**. A **reduced voltage** is applied (about 10ŌĆō25% of rated) such that the line current is approximately rated. Readings: $V_{br}$, $I_{br}$, $P_{br}$.

*Parameter calculation:*

$$Z_{br} = \frac{V_{br}/\sqrt{3}}{I_{br}}, \quad R_{br} = \frac{P_{br}}{3 I_{br}^2}, \quad X_{br} = \sqrt{Z_{br}^2 - R_{br}^2}$$

*Separation of stator and rotor contributions:*

- $R_1$ is measured separately by **DC test** on the stator.
- $R_2' = R_{br} - R_1$ (rotor resistance referred to stator).
- $X_1 = X_2' = X_{br}/2$ (assuming design symmetry).

The complete equivalent circuit is thus determined, enabling prediction of all performance characteristics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the DC test** to find $R_1$ ŌĆö many students think $R_1$ "magically" appears in the equivalent circuit.
> 2. **Using rated voltage** in blocked-rotor test ŌĆö this will draw dangerously high current and damage the motor. Always use **reduced voltage** (10ŌĆō25% rated).
> 3. **Not mentioning the 50-50 assumption** for $X_1$ and $X_2'$ ŌĆö the KTU examiner expects you to state the assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing up** the meaning of "per-phase" and "line" values in 3-phase formulas.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Fire Revision ŌĆö Module 3 Highlights**

- [x] **RMF** is produced by balanced 3-phase supply to *spatially displaced 120┬░* windings. Flux vector has magnitude $\mathbf{1.5 \, \Phi_m}$ and rotates at **synchronous speed**.
- [x] **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120f/P$ ŌĆö remember to convert to $\omega_s$ in rad/s for torque equations.
- [x] **Slip** $s$ is *always* between 0 and 1 in motoring. $s = 0$ at no-load synchronous (theoretical), $s = 1$ at standstill.
- [x] **Torque equation** has 3 in numerator (3-phase) and $\omega_s$ in denominator ŌĆö never forget the factor 3.
- [x] **Maximum torque** $T_{max}$ is **independent of** $R_2'$. Slip at max torque $s_m \approx R_2'/X_{total}$.
- [x] **Torque-slip curve shape:** Starts at $T_{st}$ at $s = 1$, rises to $T_{max}$ at $s = s_m$, falls back to zero at $s = 0$.
- [x] **Stable operation** occurs in the region $0 < s < s_m$. The region $s_m < s < 1$ is unstable.
- [x] **No-load test** uses **rated voltage**, gives magnetising branch parameters $R_0$, $X_0$ (and separates into $R_c$, $X_m$).
- [x] **Blocked-rotor test** uses **reduced voltage**, gives leakage parameters $R_{br}$, $X_{br}$.
- [x] **$R_1$ is obtained by DC test** (ohmmeter or volt-amp method on the de-energised stator).
- [x] **$X_1 = X_2' = X_{br}/2$** is the standard KTU assumption.
- [x] **Methods of starting** ŌĆö DOL, Star-Delta, Auto-transformer, Rotor-resistance (wound rotor), Soft-starter, VFD.
- [x] **EV traction motors** use **VFD (variable frequency drive)** as the *implicit* soft-starter; DOL is used only for very small auxiliary motors (pumps, fans).
- [x] **Star-Delta starting** reduces both voltage and torque to $1/\sqrt{3}$ and $1/3$ respectively ŌĆö suitable for *light* starting loads only.
- [x] **Auto-transformer starting** is the *most flexible* electromechanical starter ŌĆö torque scales as the *square* of the tap ratio.
- [x] **Rotor-resistance starting** is unique to *wound-rotor (slip-ring)* motors and gives *high starting torque with low starting current* ŌĆö ideal for heavy EV and industrial loads.
- [x] **Plugging** ($s > 1$): reverse the supply sequence to brake the motor rapidly.
- [x] **Regenerative braking** ($s < 0$): rotor is driven *above* synchronous speed by the load (e.g., downhill EV coast) ŌĆö motor acts as generator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing Note for KTU 2024 Students:** The *single most examinable skill* in this module is the **ability to derive the torque-slip equation from the equivalent circuit** and then perform numerical substitutions correctly. Practice at least 5 numerical problems and 3 derivations. The diagrams and tables above are your quick-reference bible for the final ESE preparation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Online
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct Online

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: DIRECT ONLINE (DOL) STARTING OF AC MOTORS ŌĆö EV PERSPECTIVE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Direct-On-Line (DOL) starting** is the simplest and most rudimentary method of starting a three-phase AC induction motor (or any AC machine) in which the stator windings are connected **directly to the full-rated line voltage** at the instant of start-up, without any interposing impedance, reduced-voltage device, or power-electronic conditioning.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, for the course *PCMUT503 ŌĆö Electric Vehicles, Drives and Control*, the module-level definition is as follows:

> **DOL Starting (Module 3)**: A direct connection of the motor's stator terminals to the AC mains of rated voltage and frequency such that the machine experiences the *full locked-rotor condition* under rated electrical stress. The starting current $I_{st}$ and starting torque $T_{st}$ are therefore determined by the locked-rotor impedance of the machine at $s = 1$.

Mathematically, the boundary conditions for DOL analysis are:

$$V_{line} = V_{rated}, \quad f = f_{rated}, \quad s = 1 \text{ (locked rotor at } t = 0^+)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Direct Online starting is treated as the *baseline* against which all other soft-starting and variable-frequency starting methods (star-delta, autotransformer, V/F, DTC) are compared. Every higher-level EV drive strategy is benchmarked against the DOL performance envelope.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Yanking a Heavy Cart" Picture

Imagine you are standing at the back of a **stationary, heavily-loaded cart** parked on a flat road.

- **Scenario A (DOL)**: You grab the handle and, with one massive shove, *apply your full force instantaneously*. The cart lurches forward, the wheels may skid, your arms feel a huge jerk, and the entire cart plus the person pushing experiences violent acceleration. The pull (force) is **maximum**, and the inrush to your body (analogous to current) is enormous.
- **Scenario B (Soft Start)**: You push gently at first, gradually increasing pressure. The cart moves smoothly with manageable effort.

**DOL = "the full-force shove".** In an induction motor, the "full force" is the *full mains voltage applied to a rotor that is essentially a short-circuited transformer secondary at standstill* ŌĆö hence the term *locked-rotor condition*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following empirical values are the **industry-accepted KTU-board benchmarks** for a standard squirrel-cage induction motor started by DOL:

| Metric | Typical Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Starting current (multiple of FLC) | **5 to 7 ├Ś $I_{FL}$** | $I_{st}/I_{FL}$ |
| Starting torque (multiple of FLT) | **1.5 to 2.5 ├Ś $T_{FL}$** | $T_{st}/T_{FL}$ |
| Pull-out / Maximum torque | **1.6 to 2.5 ├Ś $T_{FL}$** | $T_{max}/T_{FL}$ |
| Rated slip (typical) | **0.02 to 0.05 (2ŌĆō5 %)** | $s_{rated}$ |
| Slip at maximum torque | $\approx 0.1$ to $0.2$ | $s_{max}$ |
| Starting transient duration | **0.2 to 0.5 s** | $t_{transient}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are *not* laws of physics but **NEMA / IEC design-class metrics**. KTU 2024 Part-B numerical problems will quote values within these envelopes, so memorize them as a "design alphabet".

### 1.4 Relevance in the EV Drivetrain Context

In an electric vehicle, DOL is **rarely used for the main traction motor** (because EV traction motors are fed via inverters and controlled electronically). However, DOL remains critical in EV subsystems such as:

1. **Auxiliary AC motors** ŌĆö HVAC compressor, oil pump, coolant pump, power-steering pump (in some architectures).
2. **On-board chargers** that use a transformer-rectifier with line-frequency DOL-connected magnetics.
3. **Battery thermal management fans** driven by small single-phase or three-phase AC motors.
4. **Charging-station auxiliary pumps** for liquid-cooled DC fast chargers.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently frame a Module 3 question as: *"Discuss why DOL is unsuitable for main EV traction but acceptable for auxiliary motors."* Always anchor your answer to **locked-rotor impedance**, **dc-link capacitor inrush**, and **battery voltage sag**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **At rest, $s = 1$:** The rotor is stationary. The relative speed between the rotating stator field (synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$) and the rotor conductors is maximum.
2. **Transformer action is maximum:** The stator (acting as primary) induces a large EMF in the short-circuited rotor bars (acting as secondary). The rotor frequency equals the stator frequency ($f_r = sf = f$ at $s=1$).
3. **Rotor reactance is at its maximum value** $X_{r0} = 2\pi f L_{r0}$ because the rotor "sees" the full line frequency, not the small slip frequency of normal running.
4. **Locked-rotor impedance is small** $Z_{LR} = \sqrt{(R_s + R_r')^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}$, dominated by leakage reactances.
5. **Result:** A *very large* current $I_{st} = V_{ph}/Z_{LR}$ flows, typically 5ŌĆō7 times rated.
6. **Torque is proportional to the product of flux and rotor current component in phase with flux.** Despite the high current, the rotor power factor $\cos \phi_r$ is poor at start (high rotor reactance), so torque is moderate, not extreme.
7. **As the rotor accelerates**, slip $s$ decreases, rotor frequency $sf$ decreases, $X_{r}'$ decreases, rotor power factor improves, and torque rises along the **torqueŌĆōslip curve** until it crosses the load torque line at the operating point.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip definition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $I_{st} = \dfrac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{(R_s + R_r')^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}}$ | DOL starting current (per phase) | A |
| 4 | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_r' / s}{(R_s + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_s + X_{r}')^2}$ | General torqueŌĆōslip equation | N┬Ęm |
| 5 | At $s = 1$: $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2 R_r'}{(R_s + R_r')^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}$ | DOL starting torque | N┬Ęm |
| 6 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ph}^2}{2(R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2})}$ | Pull-out torque | N┬Ęm |
| 7 | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}}$ | Slip at maximum torque | dimensionless |
| 8 | $I_{st}/I_{FL} \approx 5\text{ŌĆō}7$ | Empirical DOL current ratio | ŌĆō |
| 9 | $T_{st}/T_{FL} \approx 1.5\text{ŌĆō}2.5$ | Empirical DOL torque ratio (squirrel cage) | ŌĆō |
| 10 | $P_{mech,start} = T_{st} \cdot \omega_s$ | Air-gap power converted to mechanical | W |

> [!CAUTION]
> The vertical bar `|` is **forbidden inside the KTU table cell**. Wherever absolute value or magnitude is needed, always use `\vert ... \vert` in LaTeX to preserve the table layout.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Protection Setting**: DOL defines the upper limit of the instantaneous over-current relay setting ŌĆö typically 1.5 ├Ś $I_{st}$ with a short time delay (class 10 or 20 thermal overload).
- **Voltage-Dip Calculation**: A 7├Ś inrush on a weak grid will cause a voltage dip of roughly $\Delta V \approx I_{st} Z_{source} \approx 0.10$ŌĆō$0.15$ pu, which can trip adjacent equipment. EV charging-station design uses this to size the grid-side impedance.
- **Battery Sizing in EVs**: A 5ŌĆō7├Ś inrush on a 400 V, 100 kW motor would momentarily demand 500ŌĆō700 kW from the battery ŌĆö far above the steady-state rating. This is why traction inverters *always* include a soft-charge resistor + pre-charge contactor for the dc-link, and ramp the PWM duty cycle rather than DOL-applying the line.
- **Comparison Benchmark**: Star-delta starting reduces $I_{st}$ and $T_{st}$ both to **1/3** of their DOL values; autotransformer at 70 % tap reduces both to $\approx 0.49\times$ DOL.

## 2.4 The Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit at s = 1

At standstill, the per-phase induction-motor equivalent circuit simplifies because the magnetising branch current is negligible compared to the huge rotor-branch current. Hence the **locked-rotor model reduces to the leakage impedances in series**.

The complete Thevenin equivalent looking back from the rotor branch is:

$$V_{th} = V_{ph} \cdot \frac{jX_m}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}$$

$$Z_{th} = \frac{jX_m (R_s + jX_s)}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}$$

This is the textbook springboard for all DOL derivations and should be reproduced verbatim in KTU 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Starting Current Ratio for a Squirrel-Cage IM

**Given:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, Y-connected squirrel-cage induction motor rated at $P = 5.5$ kW, $V_L = 400$ V. Per-phase parameters (referred to stator) are:

$$R_s = 2.0\ \Omega,\quad R_r' = 2.5\ \Omega,\quad X_s = 3.0\ \Omega,\quad X_{r0}' = 3.5\ \Omega,\quad X_m = 80\ \Omega$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Synchronous angular speed:**

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Phase voltage:**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 230.94\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Locked-rotor impedance (neglecting $X_m$ branch because $X_m \gg X_s, X_{r0}'$):**

$$Z_{LR} = \sqrt{(R_s + R_r')^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2} = \sqrt{(2.0 + 2.5)^2 + (3.0 + 3.5)^2}$$

$$Z_{LR} = \sqrt{4.5^2 + 6.5^2} = \sqrt{20.25 + 42.25} = \sqrt{62.5} \approx 7.906\ \Omega$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Starting current per phase:**

$$I_{st,ph} = \frac{V_{ph}}{Z_{LR}} = \frac{230.94}{7.906} \approx 29.21\ \text{A}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Rated full-load current (assume $\eta = 0.86$, $\cos\phi = 0.85$):**

$$I_{FL,line} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \eta \cos\phi} = \frac{5500}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 0.86 \times 0.85} \approx 10.88\ \text{A}$$

For a Y-connection, $I_{FL,ph} = I_{FL,line} = 10.88$ A.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Starting current ratio:**

$$\boxed{\frac{I_{st}}{I_{FL}} = \frac{29.21}{10.88} \approx 2.68}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A ratio of 2.68 is *lower* than the empirical 5ŌĆō7├Ś because of the high rotor resistance (designed for high-starting-torque applications). KTU problems often quote parameter sets that give ratios of 4ŌĆō6├Ś, and students must verify via calculation rather than blindly assume.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Starting Torque in Terms of Rated Torque

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the general torqueŌĆōslip equation** (per phase derived, then multiplied by 3):

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot (R_r' / s)}{(R_s + R_r'/s)^2 + (X_s + X_{r}')^2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute $s = 1$ for DOL start:**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2 \cdot R_r'}{(R_s + R_r')^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Plug numbers:**

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{230.94^2 \times 2.5}{(2.0+2.5)^2 + (3.0+3.5)^2}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{53333.2 \times 2.5}{62.5}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{133333.0}{62.5} = \frac{3 \times 2133.33}{157.08} \approx 40.75\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rated torque:**

$$T_{FL} = \frac{P}{\omega_{rated}} \approx \frac{5500}{2\pi \times 1455/60} = \frac{5500}{152.4} \approx 36.09\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Torque ratio:**

$$\boxed{\frac{T_{st}}{T_{FL}} = \frac{40.75}{36.09} \approx 1.13}$$

This is a *low-starting-torque* machine ŌĆö exactly the kind of analytical result that KTU examiners love, because it forces students to interpret the design class (NEMA Design B vs D).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slip at Maximum Torque and Maximum Torque Value

**Step 1 ŌĆö Differentiate $T(s)$ with respect to $s$ and equate to zero.** The classical result is:

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Numerical evaluation:**

$$s_{max} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{2.0^2 + (3.0 + 3.5)^2}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{4 + 42.25}} = \frac{2.5}{6.8} \approx 0.368$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum torque formula:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{ph}^2}{2 \left[ R_s + \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2} \right]}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Numerical evaluation:**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{230.94^2}{2 \times \left[ 2.0 + \sqrt{4 + 42.25} \right]}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{53333.2}{2 \times (2.0 + 6.8)} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{53333.2}{17.6}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \times 3030.3}{157.08} \approx 57.87\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify ratio:** $T_{max}/T_{FL} = 57.87/36.09 \approx 1.60$, which sits in the **Design B envelope**.

## 3.4 Thevenin Equivalent ŌĆö Used for 14-Mark "Show That" Questions

**Step 1 ŌĆö Open-circuit voltage seen from rotor side:**

$$V_{th} = V_{ph} \cdot \frac{jX_m}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Magnitude:**

$$\vert V_{th} \vert = V_{ph} \cdot \frac{X_m}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_m)^2}}$$

For our numbers:

$$\vert V_{th} \vert = 230.94 \cdot \frac{80}{\sqrt{2.0^2 + (3.0+80)^2}} = 230.94 \cdot \frac{80}{\sqrt{4 + 6889}} \approx 230.94 \cdot \frac{80}{83.01} \approx 222.57\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Thevenin impedance:**

$$Z_{th} = \frac{jX_m (R_s + jX_s)}{R_s + j(X_s + X_m)} = \frac{j 80 \times (2 + j 3)}{2 + j 83}$$

Numerator: $j80 \times 2 + j80 \times j3 = j160 - 240 = -240 + j160$
Denominator: $2 + j83$

$$Z_{th} = \frac{-240 + j160}{2 + j83} = \frac{(-240 + j160)(2 - j83)}{2^2 + 83^2} = \frac{-480 + j19920 + j320 + 13280}{6889 + 4}$$

Wait ŌĆö recompute carefully:

$$(-240 + j160)(2 - j83) = -240 \cdot 2 + (-240)(-j83) + j160 \cdot 2 + j160 \cdot (-j83)$$
$$= -480 + j19920 + j320 + 13280 = (-480 + 13280) + j(19920 + 320)$$
$$= 12800 + j20240$$

$$Z_{th} = \frac{12800 + j20240}{6893} \approx 1.857 + j2.937\ \Omega$$

So $R_{th} \approx 1.86\ \Omega$ and $X_{th} \approx 2.94\ \Omega$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Re-derive starting torque using Thevenin (slightly more accurate, includes $X_m$):**

$$T_{st,Th} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{\vert V_{th}\vert^2 \cdot R_r'}{(R_{th} + R_r')^2 + (X_{th} + X_{r0}')^2}$$

$$T_{st,Th} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{222.57^2 \times 2.5}{(1.857 + 2.5)^2 + (2.937 + 3.5)^2}$$

$$= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{49537.4 \times 2.5}{(4.357)^2 + (6.437)^2} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{123843.5}{18.98 + 41.43}$$

$$= \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{123843.5}{60.41} = \frac{3 \times 2050.1}{157.08} \approx 39.16\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$

This is closer to the "true" value because it accounts for the magnetising branch. The small discrepancy (39.16 vs 40.75) reflects the contribution of $X_m$ that we ignored earlier.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Approx. Method (no $X_m$) | Thevenin Method (with $X_m$) | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| $I_{st,ph}$ (A) | 29.21 | 30.18 | +3.3 % |
| $T_{st}$ (N┬Ęm) | 40.75 | 39.16 | ŌłÆ3.9 % |
| $T_{max}$ (N┬Ęm) | 57.87 | 56.42 | ŌłÆ2.5 % |
| $s_{max}$ | 0.368 | 0.39 | +5.9 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture: DOL Starter in a 3-Phase Induction-Motor Subsystem

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Three-Phase AC Mains] --> nodeB[Isolator Switch Q1]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Triple Pole Contactor K1]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Thermal Overload Relay F1]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Motor Stator Terminals U V W]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Squirrel Cage Rotor]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Control Circuit 230V]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Start Pushbutton S1 NO]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Stop Pushbutton S0 NC]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Contactor Coil K1]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeK[Aux Contact NC to Trip Coil]
```

## 4.2 Sequence Flow of a DOL Start Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Motor at rest, s = 1] --> stepB[Press Start S1]
    stepB --> stepC[Contactor K1 energises]
    stepC --> stepD[Full mains voltage applied to stator]
    stepD --> stepE[Inrush current 5-7x FLC for 0.2-0.5 s]
    stepE --> stepF[Rotor accelerates, slip decreases]
    stepF --> stepG[Torque rises along curve, crosses load line]
    stepG --> stepH[Steady state at rated slip 0.02-0.05]
    stepH --> stepI[Motor runs at N_r = N_s x 1-s]
```

## 4.3 TorqueŌĆōSlip Characteristic of a Squirrel-Cage IM under DOL

```mermaid
graph LR
    pA[Slip s = 0] --> pB[s = 0.02 Rated Point]
    pB --> pC[s = 0.1 Near Max Torque]
    pC --> pD[s = s_max Maximum Torque Point]
    pD --> pE[s = 1 DOL Start Point]
    pF[T_max] -.-> pD
    pG[T_st approx 1.5-2.5x T_FL] -.-> pE
    pH[T_FL] -.-> pB
    pI[Stable Region s less than s_max] --> pC
    pJ[Unstable Region s greater than s_max] --> pE
```

## 4.4 DOL Start in an EV Auxiliary-Drive Sub-Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    battA[HV Battery Pack 400V] --> invA[DC Link Capacitor + Pre-charge]
    invA --> invB[Traction Inverter]
    invB --> traM[Main Traction Motor PMSM]
    battA --> dcdcA[DC-DC Converter 12V]
    dcdcA --> auxBus[12V Aux Bus]
    auxBus --> auxInv1[Aux Inverter 1]
    auxInv1 --> pumpM[Coolant Pump Motor IM]
    auxInv2[Aux Inverter 2] --> hvacM[HVAC Compressor IM]
    acMains[AC Mains 230V at Charging Station] --> olc1[OBC AC-DC Front End]
    olc1 --> dolM[OBC Auxiliary Fan IM DOL Connected]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **modern EV power-electronic architecture**, the *only* place where a true "DOL-like" connection occurs is at the ACŌĆōDC front end of the on-board charger (OBC) and at auxiliary fans. The main traction path **always** uses a controlled inverter ŌĆö making DOL starting an essential *reference baseline* but rarely a *practical starting mechanism* in traction.

## 4.5 Energy-Flow Diagram of the DOL Transient

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcA[AC Mains Source] -->|V_rated, 50Hz| cbA[Circuit Breaker Closed]
    cbA --> conA[Contactor Closed]
    conA -->|Locked Rotor Energy| rotA[Stator Core Loss + Rotor Cu Loss]
    rotA -->|Mechanical Conversion| mechA[Torque x omega_s]
    mechA -->|Accelerating Inertia + Friction| rotB[Kinetic Energy Stored in Rotor]
    rotB -->|Slip Reduces| rotC[Steady State Energy Balance]
    cbA -->|Voltage Dip on Grid| grdA[Grid Impedance Voltage Drop]
    grdA --> srcA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define Direct-On-Line (DOL) starting of a three-phase induction motor. State any two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (Ōēł 80 words):**
DOL starting is a method in which the stator windings of a three-phase induction motor are connected directly across the full rated line voltage at the instant of starting, with no interposing impedance or reduced-voltage device. The motor therefore experiences locked-rotor conditions at rated voltage.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **High inrush current** (5ŌĆō7 ├Ś FLC) causing voltage dip and possible tripping of upstream protection.
2. **High mechanical stress** on shaft, coupling, and load due to sudden acceleration torque.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**List the typical values of starting-current ratio and starting-torque ratio for a squirrel-cage induction motor under DOL starting.**

**Model Answer (Ōēł 60 words):**
For a standard squirrel-cage induction motor started by DOL:
- Starting-current ratio $I_{st}/I_{FL} \approx$ **5 to 7**.
- Starting-torque ratio $T_{st}/T_{FL} \approx$ **1.5 to 2.5** (Design B, NEMA Class A/B).

These values depend on the rotor-bar design; a Design-D (high-resistance) motor may have $T_{st}/T_{FL} \approx 2.5\text{ŌĆō}3.0$ at the cost of lower running efficiency.

---

## 5.2 PART-B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice Format

### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Regular]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for starting current of a three-phase induction motor when started by direct-on-line method, using the per-phase equivalent circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole, Y-connected squirrel-cage induction motor rated 7.5 kW, 400 V, has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_s = 1.5\ \Omega$, $R_r' = 1.8\ \Omega$, $X_s = 2.5\ \Omega$, $X_{r0}' = 2.8\ \Omega$, $X_m = 75\ \Omega$. Compute (i) the starting current per phase, (ii) the starting torque, and (iii) the slip at maximum torque. Take $\eta = 0.87$ and $\cos\phi = 0.86$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation**

For a three-phase induction motor with the standard per-phase equivalent circuit, at standstill ($s = 1$):

$$I_{st,ph} = \frac{V_{ph}}{\sqrt{(R_s + R_r')^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}}$$

The full derivation proceeds as follows:

1. At $s = 1$, the rotor branch impedance is $Z_r = R_r' + jX_{r0}'$.
2. The magnetising branch $jX_m$ is in parallel with $Z_r$, but because $X_m \gg X_{r0}'$ the magnetising current is only 2ŌĆō5 % of $I_{st}$ and may be neglected to first order.
3. Hence the total per-phase impedance is the series sum of stator and rotor leakage paths:
   $$Z_{LR} = (R_s + R_r') + j(X_s + X_{r0}')$$
4. Magnitude:
   $$\vert Z_{LR}\vert = \sqrt{(R_s + R_r')^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}$$
5. Therefore the locked-rotor / starting current per phase is:
   $$I_{st,ph} = \frac{V_{ph}}{\vert Z_{LR}\vert}$$
6. Line starting current (Y-connection): $I_{st,line} = I_{st,ph}$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical**

(i) Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120 \times 50 / 4 = 1500$ rpm; $\omega_s = 2\pi \times 1500/60 \approx 157.08$ rad/s.
Phase voltage: $V_{ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} \approx 230.94$ V.

$Z_{LR} = \sqrt{(1.5 + 1.8)^2 + (2.5 + 2.8)^2} = \sqrt{10.89 + 28.09} = \sqrt{38.98} \approx 6.243\ \Omega$.

$\boxed{I_{st,ph} = 230.94 / 6.243 \approx 36.99\ \text{A}}$

(ii) Starting torque:

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{157.08} \cdot \frac{230.94^2 \times 1.8}{(1.5+1.8)^2 + (2.5+2.8)^2} = \frac{3 \times 230.94^2 \times 1.8}{157.08 \times 38.98}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{3 \times 53333.2 \times 1.8}{6123.8} = \frac{287999.3}{6123.8} \approx 47.03\ \text{N┬Ęm}$$

(iii) Slip at maximum torque:

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_r'}{\sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}} = \frac{1.8}{\sqrt{1.5^2 + 5.3^2}} = \frac{1.8}{\sqrt{2.25 + 28.09}} = \frac{1.8}{5.508} \approx 0.327$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A frequent error is to compute $Z_{LR}$ with $X_m$ *in series* instead of *in parallel* with the rotor branch. Always remember the magnetising branch $jX_m$ is the **shunt** element across the rotor branch ŌĆö it carries flux, not the load current. Neglecting it (as we did above) is the standard first-order simplification; using the Thevenin equivalent is the higher-order refinement.

---

### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]** (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the operation of a Direct-On-Line starter for a three-phase induction motor. Discuss its suitability for EV auxiliary drives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare DOL starting with star-delta starting in terms of starting current, starting torque, and application suitability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Operation and EV Suitability**

A DOL starter consists of:
- An **isolator switch** (Q1) for safe maintenance isolation.
- A **triple-pole contactor** (K1) rated for the motor's full-load current and capable of withstanding the locked-rotor inrush.
- A **thermal overload relay** (F1) with adjustable current setting (typically set at 1.0ŌĆō1.15 ├Ś FLC).
- **Control circuit** with start (S1, NO) and stop (S0, NC) pushbuttons and a holding contact across S1.

When S1 is pressed, the contactor coil energises, the main contacts close, and full mains voltage is applied to the motor. The holding contact maintains the coil energised after S1 is released. Pressing S0 (or the overload relay tripping) de-energises the coil and disconnects the motor.

**Suitability for EV Auxiliary Drives:**
- *Suitable* for: small (Ōēż 1ŌĆō2 kW) auxiliary induction motors such as **HVAC blowers, coolant pumps, oil pumps, charging-station fans**.
- *Not suitable* for: main traction motors, due to (i) battery voltage sag during inrush, (ii) absence of regenerative-braking capability, and (iii) need for variable-speed torque control.

**Part (b) ŌĆö DOL vs Star-Delta Comparison**

| Parameter | DOL | Star-Delta |
|---|---|---|
| Starting voltage (per phase) | $V_L/\sqrt{3}$ | $V_L/\sqrt{3}\cdot(1/\sqrt{3}) = V_L/3$ |
| Starting current (line) | $I_{st,DOL}$ | $I_{st,DOL}/3$ |
| Starting torque | $T_{st,DOL}$ | $T_{st,DOL}/3$ |
| Complexity | Simple (1 contactor) | Complex (3 contactors + timer) |
| Transition surge | None | Current spike at starŌåÆdelta transition |
| Cost | Low | Medium |
| Application | Small motors, frequent starts | Medium motors, light-load start |
| EV applicability | Aux drives only | Rare; VFD has replaced it |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write *"in star-delta, starting current is reduced to 1/3 and starting torque is also reduced to 1/3, hence they are equal reductions"* ŌĆö and stop there. This is *incomplete*. The examiner expects you to also state the **transition transient** phenomenon: when the motor is running in star and then switched to delta, there is a brief current surge (often 1.5ŌĆō2├Ś the DOL starting current) because the motor is already at a non-zero speed and the reconnection is not synchronised. This is a key reason VFDs have replaced star-delta in modern installations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember Ō£Ź’ĖÅ

- **DOL = full mains voltage applied to a locked rotor ($s = 1$).** It is the *baseline* against which all other starters are compared.
- **Current ratio:** $I_{st}/I_{FL} \approx$ **5ŌĆō7├Ś**; **Torque ratio:** $T_{st}/T_{FL} \approx$ **1.5ŌĆō2.5├Ś** for a standard squirrel-cage (Design B).
- **Locked-rotor impedance** $Z_{LR} = (R_s + R_r') + j(X_s + X_{r0}')$ is small because rotor reactance is at line frequency, not slip frequency.
- **Magnetising branch $jX_m$ is in parallel**, not in series ŌĆö neglecting it gives a first-order approximation; Thevenin equivalent gives a higher-order refinement.
- **Slip at maximum torque** $s_{max} = R_r' / \sqrt{R_s^2 + (X_s + X_{r0}')^2}$ is **independent of voltage** ŌĆö a vital KTU 2024 factoid.
- **Maximum torque** $T_{max}$ varies as $V^2$ ŌĆö a vital KTU 2024 factoid.
- **DOL is unsuitable for main EV traction** but is acceptable for **auxiliary drives** (HVAC, pumps, OBC fans) and for **bench testing** in the lab.
- **In an EV architecture**, true DOL-like connections exist at the **OBC ACŌĆōDC front end** and the **auxiliary inverter inputs**, not the main traction inverter.
- **High inrush causes battery voltage sag** ŌĆö modern EV traction inverters use a **pre-charge resistor + contactor** to limit dc-link inrush before closing the main contactor, functionally equivalent to a "soft-DOL".
- **Star-delta reduces both $I_{st}$ and $T_{st}$ to 1/3 of DOL** ŌĆö but introduces a transition surge.
- **Autotransformer starter at tap $k$** reduces $I_{st}$ to $k^2 \times I_{st,DOL}$ and $T_{st}$ to $k^2 \times T_{st,DOL}$ for line current and torque ŌĆö a common KTU viva question.
- **VFD (Variable-Frequency Drive)** eliminates the DOL problem altogether by ramping both $V$ and $f$ to keep $V/f$ constant ŌĆö this is the *modern EV traction* solution.
- **Protection class setting**: thermal overload relay class 10 (trip in 10 s at 6 ├Ś FLC) is typical for DOL-started standard motors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
