> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Auto Transformer Starting, Speed Control of Induction Motors - V/f Control, FOC Control, Field Weakening Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Auto-Transformer Starting of Induction Motors

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Auto-Transformer Starting** is an *indirect* reduced-voltage starting method for three-phase squirrel-cage induction motors in which a tapped **single-winding autotransformer** is inserted between the supply mains and the stator terminals during start-up. The motor draws a **reduced voltage** proportional to the tap ratio $K = N_2 / N_1$, thereby reducing the **locked-rotor (starting) current** by a factor of $K^2$ and the **starting torque** by a factor of $K^2$ as well.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Pressure Reducer" Tap
Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline (the 400 V mains) feeding a household tap. If you connect a fire hose directly, the tap gushes uncontrollably and may burst. You install a **pressure-reducer valve** between the line and the tap, and during start-up the tap receives only, say, **60% pressure**. The motor "sees" a softer supply, so its inrush current shrinks, the rotor accelerates gently, and after a few seconds the bypass valve (K1/K2 contactors) cuts the reducer out, feeding the motor with full pressure for steady running. In EVs and heavy industrial drives, this same philosophy protects the battery pack and DC-link capacitors from inrush surges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** Autotransformer starting is preferred over star-delta starting when **(a)** the starting torque required is more than 33% of full-load torque, or **(b)** the motor rating is above **5 kW** with a stiff supply.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Standard Tapping Voltages:** $40\%$, $60\%$, $75\%$ (or $50\%$, $65\%$, $80\%$) of line voltage.
- **Typical Tap Ratios ($K$):** $0.4$, $0.6$, $0.75$.
- **Line Frequency:** **$50 \text{ Hz}$** (India, KTU region).
- **Locked-Rotor Current Multiplier:** Reduction factor $= K^2$.
- **Locked-Rotor Torque Multiplier:** Reduction factor $= K^2$.

---

## 1.2 V/f (Volts-per-Hertz) Control of Induction Motors

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **V/f Control**, also called *scalar control* or *constant volts-per-hertz control*, is an open-loop speed-control technique for induction motors in which the **stator voltage amplitude is varied in direct proportion to the stator supply frequency** so that the **air-gap flux $\phi$ is maintained approximately constant** over the operating range from zero to base speed. The defining relationship is the **V/f law**: $\dfrac{V_s}{f_s} = \text{constant}$.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Cruise Control of Magnetic Pull"
Think of the magnetic field in the air gap as a stretched rubber band. The *voltage* pulls it apart (magnetising force) and the *frequency* shakes it back. If you increase the shaking (frequency) without also strengthening the pull (voltage), the rubber band becomes slack ŌĆö flux collapses, and the motor loses torque. The V/f law is like an **automatic gain controller** that turns up the volume (voltage) exactly in step with the tempo (frequency), so the "rubber band tension" ŌĆö i.e. the air-gap flux ŌĆö never changes. In an EV, this is what gives smooth pedal-response from crawl speed to highway cruising without jerky torque dips.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** V/f control is the **industry workhorse** for fan, pump, HVAC, and low-performance EV auxiliary drives because it is simple, sensorless, and inverter-friendly. However, it has **slow dynamic response** and **no inherent torque loop** ŌĆö these limitations motivate FOC.

### Physical Constants
- **Base Frequency ($f_{base}$):** typically **$50 \text{ Hz}$** in India.
- **Base Voltage ($V_{base}$):** line-to-line RMS rating of the motor.
- **Boost Voltage ($V_{boost}$):** an extra voltage (3ŌĆō10% of $V_{base}$) added at very low frequencies to compensate for stator **$I_s R_s$** drop.
- **Slip Range for Linear Torque:** $s = 0$ to $s_{max} \approx 0.05$.

---

## 1.3 Field-Oriented Control (FOC) / Vector Control

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)**, also known as **Vector Control** (and trademarked by Siemens as *Transvektor* and by Rockwell as *Force Technology*), is a high-performance variable-frequency drive control method in which the three-phase stator currents ($i_a, i_b, i_c$) are mathematically transformed into a **two-axis rotating reference frame** aligned with the rotor flux vector, so that they appear as two independent **DC quantities**: the **direct-axis current $i_d$** (which controls flux) and the **quadrature-axis current $i_q$** (which controls torque). This emulates the **decoupled torqueŌĆōflux control** of a separately-excited DC motor.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Compass and the Treadmill"
Imagine you are walking on a treadmill (the rotor's magnetic field), and a friend (the stator) is walking beside you, throwing a ball (current) at you. If the friend doesn't know *which direction* you are facing, the ball just glances off ŌĆö wasteful, and you can't predict where it goes. But if the friend has a **compass** that always points to your chest (the flux axis), she can throw the ball either **forward into your chest** (the $i_d$ "flux" component) or **sideways at your hands** (the $i_q$ "torque" component), independently. The treadmill rotates, but the *relative direction* is fixed. That is what the **Park transform** does: it "un-rotates" the rotor reference frame so the stator sees a stationary target. After that, controlling torque becomes as easy as adjusting a DC motor's field and armature currents separately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** FOC is the **de-facto standard for traction motors in modern EVs** (Tesla Model S/3, Nissan Leaf, BMW i3, Tata Nexon EV) because it provides **(i)** fast torque response ($\sim 1$ŌĆō$2 \text{ ms}$), **(ii)** full torque at zero speed, and **(iii)** efficient field-weakening for high-speed cruising.

### Standard Physical Constants
- **Switching Frequency ($f_{sw}$):** typically **$5$ŌĆō$20 \text{ kHz}$** for EV traction inverters.
- **DC-Link Voltage ($V_{dc}$):** **$400 \text{ V}$** (standard) or **$800 \text{ V}$** (modern 800-V architectures like Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP).
- **Bandwidth of Current Loop:** **$1$ŌĆō$2 \text{ kHz}$**; Speed Loop: **$50$ŌĆō$100 \text{ Hz}$**.

---

## 1.4 Field Weakening Control

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Field Weakening Control** is the operating regime of an induction motor (or any AC machine) in which the rotor flux is **deliberately reduced below its rated value** so that the motor can rotate at speeds **above base speed**, while remaining within the **voltage and current limits of the inverter**. The motor transitions from the **constant-torque region** (below base speed) into the **constant-power region** (above base speed).

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Gear Shift on a Bicycle"
Picture climbing a hill in first gear ŌĆö high torque, low speed. As you reach the top, you shift to a higher gear: you are now spinning faster but pushing less hard. Field weakening is the **electrical equivalent of this gear shift**. You "let some air out" of the magnetic field (reduce flux), so for the same inverter voltage you can spin the rotor faster. The trade-off: torque drops inversely with speed, but **power stays approximately constant** ŌĆö perfect for an EV cruising on the highway at 120 km/h.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Field weakening is essential for EVs to extend the **constant-power speed range (CPSR)** from base speed (typically $4{,}000$ŌĆō$6{,}000 \text{ rpm}$) up to maximum speed (typically $12{,}000$ŌĆō$20{,}000 \text{ rpm}$) without oversizing the inverter.

### Standard Metrics
- **Base Speed ($N_{base}$):** speed at rated voltage, rated frequency, rated flux.
- **Maximum Speed ($N_{max}$):** typically **$2\times$ to $3\times$ $N_{base}$**.
- **Field-Weakening Ratio ($\lambda$):** $\lambda = \dfrac{\phi}{\phi_{rated}}$, with $0 < \lambda \leq 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Auto-Transformer Starting ŌĆö Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 Circuit Operation (Three-Step Sequence)
1. **Initial state** ŌĆö Contactors **K1 (line)** and **K3 (tap)** are **OPEN**, **K2 (run)** is **OPEN**. Motor is isolated.
2. **Starting state** ŌĆö K1 closes, connecting the line to the **primary of the autotransformer**. The **tapped secondary** (e.g., at 60%) is connected to the motor via K3. The motor sees a *reduced voltage* $V_{motor} = K \cdot V_{line}$, where $K$ is the tap ratio. The **line current** drawn is $K^2$ times smaller than the direct-on-line (DOL) starting current, but the **motor terminal current** is only $K$ times smaller.
3. **Running state** ŌĆö Once the motor has accelerated to ~70ŌĆō80% of full speed, K3 opens (disconnecting the transformer tap) and K2 closes (bypassing the transformer), applying full line voltage to the motor. K1 may remain closed, or it too opens if a separate run contactor is used.

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind the Math
- **Voltage reduction:** An autotransformer with turns ratio $K = N_2/N_1 < 1$ produces a secondary voltage $V_2 = K \cdot V_1$. The motor is connected to the secondary, so it sees a lower voltage.
- **Current reduction:** Because the motor's impedance at start is essentially the **leakage reactance $X_{lr}$**, the locked-rotor current is approximately $I_{LR} = V_{motor}/Z_{LR} = K \cdot V_{line}/Z_{LR}$. The line (primary) current is **reflected** through the turns ratio: $I_{line} = K \cdot I_{LR} = K^2 \cdot (V_{line}/Z_{LR})$.
- **Torque reduction:** Since $T \propto V^2$ for a given slip, $T_{start} = K^2 \cdot T_{DOL}$.

### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility
- Used in **industrial compressors, conveyors, crushers, mixers** and large **HVAC chillers**.
- In EV charging stations, autotransformers are used for **stepping the grid voltage** to feed the ACŌĆōDC charger.
- Modern soft-starters have largely replaced electromechanical autotransformer starters, but the **principle** of tap-changing remains in solid-state form (thyristor phase-angle control).

---

## 2.2 V/f Control ŌĆö Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Derivation of the V/f Law
The **induced EMF per phase** of an induction motor stator is given by Faraday's law:

$$E_s = 4.44 \, f_s \, N_s \, \phi_m \, k_w$$

where $f_s$ is the stator frequency, $N_s$ is the number of turns per phase, $\phi_m$ is the air-gap flux per pole, and $k_w$ is the winding factor (typically $0.95$ŌĆō$0.98$ for distributed windings). For a given motor, $N_s$ and $k_w$ are fixed, so:

$$\phi_m \;\propto\; \dfrac{E_s}{f_s}$$

If we approximate $E_s \approx V_s$ (neglecting the stator impedance drop), then **flux is proportional to V/f**. To keep $\phi_m$ constant, we must keep $V_s/f_s$ constant ŌĆö the **V/f law**.

### 2.2.2 The V/f Profile (Three Regions)

| Region | Frequency Range | Voltage Range | Flux $\phi_m$ | Torque Capability | Power Capability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-speed boost** | $0$ to $f_{min}$ ($\approx 3$ Hz) | $V_{boost}$ to $V_{min}$ | Constant (with boost) | Constant | Variable (ŌłØ $f$) |
| **Constant-flux** | $f_{min}$ to $f_{base}$ | $V_{min}$ to $V_{base}$ | Constant | Constant | Variable (ŌłØ $f$) |
| **Field-weakening** | $f_{base}$ to $f_{max}$ | Constant at $V_{base}$ | Decreasing ($\propto 1/f$) | Decreasing ($\propto 1/f^2$) | Constant |

### 2.2.3 TorqueŌĆōSpeed Characteristic
The air-gap torque of an induction motor is:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r/s)}{(R_s + R_r/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$$

Under V/f control, $V_s = k \cdot f_s$ (where $k = V_{base}/f_{base}$), so $V_s^2 = k^2 f_s^2$. Also, $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / p$, so $V_s^2/\omega_s \propto f_s$. The torque expression in the **linear region** (small slip) becomes:

$$T_e \approx 3 p \, k^2 \, f_s \cdot \dfrac{s}{R_r}$$

The slip $s$ at which maximum torque occurs decreases with frequency. However, the **maximum torque itself remains constant** because:

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 p \, V_s^2}{4 \pi f_s (X_s + X_r)} = \dfrac{3 p \, k^2 f_s}{4 \pi (X_s + X_r)} \cdot f_s = \text{constant} \cdot \text{const.}$$

Wait ŌĆö let me correct: $T_{max} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{p}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2}{2 (X_s + X_r)} = \dfrac{3 p V_s^2}{4 \pi f_s (X_s + X_r)} = \dfrac{3 p k^2 f_s^2}{4 \pi f_s (X_s + X_r)} = \text{constant}$.

So with V/f control, the **maximum torque is constant** across the frequency range ŌĆö this is the *constant-torque region*.

---

## 2.3 FOC ŌĆö Operational Logic

### 2.3.1 The Three Coordinate Transforms
FOC requires three mathematical transforms to move between reference frames:

1. **abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓ (Clarke Transform):** Converts balanced three-phase quantities into a two-axis stationary orthogonal system.
2. **╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq (Park Transform):** Rotates the stationary frame by the rotor flux angle $\theta_e$ to align with the rotor flux.
3. **dq ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓ (Inverse Park):** Used for the inverse modulator path.
4. **╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ abc (Inverse Clarke):** Converts the two-axis modulating signals back into three-phase PWM references.

### 2.3.2 Why Decoupling Works
In the **d-q frame aligned with rotor flux**, the induction motor equations reduce to DC-like forms:

- **d-axis (flux control):** $v_{ds} = R_s i_{ds} + \sigma L_s \dfrac{di_{ds}}{dt} - \omega_e \sigma L_s i_{qs}$
- **q-axis (torque control):** $v_{qs} = R_s i_{qs} + \sigma L_s \dfrac{di_{qs}}{dt} + \omega_e \sigma L_s i_{ds} + \omega_e \dfrac{L_m^2}{L_r} i_{mr}$

And the **electromagnetic torque** simplifies to:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \dfrac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_{mr} \cdot i_{qs}$$

Since the rotor flux is controlled by $i_{ds}$ (and $i_{mr} = \dfrac{L_m}{1 + \tau_r s} i_{ds}$), torque is **linearly proportional to $i_{qs}$** ŌĆö exactly like a DC motor. The cross-coupling terms ($-\omega_e \sigma L_s i_{qs}$ and $+\omega_e \sigma L_s i_{ds}$) are decoupled using *feed-forward decoupling voltages* in modern DSP/FPGA implementations.

### 2.3.3 Real-World Utility
- **EV traction drives:** Tesla, Nissan Leaf, BMW i3, Hyundai Ioniq 5.
- **Industrial servo drives** and CNC spindles.
- **Wind turbine generators** (indirect FOC for grid-side control).
- **Electric ship propulsion.**

---

## 2.4 Field Weakening ŌĆö Operational Logic

### 2.4.1 Why Field Weakening Is Necessary
Above base speed, the back-EMF $E_s = 4.44 f_s N_s \phi_m k_w$ grows linearly with $f_s$. But the **maximum voltage** the inverter can deliver is limited by the **DC-link voltage** $V_{dc}$ (and PWM strategy). For sinusoidal PWM, the peak phase voltage is at most $V_{dc}/2$, so the RMS line-to-neutral voltage is bounded by $V_{dc}/(2\sqrt{2})$. As $f_s$ increases, $V_s$ cannot keep up ŌĆö so $\phi_m$ must be reduced:

$$\phi_m = \dfrac{V_{s,max}}{4.44 f_s N_s k_w} \;\propto\; \dfrac{1}{f_s}$$

### 2.4.2 The Voltage and Current Limits
Two simultaneous constraints apply:

1. **Voltage limit (circle in the d-q plane):** $v_{ds}^2 + v_{qs}^2 \le V_{max}^2$
2. **Current limit (circle in the d-q plane):** $i_{ds}^2 + i_{qs}^2 \le I_{max}^2$

The **optimal field-weakening trajectory** keeps the operating point on the intersection of these two circles ŌĆö a strategy known as **"maximum torque per ampere (MTPA) in field weakening"** or **"voltage-limited flux trajectory."**

### 2.4.3 Constant-Power Speed Range (CPSR)
The ratio of maximum speed to base speed defines the CPSR:

$$\text{CPSR} = \dfrac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_{base}}$$

For a typical EV, CPSR $= 3$ to $5$. Modern 800-V inverters with silicon-carbide (SiC) MOSFETs can achieve CPSR $\approx 4$ŌĆō$5$ for the same motor.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Auto-transformer tap ratio | $K = N_2/N_1$ | $N_1$ = primary turns, $N_2$ = secondary turns | dimensionless |
| 2 | Reduced starting voltage | $V_{motor} = K \cdot V_{line}$ | $K < 1$ | V |
| 3 | Line starting current ratio | $I_{line,start} = K^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$ | Line current vs DOL | A |
| 4 | Motor starting current ratio | $I_{motor,start} = K \cdot I_{DOL}$ | Motor terminal current | A |
| 5 | Starting torque ratio | $T_{start} = K^2 \cdot T_{DOL}$ | vs DOL start | N┬Ęm |
| 6 | V/f law | $V_s / f_s = V_{base} / f_{base} = k$ | Constant flux coefficient | V/Hz |
| 7 | Air-gap EMF | $E_s = 4.44 \, f_s N_s \phi_m k_w$ | Faraday's law for AC machines | V |
| 8 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120 f_s / p$ | $p$ = number of poles | rpm |
| 9 | Slip | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | Rotor slip | dimensionless |
| 10 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 (R_r/s)}{(R_s + R_r/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2}$ | Full equivalent-circuit torque | N┬Ęm |
| 11 | Max torque (Maxwell) | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 p V_s^2}{4 \pi f_s (X_s + X_r)}$ | Pull-out torque | N┬Ęm |

| 13 | Park transform | $\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$ | ╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq | A |
| 14 | FOC torque equation | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} p \dfrac{L_m^2}{L_r} i_{mr} i_{qs}$ | Rotor-flux-oriented | N┬Ęm |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reduction Factors in Auto-Transformer Starting

**Problem statement:** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 15 kW induction motor draws a DOL starting current of $6 \times$ full-load current, with starting torque of $1.5 \times$ full-load torque. An autotransformer with **60% tap** is used for starting. Compute: (a) motor terminal voltage, (b) line current, (c) motor starting current, (d) starting torque.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the tap ratio
The 60% tap means the secondary voltage is 60% of the line voltage:

$$K = 0.60$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Motor terminal voltage (secondary of autotransformer)
The motor is connected across the secondary of the autotransformer. The line-to-line voltage applied to the motor is:

$$V_{motor} = K \cdot V_{line} = 0.60 \times 400 \text{ V} = 240 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Motor starting current
At start, the rotor is locked. The motor's per-phase impedance is $Z_{LR} = R_{LR} + jX_{LR}$. The locked-rotor current at **reduced voltage** is:

$$I_{motor,start} = \dfrac{V_{motor}}{Z_{LR}} = \dfrac{K \cdot V_{line}}{Z_{LR}} = K \cdot I_{DOL}$$

If $I_{FL}$ is full-load current, then $I_{DOL} = 6 I_{FL}$, so:

$$I_{motor,start} = 0.60 \times 6 I_{FL} = 3.6 \, I_{FL}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Line current (the current drawn from the supply)
By the **autotransformer ampere-turn balance**, the line (primary) current is:

$$I_{line} = K \cdot I_{motor,start} = K^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$$

$$I_{line} = (0.60)^2 \times 6 I_{FL} = 0.36 \times 6 I_{FL} = 2.16 \, I_{FL}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Starting torque
Torque is proportional to the **square of the voltage** (at a given slip):

$$T_{start} = K^2 \cdot T_{DOL} = (0.60)^2 \times 1.5 \, T_{FL} = 0.36 \times 1.5 \, T_{FL} = 0.54 \, T_{FL}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Tabulated results

| Quantity | DOL | With 60% Autotransformer | Reduction Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Motor terminal voltage | 400 V | 240 V | $K = 0.60$ |
| Motor starting current | $6.0 \, I_{FL}$ | $3.6 \, I_{FL}$ | $K = 0.60$ |
| Line starting current | $6.0 \, I_{FL}$ | $2.16 \, I_{FL}$ | $K^2 = 0.36$ |
| Starting torque | $1.5 \, T_{FL}$ | $0.54 \, T_{FL}$ | $K^2 = 0.36$ |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: V/f Curve and Boost Voltage

**Problem statement:** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor has a stator resistance $R_s = 1.2 \, \Omega$ per phase and full-load current $I_{FL} = 8 \text{ A}$. (a) Compute the V/f constant. (b) Compute the voltage at $f_s = 25 \text{ Hz}$. (c) Compute the boost voltage needed at $f_s = 2 \text{ Hz}$ to maintain rated flux, assuming the $I_s R_s$ drop must be compensated.

### Step 1 ŌĆö V/f constant
By the V/f law:

$$k = \dfrac{V_{base}}{f_{base}} = \dfrac{400 \text{ V}}{50 \text{ Hz}} = 8 \text{ V/Hz}$$

(For 3-phase, $V_{base}$ is line-to-line; some textbooks use phase voltage. The result is consistent as long as the same convention is used throughout.)

### Step 2 ŌĆö Voltage at $f_s = 25 \text{ Hz}$
By the V/f law:

$$V_s = k \cdot f_s = 8 \times 25 = 200 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Boost voltage computation
At $f_s = 2 \text{ Hz}$, the V/f law gives a "naive" voltage:

$$V_{s,naive} = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ V}$$

But at this low frequency, the **stator resistance drop** $I_s R_s$ becomes a significant fraction of the small induced EMF. The approximate flux relation (neglecting leakage reactance) is:

$$E_s \approx V_s - I_s R_s$$

To maintain rated flux, $E_s = k \cdot f_s = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ V}$. The required terminal voltage is:

$$V_s = E_s + I_s R_s = 16 + 8 \times 1.2 = 16 + 9.6 = 25.6 \text{ V}$$

The **boost voltage** is:

$$V_{boost} = V_s - V_{s,naive} = 25.6 - 16 = 9.6 \text{ V}$$

This is $9.6/400 = 2.4\%$ of rated ŌĆö small but essential for low-speed torque.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Clarke and Park Transforms from First Principles

### 3.3.1 Clarke Transform (abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓)

**Goal:** Project three-phase currents $(i_a, i_b, i_c)$ onto two orthogonal axes $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in the stationary plane.

Assume balanced 3-phase currents:

$$i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$$

Set the $\alpha$-axis along the $a$-phase direction. The projection of each phase onto $\alpha$ is $\cos(0)$, $\cos(120┬░)$, $\cos(240┬░)$ for $a$, $b$, $c$ respectively:

$$i_\alpha = k_1 \left[ i_a \cdot 1 + i_b \cdot \cos(120┬░) + i_c \cdot \cos(240┬░) \right]$$

$$i_\alpha = k_1 \left[ i_a - \tfrac{1}{2} i_b - \tfrac{1}{2} i_c \right]$$

Using $i_b + i_c = -i_a$:

$$i_\alpha = k_1 \left[ i_a - \tfrac{1}{2}(-i_a) \right] = k_1 \cdot \tfrac{3}{2} i_a$$

Choosing $k_1 = 2/3$ (amplitude-invariant form) gives $i_\alpha = i_a$, as desired.

The $\beta$-axis is orthogonal to $\alpha$, in the direction $\sin(90┬░) = 1$, $\sin(210┬░) = -1/2$, $\sin(330┬░) = 1/2$:

$$i_\beta = k_1 \left[ 0 \cdot i_a - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} i_b + \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} i_c \right]$$

The final Clarke transform matrix is:

$$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \\ i_0 \end{bmatrix} &= \dfrac{2}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ \tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

The zero-sequence component $i_0 = (i_a + i_b + i_c)/3 = 0$ for balanced 3-phase, so it can be dropped.

### 3.3.2 Park Transform (╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq)

**Goal:** Rotate the ╬▒╬▓ frame by angle $\theta_e$ to align with the rotor flux.

The rotation matrix in 2D is:

$$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Inverse Park transform** is the transpose (orthogonal rotation):

$$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & -\sin\theta_e \\ \sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: FOC Torque Equation

Starting from the cross-product form of torque in the d-q frame:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \, p \, \left( \lambda_{ds} i_{qs} - \lambda_{qs} i_{ds} \right)$$

In the **rotor-flux-oriented frame**, the rotor flux is aligned with the $d$-axis, so $\lambda_{qr} = 0$ and $\lambda_{dr} = \lambda_r$. The stator flux linkages are:

$$\lambda_{ds} = L_s i_{ds} + L_m i_{dr}, \quad \lambda_{qs} = L_s i_{qs} + L_m i_{qr} = L_s i_{qs}$$

(using $i_{qr} = 0$ in the rotor-flux frame).

Substituting:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \, p \, \left[ (L_s i_{ds} + L_m i_{dr}) i_{qs} - L_s i_{qs} i_{ds} \right]$$

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \, p \, L_m i_{dr} \, i_{qs}$$

But $i_{dr} = \lambda_r / L_r$ and $\lambda_r = L_m i_{ds}/(1 + s\tau_r)$. At steady state, $i_{dr} = (L_m/L_r) i_{ds}$. Therefore:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \, p \, L_m \cdot \dfrac{L_m}{L_r} i_{ds} \cdot i_{qs} = \dfrac{3}{2} \, p \, \dfrac{L_m^2}{L_r} \, i_{ds} \, i_{qs}$$

This is the **classical FOC torque equation** ŌĆö torque is the product of a flux-producing current $i_{ds}$ and a torque-producing current $i_{qs}$, just like a DC motor with $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Field-Weakening Torque Profile

**Problem statement:** A 4-pole induction motor has base speed $N_{base} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$ at 50 Hz, 400 V. The maximum allowable phase voltage is $V_{max} = 230 \text{ V}$ (RMS). At $f_s = 75 \text{ Hz}$, compute: (a) the new flux, (b) the maximum torque, and (c) verify that the power is approximately constant.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Synchronous speed
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f_s}{p} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm at base}$$

At $f_s = 75$ Hz:

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \times 75}{4} = 2250 \text{ rpm}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö New flux (assuming $V_s$ limited to 230 V)
Above base frequency, $V_s$ is held at $V_{max} = 230$ V (we cannot increase it further due to inverter limits). The new flux is:

$$\phi_{new} = \phi_{base} \cdot \dfrac{f_{base}}{f_{new}} = \phi_{base} \cdot \dfrac{50}{75} = \dfrac{2}{3} \phi_{base}$$

The flux has reduced to 2/3 of its rated value.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum torque
Since $T_{max} \propto V_s^2 / f_s$ (from the Maxwell formula), and $V_s$ is constant at $V_{max}$:

$$\dfrac{T_{max,new}}{T_{max,base}} = \dfrac{V_{max}^2 / f_{new}}{V_{base}^2 / f_{base}} = \dfrac{230^2 / 75}{400^2 / 50}$$

Wait ŌĆö the *base* condition has $V_s = 400$ V (line-to-line, the motor's rating). The *field-weakening* condition has $V_s = 230$ V (line-to-neutral, the inverter's max phase voltage). These are different reference frames! Let me convert: in line-to-neutral terms, the base voltage is $400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94 \text{ V}$. So the ratio simplifies:

$$\dfrac{T_{max,new}}{T_{max,base}} = \dfrac{230^2 / 75}{230.94^2 / 50} \approx \dfrac{50}{75} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

So the maximum torque is reduced to **2/3** of its base value.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Constant power check
$$P_{base} = T_{max,base} \cdot \omega_{base}, \quad P_{new} = T_{max,new} \cdot \omega_{new}$$

$$\dfrac{P_{new}}{P_{base}} = \dfrac{T_{max,new}}{T_{max,base}} \cdot \dfrac{\omega_{new}}{\omega_{base}} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{75}{50} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{3}{2} = 1$$

Ō£ģ **Power is constant** ŌĆö confirming the **constant-power region** of field-weakening operation.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of All Four Methods

| Parameter | Auto-Transformer Starting | V/f Control | FOC | Field Weakening |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Starting method | Open-loop scalar speed control | Closed-loop vector torque control | Operating regime (above base speed) |
| **Primary purpose** | Limit inrush current at start | Smooth speed variation 0ŌĆō$N_{base}$ | Decoupled torque/flux control | Extend speed range above $N_{base}$ |
| **Speed range** | 0 to $N_{base}$ (only at start) | 0 to $N_{base}$ | 0 to $N_{base}$ (and beyond with FW) | $N_{base}$ to $N_{max}$ |
| **Torque capability** | Reduced ($K^2 \cdot T_{DOL}$) | Constant max torque | Constant max torque (regulate $i_{qs}$) | Decreasing ($\propto 1/f$) |
| **Power capability** | Reduced at start | Variable (ŌłØ $f$) | Variable (ŌłØ $f$) | **Constant** |
| **Feedback** | Not required | Open loop (no speed sensor) | Speed + current sensors required | Speed + current sensors required |
| **Dynamic response** | Slow (mechanical contactors) | Slow (no torque loop) | **Fast ($\sim 1$ ms)** | Fast (inherits FOC's bandwidth) |
| **Complexity** | Low (3 contactors) | Low (PWM + V/f table) | **High (transforms + PI + SVPWM)** | High (voltage-limit trajectory) |
| **Sensors** | None | None (VSI) | Encoder/resolver + current sensors | Encoder + current sensors |
| **KTU typical marks** | 7ŌĆō10 | 7ŌĆō10 | 10ŌĆō14 | 5ŌĆō7 |

```mermaid
    ATrfCore -- Tap at 75 percent --> TapOut75[Reduced Voltage 300V]
    ATrfCore -- Tap at 40 percent --> TapOut40[Reduced Voltage 160V]
    TapOut --> K3{Contactor K3 Start}
    TapOut75 --> K3
    TapOut40 --> K3
    K3 --> Motor[3-Phase Induction Motor]
    Supply --> K2{Contactor K2 Run Bypass}
    K2 --> Motor

    Timer -.-> K2
    Timer -.-> K1
```

**Operating Logic:**
- **Start:** K1 + K3 close (timer triggers). Motor starts at reduced voltage.
- **Run:** After timer expires, K3 opens and K2 closes, bypassing the autotransformer.

- In **open-loop V/f**, the PI controller and Park/Clarke blocks are absent ŌĆö the V/f table directly feeds the modulator.
- The **DC link** is fed by the EV battery pack (400 V or 800 V).

## 4.3 FOC (Field-Oriented Control) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    IqRefCalc --> IqPI[PI Controller for q axis Current]

    FluxEst[Rotor Flux Estimator using V or I Model] --> FluxAng[Flux Angle theta_e]
    FluxAng --> ParkInv[Inverse Park dq to alpha beta]
    FluxAng --> Park[Park alpha beta to dq]

    IdPI --> VdRef[Voltage Reference Vd]
    IqPI --> VqRef[Voltage Reference Vq]

    VdRef --> ParkInv
    VqRef --> ParkInv

    ParkInv --> ClarkeInv[Inverse Clarke alpha beta to abc]
    ClarkeInv --> SVPWM[SVPWM Modulator]
    SVPWM --> VSI[Three Phase Inverter]
    VSI --> IM[Induction Motor]

    IM -- Phase Currents --> Clarke[Clarke abc to alpha beta]
    Clarke --> Park
    Park -- Id Iq measured --> IdPI
    Park -- Id Iq measured --> IqPI

    IM -- Speed Sensor Encoder or Resolver --> PISpeed

    style SpeedRef2 fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff

    style IqPI fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff

```

**Key Observations:**
- The **outer speed loop** (PI) generates the torque reference.
- The **two inner current loops** (Id and Iq) regulate flux and torque independently.
- The **rotor flux estimator** is critical: a Voltage Model works at high speed, a Current Model works at low speed, and a hybrid crossover is used in production EVs.
- The **inverse Park + inverse Clarke + SVPWM** chain converts DC-like Id/Iq references back to three-phase PWM.

## 4.4 Field Weakening Operating Trajectory (d-q Plane)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Origin
        O[Origin dq plane]
    end

    subgraph Region1[Region 1 Below Base Speed]
        MTPA[MTPA Trajectory\nConstant Torque Locus]
        IdsRated[Id equals Id rated at full flux]
        Region1Note[Id is high, Iq is varied for torque]
    end

    subgraph Region2[Region 2 Field Weakening Mode 1]
        FW1[Voltage Limit Circle Intersection]
        CurrLim[Current Limit Circle I max]
        VoltLim[Voltage Limit Circle V max]
        Region2Note[Id reduced to keep within voltage circle]
    end

    subgraph Region3[Region 3 Deep Field Weakening]
        FW2[Mode 2 Maximum Speed]
        IdMin[Id at minimum value]
        Region3Note[Id reaches minimum, current limit alone active]
    end

    O --> MTPA
    MTPA --> FW1
    FW1 --> FW2

    style O fill:#1e293b,color:#ffffff
    style MTPA fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style FW1 fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff
    style FW2 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style CurrLim fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style VoltLim fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
    style IdsRated fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff
    style IdMin fill:#be185d,color:#ffffff
```

**Key Observations:**
- **Region 1 (Below base speed):** The operating point rides along the **MTPA (Maximum Torque Per Ampere)** curve, which for the rotor-flux-oriented frame is a vertical line at $I_d = I_{d,rated}$.
- **Region 2 (Field Weakening Mode 1):** Both the voltage and current limits are active. The operating point traces the **intersection of the two circles**, sliding leftward as speed rises.
- **Region 3 (Deep Field Weakening):** The voltage limit saturates; only the current limit remains active. The motor operates with minimum $I_d$ and the maximum possible speed.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Auto-Transformer Starting Principle `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain with a neat sketch the principle of autotransformer starting of a three-phase induction motor. State the relationship between the line current and the motor current during starting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle (1.5 marks):** An autotransformer with a **tapped secondary** is connected between the supply and the motor. The tap is set to a fraction $K$ of the line voltage (typically $0.4$, $0.6$, or $0.75$). During start, the motor receives a reduced voltage $V_m = K \cdot V_L$, which limits the inrush current. After the motor accelerates to ~70ŌĆō80% of rated speed, the autotransformer is **bypassed** by a run contactor, applying full line voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Current relationship (1.5 marks):** The motor terminal current is reduced by factor $K$: $I_{motor} = K \cdot I_{DOL}$. The **line current** is further reduced by the turns ratio: $I_{line} = K \cdot I_{motor} = K^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$. Thus, with a 60% tap, line current is only $0.36$ of the direct-on-line starting current, while the starting torque (ŌłØ $V^2$) is also reduced to $0.36 T_{DOL}$.

---

### Question 2: V/f Control vs FOC Comparison `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between V/f control and Field-Oriented Control (FOC) of induction motors. Mention two advantages of FOC over V/f control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | V/f Control | FOC |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Open-loop, scalar | Closed-loop, vector |
| **Decoupling** | None (coupled torqueŌĆōflux) | Full decoupling of torque and flux |
| **Speed range** | Up to base speed | 0 to $N_{base}$ (and beyond with FW) |
| **Dynamic response** | Slow | Fast (sub-ms current loop) |
| **Sensors** | Not required | Speed + current sensors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two advantages of FOC (1 mark):**
> 1. **Faster dynamic response** ŌĆö torque loop bandwidth of 1ŌĆō2 kHz vs 10ŌĆō50 Hz for V/f, enabling precise pedal response in EVs.
> 2. **Full torque at zero speed** ŌĆö V/f can struggle at very low frequencies due to stator resistance drop, whereas FOC uses boost compensation via $I_d$ control.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Autotransformer Starting + V/f Control `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q (a)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz, 11 kW squirrel-cage induction motor has a full-load current of 22 A and a DOL starting current of $6 \times I_{FL}$. An autotransformer with **65% tap** is used for starting. Compute: **(i)** the motor terminal voltage at start, **(ii)** the motor starting current, **(iii)** the line current drawn from the supply, and **(iv)** the starting torque as a fraction of $T_{FL}$ if DOL starting torque is $1.8 \times T_{FL}$. **[7 marks]**

**Q (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle of **V/f control** of a 3-phase induction motor. Why is a **voltage boost** required at low frequencies? Mention the regions of constant torque, constant power, and field weakening on the torque-speed plane. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q(a):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Tap ratio:** $K = 0.65$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Motor terminal voltage:**
$$V_{motor} = K \cdot V_{line} = 0.65 \times 400 = 260 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Motor starting current (reduced):**
$$I_{motor,start} = K \cdot I_{DOL} = 0.65 \times 6 \times 22 = 0.65 \times 132 = 85.8 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Line current from supply:**
$$I_{line} = K^2 \cdot I_{DOL} = (0.65)^2 \times 6 \times 22 = 0.4225 \times 132 = 55.77 \text{ A}$$

This is **42.25%** of the DOL starting line current ŌĆö a 57.75% reduction.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Starting torque:**
$$T_{start} = K^2 \cdot T_{DOL} = 0.4225 \times 1.8 \, T_{FL} = 0.7605 \, T_{FL}$$

$$\boxed{T_{start} \approx 0.76 \, T_{FL}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Summary Table:**

| Quantity | DOL | With 65% Autotransformer |
|---|---|---|
| Motor terminal voltage | 400 V | 260 V |
| Motor starting current | 132 A | 85.8 A |
| Line starting current | 132 A | 55.77 A |
| Starting torque | 1.8 $T_{FL}$ | 0.76 $T_{FL}$ |

---

#### Model Solution to Q(b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö V/f Control Principle (3 marks):**
V/f control maintains the **air-gap flux constant** by varying the stator voltage in proportion to the stator frequency:

$$V_s / f_s = \text{constant} = V_{base} / f_{base}$$

The reference three-phase sinusoidal voltages (with amplitude $V_{ref} = k \cdot f_{ref}$) are generated by a microcontroller/DSP, modulated using **SPWM or SVPWM**, and applied to the motor through a **three-phase VSI** fed by the battery.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Voltage Boost at Low Frequencies (2 marks):**
At very low frequencies, the **stator resistance drop $I_s R_s$** becomes a significant fraction of the small terminal voltage. To maintain rated flux, the induced EMF must still be $E_s = k \cdot f_s$. The required terminal voltage is:

$$V_s = E_s + I_s R_s = k f_s + I_s R_s$$

A **boost voltage** $V_{boost} = I_s R_s$ is added at low frequencies (typically below 5ŌĆō10 Hz) to compensate.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Torque-Speed Regions (2 marks):**

- **Region I ŌĆö Constant Torque (0 to $N_{base}$):** With V/f held constant, flux is constant, and $T_{max}$ is independent of frequency. Torque is constant.
- **Region II ŌĆö Field Weakening ($N_{base}$ to $N_{max}$):** Voltage is saturated at $V_{max}$, frequency rises, flux drops as $1/f$, torque drops as $1/f^2$.
- **Region III ŌĆö Constant Power ($N_{base}$ to $N_{max}$):** Power = $T \cdot \omega$ remains approximately constant because $T \propto 1/f$ and $\omega \propto f$, so $P \propto \text{const}$.

---

### Question B: FOC + Field Weakening `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Modified]`

**Q (a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the operation of **Indirect Field-Oriented Control (IFOC)** of a 3-phase induction motor. Derive the **FOC torque equation** in the rotor-flux-oriented reference frame. **[7 marks]**

**Q (b)** Explain the concept of **field weakening** in induction motor drives. A 4-pole, 50 Hz, 400 V induction motor is operated in field-weakening mode at 75 Hz. Compute: **(i)** the ratio of new flux to rated flux, **(ii)** the ratio of new maximum torque to rated maximum torque, and **(iii)** verify that the power is constant. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q(a):

**Step 1 ŌĆö IFOC Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Indirect FOC uses the **slip relation** to compute the flux angle. The key blocks are:

1. **Speed reference** ŌåÆ PI speed controller ŌåÆ torque reference $T_e^*$.
2. **Flux reference** $\lambda_r^*$ (constant in Region 1, reduced in Region 2).
3. **$i_{ds}$ reference** from flux: $i_{ds}^* = \lambda_r^* / L_m$.
4. **$i_{qs}$ reference** from torque: $i_{qs}^* = \dfrac{2 L_r T_e^*}{3 p L_m \lambda_r^*}$.
5. **PI current controllers** for $i_{ds}$ and $i_{qs}$ ŌåÆ voltage references $V_{ds}^*$, $V_{qs}^*$.
6. **Slip calculator:** $\omega_{sl} = \dfrac{L_m i_{qs}^*}{\tau_r \lambda_r^*}$.
7. **Flux angle:** $\theta_e = \int (\omega_{sl} + \omega_r) \, dt$ where $\omega_r$ is the measured rotor speed.
8. **Inverse Park + Inverse Clarke + SVPWM** ŌåÆ 3-phase gating signals ŌåÆ VSI ŌåÆ motor.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Torque Equation Derivation (4 marks):**

The general d-q torque expression is:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} p \left( \lambda_{ds} i_{qs} - \lambda_{qs} i_{ds} \right)$$

In the **rotor-flux-oriented frame**, the rotor flux linkage is on the d-axis only:

$$\lambda_{dr} = \lambda_r, \quad \lambda_{qr} = 0$$

The stator flux linkages are:

$$\lambda_{ds} = L_s i_{ds} + L_m i_{dr}, \quad \lambda_{qs} = L_s i_{qs} + L_m i_{qr} = L_s i_{qs}$$

(using $i_{qr} = 0$ in the rotor-flux frame). Substituting:

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} p \left[ (L_s i_{ds} + L_m i_{dr}) i_{qs} - L_s i_{qs} i_{ds} \right] = \dfrac{3}{2} p L_m i_{dr} i_{qs}$$

Since $i_{dr} = (L_m / L_r) i_{ds}$ (at steady state):

$$\boxed{T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} p \dfrac{L_m^2}{L_r} i_{ds} i_{qs}}$$

This shows that **torque is the product of flux-producing current $i_{ds}$ and torque-producing current $i_{qs}$** ŌĆö emulating a DC motor.

---

#### Model Solution to Q(b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Concept of Field Weakening (2 marks):**
Above base speed, the back-EMF grows with frequency. The inverter cannot supply more than its DC-link voltage, so the stator voltage $V_s$ must be **clamped at $V_{max}$**. To obey $E_s = 4.44 f_s N_s \phi_m k_w$ with $V_s = V_{max}$ fixed, the flux must be **reduced inversely with frequency**:

$$\phi_{new} = \phi_{base} \cdot \dfrac{f_{base}}{f_{new}}$$

This is field weakening ŌĆö the rotor flux is intentionally reduced to enable higher speeds.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Given Data:**
- $p = 4$, $f_{base} = 50$ Hz, $V_{base} = 400$ V (line-to-line).
- $f_{new} = 75$ Hz.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Flux Ratio (2 marks):**
$$\dfrac{\phi_{new}}{\phi_{base}} = \dfrac{f_{base}}{f_{new}} = \dfrac{50}{75} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\phi_{new} = 0.667 \, \phi_{base}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Torque Ratio (2 marks):**
Maximum torque is $T_{max} \propto V_s^2 / f_s$. With $V_s$ clamped at $V_{max}$ (constant):

$$\dfrac{T_{max,new}}{T_{max,base}} = \dfrac{V_{max}^2 / f_{new}}{V_{max}^2 / f_{base}} = \dfrac{f_{base}}{f_{new}} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max,new} = 0.667 \, T_{max,base}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Power Verification (1 mark):**
$$P_{new} = T_{max,new} \cdot \omega_{new} = (2/3 \, T_{max,base}) \cdot (3/2 \, \omega_{base}) = T_{max,base} \cdot \omega_{base} = P_{base}$$

Ō£ģ **Power is constant** ŌĆö confirming the **constant-power region** of field-weakening operation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Autotransformer ŌĆö Line vs Motor Current confusion:** Many students write $I_{line} = K \cdot I_{DOL}$ instead of the correct $I_{line} = K^2 \cdot I_{DOL}$. Remember: the line current is the *primary* current of the autotransformer, so it gets reduced by the turns ratio **twice** (once by the voltage reduction, once by the current reflection).
> 2. **V/f Law ŌĆö Units Mismatch:** Some students compute $V_s = k \cdot f_s$ in **line-to-line** volts but apply it to a **phase** voltage, or vice versa. Always state the convention explicitly.
> 3. **FOC ŌĆö Forgetting the 3/2 Factor:** The torque equation is $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} p (L_m^2/L_r) i_{ds} i_{qs}$, **not** $T_e = p (L_m^2/L_r) i_{ds} i_{qs}$. The 3/2 comes from the amplitude-invariant Park transform.
> 4. **Field Weakening ŌĆö Confusing Voltage Limits:** The "voltage limit" is set by the **DC-link voltage** and the **PWM strategy**, not by the motor's nameplate. State $V_{max} = V_{dc}/(2\sqrt{2})$ for SPWM, or $V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$ for SVPWM (linear modulation).
> 5. **Sketching the Block Diagram:** KTU examiners award marks for **labeled arrows** (signal flow direction), not just boxes. Always label: speed ref ŌåÆ PI ŌåÆ $i_{qs}^*$ ŌåÆ inverse Park ŌåÆ SVPWM ŌåÆ inverter ŌåÆ motor.
> 6. **Units and Significant Figures:** Always carry **3 significant figures** in numerical answers. E.g., write "55.8 A", not "Ōēł 55 A".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Auto-Transformer Starting
- Reduces **motor voltage** by $K$ and **line current** by $K^2$.
- **Torque reduction** is $K^2$.
- Standard taps: $40\%$, $60\%$, $75\%$.
- Three contactors: K1 (line), K3 (start/tap), K2 (run/bypass).
- Used when $T_{start} > 0.33 \, T_{FL}$ requirement exists (vs star-delta).

### V/f Control
- **Law:** $V_s / f_s = V_{base} / f_{base} = k$ (V/Hz).
- **Boost voltage** at low $f$ to compensate $I_s R_s$ drop.
- **Open-loop**, no speed sensor needed.
- **Constant-torque region** below base speed; flux held constant.
- **Slow dynamic response** ŌĆö no torque loop.

### FOC (Vector Control)
- **Clarke transform** abc ŌåÆ ╬▒╬▓ (stationary 2-phase).
- **Park transform** ╬▒╬▓ ŌåÆ dq (rotating 2-phase, aligned with rotor flux).
- **Torque equation:** $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} p (L_m^2/L_r) i_{ds} i_{qs}$.
- **$i_{ds}$** controls flux; **$i_{qs}$** controls torque.
- **Indirect FOC** uses slip relation; **Direct FOC** uses flux estimator.
- **SVPWM** is the preferred modulation (15% more DC-link utilization than SPWM).

### Field Weakening
- Operates **above base speed** with $V_s$ clamped at $V_{max}$.
- **Flux** $\propto 1/f_s$; **Torque** $\propto 1/f_s$; **Power** = constant.
- **CPSR** (constant-power speed range) = 3 to 5 for EV traction.
- Two operating modes: voltage-and-current-limited (Mode 1) and current-limited only (Mode 2).
- **MTPA** in Region 1 ŌåÆ **Voltage-limit trajectory** in Region 2.

### KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Concepts
- ŌŁÉ Derivation of $V_s/f_s$ constancy from $E_s = 4.44 f_s N_s \phi_m k_w$.
- ŌŁÉ Numerical on autotransformer starting (line current, motor current, torque).
- ŌŁÉ Clarke and Park transform matrices ŌĆö write them from memory.
- ŌŁÉ Field-weakening numerical: compute new flux, torque, and verify power constancy.
- ŌŁÉ Block diagrams ŌĆö always draw with **labeled signal flow arrows**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** For the 14-mark questions, structure your answer in **three clear sections**: (1) Principle, (2) Block diagram or circuit, (3) Derivation or numerical example. Examiners reward **clarity, neatness, and explicit unit declarations**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Operation of Synchronous Motors, Methods of Starting, Working of Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor, BLDC - Brushless DC Motor - Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Operation of Synchronous Motors, Methods of Starting, Working of Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor, BLDC - Brushless DC Motor - Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Principles of Operation of Synchronous Motors

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> A **Synchronous Motor** is a doubly-excited AC machine whose rotor rotates at a *constant speed* (synchronous speed) that is *independent of the load*, provided the field excitation and supply frequency remain constant. The rotor and stator magnetic fields rotate at the *same speed* and are *locked* in synchronism, with no slip in the steady state.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "Two Swinging Pendulums Locked in Step"

Imagine two people (Stator and Rotor) standing on a giant rotating turntable. The **stator person** swings a magnetic ball in a perfect circle at exactly 50 revolutions per second (driven by the 3-phase AC supply). The **rotor person** holds their own permanent magnet and *must* follow this swinging ball. If the rotor magnet is strong enough and is given a small push to start, it will lock onto the stator's rotating field and "chase" it forever at exactly the same speed ŌĆö *no matter how much weight (load) is added to the rotor*. This is **synchronous operation**: the rotor literally *synchronizes* with the rotating magnetic field of the stator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industrial Metric for EV Application:**
> Synchronous speed: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ where $f$ is the supply frequency in **Hz** and $P$ is the number of poles. For a typical EV PMSM with $P = 8$ and $f = 200\,\text{Hz}$, $N_s = 3000\,\text{rpm}$.

---

## 1.2 Methods of Starting Synchronous Motors

A synchronous motor is **not self-starting** because at standstill, the rotor is stationary while the stator produces a fast-rotating magnetic field. The alternating torque averages to zero during a half-cycle, preventing rotation. Three classical methods are taught in the KTU syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Classical Starting Methods (KTU Board-Favorite List):**
> 1. **Pony Motor (Auxiliary Motor) Starting** ŌĆö A small auxiliary motor brings the rotor close to synchronous speed, then DC field is applied, and the motor "pulls into step."
> 2. **Damper Winding (Amortisseur Winding) Starting** ŌĆö A squirrel-cage-like winding on the rotor poles allows *asynchronous* (induction) starting; once near-synchronous speed, DC excitation locks the rotor.
> 3. **Variable Frequency (VFD) Starting** ŌĆö The supply frequency is gradually increased from 0 to rated value, making the rotating field slow enough for the rotor to follow (most relevant for EV drives).

### Intuitive Analogy

Think of starting a **merry-go-round** with a sleeping child on a horse. If you spin the platform too fast, the child gets thrown off. Solution: spin the platform *slowly first* (variable frequency), then gradually speed it up ŌĆö the child can now hold on. This is exactly how a **VFD starts a synchronous motor** in an EV drivetrain.

---

## 1.3 Working of Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:**
> A **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** is a synchronous motor in which the rotor field is produced by **permanent magnets** (typically Neodymium-Iron-Boron, NdFeB, or Samarium-Cobalt, SmCo) instead of a DC-excited winding. This eliminates the need for slip rings, brushes, and rotor field copper losses, making it the *preferred EV traction motor*.

**Operation Principle (The "Magnetic Lock"):**
- The 3-phase stator winding, when energized by an inverter, produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at synchronous speed $N_s$.
- The rotor, embedded with permanent magnets, has its own fixed **north and south poles**.
- The stator field and rotor field *attract and repel* each other, creating a continuous torque.
- The rotor **locks** to the stator RMF and rotates at the same speed: $N = N_s$.

### Intuition ŌĆö "Two Magnets Dancing on a Table"

Hold a bar magnet (rotor) on a wooden stick pivot. Move another magnet (stator field) in a circle around it. If you move it slowly, the first magnet will follow, twisting on its pivot. The stick magnet *cannot lag or lead* ŌĆö it must keep perfect pace. This is a PMSM: the rotor magnet is "dragged" by the rotating stator field.

---

## 1.4 BLDC ŌĆö Brushless DC Motor ŌĆö Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:**
> A **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** is a *surface-mounted* (or interior) permanent magnet synchronous motor that is *electronically commutated* using an inverter. The "DC" refers to the fact that the trapezoidal back-EMF (similar to a DC motor) is used, and the rotor position feedback (from Hall sensors or sensorless estimation) replaces the mechanical brushes and commutator of a conventional DC motor.

**Construction Components:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BLDC Motor Main Parts (Must memorize for KTU 2-mark questions):**
> 1. **Stator** ŌĆö Laminated silicon steel core with 3-phase windings (star or delta connected). Stationary part.
> 2. **Rotor** ŌĆö Permanent magnets mounted on the surface (Surface Permanent Magnet ŌĆö SPM) or buried inside (Interior Permanent Magnet ŌĆö IPM). Rotating part.
> 3. **Shaft** ŌĆö Mechanical output, transfers torque to the EV gearbox.
> 4. **Position Sensors** ŌĆö Three Hall-effect sensors (60┬░ apart) detect rotor angular position.
> 5. **Bearing Housing & Frame** ŌĆö Mechanical support, heat dissipation.
> 6. **No Brushes, No Commutator** ŌĆö Commutation is done *electronically* by the inverter/controller.

### Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Smart Music Box"

Imagine a wind-up music box where the rotating drum (rotor) has bumps (magnets) and instead of a mechanical pin hitting the bumps, a small computer *electromagnetically* plays the notes at exactly the right moment. The computer *knows* the position of the drum via a tiny eye (Hall sensor). This is a BLDC: the **mechanical commutation is replaced by intelligent electronic switching**.

---

## 1.5 Why These Motors Dominate Modern EVs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Perspective ŌĆö Why PMSM & BLDC are the EV Standard:**
> - **High efficiency** (95ŌĆō98%) ŌĆö critical for range extension.
> - **High power density** ŌĆö more kW per kg, allowing compact traction motors.
> - **Excellent torque-to-inertia ratio** ŌĆö instant response for acceleration.
> - **Regenerative braking capability** ŌĆö energy recovery when decelerating.
> - **No brush maintenance** ŌĆö long life, suitable for automotive duty cycle.
> - **Liquid cooling friendly** ŌĆö water jackets integrated into stator housing.
> Examples: **Tesla Model 3 (PMSM)**, **Toyota Prius (PMSM)**, **Chevy Bolt (PMSM)**, **many e-bikes and drones (BLDC)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Synchronous Motor ŌĆö Detailed Operating Principle

### The Magnetic Locking Mechanism (Why Synchronism Happens)

1. **Three-phase stator windings** are physically displaced by **120┬░** in space.
2. When supplied with a balanced 3-phase AC, a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** is produced.
3. The RMF rotates at **synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$.
4. The rotor carries a DC-excited winding (or permanent magnets) producing its own steady NŌĆōS poles.
5. The stator RMF and rotor field *couple* via magnetic attraction/repulsion.
6. **Crucially**, in steady state, the rotor field must rotate at the *same speed* as the stator RMF. If it tried to slip, the relative motion would induce currents and a restoring torque would immediately correct it.

### The Torque Equation (Electromagnetic)

The electromagnetic torque developed in a synchronous motor is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

Where:
- $T_e$ = Electromagnetic torque (N┬Ęm)
- $omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60}$ = Synchronous angular speed (rad/s)
- $V$ = Stator phase voltage (V)
- $E_f$ = Excitation-induced EMF (V)
- $X_s$ = Synchronous reactance (╬®)
- $\delta$ = **Load angle (torque angle)** ŌĆö the angular displacement between rotor and stator fields (degrees or radians).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:**
> When $\delta$ increases (more load), $\sin \delta$ increases, producing more torque ŌĆö but only up to $\delta = 90┬░$. At $\delta = 90┬░$, $T_e$ is **maximum**. If load exceeds this, $\sin \delta$ decreases, motor loses synchronism and **pulls out of step** (a KTU viva-favorite phrase).

---

## 2.2 Methods of Starting ŌĆö Detailed Engineering View

| Method | Working Logic | Advantages | Disadvantages | EV Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pony Motor** | Auxiliary motor accelerates rotor to near-$N_s$, then DC field is applied and stator is energized ŌĆö rotor pulls into step. | Simple, robust, predictable | Requires extra motor, costly, large footprint | **Not used** in EVs |
| **Damper Winding (Amortisseur)** | Rotor is fitted with copper/aluminum bars shorted at ends (like squirrel cage). At start, motor runs as **induction motor** due to induced currents; once near-synchronous speed, DC is applied and rotor synchronizes. | No auxiliary motor needed, simple, used for large industrial machines | Lower starting torque than induction motor, requires damper winding hardware | **Not used** in EVs (extra rotor copper losses) |
| **Variable Frequency (VFD / Soft Starter)** | Inverter output frequency is slowly ramped from 0 Hz to rated Hz. The RMF starts slow, so rotor can follow. | Smooth start, full torque at zero speed, current-limited, regenerative capable | Requires expensive inverter/controller | **The standard EV method** ŌĆö every PMSM/BLDC in an EV uses this |
| **Reduced Voltage Starting** | Stator voltage is reduced using autotransformer/series reactor at start, then gradually raised. | Reduced starting current | Reduced starting torque (cubic relation) | Limited use |
| **Capacitor Starting (for small single-phase)** | Capacitor creates phase-shifted auxiliary winding for starting torque. | Simple for small motors | Not for 3-phase industrial | Not EV |

### Damper Winding ŌĆö Why It Works (The Physics)

At standstill, the rotor is stationary but the stator RMF is at full speed. The damper bars *cut* the stator flux, inducing currents (Faraday's law). These currents, by Lenz's law, produce a torque in the *opposite* direction of relative motion. This is exactly how an induction motor produces torque! The motor *temporarily* behaves like an induction motor, accelerating to ~95ŌĆō98% of $N_s$. At this point, the rotor speed is close enough to the stator RMF that the slip is small; DC excitation is then applied, and the rotor **pulls into synchronism** by magnetic locking.

---

## 2.3 PMSM ŌĆö Detailed Operating Principle

### Mathematical Model (KTU High-Yield)

The stator voltage equations in the rotating **d-q reference frame** (aligned with the rotor flux) are:

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \psi_m$$

Where:
- $v_d, v_q$ = d-axis and q-axis stator voltages
- $i_d, i_q$ = d-axis and q-axis stator currents
- $R_s$ = Stator resistance
- $L_d, L_q$ = d-axis and q-axis inductances
- $omega_e = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{pole\_pairs}$ = Electrical angular speed
- $\psi_m$ = Permanent magnet flux linkage (Wb)

### Electromagnetic Torque of PMSM

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \left[ \psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$$

Where $p$ is the number of pole pairs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Torque Components in PMSM:**
> 1. **Magnet (Alignment) Torque**: $\dfrac{3}{2} p \psi_m i_q$ ŌĆö dominant in *Surface-Mount* PMSM (SPM) where $L_d = L_q$.
> 2. **Reluctance Torque**: $\dfrac{3}{2} p (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q$ ŌĆö dominant in *Interior* PMSM (IPM) where $L_d \neq L_q$. This is the secret of Toyota Prius's high efficiency.

### Control Strategy: $i_d = 0$ Control

For a **Surface-Mount PMSM (SPM)**, $L_d = L_q$, so the reluctance term vanishes. The torque becomes:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot i_q$$

By controlling $i_d = 0$ and varying $i_q$ (the q-axis current), we get a **linear torque-current relationship** ŌĆö perfect for precise EV torque control. This is the standard "MTPA" (Maximum Torque Per Ampere) technique for SPM machines.

---

## 2.4 BLDC Motor ŌĆö Construction Deep Dive

### Physical Construction (In-depth)

| Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Stator Core** | Laminated silicon steel (0.35 mm or 0.5 mm lamination) | Reduces eddy current losses, carries flux |
| **Stator Windings** | Copper (often hairpin for high power density) | Carry 3-phase AC, produce RMF |
| **Rotor Core** | Solid or laminated steel with surface grooves | Holds permanent magnets |
| **Permanent Magnets** | NdFeB (most common) or SmCo | Produce rotor field |
| **Hall Sensors** | 3 Hall-effect ICs at 60┬░ electrical spacing | Detect rotor position for commutation |
| **Bearings** | Sealed deep-groove ball bearings | Support shaft, allow rotation |
| **Housing** | Aluminum alloy (often water-cooled jacket) | Mechanical support, heat dissipation |
| **Shaft** | High-grade alloy steel | Transfer torque to load |
| **End Caps** | Aluminum or steel | Seal motor, hold bearings |

### Rotor Configurations

1. **Surface Permanent Magnet (SPM) Rotor** ŌĆö Magnets glued to outer surface of rotor core. Mechanically fragile at high speed, but has $L_d \approx L_q$ (no reluctance torque). Used in BLDC for low-cost applications.
2. **Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) Rotor** ŌĆö Magnets buried *inside* the rotor. Mechanically robust, suitable for high-speed operation, and $L_d < L_q$ gives reluctance torque. **Standard in EV traction PMSMs** (e.g., Toyota Prius, BMW i3).
3. **Spoke-Type Rotor** ŌĆö Magnets arranged radially inside. Concentrates flux, gives very high air-gap flux density. Used in high-performance EVs.

### Sensor Configurations in BLDC

- **Trapezoidal Commutation (6-step)** ŌĆö Uses 3 Hall sensors, gives 6 discrete commutation steps per electrical cycle. Simpler control, more torque ripple.
- **Sinusoidal Commutation (FOC / Vector Control)** ŌĆö Uses high-resolution encoder or sensorless estimation. Smoother torque, lower ripple. **PMSM in EV traction uses this.**

---

| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T_e \omega_m}{V I}$ | Efficiency | dimensionless | EV range calculation |
| 14 | $\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_m$ | Electrical speed = pole pairs ├Ś mechanical speed | rad/s | Reference frame transform |

> **Where These Equations Are Used in the EV Industry:**
> - **Traction inverter design** (Tesla, Tata, Ather): Equations (7), (8), (9) are coded into the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) for **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)**.
> - **BMS integration**: Power equations (12), (13) predict state-of-charge (SOC) depletion.
> - **Cooling system sizing**: The $I^2 R$ losses predicted by (7), (8) determine radiator capacity.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Synchronous Motor Torque Equation

**Starting from First Principles**

Consider a 3-phase synchronous motor with per-phase stator voltage $V$ and induced EMF $E_f$ displaced by load angle $\delta$. The phasor diagram gives the per-phase power input to the rotor as the in-phase component of $\bar{V} \cdot \bar{I^*}$.

**Step 1: Power Input per Phase**

The power transferred across the air gap per phase is:

$$P_{gap, phase} = \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

This is the classical "synchronous power" equation ŌĆö the air-gap power per phase. It represents the power that crosses from stator to rotor electromagnetically.

**Step 2: Total 3-Phase Air-Gap Power**

For a balanced 3-phase machine, total air-gap power is three times the per-phase value:

$$P_{gap} = 3 \cdot \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

**Step 3: Convert to Mechanical Power**

In a synchronous motor, the air-gap power equals the mechanical power developed (since rotor copper losses are absent in PMSM and negligible in DC-excited synchronous motors without damper):

$$P_m = P_{gap} = 3 \cdot \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

**Step 4: Convert Power to Torque**

Using the relation $P = T \cdot \omega_s$:

$$T_e \cdot \omega_s = 3 \cdot \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

**Step 5: Solve for Torque**

$$T_e = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$$

This is the **canonical synchronous motor torque equation** used in all KTU problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for the Equation:**
> - At no load: $\delta \to 0$, $\sin \delta \to 0$, $T_e \to 0$ Ō£ō
> - At maximum torque: $\delta = 90┬░$, $\sin \delta = 1$, $T_{e,max} = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s}$ Ō£ō
> - At pull-out: If load $\delta$ tries to exceed 90┬░, $\sin \delta$ decreases, motor loses synchronism Ō£ō

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example (KTU 14-mark style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz synchronous motor has synchronous reactance $X_s = 8\,\Omega$ per phase. Stator phase voltage $V = 400\,\text{V}$, induced EMF $E_f = 380\,\text{V}$. The motor runs at a load angle of $\delta = 30┬░$. Calculate:
(a) Synchronous speed $N_s$
(b) Synchronous angular speed $\omega_s$
(c) Electromagnetic torque $T_e$
(d) Maximum (pull-out) torque

**Solution:**

### Part (a): Synchronous Speed

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4}$$

$$N_s = \frac{6000}{4} = 1500\,\text{rpm}$$

### Part (b): Synchronous Angular Speed

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{3000 \pi}{60} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08\,\text{rad/s}$$

### Part (c): Electromagnetic Torque

$$T_e = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta = \frac{3 \times 400 \times 380}{157.08 \times 8} \sin 30┬░$$

Compute numerator: $3 \times 400 \times 380 = 456000$
Compute denominator: $157.08 \times 8 = 1256.64$
Compute ratio: $\dfrac{456000}{1256.64} = 362.87$

Apply $\sin 30┬░ = 0.5$:

$$T_e = 362.87 \times 0.5 = 181.44\,\text{N┬Ęm}$$

### Part (d): Maximum (Pull-Out) Torque

At pull-out, $\delta = 90┬░$, so $\sin \delta = 1$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} = 362.87\,\text{N┬Ęm}$$

Equivalently, $T_{max} = 2 \times T_e = 2 \times 181.44 = 362.88\,\text{N┬Ęm}$ (since $\delta = 30┬░$ gives half of max).

**Interpretation:** This motor can develop a maximum torque of **362.87 N┬Ęm** before losing synchronism. The current operating torque is **181.44 N┬Ęm** at half the limit ŌĆö a safe operating margin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Synchronous Motor vs PMSM vs BLDC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is a high-yield KTU question ŌĆö it has appeared in multiple university exams in both 3-mark and 14-mark formats. Memorize it thoroughly.**

| Parameter | Synchronous Motor (DC-Excited) | PMSM | BLDC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotor Field Source** | DC-excited winding via slip rings | Permanent magnets (NdFeB/SmCo) | Permanent magnets (NdFeB) |
| **Slip Rings** | Yes (for DC excitation) | No | No |
| **Brushes** | Yes | No | No |
| **Back-EMF Waveform** | Sinusoidal | Sinusoidal | Trapezoidal |
| **Stator Current Waveform** | Sinusoidal | Sinusoidal | Rectangular (6-step) |
| **Position Sensor** | Not required (self-start not possible) | Encoder / Resolver / Sensorless | 3 Hall sensors / Sensorless |
| **Torque Ripple** | Lowest | Very low | Moderate (6-step commutation) |
| **Power Density** | Medium | High | High |
| **Efficiency** | 90ŌĆō95% | 95ŌĆō98% | 95ŌĆō97% |
| **Cost** | High (brushes, exciter) | High (magnets) | ModerateŌĆōHigh |
| **Cooling** | Complex | Easy (no rotor losses) | Easy |
| **Control Complexity** | Simple (DOL start) | Complex (FOC/VF control) | Moderate (6-step or FOC) |
| **EV Application** | Rare (industrial use) | **Standard in EVs (Tesla, Toyota, BMW)** | **Common in e-bikes, drones, low-power EVs** |
| **Thermal Stress on Rotor** | High (rotor copper losses) | Low (no rotor copper) | Low |
| **Field Weakening** | Easy (adjust $E_f$) | Harder (PM fixed) | Harder (PM fixed) |
| **Max Speed (mechanical)** | Limited by rotor winding | High (IPM design) | High (SPM limited by magnet glue) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PMSM d-q Transformation (Conceptual Walkthrough)

**Why Transform?**

In the stationary ABC frame, the stator inductances are *time-varying* because the rotor is moving. This makes the differential equations non-linear and hard to solve.

**Park's Transformation (1929)** transforms the 3-phase stationary quantities into 2-phase rotating quantities (d-q) that are aligned with the rotor flux. In this new frame, all inductances become constants, and the equations become linear time-invariant.

**Transformation Matrix (abc ŌåÆ dq):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_d \\ v_q \\ v_0 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \cos(\theta_e - 120┬░) & \cos(\theta_e + 120┬░) \\ -\sin\theta_e & -\sin(\theta_e - 120┬░) & -\sin(\theta_e + 120┬░) \\ \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_a \\ v_b \\ v_c \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $\theta_e$ is the electrical rotor angle (from encoder/resolver).

**Result in d-q Frame (already derived above):**

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \psi_m$$

**Significance for EV Control:**

The two currents $i_d$ and $i_q$ are *decoupled*:
- $i_d$ controls the **flux** (used for field-weakening at high speed).
- $i_q$ controls the **torque** directly (used for acceleration).

This decoupled control is the **heart of EV vector control** ŌĆö it gives instant, precise, linear torque response from 0 to maximum speed, which is essential for the driver's accelerator pedal feel.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Problem: PMSM Torque Calculation

**Problem:** A 3-phase PMSM has 8 poles, NdFeB magnets giving $\psi_m = 0.15\,\text{Wb}$. The d-q inductances are $L_d = 5\,\text{mH}$, $L_q = 8\,\text{mH}$. The controller commands $i_d = -5\,\text{A}$ (field-weakening) and $i_q = 30\,\text{A}$. Calculate the electromagnetic torque.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify pole pairs**

$$p = \frac{P}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4$$

**Step 2: Compute Magnet (Alignment) Torque**

$$T_{magnet} = \frac{3}{2} p \psi_m i_q = \frac{3}{2} \times 4 \times 0.15 \times 30$$

$$T_{magnet} = 6 \times 0.15 \times 30 = 6 \times 4.5 = 27.0\,\text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 3: Compute Reluctance Torque**

$$T_{reluctance} = \frac{3}{2} p (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q = \frac{3}{2} \times 4 \times (0.005 - 0.008) \times (-5) \times 30$$

Note $L_d - L_q = -0.003$ H, $i_d = -5$ A, $i_q = 30$ A:

$$T_{reluctance} = 6 \times (-0.003) \times (-150) = 6 \times 0.45 = 2.7\,\text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Step 4: Total Torque**

$$T_e = T_{magnet} + T_{reluctance} = 27.0 + 2.7 = 29.7\,\text{N┬Ęm}$$

**Interpretation:** The reluctance torque adds ~10% to the total, which is significant for an IPM design. In surface-mount PMSM, this term would vanish, and the total torque would be only 27.0 N┬Ęm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question:**
> "What is the effect of applying negative $i_d$ in an IPM?"
> **Answer:** Negative $i_d$ (demagnetizing current) reduces air-gap flux (used for *field weakening* at high speed) **AND** generates *positive reluctance torque* (because $L_q > L_d$ and both $L_d - L_q$ and $i_d$ are negative, their product is positive). This is a free lunch ŌĆö you get torque *and* flux reduction simultaneously. This is why IPM dominates EV traction.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Starting Method Selection for EV

| EV Component | Starting Method Used | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Tesla Model S PMSM** | Variable Frequency (VFD) via inverter | Smooth, full torque at 0 rpm, regenerative braking integrated |
| **Toyota Prius PMSM** | Variable Frequency + IPM design | High efficiency, reluctance torque adds free boost |
| **E-bike BLDC** | 6-step trapezoidal commutation from Hall sensors | Low cost, simple controller, adequate for 250ŌĆō500 W |
| **Drone BLDC** | Sensorless FOC with back-EMF sensing | Lightweight, no Hall sensor weight |
| **Industrial Synchronous Motor (3-phase AC)** | Damper winding + Pony motor for very large | Reliability, robustness for 1000+ kW |

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    D[VCU Vehicle Control Unit] --> E[Motor Control Unit MCU]
    F[Accelerator Pedal Sensor] --> E
    G[Brake Pedal Sensor] --> E
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The driver presses the accelerator ŌåÆ VCU sends torque request ŌåÆ MCU computes FOC algorithm ŌåÆ inverter switches at high frequency ŌåÆ 3-phase AC current flows into stator ŌåÆ rotor with permanent magnets turns ŌåÆ torque is delivered to wheels.
- Feedback loop: Position sensor (encoder) tells MCU the exact rotor angle, current sensors tell MCU the actual current, temperature sensor protects against overheating. This forms a **closed-loop vector control** ŌĆö the heart of modern EV drivetrains.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

## 4.5 BLDC Six-Step Commutation Sequence (Hall Sensor Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotor at Position 0 deg] --> B[Hall Sensors H1 H2 H3 101]
    B --> C[Inverter Switches: Phase A High, Phase B Low, Phase C Off]
    C --> D[Current flows A to B - Rotor turns CW]
    D --> E[Rotor at 60 deg]
    E --> F[Hall: 100]
    F --> G[Switch: A High, C Low, B Off]
    G --> H[Rotor at 120 deg]
    H --> I[Hall: 110]
    I --> J[Switch: B High, C Low, A Off]
    J --> K[Rotor at 180 deg]
    K --> L[Hall: 010]
    L --> M[Switch: B High, A Low, C Off]
    M --> N[Rotor at 240 deg]
    N --> O[Hall: 011]
    O --> P[Switch: C High, A Low, B Off]
    P --> Q[Rotor at 300 deg]
    Q --> R[Hall: 001]
    R --> S[Switch: C High, B Low, A Off]
    S --> T[Back to 0 deg - Cycle Complete]
```

**Reading:** Each 60┬░ of mechanical rotation, the Hall sensor pattern changes, triggering a different pair of inverter switches. Six such steps complete one electrical cycle, and the rotor turns by 360┬░/electrical = 360┬░/pole_pairs mechanical.

## 4.6 PMSM Vector Control (FOC) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Torque Reference Tref from Accelerator] --> B[Inverse Park Transform]
    C[Flux Reference psi_ref] --> B
    D[Encoder Angle theta_e] --> B
    B --> E[Reference Vd Vq]
    E --> F[SVPWM Modulator]
    F --> G[3-Phase Inverter IGBT]
    G --> H[PMSM Stator]
    H --> I[Mechanical Output Shaft]
    H -.3-Phase Current.- J[Current Sensors]
    J --> K[Clarke Transform abc to alpha-beta]
    K --> L[Park Transform alpha-beta to dq]
    L --> M[Measured id iq]
    M --> N[PI Controller for d axis]
    M --> O[PI Controller for q axis]
    N --> P[Reference Vd]
    O --> Q[Reference Vq]
    P --> R[SVPWM]
    Q --> R
    I -.Position.- D
```

This is the **complete FOC loop** used in every modern EV traction motor. The Clarke-Park transform converts measured AC currents to DC-equivalent values, PI controllers regulate them, and SVPWM generates the inverter switching signals.
