# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 2-Mark Short Answer Questions (Model Answers)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part A consists of short-answer questions carrying **3 marks each**. Answers should be concise, ~4-5 lines, with a clear definition and one supporting point.

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**"Why is a synchronous motor not self-starting? List two starting methods."**

**Model Answer (5 lines):**

A synchronous motor is not self-starting because, at standstill, the rotor is stationary while the stator produces a rotating magnetic field at synchronous speed. The alternating torque produced during one half-cycle is reversed in the next half-cycle, giving a *net average torque of zero*. Therefore, the rotor cannot accelerate on its own. Two common starting methods are: (1) **Pony motor (auxiliary motor) starting**, and (2) **Damper winding (amortisseur) starting**. Modern EV traction motors use **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) starting** where the inverter frequency is gradually increased from 0 Hz to rated value, allowing the rotor to lock onto a slow-rotating field and then accelerate smoothly.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**"Differentiate between PMSM and BLDC motor based on back-EMF and stator current waveforms."**

**Model Answer (5 lines):**

| Parameter | PMSM | BLDC |
|---|---|---|
| Back-EMF | Sinusoidal | Trapezoidal |
| Stator current | Sinusoidal | Rectangular (quasi-square) |
| Control method | FOC / Vector control | 6-step trapezoidal commutation |
| Torque ripple | Very low | Moderate (up to 15%) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

In PMSM, the smooth sinusoidal waveforms result in lower torque ripple, making it preferred for high-end EV traction. In BLDC, the rectangular current drives a trapezoidal EMF, giving higher torque ripple but simpler, cheaper control electronics ŌĆö used in e-bikes and drones.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B has internal choice: students answer **EITHER Question A OR Question B**. Each carries **14 marks** and typically has 2 sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain the principle of operation of a 3-phase synchronous motor with a neat phasor diagram. Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque and discuss the concept of load angle. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Construction (2 marks)**

A 3-phase synchronous motor has two main parts:
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with 3-phase windings physically displaced by 120┬░ in space. The windings are supplied with balanced 3-phase AC, producing a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$.
- **Rotor:** DC-excited winding (or permanent magnets) producing a constant magnetic field with fixed N-S poles. The rotor winding is supplied via slip rings and brushes.

**Step 2: Operating Principle (2 marks)**

When the stator is energized, the RMF rotates at $N_s$. The rotor field, being stationary at start, is subjected to an alternating torque. Once the rotor is brought close to $N_s$ (by a starting method) and DC field is applied, the rotor's N-pole locks with the stator S-pole (and vice versa). The rotor is then dragged at exactly $N_s$, with a small angular lag $\delta$ called the **load angle**.

**Step 3: Phasor Diagram (1 mark)**

The phasor diagram shows:
- $V$ = Stator phase voltage (reference)
- $E_f$ = Excitation-induced EMF, lagging $V$ by angle $\delta$
- $I_a R_a$ = Stator resistance drop
- $jI_a X_s$ = Synchronous reactance drop
- $\phi$ = Power factor angle

**Step 4: Torque Derivation (2 marks)**

The power transferred across the air gap per phase is:

$$P_{gap/phase} = \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

Total 3-phase power:

$$P_{gap} = 3 \cdot \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

Mechanical power (no rotor copper loss in synchronous motor):

$$P_m = 3 \cdot \frac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta = T_e \cdot \omega_s$$

Electromagnetic torque:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$$

**Step 5: Load Angle Discussion (KEE marks)**

The load angle $\delta$ is the angle between the rotor axis and the resultant air-gap flux axis. As load increases, $\delta$ increases (up to a maximum of 90┬░). At $\delta = 90┬░$, $T_e$ is maximum ŌĆö this is the **pull-out torque**. If the mechanical load exceeds this, the motor loses synchronism and comes to a halt.

**Final Answer Summary:** $T_e = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$; pull-out occurs at $\delta = 90┬░$.

---

**(b) A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz synchronous motor has a synchronous reactance of $X_s = 10\,\Omega$ per phase. The induced EMF $E_f = 400\,\text{V}$ per phase, and the supply voltage is $V = 440\,\text{V}$ per phase. Calculate: (i) Synchronous speed, (ii) Synchronous angular speed, (iii) Torque developed at a load angle of 20┬░, (iv) Maximum torque, (v) Power developed at this load. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Synchronous Speed**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000\,\text{rpm}$$

**(ii) Synchronous Angular Speed**

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72\,\text{rad/s}$$

**(iii) Torque at $\delta = 20┬░$**

$$T_e = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta = \frac{3 \times 440 \times 400}{104.72 \times 10} \sin 20┬░$$

Numerator: $3 \times 440 \times 400 = 528000$
Denominator: $104.72 \times 10 = 1047.2$
Ratio: $528000 / 1047.2 = 504.20$
$\sin 20┬░ = 0.342$

$$T_e = 504.20 \times 0.342 = 172.43\,\text{N┬Ęm}$$

**(iv) Maximum Torque (Pull-Out, at $\delta = 90┬░$)**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} = 504.20\,\text{N┬Ęm}$$

Equivalently: $T_{max} = T_e / \sin 20┬░ = 172.43 / 0.342 = 504.20\,\text{N┬Ęm}$ Ō£ō

**(v) Power Developed**

$$P = T_e \cdot \omega_s = 172.43 \times 104.72 = 18056.4\,\text{W} \approx 18.06\,\text{kW}$$

Or equivalently: $P = 3 \cdot \dfrac{V E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta = 528000 \times 0.342 / 10 = 18057.6\,\text{W}$ Ō£ō

**Final Answer Summary:** $N_s = 1000\,\text{rpm}$, $\omega_s = 104.72\,\text{rad/s}$, $T_e = 172.43\,\text{N┬Ęm}$, $T_{max} = 504.20\,\text{N┬Ęm}$, $P = 18.06\,\text{kW}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls in This Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting factor of 3** ŌĆö Students often write per-phase torque and lose 2 marks. Always multiply by 3 for 3-phase machines.
> 2. **Using frequency instead of poles** ŌĆö $N_s = 120f/P$ is forgotten in stress; memorize it. A wrong answer here is 4 marks lost.
> 3. **Radian-degree confusion in $\sin\delta$** ŌĆö Always convert $\delta$ to degrees before using a calculator's $\sin$ button.
> 4. **Mixing up $\omega_s$ and $\omega_m$** ŌĆö $\omega_s$ is in rad/s (electrical), $\omega_m$ is mechanical. For a 6-pole motor, $\omega_s = \omega_m$ because there is no slip, but write both clearly.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption** ŌĆö Always state "neglecting stator resistance $R_a$" in synchronous motor problems unless given. Examiners reward this statement with 0.5 bonus marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]

**Q: (a) Explain the construction and working of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) with a neat diagram. How does it differ from a conventional synchronous motor? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of PMSM (3 marks):**

A PMSM consists of the following main parts:

1. **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with slots containing 3-phase distributed windings (U, V, W). The windings are excited by a 3-phase inverter to produce a rotating magnetic field.

2. **Rotor:** High-energy permanent magnets (typically Neodymium-Iron-Boron ŌĆö NdFeB, or Samarium-Cobalt ŌĆö SmCo) mounted either:
   - **On the surface (SPM):** For high-speed, low-inertia applications.
   - **Inside the rotor (IPM):** For high-performance EV traction.

3. **Shaft and Bearings:** Output shaft coupled to the gearbox, supported by sealed bearings.

4. **Position Sensor:** Encoder or resolver mounted on the shaft, providing rotor angular position to the controller for vector control.

5. **No slip rings, no brushes, no rotor field winding** ŌĆö major construction difference from conventional synchronous motor.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

When the 3-phase stator is energized by a variable-frequency inverter, a rotating magnetic field (RMF) is produced at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$. The rotor permanent magnets create a fixed N-S magnetic field. The interaction between the stator RMF and rotor PM field produces electromagnetic torque, and the rotor rotates at $N_s$, locked in synchronism with the stator field. The inverter is controlled via **FOC (Field-Oriented Control)** to vary torque and speed independently.

**Differences from Conventional Synchronous Motor (2 marks):**

| Feature | Conventional Synchronous Motor | PMSM |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor field | DC-excited winding | Permanent magnets |
| Slip rings & brushes | Yes (for DC supply) | No |
| Rotor copper loss | Yes | No |
| Efficiency | 90ŌĆō95% | 95ŌĆō98% |
| Cooling requirement | Complex (rotor needs cooling) | Simple (only stator) |
| Field weakening range | Wide (adjustable $E_f$) | Limited |
| EV application | Rare | Standard |

**Final Answer Summary:** PMSM replaces DC field winding with PM, eliminating brushes and slip rings, giving higher efficiency and power density ŌĆö making it the standard EV traction motor.

---

**(b) Explain the construction of a BLDC motor with a neat diagram. List the function of each major component. Compare the BLDC motor with a conventional brushed DC motor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**BLDC Construction (3 marks):**

A BLDC motor is essentially an "inside-out" DC motor: the magnets are on the **rotor** (instead of stator) and the windings are on the **stator** (instead of rotor).

Major components:

1. **Stator (Stationary Part):**
   - Laminated silicon steel core.
   - 3-phase windings (U, V, W) placed in slots, typically connected in star.
   - The windings are excited by a 3-phase inverter with electronic commutation.

2. **Rotor (Rotating Part):**
   - Permanent magnets (NdFeB or ferrite) ŌĆö usually 4 or 6 poles, alternating N and S.
   - Arranged on the surface (SPM) or inside (IPM).
   - Mechanically robust ŌĆö no windings, no commutator.

3. **Position Sensors:**
   - 3 Hall-effect sensors mounted on the stator, 60┬░ apart.
   - Detect rotor angular position and feed it to the controller.
   - Sensorless variants use back-EMF detection instead.

4. **Shaft and Bearings:**
   - Output shaft transmits torque to the load.
   - Sealed bearings for maintenance-free operation.

5. **Inverter/Controller (External):**
   - Not part of the motor but essential ŌĆö performs electronic commutation.
   - Replaces the mechanical commutator and brushes.

**Comparison with Brushed DC Motor (2 marks):**

| Feature | Brushed DC Motor | BLDC Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Commutation | Mechanical (brushes + commutator) | Electronic (inverter + Hall sensors) |
| Brushes | Yes (wear out, need replacement) | No |
| Maintenance | High | Almost zero |
| Rotor | Windings (heat up, limited) | Permanent magnets (cool, high power) |
| Efficiency | 75ŌĆō85% | 90ŌĆō97% |
| Speed range | Limited by brushes | Very high (no mechanical commutation) |
| Torque/Weight | Lower | Higher (rotor has no copper) |
| Cost | Low | Higher (electronics) |
| EMI | Low | Higher (switching) |
| Lifetime | Limited (brush wear) | Very long |
| EV/E-bike use | Old EVs | Modern e-bikes, drones, low-cost EVs |

**Final Answer Summary:** BLDC motor moves windings to stator and magnets to rotor, replacing mechanical commutation with electronic switching, resulting in higher efficiency, longer life, and better power density ŌĆö the standard for modern e-bikes, drones, and auxiliary EV systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö BLDC/PMSM Comparison Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "electronic commutation"** ŌĆö This is the *defining feature* of BLDC. If you miss it, expect 2 marks deducted.
> 2. **Confusing rotor vs stator** ŌĆö In BLDC, the **rotor has magnets, the stator has windings** ŌĆö opposite of brushed DC. Drawing them reversed is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Not listing Hall sensors** ŌĆö Examiners consider this a compulsory component. Mentioning "3 Hall sensors at 60┬░ electrical spacing" fetches full marks.
> 4. **Missing the role of the inverter** ŌĆö The inverter is *external* to the motor but functionally part of the system. Saying "BLDC needs an inverter" is required.
> 5. **Not stating "no brushes" twice** ŌĆö Once in construction, once in comparison. Examiners reward repetition of key phrases.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this checklist the night before your KTU University Exam ŌĆö it contains every high-yield point from this topic.**

### ŌÜĪ Synchronous Motor Essentials
- [ ] Synchronous speed formula: $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ (in rpm)
- [ ] Synchronous angular speed: $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi N_s}{60}$ (in rad/s)
- [ ] Slip $s = 0$ in synchronous motor (always)
- [ ] Torque equation: $T_e = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta$
- [ ] Pull-out torque at $\delta = 90┬░$, $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V E_f}{\omega_s X_s}$
- [ ] Not self-starting because RMF alternates faster than rotor can follow
- [ ] Three starting methods: **Pony motor**, **Damper winding**, **Variable Frequency**
- [ ] Damper winding acts as induction motor during start, pulls into step at ~95% $N_s$
- [ ] VFD is the standard EV starting method (inverter ramps frequency from 0 Hz)

### ŌÜĪ PMSM Essentials
- [ ] Permanent magnets replace DC field winding ŌåÆ no slip rings, no brushes
- [ ] Typical magnets: **NdFeB** (most common) or **SmCo** (high-temp)
- [ ] Two rotor types: **SPM** (Surface Permanent Magnet) and **IPM** (Interior Permanent Magnet)
- [ ] IPM is preferred in EVs because it provides *reluctance torque* (free boost)
- [ ] d-q voltage equations (must memorize both)
- [ ] Torque equation: $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} p \left[ \psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$
- [ ] Two torque components: **magnet (alignment) torque** + **reluctance torque**
- [ ] $i_d = 0$ control is used for **SPM** (linear torque-current)
- [ ] Negative $i_d$ is used for **field-weakening** at high speed in IPM
- [ ] Standard EV control: **FOC (Field-Oriented Control)** aka vector control
- [ ] Used in: **Tesla Model 3, Toyota Prius, BMW i3, Tata Nexon EV, Ather 450X**

### ŌÜĪ BLDC Motor Essentials
- [ ] "Inside-out" DC motor: magnets on rotor, windings on stator
- [ ] Electronic commutation replaces mechanical brushes + commutator
- [ ] **Trapezoidal back-EMF** and **rectangular stator current**
- [ ] **3 Hall-effect sensors** at 60┬░ electrical spacing for rotor position
- [ ] 6-step commutation sequence (6 switching states per electrical cycle)
- [ ] Torque equation: $T_e = k_t I$ (linear, like brushed DC)
- [ ] Power density: high (rotor has only magnets, no copper)
- [ ] Efficiency: 90ŌĆō97%
- [ ] Advantages: no brush maintenance, long life, high efficiency
- [ ] Disadvantages: requires position sensor and inverter, higher cost
- [ ] Applications: **e-bikes, drones, low-power EVs, computer fans, washing machines**
- [ ] Sensorless BLDC uses back-EMF zero-crossing detection

### ŌÜĪ Key Differences to Remember (Memorize for Comparison Questions)
- [ ] **PMSM** = sinusoidal back-EMF, sinusoidal current, FOC control
- [ ] **BLDC** = trapezoidal back-EMF, rectangular current, 6-step control
- [ ] **Synchronous motor** = DC field winding, needs slip rings, can use damper for start
- [ ] **PMSM/BLDC** = PM on rotor, no slip rings, need VFD for start
- [ ] **SPM**: $L_d = L_q$, no reluctance torque, lower cost
- [ ] **IPM**: $L_d < L_q$, reluctance torque adds boost, preferred in EV traction

### ŌÜĪ Final High-Yield Numerical Problem-Solving Steps
- [ ] Step 1: Identify P (poles), f (Hz), V, E_f, X_s, ╬┤ from the problem
- [ ] Step 2: Compute $N_s = 120f/P$
- [ ] Step 3: Compute $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$
- [ ] Step 4: Compute $T_e = (3 V E_f / (\omega_s X_s)) \sin\delta$
- [ ] Step 5: For pull-out, set $\delta = 90┬░$, $\sin\delta = 1$
- [ ] Step 6: For power, use $P = T_e \cdot \omega_s$
- [ ] Always state assumption: "neglecting stator resistance $R_a$" if not given
- [ ] Always include units in the final answer (N┬Ęm, rad/s, rpm, kW)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ŌÜÖ’ĖÅ MODULE 4: WORKING PRINCIPLE OF EV DRIVE MOTORS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 SYLLABUS FOCUS:** Module 4 of PCMUT503 focuses on the **Working Principle, construction, and control of electric drive motors** used as prime movers in Electric Vehicles. The principal machines examined are the **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor**, **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)**, **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)**, and **Induction Motor (IM)**.

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Working Principle** of an electric drive motor refers to the electromagnetic mechanism by which electrical energy is converted into mechanical rotational energy through the interaction of magnetic fields, governed by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the **Lorentz Force Equation**. In the context of Electric Vehicles, the drive motor must deliver high starting torque, wide constant-power operating range, high efficiency (> **90%**), and regenerative braking capability.

> **FŌéś = BIL** (Lorentz Force) and **e = -N(d╬”/dt)** (Faraday's Law)
> 
> where **FŌéś** is the mechanical force, **B** is the magnetic flux density (in **Tesla**), **I** is the current (in **Amperes**), **L** is the length of the conductor (in **meters**), **e** is the induced EMF (in **Volts**), **N** is the number of turns, and **╬”** is the magnetic flux (in **Weber**).

### ­¤¦Ā Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Magnetic Tug-of-War"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine two bar magnets placed on a turntable. The **rotor** (the part that spins) is a permanent magnet, and the **stator** (the part that stays still) has coils of wire. When you push current through the coils, they temporarily become electromagnets. By cleverly switching the current between coils in the **correct sequence**, you create a *rotating magnetic field* that "chases" the permanent magnet on the rotor. The permanent magnet always tries to catch up with this rotating field ŌĆö and this "tug-of-war" produces continuous rotation. The motor controller (an **inverter**) is the "referee" that switches the current at precisely the right time.

### ­¤öŹ Key Physical Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Supply Voltage (V)** | 48 V ŌĆō 800 V (typical EV traction) | High voltage for power density |
| **Efficiency (╬Ę)** | **90% ŌĆō 97%** | Higher than ICE (~30-40%) |
| **Specific Power** | 3 ŌĆō 6 kW/kg | Power-to-weight ratio |
| **Base Speed** | 1500 ŌĆō 4000 rpm | Cruise operating point |
| **Max Speed** | 10,000 ŌĆō 20,000 rpm | Field-weakening region |
| **Torque Density** | 5 ŌĆō 10 Nm/kg | Compact packaging |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The 2024 Scheme NEP 2020 curriculum emphasizes **CO-based learning** ŌĆö students must demonstrate ability to *explain*, *analyze*, and *compare* the working principles. Rote memorization is penalised.

---

## 1.2 Classification of EV Drive Motors (Module 4 Context)

The following motor types are typically covered under "Working Principle" in Module 4:

1. **DC Motor** ŌĆö Simple but bulky; rarely used in modern EVs
2. **BLDC Motor** ŌĆö Most common in 2W/3W EVs
3. **PMSM** ŌĆö Dominant in premium 4W EVs (Tesla, Nissan Leaf, BMW i3)
4. **SRM** ŌĆö Rugged, fault-tolerant (used in some commercial vehicles)
5. **Three-Phase Induction Motor** ŌĆö Used in Tesla Roadster, Tata Nexon EV (older variants)

The remainder of this document focuses on the **detailed working principle of BLDC and PMSM** as these constitute **60-70% of KTU examination questions** on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōś DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Working Principle of a BLDC Motor

### ŌÜĪ Conceptual Breakdown

A **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** is a synchronous motor with a **trapezoidal back-EMF** waveform and **rectangular stator current** excitation. The "brushless" designation refers to the absence of mechanical commutator-brush assembly ŌĆö commutation is performed **electronically** using a 3-phase inverter.

### ­¤öä Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Rotor Construction:** Permanent magnets (NdFeB or SmCo) mounted on the surface or interior of a ferromagnetic rotor core. The number of pole pairs **p** typically ranges from **2 to 8**.
   
2. **Stator Construction:** Three-phase star-connected (Y) or delta-connected winding distributed in slots. Each phase is energized by an **electronic commutator** (inverter bridge with 6 IGBTs/MOSFETs).

3. **Rotor Position Sensing:** **Hall-effect sensors** (3 sensors, 120┬░ apart) detect the angular position of the rotor and send feedback to the **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**.

4. **Sequential Excitation:** Based on Hall sensor signals, the ECU energizes exactly **two of the three phases** at any instant. The energized phases create a **stator magnetic field** that is always **120┬░ ahead** of the rotor field in terms of electrical angle.

5. **Torque Production:** The interaction between the stator and rotor fields produces an **attractive/repulsive force** causing rotation. The torque developed is given by:

   > **T = (3/2) ├Ś p ├Ś [╬©_m ├Ś I_q + (L_d ŌłÆ L_q) ├Ś I_d ├Ś I_q]**

6. **Commutation Sequence:** In a 6-step trapezoidal commutation, there are **6 switching states per electrical cycle** (each lasting 60┬░ electrical). At any instant, one phase is "ON-positive," one is "ON-negative," and one is floating.

7. **Self-Synchronization:** Unlike a brushed DC motor, the BLDC rotor *must* rotate in synchronism with the stator rotating field. The Hall sensors ensure precise synchronization at all speeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept ŌĆö The "Why" Behind Brushless Design:**
> Mechanical brushes in conventional DC motors suffer from **wear, sparking, EMI, and low reliability**. By replacing mechanical commutation with **electronic switching**, BLDC motors achieve **5-10x longer life**, **higher efficiency (85-95%)**, and **better heat dissipation** (since windings are on the stator, which can be water/oil cooled).

### ­¤ōŖ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **e = K_e ├Ś Žē** | Back-EMF proportional to angular velocity | e [V], Žē [rad/s] |
| 2 | **T = K_t ├Ś I** | Electromagnetic torque | T [Nm], I [A] |
| 3 | **K_e = K_t** (in SI) | EMF constant = Torque constant | V┬Ęs/rad = Nm/A |
| 4 | **Žē = (V ŌłÆ I┬ĘR) / K_e** | Speed equation (steady-state) | rad/s |
| 5 | **T = (P_out / Žē)** | Mechanical output power relationship | Nm |
| 6 | **P_mech = T ├Ś Žē = 3┬ĘV_ph┬ĘI_ph┬Ęcos(Žå)** | Power balance for 3-phase | Watts |
| 7 | **f_e = (p ├Ś N) / 120** | Electrical frequency from RPM | Hz |
| 8 | **╬Ė_e = p ├Ś ╬Ė_m** | Electrical angle to mechanical angle | radians |
| 9 | **╬Ę = P_out / P_in = T┬ĘŽē / (V┬ĘI)** | Efficiency of motor drive | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Žä = L/R** | Electrical time constant of winding | seconds |

> **Variables Glossary:**
> - **K_e** = Back-EMF constant (V┬Ęs/rad)
> - **K_t** = Torque constant (Nm/A)
> - **p** = Number of pole pairs
> - **N** = Speed in RPM
> - **╬Ė_e, ╬Ė_m** = Electrical and mechanical angles
> - **╬©_m** = Permanent magnet flux linkage (Wb)
> - **L_d, L_q** = d-axis and q-axis inductances (H)
> - **I_d, I_q** = d-axis and q-axis currents (A)

---

## 2.2 Working Principle of a PMSM

The **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** is the premium choice for high-performance EVs due to its **sinusoidal back-EMF** and **smooth torque production**.

### Operating Principle

1. The stator carries a **3-phase distributed winding** producing a **sinusoidal MMF** distribution.
2. The rotor carries **surface-mounted or interior permanent magnets** providing a **constant rotor flux ╬©_m**.
3. A **3-phase voltage source inverter (VSI)** with **SVPWM (Space Vector Pulse Width Modulation)** generates a **sinusoidal stator current** that produces a smooth rotating field.
4. The rotor magnets lock with this rotating field and rotate at **synchronous speed**:

   > **N_s = (120 ├Ś f_e) / p**

5. The electromagnetic torque in the **d-q reference frame** (rotating with the rotor) is:

   > **T_e = (3/2) ├Ś p ├Ś [╬©_m ├Ś I_q + (L_d ŌłÆ L_q) ├Ś I_d ├Ś I_q]**

   For a **surface-mounted PMSM (SPMSM)**, L_d = L_q, so the **reluctance term vanishes**, giving:
   > **T_e = (3/2) ├Ś p ├Ś ╬©_m ├Ś I_q**

6. For **interior PMSM (IPMSM)**, L_d ŌēĀ L_q, and the **reluctance torque** component adds to the total torque, providing higher power density.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** PMSMs are used in **Tesla Model S/3/X, BMW i-series, Toyota Prius** hybrid system, and **Renault Zoe**. Their high efficiency (> **95%**), high power density, and excellent dynamic response make them the gold standard for modern EVs.

### ­¤ōŖ Comparison Table: BLDC vs PMSM (High-Yield for KTU)

| Feature | BLDC | PMSM |
|---|---|---|
| **Back-EMF** | Trapezoidal | Sinusoidal |
| **Current Waveform** | Rectangular (6-step) | Sinusoidal (SVPWM) |
| **Torque Ripple** | Higher (~10-15%) | Lower (~2-5%) |
| **Controller Cost** | Lower (simple 6-step) | Higher (SVPWM DSP) |
| **Efficiency** | 85-92% | 92-97% |
| **NVH Performance** | Noisier | Quieter |
| **Position Sensor** | Hall sensors (3) | Resolver/Encoder (high-res) |
| **Application** | 2W, 3W, low-cost EVs | 4W premium EVs |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | Very High |

---

## 2.3 Working Principle of Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)

The SRM operates on a fundamentally different principle ŌĆö **variable reluctance**.

1. The rotor is a **solid salient-pole iron piece** with **no magnets or windings**.
2. The stator has **concentrated windings** on salient poles.
3. When a phase is energized, the rotor moves to a position of **minimum reluctance** (maximum inductance) to align the excited stator pole pair.
4. Sequential phase excitation produces rotation.
5. Torque equation:

   > **T = (1/2) ├Ś i┬▓ ├Ś (dL/d╬Ė)**

The negative sign of dL/d╬Ė ensures torque is always in the direction of decreasing inductance.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** SRM is fault-tolerant, robust, and can operate at very high temperatures. It is increasingly used in commercial EVs and aerospace applications. Expect a 7-mark question on SRM working principle.

---

## 2.4 Working Principle of Three-Phase Inverter (The "Heart" of EV Drive)

The **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is the power electronic interface between the **DC battery** and the **AC motor**.

### Operating Steps

1. **DC Input:** Battery pack (e.g., 400V DC) feeds a large DC-link capacitor for ripple smoothing.
2. **Six IGBTs/MOSFETs:** Arranged in three legs (one per phase). Each leg has a **high-side** and **low-side** switch.
3. **PWM Modulation:** Control signals (SVPWM or SPWM) turn switches ON/OFF at high frequency (typically **5-20 kHz**).
4. **3-Phase AC Output:** The switching creates a **3-phase PWM-modulated AC voltage** with adjustable magnitude and frequency.
5. **Motor Response:** The motor's inductance filters the PWM to produce near-sinusoidal current.

The **line-to-line RMS output voltage** in linear modulation region:

> **V_LL(rms) = (ŌłÜ3/2ŌłÜ2) ├Ś m_a ├Ś V_dc = 0.612 ├Ś m_a ├Ś V_dc**

where **m_a** is the **modulation index** (0 Ōēż m_a Ōēż 1).

> **Engineering Utility:** In overmodulation (m_a > 1) and six-step mode, maximum fundamental voltage is reached, enabling **field-weakening** operation at high speeds.

# ­¤¦« STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Torque Equation of a PMSM in d-q Reference Frame

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Derivation:** This derivation appears almost every year in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for 7-14 marks. Master it completely.

### Given:

- 3-phase stator voltages: **v_a, v_b, v_c**
- 3-phase stator currents: **i_a, i_b, i_c**
- Rotor permanent magnet flux linkage: **╬©_m**
- Stator phase resistance: **R_s**
- d-axis and q-axis inductances: **L_d, L_q**
- Pole pairs: **p**

### Step 1: Write the stator voltage equations in the natural (a-b-c) frame

$$v_a = R_s \cdot i_a + \frac{d\Psi_a}{dt}$$

$$v_b = R_s \cdot i_b + \frac{d\Psi_b}{dt}$$

$$v_c = R_s \cdot i_c + \frac{d\Psi_c}{dt}$$

> The flux linkages **╬©_a, ╬©_b, ╬©_c** are functions of the rotor position **╬Ė_e** (since rotor magnets rotate).

### Step 2: Apply Park's Transformation to convert to d-q frame

The Park transformation matrix converts balanced 3-phase quantities into a **two-axis rotating reference frame** aligned with the rotor flux:

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_d \\ v_q \\ v_0 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \cos(\theta_e - 120┬░) & \cos(\theta_e + 120┬░) \\ -\sin\theta_e & -\sin(\theta_e - 120┬░) & -\sin(\theta_e + 120┬░) \\ \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_a \\ v_b \\ v_c \end{bmatrix}$$

> The **d-axis** is aligned with the rotor magnet flux; the **q-axis** is 90┬░ ahead. This transformation eliminates the position-dependence of inductances, simplifying the analysis dramatically.

### Step 3: Obtain the simplified d-q voltage equations

After transformation, the position-dependent inductances become constant:

$$v_d = R_s \cdot i_d + L_d \cdot \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e \cdot L_q \cdot i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s \cdot i_q + L_q \cdot \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e \cdot (L_d \cdot i_d + \Psi_m)$$

> **Where:**
> - **Žē_e = p┬ĘŽē_m** = electrical angular velocity (rad/s)
> - The term **Žē_e┬Ę╬©_m** is the **back-EMF** in the q-axis equation
> - The terms **ŌłÆŽē_e┬ĘL_q┬Ęi_q** and **+Žē_e┬ĘL_d┬Ęi_d** are **cross-coupling terms** representing the rotational EMF

### Step 4: Compute the electromagnetic power input

For a balanced 3-phase system, the total input power is:

$$P_{in} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot (v_d \cdot i_d + v_q \cdot i_q)$$

### Step 5: Substitute the voltage equations into the power equation

$$P_{in} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \left[ (R_s \cdot i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q) i_d + (R_s \cdot i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \Psi_m)) i_q \right]$$

### Step 6: Expand and identify the loss terms

The copper losses (resistive heating) are:

$$P_{cu} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot R_s \cdot (i_d^2 + i_q^2)$$

The rate of change of stored magnetic energy is:

$$P_{field} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \left( L_d i_d \frac{di_d}{dt} + L_q i_q \frac{di_q}{dt} \right)$$

### Step 7: Isolate the air-gap (electromechanical conversion) power

Subtracting the losses from input power:

$$P_{gap} = P_{in} - P_{cu} - P_{field} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \omega_e \cdot \left[ \Psi_m \cdot i_q + (L_d - L_q) \cdot i_d \cdot i_q \right]$$

### Step 8: Convert air-gap power to electromagnetic torque

Since **P_gap = T_e ├Ś Žē_m** and **Žē_e = p ├Ś Žē_m**, we have:

$$T_e \cdot \omega_m = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \omega_m \cdot \left[ \Psi_m \cdot i_q + (L_d - L_q) \cdot i_d \cdot i_q \right]$$

### Step 9: Final torque equation

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \left[ \Psi_m \cdot i_q + (L_d - L_q) \cdot i_d \cdot i_q \right]}$$

> **First term (╬©_m┬ĘI_q):** The **magnet/excitation torque** ŌĆö present in all PMSMs.
> **Second term (L_dŌłÆL_q)┬ĘI_d┬ĘI_q:** The **reluctance torque** ŌĆö present only in IPMSMs (salient-pole machines).

### Special Case: Surface-mounted PMSM (SPMSM)

For SPMSM, **L_d = L_q = L_s**, so:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \Psi_m \cdot I_q$$

This shows that torque is controlled **linearly** by the q-axis current ŌĆö a major advantage for EV control.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Speed Equation of a Brushed DC Motor (Baseline)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **simplest motor** in Module 4 and is often the starting point of KTU questions on EV drive working principles.

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature circuit

$$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$

where:
- **V** = applied armature voltage (V)
- **E_b** = back-EMF (V)
- **I_a** = armature current (A)
- **R_a** = armature resistance (╬®)

### Step 2: Express back-EMF in terms of speed

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega_m$$

> **╬”** is the field flux per pole (Wb). For a separately excited or shunt motor, ╬” is approximately constant.

### Step 3: Substitute and solve for speed

$$V = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega_m + I_a \cdot R_a$$

$$\omega_m = \frac{V - I_a \cdot R_a}{K_e \cdot \Phi}$$

### Step 4: Speed-torque relationship

Since **T = K_t┬Ę╬”┬ĘI_a**, we have **I_a = T / (K_t┬Ę╬”)**:

$$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V}{K_e \cdot \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K_e \cdot K_t \cdot \Phi^2)} \cdot T}$$

> This is a **straight-line** equation: as torque T increases, speed decreases linearly. The **no-load speed** is **V/(K_e┬Ę╬”)**, and the **stall torque** is **(V┬ĘK_t┬Ę╬”)/R_a**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Working Principle of All EV Motors

| Motor Type | Governing Physical Law | Rotor | Stator | Commutation | Speed (N_s) | Torque Ripple | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brushed DC** | Lorentz Force | Wound armature | Permanent magnet field | Mechanical (brushes) | N = (VŌłÆI┬ĘR)/K┬Ę╬” | Medium | 70-85% |
| **BLDC** | Lorentz Force | Permanent magnets | 3-phase winding | Electronic (Hall/6-step) | N_s = 120f/p | High (6-step) | 85-92% |
| **PMSM** | Lorentz + Reluctance | Permanent magnets | 3-phase winding | Electronic (SVPWM) | N_s = 120f/p | Low (sinusoidal) | 92-97% |
| **SRM** | Variable Reluctance | Salient iron (no magnet) | Concentrated winding | Electronic (asymmetric) | N = variable | Very High | 85-90% |
| **Induction Motor** | Faraday's Induction | Squirrel-cage / Wound | 3-phase winding | Indirect (V/f control) | N_s = 120f/p (sync) | Low | 90-95% |

# ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö EV Powertrain Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Battery Pack] --> B[DC Link Capacitor]
    B --> C[3-Phase VSI Inverter]
    C --> D[Electric Drive Motor]
    D --> E[Transmission Gearbox]
    E --> F[Wheels]
    G[VCU ECU] --> H[Position Sensor]
    H --> G
    G --> C
    I[Pedal Accelerator] --> G
    J[Brake Pedal] --> G
    G --> K[Regenerative Braking Control]
    K --> C
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Energy flows from the battery through the inverter to the motor, and finally to the wheels. The VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) is the brain that orchestrates the inverter switching based on pedal inputs and rotor position feedback. During braking, the inverter reverses power flow ŌĆö the motor acts as a generator, charging the battery.

---

## 4.2 6-Step Commutation Sequence of a BLDC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: 0 to 60 deg] --> S2[Step 2: 60 to 120 deg]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: 120 to 180 deg]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: 180 to 240 deg]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: 240 to 300 deg]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: 300 to 360 deg]
    S6 --> S1
    SA[Phase A HIGH] -.-> S1
    SB[Phase B HIGH] -.-> S2
    SC[Phase C HIGH] -.-> S3
    SD[Phase A HIGH] -.-> S4
    SE[Phase B HIGH] -.-> S5
    SF[Phase C HIGH] -.-> S6
```

> **Explanation:** In each 60┬░ electrical interval, the inverter energizes one phase as positive and another as negative. The third phase is floating. The Hall sensor signals determine which pair is active at any instant.

---

## 4.3 d-q Reference Frame Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[abc Natural Frame] --> B[Clarke Transform]
    B --> C[alpha-beta Stationary Frame]
    C --> D[Park Transform]
    D --> E[dq Rotating Frame]
    E --> F[Torque Calculation]
    F --> G[Inverse Park Transform]
    G --> H[SVPWM Generation]
    H --> I[Inverter Switching Signals]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The control algorithm first transforms measured 3-phase currents into the d-q frame, computes the required voltage vectors, and then transforms back to 3-phase switching signals for the inverter.

---

## 4.4 Power Flow Modes in an EV

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Demand] --> B{Mode}
    B -->|Accelerate| C[Forward Motoring]
    B -->|Decelerate| D[Regenerative Braking]
    B -->|Idle| E[Coast / Stop]

```

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Explain the working principle of a BLDC motor in an EV with a neat block diagram. Why is it called "brushless"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Brushless DC (BLDC) motor is a synchronous motor in which the rotor carries **permanent magnets** and the stator carries **three-phase windings**. The term "brushless" refers to the **absence of mechanical brushes and commutator** ŌĆö commutation is achieved **electronically** using a 3-phase inverter and rotor position feedback (Hall sensors). The stator windings are energized in a **6-step sequence** based on rotor position, producing a **rotating magnetic field** that drags the permanent-magnet rotor along synchronously. The principle of operation is governed by **Faraday's Law** and the **Lorentz Force Law**. BLDC motors are preferred in EVs due to their **high efficiency (85-92%)**, **long life**, **low maintenance**, and **high power density**.

> **Mark Distribution:** (1) Brushless explanation ŌĆö 1m; (2) Working principle ŌĆö 1m; (3) EV advantages ŌĆö 1m.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]
**List any three differences between the working principle of a BLDC motor and a PMSM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No | BLDC | PMSM |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Trapezoidal back-EMF and rectangular current (6-step) | Sinusoidal back-EMF and sinusoidal current (SVPWM) |
| 2 | Higher torque ripple (~10-15%) | Lower torque ripple (~2-5%) |
| 3 | Position sensed by Hall sensors (low resolution) | Position sensed by resolver/encoder (high resolution) |
| 4 | Simpler, cheaper controller | More complex DSP-based controller |

> **Mark Distribution:** Any three valid differences ŌĆö 1m each.

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ŌØō QUESTION A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Model]

**(a)** Derive the electromagnetic torque equation of a **3-phase PMSM** in the d-q reference frame. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of the EV drivetrain**, including the role of the inverter, motor, and VCU. **(7 marks)**

#### Ō£ģ MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (a) (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Balanced 3-phase sinusoidal supply
2. Sinusoidal MMF distribution
3. Magnetic linearity (no saturation)
4. Negligible core losses
5. Symmetric, non-salient rotor for SPMSM

**Derivation Steps (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the stator voltage equations in a-b-c frame (1 mark):

$$v_a = R_s i_a + \frac{d\Psi_a}{dt}, \quad v_b = R_s i_b + \frac{d\Psi_b}{dt}, \quad v_c = R_s i_c + \frac{d\Psi_c}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Park's transformation to convert to d-q frame. The rotor position-dependent inductances become constant **L_d** and **L_q** (1 mark):

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \Psi_m)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the input power from 3-phase (0.5 marks):

$$P_{in} = \frac{3}{2}(v_d i_d + v_q i_q)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting, expanding, and separating losses (copper loss and field energy) gives the air-gap power (1 mark):

$$P_{gap} = \frac{3}{2} \omega_e \left[ \Psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$$

**Step 5:** Since **P_gap = T_e┬ĘŽē_m** and **Žē_e = p┬ĘŽē_m** (0.5 mark):

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{3}{2} p \left[ \Psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]}$$

**Step 6:** Final result with physical interpretation (1 mark):
- The first term is the **magnet torque** (always present).
- The second term is the **reluctance torque** (only in salient-pole IPMSM).

#### Ō£ģ MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (b) (7 marks)

**EV Drivetrain Working Principle:**

The EV drivetrain converts **electrical energy stored in the battery** into **mechanical energy at the wheels** through coordinated operation of the inverter, motor, and VCU (1 mark).

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Battery] ŌåÆ [DC-Link] ŌåÆ [3-Žå VSI Inverter] ŌåÆ [Traction Motor] ŌåÆ [Gearbox] ŌåÆ [Wheels]
                Ōåæ                              Ōåæ
              [VCU] ŌåÉ [Pedal]              [Position Sensor]
                Ōåō
         [Inverter Gate Signals]
```

**Detailed Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Battery Pack:** Provides high-voltage DC (e.g., 400V) as the primary energy source. Battery Management System (BMS) monitors SOC, voltage, current, and temperature.

2. **DC-Link Capacitor:** Smooths voltage ripple and provides instantaneous current to the inverter during switching transients.

3. **3-Phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI):** Six IGBTs/MOSFETs convert DC to variable-voltage, variable-frequency 3-phase AC. The switching pattern is generated by SVPWM in the VCU.

4. **Traction Motor:** BLDC/PMSM/IM converts electrical to mechanical energy. The motor's torque and speed are controlled by the inverter's voltage magnitude and frequency.

5. **Transmission/Gearbox:** A single-speed reducer (typical ratio 8:1 to 12:1) adapts the motor's high-speed output (10,000-15,000 rpm) to the wheel's lower speed range.

6. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU):** The "brain" that:
   - Reads accelerator pedal position
   - Reads brake pedal and rotor position
   - Computes torque demand
   - Generates SVPWM signals for the inverter
   - Manages regenerative braking

7. **Regenerative Braking Mode:** During deceleration, the motor acts as a **generator**. The inverter rectifies the back-EMF to charge the battery, recovering kinetic energy.

### ŌØō QUESTION B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024] ŌĆö ALTERNATIVE

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** with a suitable diagram. Derive its torque equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **working principle and characteristics** of BLDC and Induction Motor drives for EV applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Ō£ģ MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (a) (7 marks)

**SRM Construction (1 mark):**

The SRM has a **doubly-salient** structure. The **stator** has concentrated windings on salient poles (e.g., 8/6, 6/4 configurations). The **rotor** is a simple **solid salient-pole iron piece** with **no magnets, no windings, no cage**.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

When a stator phase is energized, the rotor experiences a **torque** that moves it to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** (maximum inductance). The rotor always tries to **align with the energized stator pole pair**. By sequentially energizing the phases (A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ ...), continuous rotation is achieved. The direction of rotation is determined by the **sequence** of phase excitation, not by any polarity of the current.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A simple cross-sectional view of an 8/6 SRM showing rotor alignment with the excited phase.

**Torque Derivation (2 marks):**

From the principle of virtual work, the instantaneous electromagnetic torque is:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m} \bigg\vert_{i=const} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta_m}$$

**Where:**
- **W'_co** = Co-energy in the magnetic field
- **L(╬Ė_m)** = Phase inductance as a function of rotor position
- **i** = Phase current

**Key Features (1 mark):**
- Torque is **independent of current polarity** ŌĆö only the **magnitude** of dL/d╬Ė matters
- Operates in **motoring** mode when dL/d╬Ė > 0
- Operates in **generating** mode when dL/d╬Ė < 0
- Highly **fault-tolerant** ŌĆö if one phase fails, motor still runs

> **Mark Distribution:** Construction ŌĆö 1m; Working ŌĆö 3m; Diagram ŌĆö 1m; Derivation ŌĆö 2m.

#### Ō£ģ MODEL SOLUTION ŌĆö Part (b) (7 marks)

**Detailed Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | BLDC Motor | Induction Motor |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Lorentz force: PM rotor synchronously follows stator rotating field | Faraday's induction: induced current in rotor interacts with stator field |
| **Rotor Type** | Permanent magnet rotor | Squirrel-cage rotor (no magnets) |
| **Speed** | Synchronous (N_s = 120f/p) | Asynchronous (slip-dependent) |
| **Rotor Position Sensor** | Required (Hall/resolver) | Not strictly required (sensorless V/f possible) |
| **Starting Torque** | High (suitable for EV) | High (cage design) |
| **Field Weakening** | Difficult (constant PM flux) | Easy (adjust rotor slip and V/f) |
| **Efficiency** | High (no rotor copper loss) | Lower (rotor copper + core loss) |
| **Cooling** | Stator-only (easier) | Both stator and rotor (harder) |
| **Cost** | Higher (PM material cost) | Lower (no rare-earth magnets) |
| **Robustness** | Sensitive to high temperature (PM demagnetization) | Very robust |
| **KTU EV Examples** | Most 2W/3W EVs, low-cost 4W | Tesla Roadster (early), Tata Nexon EV (older) |

**Conclusion (3 marks):**

- **BLDC is preferred** for **cost-sensitive 2W/3W applications** and where **sensorless control** is acceptable. Its high efficiency and compact size make it ideal for small EVs.
- **Induction Motor is preferred** for **high-power 4W applications** where **field-weakening range** is critical and **ruggedness** is essential. Tesla's use of IM demonstrated its viability for performance EVs.
- **Modern trend:** PMSM is gradually replacing both in premium 4W EVs due to its **superior efficiency (>95%)** and **power density**.

> **Mark Distribution:** Working principle explanation ŌĆö 2m; Detailed comparison ŌĆö 4m; Conclusion/Justification ŌĆö 1m.

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Common Pitfalls

- ŌśÉ Confusing **electrical** angle (╬Ė_e) with **mechanical** angle (╬Ė_m) ŌĆö remember **╬Ė_e = p┬Ę╬Ė_m**.
- ŌśÉ Forgetting that **Žē_e = p┬ĘŽē_m**, not equal.

- ŌśÉ **OLA S1 Pro, Ather 450X:** BLDC (with PMSM upgrade in newer models)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś MODULE 4: ADVANCED MOTOR DRIVES FOR ELECTRIC VEHICLES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor ŌĆö Speed-Torque Characteristics

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Speed-Torque Characteristic** of a BLDC motor is the graphical and mathematical relationship between the developed electromagnetic torque ($T_e$) and the mechanical rotor speed ($N$ or $\omega_m$) under steady-state operating conditions, typically represented as a near-linear inverse relationship in the constant-torque and constant-power regions of operation.

The characteristic is governed by the fundamental BLDC motor equation:

$$T_e = \frac{P}{\omega_m} = \frac{V_t \cdot I_a - I_a^2 R_a}{\omega_m}$$

For an ideal BLDC drive with trapezoidal back-EMF, the torque developed per phase is proportional to the phase current, while the back-EMF is proportional to speed, giving the iconic linear characteristic.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
> [!NOTE]
> Think of a BLDC motor like a person pedaling a bicycle up a hill. When the hill is flat (low load, low back-EMF), the person pedals fast (high speed, low torque). As the hill gets steeper (high load, high back-EMF), the pedaling slows down (low speed) but the force on the pedals increases (high torque). The motor behaves the same way ŌĆö speed drops linearly as load torque increases, up to a base speed. Beyond that, the controller enters a "field-weakening" mode where torque drops off rapidly with rising speed, much like shifting to a lower gear on a steep climb.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** BLDC motors are widely preferred in EV applications (e.g., Tesla Roadster, Nissan Leaf early models, Tata Nexon EV) because their flat speed-torque curve delivers maximum torque from zero speed ŌĆö perfect for vehicle launch and hill climbing.

---

### 1.2 Direct Torque Control (DTC) Drive for BLDC Motor in EV Application

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Torque Control (DTC)** is a sensorless or low-sensor vector control method in which the stator flux linkage and electromagnetic torque are directly regulated using hysteresis comparators and a switching table, eliminating the need for current controllers, PWM modulators, and coordinate transformations (Park/Clarke transforms) used in conventional Field-Oriented Control (FOC).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine driving a car with two knobs: a "speed/force" knob and a "direction" knob. Instead of slowly modulating the accelerator pedal to control speed, you just push the "force" knob harder when you need more pull, and tap the "direction" knob to switch between forward and reverse push. DTC works exactly like this ŌĆö it directly tells the inverter "give me more torque NOW" or "give me less torque NOW" using a switching lookup table, rather than computing currents first and then applying voltages. This makes it ultra-fast (response in microseconds) ŌĆö ideal for the sudden torque demands of an EV during overtaking or hill-start.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DTC for BLDC in EV applications was popularized by researchers like **French scientists Depenbrock and Takahashi (mid-1980s)**. The key advantage is the **absence of PI current loops and PWM blocks**, making it computationally light and dynamically superior for traction drives.

---

### 1.3 Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) ŌĆö Working Principle

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** is a doubly-salient, singly-excited rotary electric machine that produces torque purely by the tendency of its salient rotor to move to a position of minimum magnetic reluctance with respect to the excited stator phase, with the rotor having **no permanent magnets, no rotor windings, and no commutator**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
> [!NOTE]
> Picture a pair of strong magnets on a wall, and a metal dog (iron rotor) on a leash. The magnets attract the dog, and as soon as the dog gets close, you turn the magnet off and turn on the next magnet ahead of it ŌĆö making the dog "leap" forward. Each leap is one stroke. The SRM works the same way: stator phases are energized in a sequence (A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ AŌĆ”) and the iron rotor (with no magnets) snaps toward the energized phase, producing motion. The "reluctance" is simply the magnetic resistance ŌĆö the rotor always wants to find the path of least magnetic resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SRM is often called the "**poor man's**" EV motor because it uses **no rare-earth magnets** (no Neodymium, no Dysprosium), making it cheaper and supply-chain resilient. It is used in the **Renault Fluence Z.E.** and some **Tesla concept drives**.

---

## 2. Common Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Air gap flux density** | $B_g$ | **0.4 ŌĆō 0.7 T** |
| **Back-EMF constant** | $K_e$ | **V┬Ęs/rad** |
| **Torque constant** | $K_t$ | **Nm/A** |
| **Per-phase resistance** | $R$ | **0.1 ŌĆō 2.0 ╬®** |
| **Per-phase inductance (aligned)** | $L_a$ | **mH range** |
| **Per-phase inductance (unaligned)** | $L_u$ | **┬ĄH range** |
| **Inductance ratio** | $\xi = L_a/L_u$ | **5 ŌĆō 10 (typical)** |
| **DC bus voltage of EV** | $V_{dc}$ | **300 ŌĆō 800 V** |
| **Hysteresis band (DTC)** | $2\Delta T$ | **┬▒5% to ┬▒10% of rated torque** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 BLDC Motor ŌĆö Speed-Torque Characteristics: Theoretical Breakdown

### Operating Principle Recap
A BLDC motor is a synchronous machine with permanent magnets on the rotor and a three-phase stator winding. Commutation is performed electronically using rotor position feedback (Hall sensors or sensorless back-EMF detection). The back-EMF is **trapezoidal**, not sinusoidal.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Speed-Torque Curve

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Per-Phase Voltage Equation:** Apply KVL to a single phase of the BLDC stator:
  $$V_t = E_b + I_a R_a + L \frac{di_a}{dt}$$
  where $E_b$ is the back-EMF, $R_a$ is the phase resistance, and $L$ is the phase inductance.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Back-EMF Relationship:** The back-EMF is proportional to speed:
  $$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$$
  where $K_e$ is the back-EMF constant (V┬Ęs/rad) and $\omega_m$ is the mechanical angular speed.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Torque Equation:** For a BLDC motor with trapezoidal back-EMF, the developed electromagnetic torque is:
  $$T_e = K_t \cdot I_a$$
  where $K_t$ is the torque constant (Nm/A). For SI consistency, $K_e = K_t$ numerically.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Combining for Speed-Torque Relation:** In the steady-state constant-torque region (below base speed), the controller maintains rated current:
  $$T_e \approx T_{rated} = \text{constant}$$
  This gives the **flat horizontal segment** from zero speed up to base speed.

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Constant Power Region (Field Weakening):** Beyond base speed, the back-EMF would exceed supply voltage. The controller advances commutation angle to weaken the effective flux:
  $$P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega_m = \text{constant}$$
  Hence:
  $$T_e \propto \frac{1}{\omega_m} = \frac{1}{N}$$
  This gives the **hyperbolic falling segment** above base speed.

- **Step 6 ŌĆö The "No-Load" Point:** At no-load, the back-EMF equals applied voltage:
  $$E_b = V_t \Rightarrow \omega_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{V_t}{K_e}$$
  This is the **maximum theoretical speed**.

### Speed-Torque Curve Regions (Bulleted Summary)

| Region | Speed Range | Torque Behavior | Control Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Torque Region** | $0 \leq \omega \leq \omega_{base}$ | $T_e = T_{max}$ (rated) | Current-limited, PWM chopping |
| **Constant Power Region** | $\omega_{base} \leq \omega \leq \omega_{max}$ | $T_e \propto 1/\omega$ | Field-weakening / Advance angle |
| **Series Field-Weakening Tail** | $\omega \approx \omega_{max}$ | $T_e \to 0$ | Free-running / friction-dominated |

---

## 2.2 Direct Torque Control (DTC) for BLDC ŌĆö Theoretical Breakdown

### Core Idea of DTC
DTC uses the **stator flux linkage vector** ($\Psi_s$) and the **electromagnetic torque** ($T_e$) as the direct control variables. The error between reference and estimated values is fed to **two-level or three-level hysteresis comparators**, whose outputs select the appropriate inverter switching state from a **predefined switching table**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of DTC for BLDC in EV

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Stator Flux Estimation:** The stator flux is estimated from measured voltages and currents:
  $$\Psi_s = \int (V_s - R_s I_s) \, dt$$
  This integration is done in the stationary $\alpha$-$\beta$ frame (no Park transform needed for some DTC variants).

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Torque Estimation:** Torque is estimated from flux and current cross-product:
  $$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot (\Psi_{\alpha} I_{\beta} - \Psi_{\beta} I_{\alpha})$$
  where $P$ is the number of pole pairs.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Hysteresis Comparators:**
  - **Flux comparator output:** $c_{\Psi} = +1$ (increase), $0$ (no change), $-1$ (decrease)
  - **Torque comparator output:** $c_T = +1$ (increase), $-1$ (decrease)

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Sector Identification:** The flux vector's angular position determines which of the **six sectors** ($S_1$ to $S_6$) the motor is operating in. The sector is calculated as:
  $$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\Psi_{\beta}}{\Psi_{\alpha}}\right)$$

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Switching Table Lookup:** Based on $(c_{\Psi}, c_T, S_k)$, the inverter switching vector $V_k$ is selected. A typical DTC switching table for a 2-level VSI gives **6 active vectors + 2 zero vectors**.

- **Step 6 ŌĆö Application to BLDC:** For BLDC, the commutation logic of the rotor position sensor (Hall signals) is **merged** with the DTC switching table, allowing **torque ripple minimization** in the commutation region.

### Advantages of DTC for EV (Bulleted)
- Ō£ģ **Ultra-fast torque response** (sub-millisecond) ŌĆö critical for vehicle safety during sudden braking/acceleration.
- Ō£ģ **No current loop** ŌĆö eliminates PI tuning complexity.
- Ō£ģ **No PWM modulator** ŌĆö direct inverter switching reduces computational load on the ECU.
- Ō£ģ **Inherent sensorless capability** ŌĆö flux estimation replaces encoder for medium-high speeds.
- ŌØī **Higher torque ripple and switching losses** ŌĆö a tradeoff for the speed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse DTC with FOC. Remember: **FOC uses current control in d-q frame**, whereas **DTC uses flux and torque control in stationary frame with hysteresis**.

---

## 2.3 Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) ŌĆö Working Principle

### Construction (Bulleted)
- **Stator:** Salient poles with concentrated phase windings. Typically 6/4, 8/6, or 12/8 pole configurations.
- **Rotor:** Salient poles made of laminated silicon steel ŌĆö **no magnets, no windings**.
- **Asymmetric Half-Bridge Inverter:** Each phase requires **two switches and two freewheeling diodes** (different from BLDC's three-phase bridge).

### Working Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Energize Phase A:** Current is injected into stator phase A. A magnetic field is established across the air gap.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Reluctance Minimization:** The rotor (a piece of iron) experiences a force pulling it toward the position where the **air gap reluctance is minimum** ŌĆö i.e., where rotor and stator poles are aligned with phase A.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Align at $\theta = 0$:** When the rotor pole is fully aligned with the energized stator pole, **inductance $L$ is maximum** ($L_a$). No further torque is produced.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Switch Off Phase A, Energize Phase B:** Just before alignment, phase A is de-energized and phase B is energized. The rotor continues to rotate toward alignment with phase B.

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Continuous Rotation:** The cyclic energization (A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ AŌĆ”) produces continuous rotation. The torque direction is **independent of current direction** ŌĆö only the **timing** matters.

### Torque Production Equation
The instantaneous electromagnetic torque in an SRM phase is:

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

This is the **most fundamental equation** in SRM theory. Key observations:

- $T_e \propto i^2$ ŌåÆ torque is **always positive** (or always negative in motoring/braking), regardless of current direction.
- $T_e \propto \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ ŌåÆ torque is produced **only when inductance is changing** with rotor position.
- $T_e = 0$ at **aligned ($\theta = 0$)** and **unaligned ($\theta = \pi/N_r$)** positions.

### SRM Operating Zones (Bulleted)
| Zone | Rotor Angle | Inductance Slope | Torque Sign | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductance Rising** | $-\pi/N_r < \theta < 0$ | $\frac{dL}{d\theta} > 0$ | Positive (motoring) | Energize phase |
| **Inductance Peak** | $\theta = 0$ | $\frac{dL}{d\theta} = 0$ | Zero | De-energize |
| **Inductance Falling** | $0 < \theta < \pi/N_r$ | $\frac{dL}{d\theta} < 0$ | Negative (regenerative) | Demagnetize / Brake |
| **Inductance Valley** | $\theta = \pi/N_r$ | $\frac{dL}{d\theta} = 0$ | Zero | Wait for next phase |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = K_e \omega_m$ | Back-EMF of BLDC | V |
| 2 | $T_e = K_t I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque of BLDC | N┬Ęm |
| 3 | $V_t = K_e \omega_m + I_a R_a$ | Steady-state KVL of BLDC phase | V |
| 4 | $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = V_t / K_e$ | No-load speed | rad/s |
| 5 | $P_{out} = T_e \omega_m$ | Mechanical output power | W |
| 6 | $\Psi_s = \int (V_s - R_s I_s) \, dt$ | Stator flux linkage (DTC) | Wb |
| 7 | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} (\Psi_{\alpha} I_{\beta} - \Psi_{\beta} I_{\alpha})$ | Torque from flux-current cross product | N┬Ęm |
| 8 | $T_{SRM} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ | SRM instantaneous torque | N┬Ęm |
| 9 | $\xi = L_a / L_u$ | SRM saliency ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $W_{co\text{-}energy} = \int_0^{i} \Psi(i, \theta) \, di$ | Field energy for SRM torque calc | J |
| 11 | $T_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T T(\theta) \, d\theta$ | Average torque per stroke | N┬Ęm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in industry-grade software like **ANSYS Maxwell**, **Motor-CAD**, and **JMAG** for simulating EV traction motors. The SRM torque equation $T \propto i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ is the **mathematical heart** of every SRM design.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Motor Type | Major EV Application | Reason for Selection |
|---|---|---|
| **BLDC** | Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV, Chevrolet Bolt | High power density, mature controllers |
| **BLDC with DTC** | Concept EVs, racing EVs (Formula Student) | Ultra-fast torque response |
| **SRM** | Renault Fluence Z.E., some mining EVs | Magnet-free, high-temp tolerant, fault-tolerant |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤ōś SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: BLDC Motor Speed-Torque Characteristic

### Given
- DC bus voltage: $V_t$ (V)
- Per-phase resistance: $R_a$ (╬®)
- Back-EMF constant: $K_e$ (V┬Ęs/rad)
- Torque constant: $K_t$ (Nm/A)
- Mechanical speed: $\omega_m$ (rad/s)

### To Derive
The explicit expression $T_e = f(\omega_m)$ in the constant-torque and constant-power regions.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the steady-state KVL for one phase (ignoring transient inductance term since $\frac{di}{dt} \to 0$ in steady state):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t &= E_b + I_a R_a \\
V_t &= K_e \omega_m + I_a R_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the armature current $I_a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a R_a &= V_t - K_e \omega_m \\
I_a &= \frac{V_t - K_e \omega_m}{R_a}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the torque equation $T_e = K_t I_a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= K_t \cdot \frac{V_t - K_e \omega_m}{R_a} \\
T_e &= \frac{K_t V_t}{R_a} - \frac{K_t K_e}{R_a} \omega_m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\frac{K_t K_e}{R_a}$ is the slope of the speed-torque line. Define the **no-load speed** (when $T_e = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \frac{K_t V_t}{R_a} - \frac{K_t K_e}{R_a} \omega_{no\text{-}load} \\
\omega_{no\text{-}load} &= \frac{V_t}{K_e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $\frac{K_t V_t}{R_a}$ is the **stall torque** (when $\omega_m = 0$):

$$
T_{stall} = \frac{K_t V_t}{R_a}
$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, the linear speed-torque characteristic in the constant-torque region is:

$$
\boxed{T_e = T_{stall} \left(1 - \frac{\omega_m}{\omega_{no\text{-}load}}\right)}
$$

**Step 7:** In the **constant-power region** (field weakening), power is held constant at $P_{rated}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rated} &= T_e \cdot \omega_m \\
T_e &= \frac{P_{rated}}{\omega_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This produces the hyperbolic decay.

### Final Compact Form

$$
T_e(\omega_m) =
\begin{cases}
T_{max} & ; \quad 0 \leq \omega_m \leq \omega_{base} \\
\dfrac{P_{rated}}{\omega_m} & ; \quad \omega_{base} < \omega_m \leq \omega_{max}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: SRM Average Torque

### Given
- Phase current (assumed flat-topped, constant magnitude $I$ during conduction): $i(\theta) = I$
- Inductance profile: $L(\theta)$ varies linearly between $L_u$ and $L_a$ over the stroke $\theta_{st} = \pi/N_r$

### To Derive
Average torque per stroke per phase.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with the instantaneous torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e(\theta) &= \frac{1}{2} i^2(\theta) \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** For a constant current $i = I$ during the active stroke (turn-on at $\theta_{on}$ to turn-off at $\theta_{off}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e(\theta) &= \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Integrate over one electrical stroke to get average torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= \frac{1}{\theta_{st}} \int_{\theta_{on}}^{\theta_{off}} T_e(\theta) \, d\theta \\
&= \frac{1}{\theta_{st}} \int_{\theta_{on}}^{\theta_{off}} \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\theta_{on}}^{\theta_{off}} \frac{dL}{d\theta} \, d\theta &= L(\theta_{off}) - L(\theta_{on}) \\
&= L_a - L_u
\end{aligned}
$$

(assuming turn-on at unaligned and turn-off at aligned position for ideal operation)

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= \frac{1}{2 \theta_{st}} I^2 (L_a - L_u) \\
&= \frac{I^2 (L_a - L_u)}{2 \cdot (\pi / N_r)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Simplify:

$$
\boxed{T_{avg} = \frac{N_r \cdot I^2 \cdot (L_a - L_u)}{2\pi}}
$$

where $N_r$ is the number of rotor poles.

**Step 7:** Interpretation:
- Torque scales with the **square of current** ŌåÆ permits aggressive overcurrent for short bursts (perfect for EV acceleration).
- Torque scales with **saliency ratio** $(L_a - L_u)$ ŌåÆ design goal is to **maximize this difference**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Quick Comparison

| Feature | BLDC Motor | BLDC with DTC | SRM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Permanent Magnets** | Yes (NdFeB) | Yes (NdFeB) | **No** |
| **Rotor Windings** | No | No | No |
| **Commutation** | Electronic (Hall-based) | Sensorless / Hybrid | Asymmetric half-bridge |
| **Torque Equation** | $T = K_t I$ | Same BLDC + DTC loop | $T = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ |
| **Speed-Torque Shape** | Linear + hyperbolic | Same | Highly nonlinear |
| **Torque Ripple** | Low (6-step commutation) | Reduced (DTC hysteresis) | **High** (inherent) |
| **Efficiency** | 90ŌĆō95% | 91ŌĆō96% | 85ŌĆō92% |
| **Cost** | Medium-High | Medium-High | **Low** |
| **Thermal Limit** | Magnet demagnetization | Same | Rotor + Stator hot spots |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Medium | Medium | **Very High** (independent phases) |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | High | High | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# ­¤ōś SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 BLDC Motor Drive ŌĆö Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery Pack Vdc 300 to 800 V] --> B[DC Link Capacitor]
    B --> C[Three-Phase VSI Inverter]
    C --> D[BLDC Motor Stator 3 Phase]
    D --> E[Rotor Position Sensor Hall Sensors]
    E --> F[Decoder Logic 6 Step Commutation]
    F --> G[Gate Driver Circuit]
    G --> C
    C --> H[Current Sensor Shunt or Hall Effect]
    H --> I[Protection Unit Overcurrent OV UV]
    I --> J[ECU Throttle Pedal Input]
    J --> K[PWM Generator]
    K --> G

    subgraph ControlLoop["Control and Feedback Loop"]
        F
        G
        H
        I
        J
        K
    end

    classDef power fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000

    class A,B,C,D power
    class E,F,G,H,I,J,K control
```

## 4.2 Direct Torque Control Scheme for BLDC Motor (EV Application)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Torque Tref from Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Estimated Torque Test from Flux and Current]
    B --> C[Torque Hysteresis Comparator cT plus 1 or minus 1]
    D[Reference Flux Psi ref] --> E[Estimated Flux Psi est from V and I integration]
    E --> F[Flux Hysteresis Comparator cPsi plus 1 zero or minus 1]
    G[Rotor Position Sector S1 to S6] --> H[Switching Table Lookup]

    C --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I[Inverter Switching Pulses S1 to S6]
    I --> J[BLDC Motor Inverter]
    J --> K[BLDC Motor Stator]
    K --> L[Speed and Torque Output to EV Wheels]
    K --> E
    K --> B

    classDef ref fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef hyst fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

    class A,D,G ref
    class B,C,E,F hyst
    class H,I,J,K,L out
```

## 4.3 Switched Reluctance Motor ŌĆö Working Principle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Battery Vdc] --> B[Asymmetric Half Bridge Inverter]
    B --> C1[Phase A Winding]
    B --> C2[Phase B Winding]
    B --> C3[Phase C Winding]
    C1 --> D1[Stator Pole Pair 1]
    C2 --> D2[Stator Pole Pair 2]
    C3 --> D3[Stator Pole Pair 3]
    D1 --> E[Salient Rotor Iron Core No Magnets]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Mechanical Shaft Output to EV Transmission]
    E --> G[Rotor Position Sensor Optical or Inductive]
    G --> H[Controller ECU]
    H --> I[Commutation Logic A then B then C]
    I --> B
    H --> J[Turn On and Turn Off Angle Calculator]
    J --> I
    F --> K[Wheels of Electric Vehicle]
    F --> L[Regenerative Braking Back EMF to Battery]
    L --> A

    classDef power fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef winding fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef rotor fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

    class A,B power
    class C1,C2,C3,D1,D2,D3 winding
    class E,F rotor
    class G,H,I,J,L control
```

## 4.4 BLDC Speed-Torque Characteristic ŌĆö Graphical Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Zero Speed Stall Torque Tstall] -->|Constant Torque Region| B[Base Speed omega base]
    B -->|Constant Power Region| C[Maximum Speed omega max]
    C -->|Free Run Region| D[No Load Speed]

    subgraph CurveShape["Speed vs Torque Curve Shape"]
        A
        B
        C
        D
    end

    classDef region1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef region2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef region3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

    class A,B region1
    class B,C region2
    class C,D region3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "draw" the speed-torque curve in the exam, **always label three regions explicitly** ŌĆö Constant Torque, Constant Power, and Free-Run. Marks are awarded for region identification.

## 4.5 SRM Inductance Profile and Torque Production Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unaligned Position Theta equals pi by N_r] -->|Inductance Rising dL by d theta greater than 0| B[Mid Stroke Position]
    B -->|Energize Phase Here for Motoring| C[Aligned Position Theta equals 0]
    C -->|Inductance Falling dL by d theta less than 0| D[Demagnetize for No Braking Torque]
    D --> E[Approaching Unaligned of Next Phase]

    subgraph TorqueProduction["SRM Torque Production per Stroke"]
        A
        B
        C
        D
        E
    end

    classDef zero fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```
