# ­¤ōś SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Sketch and explain the speed-torque characteristics of a BLDC motor used in EV application. Mention the significance of the constant torque and constant power regions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The BLDC motor speed-torque characteristic consists of **three regions**:

- **Region 1 ŌĆö Constant Torque Region** (0 to $\omega_{base}$): The controller limits current to rated value, so torque remains at $T_{max}$. This is essential for **EV launch, hill-climb, and low-speed traction**.

- **Region 2 ŌĆö Constant Power Region** ($\omega_{base}$ to $\omega_{max}$): Torque falls hyperbolically as $T \propto 1/\omega$. Achieved by **field-weakening** (advancing commutation angle). Useful for **high-speed cruising** of EVs on highways.

- **Region 3 ŌĆö No-Load / Free-Run Region**: Torque approaches zero as back-EMF approaches supply voltage at no-load speed $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = V_t / K_e$.

The linear speed-torque equation in Region 1 is:

$$T_e = \frac{K_t V_t}{R_a}\left(1 - \frac{\omega_m}{\omega_{no\text{-}load}}\right)$$

**Significance for EVs:** Provides **high starting torque** for acceleration and **wide constant power range** for top-speed operation, mimicking the ideal traction curve of an ICE vehicle.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Switched Reluctance Motor. Why is it considered suitable for EV applications?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** has a salient-pole rotor (with **no magnets or windings**) and a salient-pole stator with concentrated phase windings. When a stator phase is energized, the rotor experiences a force that moves it to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** (aligned position). Sequential energization of phases (A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ A...) produces continuous rotation. The instantaneous torque is:

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$$

**Suitability for EVs (3 reasons):**

- **No rare-earth magnets** ŌåÆ lower cost and supply-chain resilience.
- **High fault tolerance** ŌåÆ phases are electrically independent.
- **High temperature tolerance** ŌåÆ no magnet demagnetization issues.

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **internal choice**. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With necessary equations, derive the **speed-torque characteristic** of a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor drive used in an electric vehicle. Clearly mark the constant-torque and constant-power regions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Direct Torque Control (DTC) scheme** for a BLDC motor in an EV application with a neat block diagram. List **four advantages** of DTC over conventional Field-Oriented Control (FOC). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** State the assumptions: trapezoidal back-EMF, constant field flux (PM), balanced three phases. **(0.5 Mark)**

**Step 2:** Write the steady-state per-phase voltage equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_t &= E_b + I_a R_a \\
V_t &= K_e \omega_m + I_a R_a
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 Mark)**

**Step 3:** Solve for armature current:

$$I_a = \frac{V_t - K_e \omega_m}{R_a} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the torque equation $T_e = K_t I_a$:

$$T_e = \frac{K_t V_t}{R_a} - \frac{K_t K_e}{R_a} \omega_m \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 5:** Identify key points ŌĆö Stall torque $T_{stall} = K_t V_t / R_a$ and No-load speed $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = V_t / K_e$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 6:** Write the piecewise characteristic:

$$
T_e(\omega_m) =
\begin{cases}
T_{max} & ; \quad 0 \leq \omega_m \leq \omega_{base} \\
\dfrac{T_{max} \cdot \omega_{base}}{\omega_m} & ; \quad \omega_{base} < \omega_m \leq \omega_{max}
\end{cases}
$$

**(1.5 Marks)**

**Step 7:** Draw the labeled speed-torque curve (refer Section 4.4) showing all three regions. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö DTC Concept (1 Mark):** DTC directly regulates **stator flux linkage** and **electromagnetic torque** using hysteresis comparators and a switching table, eliminating current loops and PWM modulators.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Block Diagram Description (2 Marks):** Reference the diagram in Section 4.2. List the blocks:
- Reference torque (from accelerator pedal)
- Estimated torque (from flux-current cross product)
- Torque hysteresis comparator (output $c_T = \pm 1$)
- Reference and estimated flux (from V-I integration)
- Flux hysteresis comparator (output $c_\Psi = +1, 0, -1$)
- Rotor position sector ($S_1$ to $S_6$)
- Switching table lookup
- Inverter output to BLDC motor

**Step 3 ŌĆö Switching Logic (1 Mark):** State that for each sector and hysteresis output combination, a specific voltage vector from the inverter's 6 active + 2 zero vectors is applied.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Advantages of DTC over FOC (3 Marks):**
- Ō£ģ Ultra-fast torque dynamic response (~┬Ąs to ms)
- Ō£ģ No need for current PI controllers or PWM modulator
- Ō£ģ Reduced parameter sensitivity (no $L_d, L_q$ dependency)
- Ō£ģ Inherent sensorless flux estimation capability

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** with a diagram. Derive the expression for **average electromagnetic torque** per stroke, starting from the co-energy principle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare BLDC, SRM, and Induction Motor (IM) drives for EV applications based on **any seven parameters**. Justify why SRM is gaining popularity in magnet-free EV designs. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Construction (1 Mark):** Salient stator poles with concentrated windings (e.g., 6/4, 8/6 configuration) and salient rotor (no magnets/windings).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Working Principle (2 Marks):** When a phase is energized, the rotor moves toward the position of **minimum reluctance** to complete the magnetic path. Sequential switching of phases (AŌåÆBŌåÆCŌåÆA) produces continuous rotation. The torque direction is independent of current polarity ŌĆö only the **timing of switching** matters.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Co-Energy Derivation (2 Marks):** The co-energy is:

$$W'_{co} = \int_0^{i} \Psi(i, \theta) \, di = \int_0^{i} L(\theta) i \, di = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$$

Differentiating with respect to $\theta$ at constant $i$:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Average Torque (2 Marks):** For a constant current $I$ during the stroke $\theta_{st} = \pi/N_r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= \frac{1}{\theta_{st}} \int_0^{\theta_{st}} \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} \, d\theta \\
&= \frac{1}{2 \theta_{st}} I^2 (L_a - L_u) \\
&= \frac{N_r I^2 (L_a - L_u)}{2\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (1 Mark per row, select 7 rows = 7 Marks):**

| Parameter | BLDC | SRM | Induction Motor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Permanent Magnets** | Yes (NdFeB) | **No** | No |
| **Rotor Construction** | Surface/Interior PM | Salient Iron | Squirrel cage / Wound |
| **Torque Density** | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Efficiency** | 92ŌĆō96% | 85ŌĆō92% | 88ŌĆō93% |
| **Torque Ripple** | Low | **High** | Medium |
| **Cost** | Medium-High | **Low** | Low |
| **Temperature Tolerance** | Limited by magnets | **Excellent** | Good |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Medium | **Excellent** | Poor |
| **Controller Complexity** | Medium | High | Medium |

**Justification for SRM Popularity in Magnet-Free EV Designs:**
- Ō£ģ **Geopolitical supply chain independence** from Chinese-controlled rare-earth mines.
- Ō£ģ **No demagnetization risk** at high temperatures (no magnets to lose).
- Ō£ģ **Excellent fault tolerance** ŌĆö phase failures don't cascade.
- Ō£ģ **High starting torque** suitable for EV launch.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤øæ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DTC with FOC** ŌĆö Many students write "DTC uses PWM" ŌĆö it does NOT use PWM. It uses direct hysteresis-based switching. **(-2 Marks penalty)**
> 2. **Forgetting to mention that SRM torque is $i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$** ŌĆö this is the **single most important equation** in SRM. Omitting it costs **-3 Marks**.
> 3. **Writing the BLDC speed-torque equation without identifying $T_{stall}$ and $\omega_{no\text{-}load}$** ŌĆö these are key boundary points examiners look for. **(-1.5 Marks)**
> 4. **Not drawing the speed-torque curve** in Part B answers ŌĆö even a hand-drawn rough sketch with **three labeled regions** fetches full marks.
> 5. **Using "BLDC and PMSM interchangeably"** ŌĆö they are NOT the same. BLDC has **trapezoidal** back-EMF, PMSM has **sinusoidal** back-EMF. **(-1 Mark)**
> 6. **Omitting units in numerical substitutions** ŌĆö KTU strictly deducts for missing SI units.

---

## Ō£ģ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ­¤ö╣ **BLDC motor** has **trapezoidal back-EMF** and **PM rotor**. Commutation is electronic via Hall sensors.
- ­¤ö╣ **Speed-torque curve** has 3 regions: **Constant Torque ŌåÆ Constant Power (Field Weakening) ŌåÆ Free Run**.
- ­¤ö╣ **Stall torque** $T_{stall} = K_t V_t / R_a$ and **no-load speed** $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = V_t / K_e$ are the two boundary points.
- ­¤ö╣ **DTC (Direct Torque Control)** uses **stator flux linkage** and **electromagnetic torque** as direct control variables.
- ­¤ö╣ **DTC eliminates** current PI loops, Park/Clarke transforms, and PWM modulators.
- ­¤ö╣ **DTC switching table** has **6 active + 2 zero voltage vectors** selected via **2 hysteresis comparators** and **sector identifier**.
- ­¤ö╣ **DTC response time** is sub-millisecond ŌĆö ideal for **EV acceleration and regenerative braking**.
- ­¤ö╣ **SRM** has **no magnets, no rotor windings** ŌĆö doubly salient, singly excited.
- ­¤ö╣ **SRM torque equation** is $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ ŌĆö torque is **independent of current direction**.
- ­¤ö╣ **SRM average torque** is $T_{avg} = \frac{N_r I^2 (L_a - L_u)}{2\pi}$ ŌĆö scales with **square of current**.
- ­¤ö╣ **SRM saliency ratio** $\xi = L_a / L_u$ is **5ŌĆō10** in good designs.
- ­¤ö╣ **SRM uses asymmetric half-bridge inverter** ŌĆö 2 switches + 2 freewheeling diodes per phase.
- ­¤ö╣ **SRM advantages** for EVs: magnet-free, fault-tolerant, high-temp, low cost.
- ­¤ö╣ **SRM disadvantage**: high torque ripple and acoustic noise.
- ­¤ö╣ **EV traction curve ideal shape**: **constant torque at low speed + constant power at high speed** ŌĆö BLDC naturally provides this.
- ­¤ö╣ **Field weakening** is achieved by **advancing the commutation angle** of the inverter.
- ­¤ö╣ **Inductance profile** of SRM has **rising** (motoring), **peak at aligned**, **falling** (regenerative), and **valley at unaligned** regions.
- ­¤ö╣ **KTU focus areas**: derivations of $T_{stall}, \omega_{no\text{-}load}$, DTC switching table, and SRM torque equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed-Torque Characteristics, Microprocessor Based Control for SRM
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUT503 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES-DRIVES AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Speed-Torque Characteristics, Microprocessor Based Control for SRM

# Speed-Torque Characteristics & Microprocessor Based Control of Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)**: A doubly salient, singly excited electric motor that produces torque by virtue of the variable reluctance principle, where the rotor tends to move to a position of minimum reluctance (maximum inductance) when a phase is energized.

The **speed-torque characteristics** of an SRM describe the relationship between the developed electromagnetic torque and the rotational speed of the rotor under various control strategies. Due to the doubly salient construction and magnetic saturation, these characteristics are highly **non-linear** and are typically divided into three distinct operating regions, each governed by a specific control philosophy.

**Microprocessor-Based Control of SRM** refers to the closed-loop electronic control architecture wherein a digital processor (microcontroller, DSP, or FPGA) is employed to manage the turn-on angle ($\theta_{on}$), turn-off angle ($\theta_{off}$), and phase current regulation by computing optimal switching instants in real time based on rotor position feedback.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy ŌĆö The Salient Pole Magnet Pull**

Imagine a **magnet with iron teeth** (the stator) and a **piece of soft iron with similar teeth** (the rotor) placed near each other but not touching. The iron rotor always wants to "snap" into the position where it can "hide" the most flux ŌĆö like a paperclip jumping toward a magnet. Now, instead of leaving the magnet always on, we **switch it ON only when the rotor teeth are about to align**, and switch it OFF just before they actually align. By controlling *when* we turn the magnet ON and OFF, we can precisely control the **push (torque)** and **speed** of the rotor.

- The **stator teeth** = the magnet
- The **rotor teeth** = the iron piece that gets attracted
- **Switching ON/OFF** = the microprocessor deciding *when* to energize
- **Phase current** = the strength of the magnetic pull
- **Rotor position sensor** = the "eyes" telling the microprocessor where the rotor is

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics**
> - Standard base speeds for SRM drives: **1500 rpm** (for 4-pole) to **3000 rpm** (for 2-pole) machines
> - Saturation flux density of typical electrical steel: **1.6 T to 1.8 T**
> - Hysteresis current band width: **┬▒ 1% to ┬▒ 5%** of rated current
> - Control update rate (DSP): **typically 20 kHz to 50 kHz**
> - Encoder resolution for SRM drives: **typically 10-bit to 12-bit** (512 to 4096 pulses/rev)

### 1.3 Why Speed-Torque Characteristics Matter in EVs

In an electric vehicle, the SRM drive must deliver:
1. **High starting torque** (for hill-climb and acceleration from rest)
2. **Constant power** over a wide speed range (for cruising)
3. **Extended field-weakening region** (for high-speed operation)

This three-region characteristic closely matches the **ideal traction requirement**, making SRM an attractive candidate for EV applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Torque Equation of SRM

The instantaneous electromagnetic torque produced by a single phase of an SRM is governed by the **co-energy principle**:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W_{co}(\theta, i)}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{i = \text{const}}$$

For a non-linear magnetic system (with saturation), the co-energy is:

$$W_{co}(\theta, i) = \int_{0}^{i} \psi(\theta, i')\, di'$$

Hence, the general non-linear torque equation is:

$$T_e(\theta, i) = \int_{0}^{i} \frac{\partial \psi(\theta, i')}{\partial \theta}\, di'$$

For the **idealized linear case** (no saturation):

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

Where:
- $T_e$ = Electromagnetic torque (N┬Ęm)
- $i$ = Phase current (A)
- $L(\theta)$ = Phase inductance as a function of rotor position (H)
- $\psi$ = Flux linkage (Wb)

## 2.2 Inductance Profile of SRM

The phase inductance varies periodically with rotor position between two extreme values:
- $L_{min}$: Inductance at **unaligned** position (rotor pole away from stator pole)
- $L_{max}$: Inductance at **aligned** position (rotor pole exactly under stator pole)

In the linear region, the rising inductance region ($\frac{dL}{d\theta} > 0$) corresponds to **motoring torque**, while the falling inductance region ($\frac{dL}{d\theta} < 0$) corresponds to **generating/regenerative braking torque**.

## 2.3 Operating Regions of the Speed-Torque Characteristic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The SRM speed-torque characteristic is divided into **three distinct regions**, each with its own control strategy. This is a frequently tested topic in KTU examinations.

### Region I ŌĆö Constant Torque Region (Below Base Speed $\omega_{base}$)

- **Speed range**: $0 \le \omega \le \omega_{base}$
- **Control Strategy**: **Current Chopping Control (CCC)** / Hysteresis Current Control
- **Back-EMF is low** ŌåÆ Inductance limits the rate of current rise
- The phase current is maintained within a hysteresis band around a reference value
- Turn-on angle $\theta_{on}$ and turn-off angle $\theta_{off}$ are kept nearly constant
- Developed torque is approximately constant (limited by current limit)

**Chopping Operation Logic:**
1. When current exceeds upper limit $I_{max}$ ŌåÆ switch OFF
2. When current falls below lower limit $I_{min}$ ŌåÆ switch ON
3. This regulates current within the band $\Delta I = I_{max} - I_{min}$

### Region II ŌĆö Constant Power Region (Above Base Speed)

- **Speed range**: $\omega_{base} \le \omega \le \omega_{max,1}$ (typically 2├Ś to 3├Ś base speed)
- **Control Strategy**: **Single Pulse Operation (Angle Control)**
- **Back-EMF is high** ŌåÆ current naturally limits itself
- No chopping required; each phase is fired for one complete pulse per stroke
- $\theta_{on}$ is **advanced** (moved earlier) and $\theta_{off}$ is **retarded** (moved later)
- Power remains constant ŌåÆ torque decreases as $1/\omega$

**Fundamental Relation in Constant Power Region:**

$$P = T_e \cdot \omega = \text{constant} \implies T_e \propto \frac{1}{\omega}$$

### Region III ŌĆö Series/Characteristic (Natural) Region (High Speed)

- **Speed range**: $\omega_{max,1} \le \omega \le \omega_{max,2}$
- **Control Strategy**: **Deep Single Pulse with Advanced Angle Control**
- The motor operates deep in magnetic saturation
- The characteristic follows a natural series law: $T_e \propto 1/\omega^2$
- Limits are set by mechanical considerations (centrifugal force, bearing) and the link voltage

$$T_e \propto \frac{1}{\omega^2}$$

## 2.4 Speed-Torque Envelope (Analytical Summary)

The complete speed-torque envelope of an SRM is governed by the following boundary conditions:

**Voltage Constraint** (from converter):
$$V = R \cdot i + \frac{d\psi(\theta, i)}{dt}$$

**Current Constraint** (from thermal and converter limits):
$$i \le I_{max}$$

**Mechanical Constraint** (from rotor dynamics):
$$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = T_e - T_L - B\omega$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Linear torque equation | N┬Ęm |
| 2 | $T_e = \int_{0}^{i} \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} di'$ | Non-linear (saturated) torque | N┬Ęm |
| 3 | $V = R i + \frac{d\psi}{dt}$ | Phase voltage equation | V |
| 4 | $E = \omega \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}$ | Back-EMF (motional) | V |
| 5 | $L_{avg} = \frac{L_{max} + L_{min}}{2}$ | Average phase inductance | H |
| 6 | $\beta_s = \theta_r - \theta_{min}$ | Stator pole arc (typical) | rad |
| 7 | $\beta_r = \theta_r - \theta_{max}$ | Rotor pole arc | rad |
| 8 | $T_{max,linear} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 (L_{max} - L_{min})$ | Peak linear torque | N┬Ęm |
| 9 | $\theta_{adv} = \theta_{on,ref} - \theta_{on,aligned}$ | Turn-on advance angle | rad |
| 10 | $P = T \cdot \omega = \text{const}$ | Constant power relation | W |
| 11 | $T \propto 1/\omega$ | Torque in CP region | N┬Ęm |
| 12 | $T \propto 1/\omega^2$ | Torque in series region | N┬Ęm |
| 13 | $\Delta I = I_{max} - I_{min}$ | Hysteresis band | A |
| 14 | $f_{sw} = \frac{1}{T_{chop}}$ | Chopping frequency | Hz |
| 15 | $J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = T_e - T_L$ | Mechanical equation | N┬Ęm |
| 16 | $\omega_{base} = \frac{V_{DC}}{k \cdot \psi_{max}}$ | Base speed | rad/s |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this matters in the EV industry:**
> - **Tesla Model 3 (rumored) and several Renault models** have explored SRM drives due to their **absence of rare-earth magnets**
> - **TATA Motors** and **Mahindra Electric** have published research on SRM for Indian 2-wheeler and 3-wheeler EV applications
> - The three-region characteristic makes SRM **ideal for traction** because ICE vehicles have similar torque-speed demand (high torque at low speed, falling torque at high speed)
> - **Microprocessor control** enables implementation of sophisticated strategies like **Direct Torque Control (DTC)** and **Torque Sharing Functions (TSF)** for vibration reduction

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Torque from Co-Energy

**Step 1 ŌĆö Statement of Co-Energy Function**

The magnetic co-energy $W_{co}$ for a single phase is defined as:

$$W_{co}(\theta, i) = \int_{0}^{i} \psi(\theta, i')\, di'$$

*Logic*: Co-energy is a mathematical construct complementary to stored magnetic energy, particularly useful in systems with non-linear magnetics because it allows torque computation at constant current.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate Co-Energy with Respect to Rotor Position**

The electromagnetic torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to position at constant current:

$$T_e(\theta, i) = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \int_{0}^{i} \psi(\theta, i')\, di'$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Interchange the Order of Integration and Differentiation**

By Leibniz's rule (since the upper limit $i$ is constant with respect to $\theta$):

$$T_e(\theta, i) = \int_{0}^{i} \frac{\partial \psi(\theta, i')}{\partial \theta}\, di'$$

*Logic*: This interchange is valid because the limits of integration are independent of $\theta$ in the inner integral.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Linear (Unsaturated) Case**

If the magnetic circuit is linear, $\psi(\theta, i) = L(\theta) \cdot i$. Then:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = i \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

Substituting:

$$T_e(\theta, i) = \int_{0}^{i} i' \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}\, di' = \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} \int_{0}^{i} i'\, di'$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Evaluate the Integral**

$$T_e(\theta, i) = \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{i^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{T_e(\theta, i) = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}$$

*Logic*: This is the **fundamental linear torque equation** of an SRM. The torque is independent of current direction ŌĆö only magnitude matters, which is why SRM converters use unipolar switches.

## 3.2 Derivation: Phase Voltage Equation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to Phase Winding**

$$V = R \cdot i + \frac{d\psi(\theta, i)}{dt}$$

*Logic*: The applied DC link voltage equals the resistive drop plus the rate of change of flux linkage.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Chain Rule of Differentiation**

The flux linkage is a function of *both* rotor position $\theta$ and current $i$. So:

$$\frac{d\psi}{dt} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial i} \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Identify Each Term**

$$V = R \cdot i + \underbrace{\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial i}}_{L_{inc}} \cdot \frac{di}{dt} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}}_{\text{back-EMF coeff.}} \cdot \omega$$

Where $\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$ is the angular velocity.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange in Standard Form**

$$V = R \cdot i + L_{inc}(\theta, i) \frac{di}{dt} + E(\theta, i, \omega)$$

Where the back-EMF is:

$$\boxed{E = \omega \cdot \frac{\partial \psi(\theta, i)}{\partial \theta}}$$

*Logic*: The term $L_{inc}$ is the **incremental inductance** and changes with both position and current (non-linear). The back-EMF term dominates at high speeds, while the $L_{inc}\frac{di}{dt}$ term dominates at low speeds.

## 3.3 Derivation: Analytical Shape of the Torque-Speed Envelope

**Step 1 ŌĆö Neglect Resistance (High-Speed Approximation)**

For $R \cdot i \ll V$, the voltage equation simplifies to:

$$V \approx L_{inc} \frac{di}{dt} + \omega \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Normalize with Respect to Base Speed**

Define the base speed as:

$$\omega_{base} = \frac{V_{DC}}{k \cdot \psi_{max}}$$

Where $k$ is a machine constant.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Three Region Analysis**

**Region I** ($0 \le \omega \le \omega_{base}$): Current is limited by **chopping**. Since $V > E$:

$$T \approx T_{max} = \frac{1}{2} I_{max}^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}\bigg|_{max}$$

Thus, **T = constant** (limited by current).

**Region II** ($\omega_{base} < \omega \le 2\omega_{base}$): Single pulse, with $\theta_{on}$ advanced. Power is:

$$P = T \cdot \omega = V \cdot I_{avg} \approx \text{const.}$$

Therefore:

$$T = \frac{P_{rated}}{\omega}$$

**Region III** ($\omega > 2\omega_{base}$): Deep saturation, the flux linkage cannot grow fast enough:

$$T \propto \frac{1}{\omega^2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Unified Envelope Equation**

$$\boxed{T(\omega) = \begin{cases} T_{max}, & 0 \le \omega \le \omega_{base} \\ \dfrac{P_{rated}}{\omega}, & \omega_{base} < \omega \le 2\omega_{base} \\ \dfrac{k_{s}}{\omega^2}, & \omega > 2\omega_{base} \end{cases}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Control Strategy vs. Region

| Region | Speed Range | Control Mode | Variables Controlled | Switching Frequency |
|--------|-------------|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| I ŌĆö Constant Torque | $0$ to $\omega_{base}$ | Current Chopping Control (CCC) | Current $i$ within hysteresis band | High (kHz) |
| II ŌĆö Constant Power | $\omega_{base}$ to $2\omega_{base}$ | Single Pulse / Angle Control | $\theta_{on}$ advanced, $\theta_{off}$ fixed | Low (one pulse per stroke) |
| III ŌĆö Series Region | $2\omega_{base}$ to $\omega_{max}$ | Deep Single Pulse | $\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$ both advanced | One pulse per stroke |

## 3.5 Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem**: A 3-phase SRM has $L_{max} = 250$ mH, $L_{min} = 50$ mH. The rated current is $I = 10$ A. Calculate the peak torque in the linear region.

**Solution**:

The peak linear torque occurs at maximum $\frac{dL}{d\theta}$. Assuming a linear inductance profile over the rising region:

$$T_{peak} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 (L_{max} - L_{min})$$

$$T_{peak} = \frac{1}{2} \times (10)^2 \times (0.250 - 0.050)$$

$$T_{peak} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 0.200$$

$$\boxed{T_{peak} = 10 \text{ N┬Ęm}}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the term "constant torque region" of an SRM speed-torque characteristic. What control strategy is employed in this region?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **constant torque region** is the low-speed operating zone of an SRM, ranging from standstill to the **base speed** $\omega_{base}$. In this region, the back-EMF is low, so the rate of rise of phase current is limited mainly by the winding inductance. To prevent the current from exceeding safe limits, **Current Chopping Control (CCC)** ŌĆö also called **Hysteresis Current Control** ŌĆö is used. The current is regulated within a band of $I_{min}$ to $I_{max}$, and the developed torque remains approximately constant at $T_{max}$. The turn-on angle $\theta_{on}$ and turn-off angle $\theta_{off}$ are kept nearly constant in this mode. (3 marks)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Q:** Why is single pulse mode used in the high-speed operating region of an SRM?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> In the high-speed region (above base speed), the back-EMF becomes very large and approaches the DC link voltage. Under this condition, the **chopping action is no longer possible** because the switches cannot be turned off fast enough to limit the current ŌĆö the current naturally stays below the rated value due to the high back-EMF. Therefore, **single pulse mode** (also called angle control) is employed, where each phase is energized for one complete pulse per stroke. The turn-on angle $\theta_{on}$ is advanced to ensure sufficient current builds up before the rising inductance region, and the turn-off angle $\theta_{off}$ is retarded. The torque in this region falls as $1/\omega$ (constant power) or $1/\omega^2$ (deep saturation). (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 ŌĆō Model Paper]`

**Q:** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete speed-torque characteristics** of a Switched Reluctance Motor. Discuss the three operating regions and the corresponding control strategies in detail.

**Model Answer ŌĆö Sub-parts (a) and (b):**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Speed-Torque Curve and Description [7 Marks]**

The speed-torque characteristic of an SRM drive is divided into **three operating regions** as shown below.

**Region I ŌĆö Constant Torque Region** (0 to $\omega_{base}$):
- Back-EMF is small
- **Current Chopping Control (CCC)** is used
- Phase current is regulated within a hysteresis band
- $\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$ are kept constant
- Torque remains at $T_{max}$ (limited by current limit)
- Used for **starting and low-speed traction**

**Region II ŌĆö Constant Power Region** ($\omega_{base}$ to $2\omega_{base}$):
- Back-EMF rises with speed
- **Single pulse operation** (angle control) takes over
- $\theta_{on}$ is **advanced** (moved towards unaligned position)
- $\theta_{off}$ may be **retarded** (moved towards aligned position)
- Power $P = T\omega$ remains constant, so $T \propto 1/\omega$
- Ideal for **cruising in EVs**

**Region III ŌĆö Series (Characteristic) Region** ($> 2\omega_{base}$):
- Deep magnetic saturation
- Both $\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$ are advanced
- $T \propto 1/\omega^2$
- Limited by **mechanical safety** and **link voltage**
- Used for **high-speed cruising** on highways

**Diagram Description (to be drawn in exam):**
```
T
|
T_max |________
|        \
|         \  Region II (T proportional 1/omega)
|          \___________
|                       \ Region III (T prop 1/omega squared)
|                        \___________
|________________________________________ omega
   0      omega_base      2 omega_base   omega_max
```

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Control Strategies and Why SRM Suits EVs [7 Marks]**

**Control Strategies in Detail:**

**1. Current Chopping Control (CCC):**
- Used in Region I
- Compares actual current $i$ with reference band $[I_{min}, I_{max}]$
- When $i > I_{max}$ ŌåÆ switches turn OFF (freewheeling)
- When $i < I_{min}$ ŌåÆ switches turn ON (energizing)
- This produces a sawtooth current waveform
- Effective in **limiting inrush current** at start

**2. Single Pulse / Angle Control (APC ŌĆö Angular Position Control):**
- Used in Regions II and III
- Only one pulse per phase per electrical cycle
- $\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$ are programmed as functions of speed and torque
- Often implemented using a **look-up table** in the microprocessor memory
- Reduces switching losses at high speed

**3. PWM Control (used at intermediate speeds):**
- Fixed $\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$, duty cycle varied by PWM
- Useful for fine control of average current

**Why SRM Suits EV Applications:**

| EV Requirement | SRM Feature |
|----------------|-------------|
| High starting torque | Region I (CCC) provides $T_{max}$ at zero speed |
| Wide constant power range | Region II extends to 2ŌĆō3├Ś base speed |
| No rare-earth magnets | Rotor has **no magnets or windings** ŌĆö low cost, robust |
| Fault tolerance | Independent phases; motor can run on 2 of 3 phases |
| High efficiency in field-weakening | Natural series characteristic (Region III) |
| Suitable for harsh environments | Rotor runs **cool** (no copper losses) |

The **natural series characteristic** of SRM closely matches the **ideal traction curve** required for electric vehicles, making it one of the most attractive motor choices for next-generation EVs. (7 marks)

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Q:** Describe the **microprocessor-based control scheme** for a Switched Reluctance Motor drive. With a block diagram, explain the function of each block in detail.

**Model Answer ŌĆö Sub-parts (a) and (b):**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Block Diagram Description [7 Marks]**

A microprocessor-based SRM control system consists of the following major functional blocks:

**1. Reference Input Block:**
- Accepts **speed reference** $\omega_{ref}$ from accelerator pedal (in EV)
- Or **torque reference** $T_{ref}$ from driver demand
- May be derived from a torque map or pedal position sensor

**2. Microprocessor / DSP Controller:**
- The "brain" of the system (e.g., TI TMS320F28335, dsPIC, or ARM Cortex-M4)
- Runs the control algorithm at high frequency (typically **20ŌĆō50 kHz**)
- Computes:
  - Speed error: $e_\omega = \omega_{ref} - \omega_{actual}$
  - Torque demand via PI controller: $T^* = K_p e_\omega + K_i \int e_\omega\, dt$
  - Current reference from torque-to-current map
  - Switching signals to converter

**3. Rotor Position Sensor Block:**
- **Optical encoder** or **resolver** mounted on the rotor shaft
- Provides absolute position $\theta$ and direction of rotation
- Resolution: typically **10-bit to 12-bit**
- Used to determine the **commutation instants** ($\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$)

**4. Current Sensing Block:**
- **Hall-effect sensors** or **shunt resistors** in each phase leg
- Provides real-time current feedback to the controller
- Used for **hysteresis current control** in Region I

**5. Look-Up Table (LUT) Block:**
- Stored in the microprocessor memory
- Maps the optimal $(\theta_{on}, \theta_{off})$ for each $(\omega, T)$ pair
- Pre-computed using **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** or off-line optimization
- Real-time access during operation

**6. Asymmetric Half-Bridge Converter:**
- One converter per phase
- Two switches and two freewheeling diodes per phase
- Provides independent phase control
- Allows **hard chopping**, **soft chopping**, and **demagnetization** modes

**7. Gate Driver Circuit:**
- Isolates the low-voltage control signals from high-power switches
- Provides level shifting and short-circuit protection
- Drives **IGBTs** or **MOSFETs** in the converter

**8. Protection & Diagnostics Block:**
- Over-current protection
- Over-voltage protection (DC link)
- Over-temperature monitoring
- Rotor position sensor fault detection

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in exam):**
```
   [Ref: omega_ref, T_ref]
              |
              v
   +------------------+
   |  Microprocessor  | <-- [Rotor Position Sensor]
   |  (DSP/MCU)       | <-- [Current Sensors]
   +------------------+
         |  |  |
         v  v  v
   [Gate Drivers]
         |
         v
   [Asymmetric Half-Bridge Converter] ---> [SRM Phases]
         ^
         |
   [DC Link V_dc]
```

(7 marks)

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Working Principle and Hysteresis Current Control [7 Marks]**

**Working Principle (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** The driver sets a torque/speed reference based on accelerator pedal position.

**Step 2:** The microprocessor reads the rotor position $\theta$ from the encoder.

**Step 3:** The microprocessor consults the LUT to determine the optimal $\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$ for the current operating point.

**Step 4:** When the rotor reaches $\theta_{on}$, the microprocessor sends a turn-ON command to the gate drivers of the appropriate phase.

**Step 5:** The phase current rises. The current sensor monitors $i$.

**Step 6 (Hysteresis Control in Region I):**
- If $i \le I_{min}$ ŌåÆ both switches ON (magnetizing mode)
- If $i > I_{max}$ ŌåÆ both switches OFF (freewheeling mode)
- If $i > I_{max}$ (hard chopping variant) ŌåÆ only one switch OFF
- The current oscillates between $I_{min}$ and $I_{max}$

**Step 7:** When the rotor reaches $\theta_{off}$, both switches are turned OFF. The current decays through the diodes (demagnetization mode).

**Step 8:** The next phase is commutated based on rotor position, and the cycle repeats.

**Commutation Sequence for 3-Phase SRM (Typical):**
- Phase A fires at $\theta_{on,A}$ and turns off at $\theta_{off,A}$
- Phase B fires $15┬░$ later (mechanical) for a 6/4 pole machine
- Phase C fires another $15┬░$ later

**Modes of Chopping:**
1. **Hard Chopping:** Both switches of a phase turn ON/OFF together. Faster demagnetization, higher ripple.
2. **Soft Chopping:** Only one switch is chopped; the other remains ON. Smoother current, lower ripple, but slower demagnetization.

**Software Algorithm (Pseudocode in KTU Answer):**
```
Read omega_ref from driver
Read theta from encoder
Read i_phase from current sensor
Compute T_ref from torque map
Compute I_ref from T_ref (using lookup)
IF i_phase > I_max THEN turn OFF both switches
IF i_phase < I_min THEN turn ON both switches
IF theta > theta_off THEN demagnetize
IF theta == theta_on THEN energize next phase
Update PWM duty cycle
Loop at 20 kHz
```

**Advantages of Microprocessor Control:**
- Precise and fast switching decisions
- Adaptive control of $\theta_{on}$ and $\theta_{off}$
- Implementation of advanced algorithms (DTC, TSF, neural control)
- Self-diagnostics and fault tolerance
- Real-time optimization for efficiency

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing regions:** Many students incorrectly state that current chopping is used at *high* speed. **Remember: CCC is for low speed (Region I); angle control is for high speed (Region II & III).**
> 2. **Forgetting the 1/Žē law:** In Region II, the torque vs. speed relation is $T \propto 1/\omega$ ŌĆö a *hyperbola*, not linear. Plot it correctly.
> 3. **Drawing only two regions:** KTU expects **all three regions** with **labels and control strategies** explicitly stated.
> 4. **In microprocessor block diagram:** Students often **miss the position sensor feedback**. This is a **mandatory feedback loop** ŌĆö the controller cannot work without it.
> 5. **Direction of flux in SRM:** Many students incorrectly assume current direction matters. **In SRM, only |i| matters** ŌĆö current can be unipolar. This is a **unique feature** worth highlighting.
> 6. **Mark allocation:** Don't spend more than 3ŌĆō4 pages on a 14-mark question. Use **bullet points and diagrams** for clarity.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Speed-Torque Characteristics & Microprocessor Control of SRM**

### **A. Core Concepts**
- Ō£ģ SRM produces torque by **variable reluctance** ŌĆö rotor moves to minimize reluctance
- Ō£ģ Torque equation: $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ (linear case)
- Ō£ģ Torque depends on **magnitude** of current, not direction (unipolar excitation)
- Ō£ģ The rotor has **no windings or magnets** ŌĆö robust, low-cost, fault-tolerant

### **B. Three Operating Regions**
- Ō£ģ **Region I (CT)**: $0$ to $\omega_{base}$ ŌåÆ Current Chopping Control ŌåÆ $T = T_{max}$ (constant)
- Ō£ģ **Region II (CP)**: $\omega_{base}$ to $2\omega_{base}$ ŌåÆ Angle Control ŌåÆ $T \propto 1/\omega$
- Ō£ģ **Region III (Series)**: $> 2\omega_{base}$ ŌåÆ Deep single pulse ŌåÆ $T \propto 1/\omega^2$

### **C. Microprocessor Control Essentials**
- Ō£ģ Asymmetric **half-bridge converter** ŌĆö one per phase
- Ō£ģ **Rotor position sensor** (encoder/resolver) ŌĆö mandatory feedback
- Ō£ģ **Current sensors** ŌĆö Hall-effect type
- Ō£ģ **Look-Up Table (LUT)** ŌĆö pre-computed optimal $\theta_{on}$, $\theta_{off}$
- Ō£ģ **PI controller** for speed/torque loop
- Ō£ģ **Hard chopping** vs. **soft chopping** modes
- Ō£ģ Control update rate: **20ŌĆō50 kHz** typical

### **D. Key Formulas to Memorize**
- Ō£ģ $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$
- Ō£ģ $V = R i + L_{inc} \frac{di}{dt} + \omega \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}$
- Ō£ģ $\omega_{base} = \frac{V_{DC}}{k \psi_{max}}$
- Ō£ģ $T_{max,linear} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 (L_{max} - L_{min})$

### **E. EV Application Context**
- Ō£ģ SRM's natural characteristic closely matches **ideal traction curve**
- Ō£ģ **No rare-earth magnets** ŌåÆ cost-effective, supply-chain independent
- Ō£ģ Suitable for **Indian EVs** ŌĆö 2W, 3W, and entry-level 4W segments
- Ō£ģ TATA and Mahindra have published R&D on SRM-based EV drivetrains

### **F. Frequently Asked Terms (for Part A)**
- Ō£ģ Hysteresis band, turn-on angle, turn-off angle, commutation
- Ō£ģ Hard chopping, soft chopping, demagnetization
- Ō£ģ Asymmetric half-bridge, magnetizing mode, freewheeling mode
- Ō£ģ Salient pole, doubly salient, variable reluctance
- Ō£ģ Co-energy, incremental inductance, back-EMF
